A **Hybrid Real-Time OS** (e.g., **QNX Neutrino with safety extensions**, **AUTOSAR Adaptive with POSIX**, or **RTLinux hybrid with a microkernel RT core under Linux**) satisfies all three:
- A small **microkernel-like RT core** runs the safety-critical hard RT tasks with predictable latency.
- A **monolithic-style service layer** (POSIX, networking, file system) runs the non-critical and soft RT functions.
- This matches the AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive architecture used by **BMW, Mercedes-Benz, and Toyota** in production vehicles.

**Conclusion [1 mark]:** The hybrid real-time architecture is the de facto industry standard for safety-critical cyber-physical systems including autonomous vehicles, avionics, and medical robotics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hybrid Systems**

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)
- ☐ **Definition:** Hybrid OS combines monolithic + microkernel features — critical services in kernel, less critical in user space.
- ☐ **Three Canonical Examples:** Windows NT, macOS XNU, Android (Linux + Binder IPC).
- ☐ **XNU = Mach 3.0 microkernel + FreeBSD monolithic layer + IOKit drivers**.
- ☐ **Trade-off Equation (Conceptual):** $C_{mono} < C_{hybrid} < C_{micro}$ (context switch cost).
- ☐ **Performance Gain:** $\alpha \in [0.6, 0.8]$ — hybrid achieves 60–80% reduction in IPC overhead vs. microkernel.
- ☐ **Hybrid RT Definition:** System with both hard RT and soft RT tasks.
- ☐ **Liu & Layland Bound:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- ☐ **Liu & Layland Limit:** $U_{max} \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$.

### Common KTU Board Question Patterns
- ☐ "Explain hybrid OS with diagram" — expects 5-layer architecture.
- ☐ "Compare Monolithic, Microkernel, Hybrid" — always in tabular form.
- ☐ "Why is Windows/macOS called hybrid?" — expect mention of selective in-kernel vs. user-space placement.
- ☐ "What is XNU? Explain its components" — Mach + FreeBSD + IOKit.
- ☐ "Check schedulability of given task set using Rate Monotonic" — apply Liu & Layland bound.
- ☐ "Give two examples of hybrid real-time systems" — autonomous vehicles, avionics, medical robots.

### Common Mistakes to Avoid (Board Deductions)
- ☐ ❌ Writing that XNU is a *pure* microkernel — it is **hybrid**.
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting to draw the **diagram** in architecture questions (–2 to –3 marks).
- ☐ ❌ Confusing **Android** with **pure Linux** — Android is hybrid.
- ☐ ❌ Applying Rate Monotonic to soft RT tasks — RM is only for hard RT.
- ☐ ❌ Stating Liu-Layland bound as *necessary* — it is **sufficient** only.

### Quick Mnemonics
- ☐ **"HIPS"** for Hybrid Layers: **H**AL → **I**n-kernel core → **P**luggable user services → **S**ervers/Apps.
- ☐ **"MFI"** for XNU: **M**ach + **F**reeBSD + **I**OKit.
- ☐ **"MSH"** for Hybrid OS Examples: **M**acOS, **S**erver (Windows), **H**ybrid Android.

---

topics.title: System Boot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Boot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🖥️ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **System Boot (Bootstrapping)** is the complete sequence of operations performed by a computer system from the instant the power supply is switched ON (or a reset signal is triggered) until the **Operating System (OS) kernel** is fully loaded into main memory (RAM) and the first user process (init/systemd) begins execution. The term derives from the expression *"pulling oneself up by one's own bootstraps"*, symbolising how a simple firmware loader successfully loads a vastly more complex software stack.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCITT403 – Operating Systems**, the boot process is the foundational bridge between **bare hardware** and the **executing OS**. It involves cooperation among three entities:

1. **Hardware (CPU, ROM, RAM, Disk Controllers)**
2. **Firmware (BIOS or UEFI)**
3. **Boot Loader (GRUB, LILO, Windows Boot Manager, etc.)**

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Opening" Metaphor

Imagine a five-star restaurant opening for the day:

| Restaurant Step | Computer System Boot Step |
|---|---|
| Staff unlocks the door and checks electricity & gas | **Power-On Self-Test (POST)** checks CPU, RAM, and devices |
| Chef turns on the kitchen stove, lights, exhaust | **BIOS/UEFI firmware** initialises hardware controllers |
| Head chef checks the recipe book (Master Menu) | **Boot Loader** locates the OS kernel image on disk |
| The full kitchen team arrives and starts cooking | **Kernel is loaded** into RAM and the OS begins execution |
| Waiter comes out and says *"Welcome, we're open!"* | **init / systemd (PID 1)** starts user-level services |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The computer cannot load the OS from disk because the disk controller itself is *not yet initialised*. This is the famous **"chicken-and-egg problem"** of booting — solved by storing a *tiny* initial program in non-volatile **ROM/Flash** memory that is *always* available, regardless of the state of the rest of the system.

---

## 🔑 Critical Terminology (Bolded Glossary)

- **Power-On Self-Test (POST):** A diagnostic routine executed by firmware to verify that essential hardware components (CPU registers, RAM, keyboard, video adapter) are functional.
- **Firmware:** Permanent, read-only software stored in **ROM / EEPROM / Flash memory** on the motherboard.
- **BIOS (Basic Input/Output System):** Legacy 16-bit firmware interface (since 1981) using the *INT 13h* disk services.
- **UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface):** Modern 32/64-bit firmware replacing BIOS, supporting **Secure Boot**, **GPT** partitioning, and larger disks.
- **MBR (Master Boot Record):** A **512-byte** sector (located at cylinder 0, head 0, sector 1) containing the first-stage boot code and partition table.
- **Boot Loader:** A small program (e.g., **GRUB2**, LILO, NTLDR) responsible for locating, loading, and transferring control to the OS kernel.
- **Bootstrap Loader:** The *initial* code in ROM that loads the *secondary* boot loader.
- **Kernel Image:** The compressed or uncompressed OS kernel binary (e.g., `/boot/vmlinuz` in Linux).
- **init / systemd (PID 1):** The **first user-mode process** spawned by the kernel, parent of all other processes.
- **Cold Boot vs. Warm Boot:** *Cold* = machine powered on from off state; *Warm* = soft restart (Ctrl+Alt+Del or `reboot` command), skips some POST steps.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "system boot" or "bootstrapping", examiners expect you to cover the **entire chain** — from POST → Firmware → MBR/GPT → Boot Loader → Kernel → init — in that logical order.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 🧩 The Boot Process — Decomposed into 6 Logical Phases

The boot process is a **strictly sequential, multi-stage pipeline**. Failure at any stage halts the entire sequence and produces a specific error symptom (e.g., blank screen, beeping, "Operating System Not Found").

---

### 🔹 Phase 1 — Power-On & Power-On Self-Test (POST)

- When the **power button is pressed**, the Power Supply Unit (PSU) sends a `POWER_GOOD` signal to the motherboard.
- The **CPU resets** and its instruction pointer is hard-wired to a fixed physical address (e.g., `0xFFFFFFF0` on x86 systems), which points into the **firmware ROM**.
- The **POST** routine executes, checking:
  - CPU registers and basic arithmetic logic unit (ALU) operations
  - **RAM integrity** (using memory pattern tests)
  - Video card and display initialization
  - Keyboard, mouse, and storage controllers
  - BIOS checksum validation
- If POST **fails**, the system emits **beep codes** (legacy BIOS) or displays an on-screen error (UEFI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why POST matters:** The OS cannot assume hardware is healthy. POST is the firmware's *contract of trust* with the rest of the boot chain.

---

### 🔹 Phase 2 — Firmware Initialization (BIOS vs. UEFI)

| Feature | **Legacy BIOS** | **UEFI (Unified EFI)** |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | IBM PC, 1981 | Intel, 2005 → standard since 2010s |
| Bit-width | **16-bit**, real mode | **32/64-bit**, protected/long mode |
| Disk Partitioning | **MBR** only (max 2 TB) | **GPT** (up to 9.4 ZB) |
| Boot Source | MBR sector only | EFI System Partition (ESP, FAT32) |
| Secure Boot | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Supported (signature verification) |
| User Interface | Text-based, blue screen | Graphical, mouse-driven |
| Boot Time | Slower | **Faster** (parallel initialization) |
| Driver Loading | Via Option ROMs | Native UEFI drivers |

**Common UEFI Vendors:** AMI (Aptio), Phoenix (SecureCore), Insyde H2O.

---

### 🔹 Phase 3 — Locating the Boot Device & Boot Sector

#### 🔸 Legacy BIOS Path — MBR Loading
- BIOS invokes **INT 13h** interrupt to read the **first sector (LBA 0)** of the configured boot device.
- The **512-byte MBR** contains:
  - **446 bytes** → Stage 1 boot code
  - **64 bytes** → Partition table (4 entries × 16 bytes)
  - **2 bytes** → Magic number `0xAA55` (validity signature)
- If `0xAA55` is missing, the system displays *"No bootable device"*.

#### 🔸 UEFI Path — ESP Loading
- Firmware reads the **GPT (GUID Partition Table)** and locates the **EFI System Partition** (typically FAT32, mounted as `/boot/efi` in Linux).
- It looks for an `.efi` application (e.g., `\EFI\ubuntu\grubx64.efi`).
- The UEFI firmware directly executes this PE-format executable.

---

### 🔹 Phase 4 — Boot Loader Execution

- **In BIOS/GRUB systems**, control is passed to the **Stage 1.5** loader (stored in the small gap between MBR and the first partition), which then loads **Stage 2 (GRUB2)**.
- GRUB2 displays its menu, reads `/boot/grub/grub.cfg`, and lets the user choose the kernel.
- The **boot loader's responsibilities** are:
  1. Locate the **kernel image** (e.g., `vmlinuz-5.15.0`) and the **initramfs/initrd** file.
  2. Load both into RAM.
  3. Decompress the kernel (if compressed with `gzip`/`xz`).
  4. Pass boot parameters via the **kernel command line** (e.g., `root=/dev/sda1 ro quiet splash`).
  5. Transfer CPU control to the kernel's entry point (`start_kernel()` in `init/main.c`).

---

### 🔹 Phase 5 — Kernel Initialization

The Linux kernel, once given control, performs the following in order:

1. **Architecture-specific setup** — enabling protected/long mode, setting up page tables.
2. **Decompresses itself** if the `decompress_kernel` stub wasn't run by GRUB.
3. **Initializes core subsystems:**
   - Scheduler
   - Memory management (sets up **virtual memory**, page tables, slab allocator)
   - Virtual File System (VFS) layer
   - Process management — creates **PID 1** (the init process)
4. **Mounts the root filesystem** using the `root=` kernel parameter, with help from `initramfs` (which contains block-device drivers needed before the real root is mountable).
5. **Transfers control to `/sbin/init`** (or `/lib/systemd/systemd` on modern systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **initramfs (Initial RAM Filesystem):** A tiny CPIO archive that is loaded into a `tmpfs` in RAM. It contains essential kernel modules (e.g., SATA, NVMe, filesystem drivers) required to mount the *real* root filesystem.

---

### 🔹 Phase 6 — Userspace Initialization (init / systemd)

- The kernel exec()'s the **init program** as the first user-space process.
- Modern Linux uses **systemd**, which:
  1. Mounts remaining filesystems (`/proc`, `/sys`, `/dev`).
  2. Starts **targets** (e.g., `multi-user.target` or `graphical.target`).
  3. Activates configured **services** (NetworkManager, SSH, D-Bus, etc.).
  4. Presents the **login prompt** (TTY) or **Display Manager** (GDM, LightDM, SDDM).
- Once the user logs in, the system is considered **fully booted**.

---

## 📊 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Table Format)

| **Stage** | **Component** | **Storage** | **Size (Typical)** | **Key Function** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Firmware POST | ROM/Flash | 8–32 MB | Hardware self-test |
| 2 | BIOS/UEFI | ROM/Flash | 8–32 MB | Hardware init, device enum. |
| 3a | MBR | Disk Sector 0 | **512 bytes** | Stage-1 loader, partition table |
| 3b | ESP (UEFI) | FAT32 partition | 100–500 MB | Holds `.efi` bootloaders |
| 4 | Boot Loader (GRUB2) | `/boot/` directory | ~10 MB | Loads kernel + initramfs |
| 5 | Kernel Image | `/boot/vmlinuz*` | ~10–15 MB | OS core, drivers, schedulers |
| 5b | initramfs | `/boot/initrd*` | ~50–200 MB | Early userspace, block drivers |
| 6 | init/systemd | `/sbin/init` | ~10–30 MB | PID 1, service orchestration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Note:** The numbers `446`, `64`, `2` and the magic number `0xAA55` are *favourite KTU questions*. Memorise the MBR layout precisely.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Boot Flow Matrix

> The following is a fully-exhaustive, line-by-line trace of what happens when you press the power button on a typical **x86_64 PC running Linux with GRUB2 on a UEFI system** (the most common configuration assessed in KTU 2024 Scheme questions).

---

## 📋 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Boot Trace

### ▶ Step 1 — Electrical Power Stabilisation

The PSU receives AC mains power. When the user presses the power button:

- A **PS_ON#** signal is asserted low, telling the PSU to switch on its DC rails.
- The PSU outputs stable **+3.3V, +5V, and +12V** lines.
- After the rails stabilise (typically within 100–500 ms), the PSU asserts the **Power-Good (PWR_OK)** signal to the motherboard.
- The chipset releases the **CPU reset pin (RESET#)**, and the CPU begins execution.

### ▶ Step 2 — CPU Vector Reset

- The x86 architecture, upon reset, forces the **CS:IP** register pair to a hard-wired value: `CS = 0xF000`, `IP = 0xFFF0`.
- This physical address `0xFFFFFFF0` lies at the very top of the **4 GB address space**, inside the firmware ROM chip.
- The instruction at that address is the **first instruction** of the UEFI/BIOS — usually a `JMP` to the actual firmware entry point (often `0xFEF00000` or so on modern systems).

### ▶ Step 3 — Firmware (SEC → PEI → DXE → BDS → TSL Phases for UEFI)

UEFI execution is divided into well-defined phases (defined in the UEFI Platform Initialization specification):

$$ \text{UEFI Phases} = \{\text{SEC}, \text{PEI}, \text{DXE}, \text{BDS}, \text{TSL}\} $$

| Phase | Name | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **SEC** | Security | CPU cache-as-RAM, verifies firmware integrity |
| **PEI** | Pre-EFI Initialization | Initializes RAM (memory training), sets up stack |
| **DXE** | Driver Execution Environment | Loads UEFI drivers — disk, network, USB, video |
| **BDS** | Boot Device Selection | Enumerates boot options, runs startup |
| **TSL** | Transient System Load | Transfers control to the OS boot loader |

### ▶ Step 4 — POST Execution (Inside PEI & DXE)

The Power-On Self-Test performs:

- **CPU self-test:** Executes built-in `CPUID` instructions and basic arithmetic checks.
- **Memory test:** Writes pattern `0xAA55AA55`, `0x55AA55AA`, then `0x00000000`, `0xFFFFFFFF` to every RAM cell and reads it back.
- **Device scan:** Probes PCI/PCIe buses, USB ports (legacy PS/2 emulation), SATA/NVMe controllers.
- **Video init:** Initializes the GPU to a basic 80×25 text mode (BIOS) or framebuffer (UEFI).
- **System timer (PIT/HPET)** is configured as the OS tick source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Modes:** Memory failure → repeated beeps (3 beeps on Award BIOS). No display → VGA failure (1 long, 2 short beeps). Keyboard absent → "Keyboard Error" message but boot continues.

### ▶ Step 5 — MBR vs. ESP Resolution

#### Legacy BIOS Path:

$$ \text{Boot Sector} = \text{Disk}[0] \rightarrow \text{LBA 0 (Sector 0)} $$

The 512 bytes are copied by `INT 13h, AH=02h` to address `0x7C00` in RAM. The BIOS verifies that the last two bytes equal:

$$ \text{Valid MBR Signature} = \texttt{0x55 0xAA} \quad (\text{little-endian} = \texttt{0xAA55}) $$

If the signature is correct, BIOS jumps to `0x7C00` and starts executing the MBR code.

#### UEFI Path:

- Firmware parses the **GPT** (a header at LBA 1 + partition entries at LBA 2).
- It identifies the **EFI System Partition (ESP)** by its **Partition Type GUID**: `C12A7328-F81F-11D2-BA4B-00A0C93EC93B`.
- It then searches the ESP for the boot entry registered in **NVRAM** (e.g., `\EFI\Ubuntu\grubx64.efi`).
- The `.efi` file is a standard **PE32+ (Portable Executable)** image, loaded and executed directly.

### ▶ Step 6 — Boot Loader Stage Transitions (GRUB2 Example)

| Stage | File | Stored At | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stage 1 | `boot.img` (446 B) | MBR | Initial code, loads Stage 1.5 |
| Stage 1.5 | `core.img` (~30 KB) | Between MBR and 1st partition, or in `/boot/grub/` | Reads filesystem drivers |
| Stage 2 | Normal GRUB module | `/boot/grub/i386-pc/*.mod` | Loads config, displays menu |
| User selection | `grub.cfg` | `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` | Lists kernels, defaults |

The Stage 2 GRUB, after reading `grub.cfg`, executes (in order):

1. `set root='(hd0,gpt2)'`
2. `linux /boot/vmlinuz-5.15.0-... root=/dev/sda2 ro quiet`
3. `initrd /boot/initramfs-5.15.0-...img`
4. `boot`

### ▶ Step 7 — Kernel Decompression & Hand-off

- The kernel is typically **gzip/xz/LZ4/LZMA** compressed (e.g., `vmlinuz` is the compressed image; `vmlinux` is uncompressed).
- A small decompressor stub at the start of the kernel image runs first.
- Decompression takes place in protected mode; the uncompressed kernel is written just above the compressed image.
- Once decompressed, control jumps to `start_kernel()` defined in `init/main.c`.
- The compressed buffer is then freed for reuse.

### ▶ Step 8 — Kernel `start_kernel()` Execution

This is the *heart* of OS initialisation. In a structured, ordered manner:

```
start_kernel()
 ├── lockdep_init()                  // Lock dependency validator
 ├── smp_setup_processor_id()        // Set CPU ID
 ├── setup_arch(&command_line)       // Architecture-specific (x86_64)
 ├── setup_command_line(command_line)// Parse kernel cmdline
 ├── setup_nr_cpu_ids()              // SMP topology
 ├── setup_per_cpu_areas()           // Per-CPU data
 ├── boot_cpu_hotplug_init()
 ├── page_address_init()
 ├── setup_log_buf(0)
 ├── pidhash_init()                  // PID hash table
 ├── vfs_caches_init_early()         // VFS slab caches
 ├── sort_main_extable()
 ├── trap_init()                     // IDT setup, exception handlers
 ├── mm_init()                       // Buddy allocator, vmalloc init
 ├── sched_init()                    // CFS scheduler initialisation
 ├── preempt_disable()               // Preemption off
 ├── rcu_init()                      // Read-Copy-Update
 ├── radix_tree_init()
 ├── workqueue_init_early()
 ├── rcu_init_tasks_generic()
 ├── do_initcalls()                  // Run all __initcall functions
 ├── smp_init()                      // Bring up secondary CPUs
 ├── sched_init_smp()
 ├── do_basic_setup()                // Filesystems, drivers, modules
 ├── prepare_namespace()             // Mount real root filesystem
 ├── free_initmem()                  // Free .init section memory
 └── rest_init()                     // → kernel_init() → exec("/sbin/init")
```

> [!NOTE]
> **rest_init()** is critical: it spawns **kernel_init (PID 1)** via `kernel_thread()` and then becomes the *idle thread* (PID 0) on the boot CPU.

### ▶ Step 9 — initramfs Handoff

- Before the real root (`/dev/sda2`) can be mounted, the kernel needs:
  - Block device drivers (e.g., NVMe, AHCI, RAID, LVM, LUKS encryption)
  - Filesystem drivers (e.g., ext4, xfs, btrfs)
- These are bundled inside **initramfs** as a **CPIO archive**.
- Linux kernel extracts this archive into a `tmpfs` mounted at `/` (the *initial* root).
- An **init script** (`/init` or `/linuxrc` in the archive) runs, loads necessary modules via `modprobe`, and then uses `switch_root` to:
  - Pivot to the real root filesystem
  - Execute `/sbin/init` from the real root

### ▶ Step 10 — Systemd / init Execution

- **Systemd (PID 1)** reads its configuration: `/etc/systemd/system/default.target`.
- It activates dependencies in parallel:
  - Mounts filesystems from `/etc/fstab`
  - Starts `systemd-logind`, `dbus-broker`, `NetworkManager`
  - Reaches `graphical.target` (or `multi-user.target`)
- The user is presented with **GDM / LightDM / SDDM** login screen (graphical) or a TTY login prompt (text).

### ▶ Step 11 — User Login & Session Initiation

- The user authenticates.
- A **session manager** (`gnome-session`, `startx`, etc.) starts the user's desktop.
- The system is now **fully booted** and ready for application workloads.

---

## 📈 Boot Time Optimisation Strategies (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Strategy | Mechanism | Speedup |
|---|---|---|
| **UEFI Fast Boot** | Skip POST, skip logo | ~3–5 s |
| **SSD over HDD** | NVMe reduces disk read from ms to μs | 5–10× |
| **systemd-analyze** profile | Identify slow services | Varies |
| **Parallel init** (systemd) | Concurrent service start | 2–4× |
| **readahead / ureadahead** | Pre-fetch boot files | 10–20% |
| **Disabled services** | `systemctl disable` unused | Varies |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Boot Schematics

> All diagrams below use **Mermaid** syntax, carefully crafted to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE node-identifier rules (alphanumeric IDs, no reserved keywords, clean uppercase labels inside quotes).

---

## 📊 Diagram 1 — Top-Level Boot Flow (Power-On to Login)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Power Button Pressed] --> A1[PSU Activates DC Rails]
    A1 --> A2[PWR_OK Signal Asserted]
    A2 --> A3[CPU Reset Released CS:IP = 0xFFFFFFF0]
    A3 --> B1[UEFI SEC Phase]
    B1 --> B2[UEFI PEI Phase Memory Init]
    B2 --> B3[UEFI DXE Phase Drivers Loaded]
    B3 --> B4[UEFI BDS Phase Boot Device Select]
    B4 --> C1{UEFI or Legacy BIOS}
    C1 -->|UEFI| D1[Read GPT and Locate ESP]
    C1 -->|Legacy| D2[Read MBR Sector to 0x7C00]
    D1 --> E1[Execute GRUB EFI Binary]
    D2 --> E2[Execute MBR Stage 1 Code]
    E1 --> F1[GRUB Stage 2 Reads grub.cfg]
    E2 --> E3[GRUB Stage 1.5 core.img]
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Load vmlinuz Kernel to RAM]
    F1 --> G2[Load initramfs CPIO to tmpfs]
    G1 --> H1[Kernel Decompresses Itself]
    H1 --> H2[Kernel start_kernel Function]
    G2 --> H2
    H2 --> I1[Init Mounts Real Root FS]
    I1 --> I2[exec sbin init or systemd PID 1]
    I2 --> J1[Systemd Activates Services]
    J1 --> J2[Display Manager Login Screen]
    J2 --> K1[Fully Booted User Session]
```

---

## 📊 Diagram 2 — MBR Sector Layout (512 Bytes)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    block:MBR_TITLE:1
        title["MBR Sector 0 Layout Total 512 Bytes"]
    end
    block:MBR_AREA:5
        code["BOOT CODE 446 BYTES"] space["PART TABLE ENTRY 1 16 BYTES"] space2["PART TABLE ENTRY 2 16 BYTES"] space3["PART TABLE ENTRY 3 16 BYTES"] space4["PART TABLE ENTRY 4 16 BYTES"] magic["MAGIC 55 AA"]
    end
    style title fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style code fill:#0ea5e9
    style space fill:#22c55e
    style space2 fill:#22c55e
    style space3 fill:#22c55e
    style space4 fill:#22c55e
    style magic fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
```

**Layout summary:**

$$
\text{MBR}_{512B} = \underbrace{\text{Boot Code}}_{446B} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Partition Table}}_{4 \times 16B = 64B} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Signature}}_{2B \;(0x55\;0xAA)}
$$

---

## 📊 Diagram 3 — Linux Kernel Boot Subsystem Initialization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Entry Point start_kernel] --> K2[setup_arch]
    K2 --> K3[mm_init Memory Mgmt]
    K2 --> K4[sched_init Scheduler]
    K2 --> K5[trap_init Interrupt Handlers]
    K3 --> K6[vfs_caches_init VFS]
    K4 --> K7[do_initcalls Drivers]
    K5 --> K8[smp_init Multi CPU]
    K6 --> K9[prepare_namespace Mount Root]
    K7 --> K9
    K8 --> K10[rest_init]
    K9 --> K10
    K10 --> K11[PID 1 init or systemd]
    K11 --> K12[User Space Services Begin]
```

---

## 📊 Diagram 4 — BIOS vs. UEFI Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEGACY [LEGACY BIOS]
        L1[16 bit Real Mode] --> L2[INT 13h Disk Service]
        L2 --> L3[Read MBR at LBA 0]
        L3 --> L4[Jump to 0x7C00]
    end
    subgraph MODERN [UEFI FIRMWARE]
        U1[32 or 64 bit Long Mode] --> U2[Native Driver Stack DXE]
        U2 --> U3[Read GPT Header]
        U3 --> U4[Locate ESP by GUID]
        U4 --> U5[Load PE32 Plus EFI Binary]
    end
    L4 --> COMMON[Stage 1 Boot Loader]
    U5 --> COMMON
    COMMON --> NEXT[Stage 2 GRUB Kernel Loading]
```

---

## 📊 Diagram 5 — System Boot State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Off
    Off --> POST : Power On
    POST --> FirmwareInit : POST Pass
    POST --> Halted : POST Fail
    FirmwareInit --> BootDeviceDetect : UEFI BDS or BIOS INT 13h
    BootDeviceDetect --> BootLoaderExec : MBR or EFI Found
    BootDeviceDetect --> NoBoot : No Bootable Device
    BootLoaderExec --> KernelLoad : GRUB Loads Kernel and initramfs
    KernelLoad --> KernelInit : start_kernel Runs
    KernelInit --> RootFSMount : VFS and initramfs Hand off
    RootFSMount --> UserspaceInit : exec init or systemd
    UserspaceInit --> Running : Login Prompt or GUI
    Running --> Reboot : systemctl reboot
    Reboot --> POST
    Running --> Off : Power Off
    NoBoot --> Off
    Halted --> Off
```

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "bootstrapping" in the context of an operating system. List the major stages involved in the Linux boot process.**

**✅ Model Answer:**

> **Bootstrapping (System Boot)** is the process of loading the operating system into the main memory and initiating its execution starting from a powered-off or reset state. The term is derived from the phrase *"pulling oneself up by one's bootstraps"*, reflecting how a simple firmware program is able to load a much more complex OS without external assistance.

**Major stages of the Linux boot process:**

1. **Power-On Self-Test (POST):** Firmware tests CPU, RAM, and essential peripherals.
2. **Firmware Initialization:** BIOS/UEFI configures hardware and selects the boot device.
3. **MBR / EFI System Partition:** Stage-1 boot code is loaded into RAM.
4. **Boot Loader Execution:** GRUB2 (or equivalent) loads the kernel and initramfs into memory.
5. **Kernel Initialization:** `start_kernel()` sets up memory management, scheduler, drivers, and mounts the root filesystem.
6. **Userspace Startup:** The kernel executes `/sbin/init` (or systemd) as **PID 1**, which launches all user-level services and presents the login interface.

---

### ❓ Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the structure of the MBR sector. Why is the magic number `0xAA55` essential?**

**✅ Model Answer:**

> The **Master Boot Record (MBR)** is the very first sector of a bootable disk, located at **Cylinder 0, Head 0, Sector 1 (LBA 0)**. It is exactly **512 bytes** in size and is divided as follows:

| Offset (Bytes) | Size | Contents |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 445 | 446 bytes | **Stage 1 boot code** (machine instructions) |
| 446 – 509 | 64 bytes | **Partition table** (4 primary entries × 16 bytes) |
| 510 – 511 | 2 bytes | **Boot signature `0x55 0xAA`** |

**Importance of the magic number `0xAA55`:**

- It acts as a **validity marker**. Without this signature, the BIOS/UEFI firmware treats the sector as non-bootable and refuses to execute it.
- The convention is in **little-endian** format: byte 510 = `0x55`, byte 511 = `0xAA`.
- If the signature is corrupted (e.g., due to malware or disk failure), the system displays errors such as *"Operating System Not Found"* or *"Missing Operating System"*.
- It is a small but critical handshake between the **firmware and the boot loader**.

---

## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**With a neat diagram, explain the complete boot process of a Linux operating system. Compare BIOS-based and UEFI-based booting.**

#### (a) Complete Linux Boot Process — 7 Marks

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Power-On & CPU Reset:** Power button triggers PSU, which asserts `PWR_OK`. CPU fetches its first instruction from address `0xFFFFFFF0`, pointing into the firmware ROM.

2. **POST Execution:** Firmware performs a hardware self-test — verifies CPU, RAM (memory test patterns), keyboard, video adapter, and storage controllers. Beep codes indicate failures.

3. **Firmware Initialization:**
   - **BIOS path:** Real-mode 16-bit code; loads Option ROMs (e.g., RAID controller) and builds the BIOS Data Area.
   - **UEFI path:** Executes the SEC → PEI → DXE → BDS phase sequence, loading UEFI drivers natively in 32/64-bit mode.

4. **Boot Device Selection & Sector Load:**
   - BIOS uses `INT 13h` to read MBR (LBA 0) into `0x7C00`. Checks `0xAA55` signature.
   - UEFI reads the GPT and locates the **EFI System Partition (ESP)** containing `.efi` boot loaders.

5. **Stage 1 Boot Code Execution:** The MBR code or EFI binary finds and loads the **Stage 1.5 / Stage 2 GRUB** files.

6. **Boot Loader (GRUB2):** Reads `/boot/grub/grub.cfg`, presents a menu, and loads the selected kernel (`vmlinuz`) and `initramfs` image into RAM.

7. **Kernel Hand-off:** GRUB transfers control to the kernel's `start_kernel()` function, which decompresses itself if needed and begins architecture-independent initialization.

8. **Kernel Initialization:** Sets up memory management, process scheduler, VFS, drivers, and mounts the initial root filesystem from `initramfs`.

9. **`init` / systemd Startup:** The kernel exec()'s `/sbin/init` (PID 1). Systemd activates targets, services, and the login manager.

10. **User Login:** A graphical (GDM) or textual (TTY) login appears — the system is fully booted.

#### (b) BIOS vs. UEFI Comparison — 7 Marks

| **Parameter** | **Legacy BIOS** | **UEFI** |
|---|---|---|
| Bit Mode | 16-bit real mode | 32/64-bit protected/long mode |
| Disk Scheme | MBR (max 2 TB, 4 partitions) | GPT (max 9.4 ZB, 128 partitions) |
| Boot Source | MBR sector only | EFI System Partition (FAT32) |
| Secure Boot | Not supported | Supported with cryptographic signatures |
| Driver Model | Option ROMs (legacy) | Native UEFI driver stack |
| User Interface | Text only | Graphical, mouse-supported |
| Boot Speed | Slower | Faster (parallel init) |
| Networking | Not native | Native TCP/IP in firmware |
| Backward Compatibility | Excellent | Optional CSM (Compatibility Support Module) |
| Future Relevance | Deprecated | Industry standard |

**Conclusion:** UEFI is the modern replacement for BIOS, offering better security, scalability, and performance.

---

### 📌 Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Set]

**Explain the role of the boot loader in the OS boot sequence. Describe GRUB2's stages in detail. What is initramfs and why is it needed?**

#### (a) Role of the Boot Loader & GRUB2 Stages — 7 Marks

**Role of the Boot Loader:**

The boot loader is a small but critical program that bridges the gap between the firmware and the operating system kernel. Its responsibilities are:

1. **Locate** the kernel image and initial RAM filesystem on the disk.
2. **Load** them into main memory at suitable addresses.
3. **Parse** the kernel command line and any configuration file (e.g., `grub.cfg`).
4. **Set up** the video mode and any required memory regions.
5. **Decompress** the kernel if necessary.
6. **Transfer** CPU control to the kernel's entry point (`start_kernel`).

**GRUB2 Stages:**

| Stage | Name | Size | Stored In | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | `boot.img` | 446 B | MBR | Minimal code; loads Stage 1.5 |
| **1.5** | `core.img` | ~30 KB | MBR gap or `/boot/grub/` | Has filesystem drivers; locates Stage 2 |
| **2** | GRUB2 modules | ~10 MB | `/boot/grub/i386-pc/*.mod` | Reads config, shows menu, loads kernel |

**Key Files:**
- `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` — Main configuration file
- `/etc/default/grub` — User-editable defaults
- `grub-mkconfig` regenerates `grub.cfg` after kernel updates.

#### (b) initramfs — Concept, Structure, and Necessity — 7 Marks

**Definition:**
> **initramfs (Initial RAM Filesystem)** is a compressed **CPIO archive** that the boot loader (GRUB) loads into RAM. The kernel extracts it into a `tmpfs` and uses it as the *initial* root filesystem before the *real* root is available.

**Why initramfs is needed:**

1. **Module availability:** The kernel image alone is monolithic, but it may not include every block-device and filesystem driver (especially for NVMe, LVM, LUKS-encrypted, or RAID arrays).
2. **Early userspace:** It contains a minimal `/init` shell script that runs `modprobe` to load required kernel modules.
3. **Asynchronous block-device setup:** Modern storage stacks (e.g., mdadm RAID, dm-crypt, multipath) require user-space tools that cannot run until userspace exists.
4. **Pivoting to real root:** The `/init` script eventually calls `switch_root` to pivot from `tmpfs` to the actual root filesystem (e.g., `/dev/sda2`).

**Structure of initramfs (CPIO archive):**

```
/init                ← Main init script
/lib/modules/...     ← Kernel modules (.ko files)
/bin, /sbin          ← BusyBox or minimal tools
/proc, /sys          ← Virtual filesystems (mounted by init)
/etc/...             ← Config files for early userspace
```

**Command to inspect an initramfs image:**

```bash
lsinitrd /boot/initramfs-$(uname -r).img
```

**Conclusion:** initramfs is the bridge that allows the kernel to start, load required drivers, and gracefully hand off to the *real* root filesystem, enabling the OS to boot on virtually any hardware/storage configuration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Don't confuse "boot" with "login"!** Boot ends when the OS is *fully* loaded and the login prompt appears — *not* when the user types their password.
> 2. **Wrong magic number:** Many students write `0x55AA` instead of `0xAA55`. The signature is little-endian; byte 510 is `0x55`, byte 511 is `0xAA`. Losing 1 mark here is extremely common.
> 3. **Skipping initramfs explanation:** Examiners specifically look for the words **"userspace pivot"** or **"switch_root"** when asking about Linux boot. Omitting these costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting PID 1:** Always state that **init/systemd is the FIRST user-space process (PID 1)**. Many students incorrectly call it PID 0.
> 5. **Confusing GRUB stages:** Stage 1.5 is *not* in the MBR itself — it lives in the small gap between the MBR and the first partition (or in `/boot/grub/`). Misrepresentation loses marks.
> 6. **BIOS vs. UEFI swap:** Don't say BIOS is 32-bit. It is firmly **16-bit real mode** — this is a very common KTU trap.
> 7. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers:** A 14-mark question explicitly asks for a *"neat diagram"*. Drawing the boot flow gets you at least 2–3 easy marks. **Always include a labelled diagram.**

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Things You MUST Remember for KTU 2024 Operating Systems Exam:**

- 🔸 **Bootstrapping = the entire process** from power-on to fully-loaded OS (including login prompt).
- 🔸 The **CPU's reset vector** is hard-wired to `0xFFFFFFF0` on x86 — points into ROM.
- 🔸 **POST** = Power-On Self-Test; runs from firmware ROM.
- 🔸 **BIOS is 16-bit, real mode**; **UEFI is 32/64-bit, long mode**.
- 🔸 **MBR is 512 bytes** = **446 (code) + 64 (4 × 16B partition table) + 2 (signature)**.
- 🔸 **Magic number = `0xAA55`** (byte 510 = `0x55`, byte 511 = `0xAA`).
- 🔸 **GPT** replaces MBR for disks > 2 TB; uses **EFI System Partition (ESP)** in **FAT32**.
- 🔸 **GRUB2 has 3 stages** — Stage 1 (MBR, 446 B), Stage 1.5 (`core.img`, ~30 KB), Stage 2 (`/boot/grub/`).
- 🔸 **Boot loader loads** the kernel image (`vmlinuz`) **and** the `initramfs` CPIO image.
- 🔸 **Kernel entry point** is `start_kernel()` in `init/main.c` — sets up memory, scheduler, VFS, drivers.
- 🔸 **`initramfs`** is a CPIO archive loaded into `tmpfs`; contains early userspace and module-loading tools.
- 🔸 **switch_root** pivots from `tmpfs` to the real root filesystem.
- 🔸 **`/sbin/init` or `/lib/systemd/systemd` is PID 1** — the parent of *all* user processes.
- 🔸 **Cold boot** = power-on from off; **Warm boot** = soft restart, skips some POST.
- 🔸 **Beep codes** indicate POST failures (Award BIOS: 1 long + 2 short = video error).
- 🔸 **UEFI phases** = SEC → PEI → DXE → BDS → TSL.
- 🔸 **Secure Boot** is a UEFI feature that cryptographically verifies the boot loader.
- 🔸 **Kernel command line** parameters (e.g., `root=`, `ro`, `quiet`) are passed by GRUB to the kernel.
- 🔸 **Boot time optimization** uses SSD, systemd parallelization, and `systemd-analyze blame`.

> **🎓 Final Exam Mantra:** *"POST → Firmware → MBR/ESP → GRUB → Kernel → initramfs → init → Services → Login"*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Management: Process Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Management: Process Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Process** is formally defined as a program in execution. It is not merely a passive collection of binary instructions stored on disk (which we call a *program*), but rather an **active entity** comprising the executing program code, its current activity state, and a dynamically evolving set of system resources allocated by the Operating System kernel.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for Operating Systems (PCITT403), a process is the fundamental unit of work upon which the operating system performs scheduling, resource allocation, and dispatching. Each process carries with it an associated data structure known as the **Process Control Block (PCB)** that acts as its identity card in kernel memory.

Mathematically, a process can be represented as the ordered tuple:

$$ P = \{ \text{Program Code}, \text{PC}, \text{Registers}, \text{Stack}, \text{Data}, \text{Heap}, \text{PCB} \} $$

where every process has its own independent address space (logical address space), including:

- **Text Section**: The compiled executable machine instructions.
- **Program Counter (PC)**: Holds the address of the next instruction to execute.
- **Registers**: Accumulator, base, limit, stack pointer, and general-purpose CPU registers.
- **Stack**: Stores temporary data such as function parameters, return addresses, and local variables.
- **Data Section**: Contains global and static variables.
- **Heap**: Dynamically allocated memory during runtime (via `malloc`, `new`, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight:** A *program* by itself is **NOT** a process. A program becomes a process only when the OS loader places it into main memory and starts executing it. Two executions of the same program (e.g., opening Chrome twice) create **two distinct processes** with two independent PCBs.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**

Imagine a large restaurant kitchen as the **CPU**. The kitchen has only **one master chef** (the actual CPU core). At any given instant, the chef can only cook **one dish** (execute one process) at a time.

- A **Recipe Card** (e.g., "How to make Biryani") is the **Program** — a static set of instructions written on paper, sitting on a shelf.
- A **Cooking Order Ticket** that is actively being prepared — with: current step number (PC), measured spices (registers), pots on the stove (stack), ingredients allocated (heap), and a clipboard tracking its status (PCB) — is the **Process**.
- The **Head Chef's Notepad** listing all active orders, their current stage (waiting, being cooked, plated), and assigned resources is the **Process Table** maintained by the OS.

So: **Program = Recipe on paper**, while **Process = Recipe being actively cooked right now**.

---

## 1.3 Why Does the Concept of a Process Exist?

Operating systems needed an abstraction to:
1. **Multiplex the CPU** among multiple competing activities.
2. **Track execution state** when a process is temporarily paused (context switch).
3. **Enforce isolation and protection** so one faulty process cannot corrupt another.
4. **Enable concurrency and parallelism** by treating each logical activity as an independent schedulable entity.

> [!TIP]
> The process abstraction is the **cornerstone** of modern multiprogramming. Without it, the OS would have no way to safely time-share the CPU among users.

---

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Definitions for KTU)

These are the **canonical metrics** every KTU OS student must commit to memory:

- **CPU Utilization**: Percentage of time the CPU is doing useful work (goal: keep this between **40%–90%**).
- **Throughput**: Number of processes completed per unit time (typically **processes/second**).
- **Turnaround Time (TAT)**: Total time from process submission to its completion.
- **Waiting Time (WT)**: Total time a process spends in the **Ready Queue** waiting for the CPU.
- **Response Time (RT)**: Time from process submission until the **first** response/output is produced.
- **Context Switch Time**: Time taken to save the state of one process and load the state of another.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-State Process Model

During its lifetime, every process transitions through a series of well-defined **states**. The KTU syllabus specifically emphasizes the **Five-State Model**, which we now study rigorously.

### 2.1.1 The Five States Explained

| # | State Name | Description |
|---|------------|-------------|
| 1 | **New** | Process is being created. OS is allocating its PCB, memory, and resources. |
| 2 | **Ready** | Process is loaded into main memory and is waiting in the Ready Queue to be assigned the CPU. |
| 3 | **Running** | Process instructions are currently being executed by the CPU. Only **one** process per core can be in this state. |
| 4 | **Blocked (Waiting)** | Process cannot proceed until some I/O operation or event completes (e.g., waiting for keyboard input, disk read). |
| 5 | **Terminated (Exit)** | Process has finished execution. OS is reclaiming its resources and deallocating its PCB. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinguish carefully**: A process in the **Blocked** state is *not* in the Ready Queue. It will only re-enter the Ready state when the awaited I/O event completes. The Ready Queue is for CPU-bound waiting; the Blocked Queue is for I/O-bound waiting.

---

### 2.1.2 State Transitions — The Why and How

Let us dissect each transition. These are **mandatory** for KTU board answers.

1. **New → Ready**: The OS has finished creating the process and admitted it into the Ready Queue. Admission control is performed by the *long-term scheduler*.
2. **Ready → Running**: The *short-term scheduler (CPU scheduler)* has selected this process via the **dispatcher** for execution.
3. **Running → Ready**: Either the process's time quantum (in round-robin) expired, or a higher-priority process preempted it.
4. **Running → Blocked**: The process issued an I/O request, called `wait()` on a child process, or invoked a system call that cannot be satisfied immediately.
5. **Blocked → Ready**: The awaited I/O completed or the awaited event occurred. The process is now eligible for CPU scheduling again.
6. **Running → Terminated**: The process executed its final statement, called `exit()`, or was killed abnormally (e.g., segmentation fault).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cannot a Blocked process go directly to Running?** Because the CPU scheduler only selects processes from the **Ready Queue**. The OS must first move it back to Ready when its I/O completes.

---

## 2.2 Process Control Block (PCB) — The Heart of Process Management

Each process is represented in the kernel by a **Process Control Block** (also called a *Task Control Block*). It is a kernel data structure containing everything the OS needs to manage and track a process.

### 2.2.1 Information Stored in the PCB

| Category | Contents |
|----------|----------|
| **Process Identification** | Process ID (PID), Parent Process ID (PPID), User ID (UID), Group ID. |
| **Processor State** | Program Counter (PC), Status Register, Stack Pointer, General-Purpose Registers. |
| **Process State** | Current state (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated). |
| **Scheduling Information** | Priority, pointers to scheduling queues, accounting data (CPU time used). |
| **Memory Management Info** | Base and Limit registers, Page Tables, Segment Tables. |
| **Resource Allocation** | Open file descriptors, list of I/O devices allocated, signal masks. |
| **Accounting & Security** | Time limits, amount of CPU time consumed, memory limits. |
| **Inter-Process Communication** | Message queues, semaphores, shared memory segment pointers. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PCB = the OS's "knowledge" of a process.** A context switch essentially saves the PCB of the outgoing process and loads the PCB of the incoming process. Without the PCB, the OS cannot "remember" what a process was doing before being preempted.

---

## 2.3 Operations on Processes

The OS provides system calls for process management. The KTU syllabus expects the following:

### 2.3.1 Process Creation

A process can create new processes via the `fork()` / `exec()` family of system calls (POSIX) or `CreateProcess()` (Windows). The creating process is called the **parent**, and the new process is the **child**.

During process creation, the OS performs these steps:
1. Assigns a unique **PID** to the child.
2. Allocates memory for the child's address space.
3. Initializes the child's PCB.
4. Places the child in the **Ready Queue**.
5. Returns the child's PID to the parent.

### 2.3.2 Process Termination

A process terminates via:
- **Normal exit** (calls `exit()` system call voluntarily).
- **Error exit** (compulsory termination due to an unrecoverable error).
- **Fatal error** (e.g., division by zero, illegal memory access).
- **Killed by another process** (e.g., `kill -9 PID` in Linux).

Upon termination, the OS reclaims all resources — memory, file descriptors, I/O devices — and the PCB is removed from the process table.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Process Performance Metrics

This is the **single most important formula table** you will need for numerical questions in KTU exams. Memorize it.

| Metric | Formula | Description | Units |
|--------|---------|-------------|-------|
| CPU Utilization | $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | Fraction of time CPU is busy | Percent (%) |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{completed}}{T_{total}}$ | Processes completed per unit time | processes/sec |
| Turnaround Time | $TAT = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | Total time in system | seconds |
| Waiting Time | $WT = TAT - T_{burst}$ | Time spent waiting in ready queue | seconds |
| Response Time | $RT = T_{first\_response} - T_{arrival}$ | Time until first output | seconds |
| Average WT | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | Mean waiting time across all processes | seconds |
| Average TAT | $\overline{TAT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | Mean turnaround time across all processes | seconds |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical relation to remember**: $TAT = WT + Burst\;Time$ for any single process.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The process concept underpins:
- **Cloud computing platforms** (AWS EC2, Azure VMs) where each tenant workload runs in an isolated process group.
- **Web servers** (Apache, Nginx) handling thousands of concurrent client requests by spawning worker processes.
- **Mobile operating systems** (Android, iOS) where every app runs in its own process sandbox for security isolation.
- **Embedded systems** (RTOS in automotive ECUs, drones) where real-time processes must meet strict deadlines.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the core performance metric formulas in full derivation form. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation of CPU Utilization

CPU Utilization is the fraction of total observation time during which the CPU is executing useful instructions (not idle, not performing kernel overhead that does nothing).

**Step 1 — Define total observation window.**

Let $T_{total}$ be the total wall-clock time over which we observe the system.

$$ T_{total} = T_{busy} + T_{idle} $$

Here $T_{busy}$ is the cumulative time during which the CPU was executing user or system code, and $T_{idle}$ is the cumulative time the CPU had no runnable process.

**Step 2 — Define the utilization ratio.**

By definition, utilization is the ratio of busy time to total time:

$$ U_{cpu} = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}} $$

**Step 3 — Express as a percentage.**

Multiplying by 100 yields the percentage form expected in KTU answers:

$$ U_{cpu}(\%) = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}} \times 100 $$

**Step 4 — Practical substitution.**

If, for example, in 1 hour the CPU was busy for 48 minutes:
$T_{busy} = 48\;\text{min}$, $T_{total} = 60\;\text{min}$.

$$ U_{cpu} = \frac{48}{60} \times 100 = 80\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition**: A 100% utilization means the CPU never idled — but this is *dangerous* in real systems because the OS cannot respond to interrupts instantly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Turnaround Time and Waiting Time

We now derive the relationship between turnaround time, waiting time, and CPU burst time.

**Step 1 — Decompose the process lifecycle.**

For any process $P_i$, the total time spent in the system from arrival to completion is the sum of:

- Time spent in the Ready Queue (waiting for CPU): $WT_i$
- Time spent actually executing on the CPU (CPU burst time): $B_i$
- Time spent in the Blocked state (waiting for I/O): $IO_i$

Therefore:

$$ TAT_i = WT_i + B_i + IO_i $$

**Step 2 — Simplification for CPU-only scheduling problems.**

In many KTU numerical problems, I/O time is either negligible or already factored into the burst. For CPU-bound scheduling problems, we define the *service time* as:

$$ S_i = B_i + IO_i $$

Thus the simplified turnaround time becomes:

$$ TAT_i = WT_i + B_i $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $IO_i$ is given separately, **must include** it. When only $B_i$ is provided, assume $TAT = WT + B$.

**Step 3 — Average turnaround time across $n$ processes.**

Summing over all $n$ processes:

$$ \overline{TAT} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i $$

**Step 4 — Average waiting time across $n$ processes.**

Similarly:

$$ \overline{WT} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i $$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Computing WT and TAT

**Problem Statement:**

Three processes arrive at time 0 with the following CPU burst times:

- $P_1$: $B_1 = 6$ units
- $P_2$: $B_2 = 2$ units
- $P_3$: $B_3 = 4$ units

Using **FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)** scheduling, compute WT and TAT for each process.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build the Gantt chart.**

Since all processes arrive at $t = 0$, the order is $P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart:} \quad
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
P_1 & P_2 & P_3 \\
\hline
0 \to 6 & 6 \to 8 & 8 \to 12 \\
\hline
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Completion Times.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_1 &= 6 \\
CT_2 &= 8 \\
CT_3 &= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Turnaround Times using $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$.**

Since all arrival times are 0:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_1 &= 6 - 0 = 6 \\
TAT_2 &= 8 - 0 = 8 \\
TAT_3 &= 12 - 0 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Waiting Times using $WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_1 &= 6 - 6 = 0 \\
WT_2 &= 8 - 2 = 6 \\
WT_3 &= 12 - 4 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the averages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{6 + 8 + 12}{3} = \frac{26}{3} \approx 8.67 \;\text{units} \\
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 6 + 8}{3} = \frac{14}{3} \approx 4.67 \;\text{units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that $P_1$ has $WT_1 = 0$ because it starts immediately. $P_2$ and $P_3$ suffer the **convoy effect** — they wait behind a long process. This is the classic drawback of FCFS.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Response Time

For interactive systems, **Response Time** is more meaningful than Turnaround Time.

**Step 1 — Define response time.**

It is the duration from submission until the *first* CPU allocation (first response, not completion):

$$ RT_i = T_{first\_response,i} - T_{arrival,i} $$

**Step 2 — Relate to waiting time before first dispatch.**

In a non-preemptive system, the process must wait for all earlier processes in the ready queue. In a preemptive system, the response time equals the waiting time accrued *before* the first execution burst begins.

For a process $P_i$ that first receives CPU at time $t_1$:

$$ RT_i = t_1 - T_{arrival,i} $$

**Step 3 — Average response time.**

$$ \overline{RT} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} RT_i $$

---

## 3.5 Summary of Performance Metric Identity

We can write the following compact identity relating all key metrics for any scheduling problem:

$$ TAT_i = WT_i + B_i = (RT_i + \text{Additional WT}_i) + B_i $$

And the cumulative system efficiency:

$$ U_{cpu} = 1 - \frac{T_{idle}}{T_{total}} $$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams have been carefully crafted to satisfy all syntax safeguards — every node ID is alphanumeric, all labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.1 Process State Transition Diagram (Five-State Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    new1["NEW PROCESS BEING CREATED"]
    ready1["READY WAITING IN READY QUEUE"]
    running1["RUNNING EXECUTING ON CPU"]
    blocked1["BLOCKED WAITING FOR I O OR EVENT"]
    terminated1["TERMINATED EXIT COMPLETED"]

    new1 -->|ADMITTED| ready1
    ready1 -->|DISPATCHED BY CPU SCHEDULER| running1
    running1 -->|TIME QUANTUM EXPIRED OR PREEMPTED| ready1
    running1 -->|I O REQUEST OR WAIT SYSTEM CALL| blocked1
    blocked1 -->|I O COMPLETION OR EVENT SIGNAL| ready1
    running1 -->|EXIT SYSTEM CALL OR KILLED| terminated1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram carefully:** A blocked process **never** transitions directly to running. It must first transition through the **Ready Queue**. This is a frequently tested KTU conceptual question.

---

## 4.2 Process Control Block (PCB) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pcb["PROCESS CONTROL BLOCK PCB"]

    pcb --> pidblock["PROCESS IDENTIFICATION"]
    pidblock --> pid1["PROCESS ID PID"]
    pidblock --> ppid1["PARENT PROCESS ID PPID"]
    pidblock --> uid1["USER ID UID"]

    pcb --> stateblock["PROCESS STATE INFO"]
    stateblock --> state1["CURRENT STATE NEW READY RUNNING BLOCKED TERMINATED"]
    stateblock --> pc1["PROGRAM COUNTER PC"]

    pcb --> cpustateblock["CPU CONTEXT"]
    cpustateblock --> reg1["GENERAL PURPOSE REGISTERS"]
    cpustateblock --> sp1["STACK POINTER SP"]
    cpustateblock --> sr1["STATUS REGISTER FLAGS"]

    pcb --> schedblock["SCHEDULING INFORMATION"]
    schedblock --> prio1["PRIORITY VALUE"]
    schedblock --> acct1["ACCOUNTING CPU TIME USED"]
    schedblock --> qptr1["QUEUE POINTERS"]

    pcb --> memblock["MEMORY MANAGEMENT"]
    memblock --> base1["BASE AND LIMIT REGISTERS"]
    memblock --> pgtbl1["PAGE TABLE POINTERS"]

    pcb --> ioblock["I O AND FILE INFO"]
    ioblock --> fd1["OPEN FILE DESCRIPTORS"]
    ioblock --> dev1["ALLOCATED I O DEVICES"]

    pcb --> ipcblock["INTERPROCESS COMMUNICATION"]
    ipcblock --> sem1["SEMAPHORES"]
    ipcblock --> msg1["MESSAGE QUEUES"]
    ipcblock --> shm1["SHARED MEMORY POINTERS"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow: Process Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userprog["USER PROGRAM ON DISK"]
    loader["OS LOADER ALLOCATES MEMORY AND PCB"]
    newstate["STATE NEW"]
    readyq["READY QUEUE IN MAIN MEMORY"]
    cpuexec["CPU EXECUTES PROCESS"]
    runningstate["STATE RUNNING"]
    iowait["I O WAIT OCCURS"]
    blockedq["BLOCKED QUEUE"]
    eventdone["I O OR EVENT COMPLETE"]
    terminate["PROCESS EXITS"]
    cleanup["OS RECLAIMS RESOURCES"]
    terminatedstate["STATE TERMINATED"]

    userprog -->|LOADED BY OS| loader
    loader -->|PCB INITIALIZED| newstate
    newstate -->|ADMITTED| readyq
    readyq -->|SCHEDULER DISPATCHES| cpuexec
    cpuexec --> runningstate
    runningstate -->|SYSTEM CALL WAIT| iowait
    iowait --> blockedq
    blockedq -->|EVENT COMPLETION| eventdone
    eventdone --> readyq
    runningstate -->|EXIT OR KILLED| terminate
    terminate --> cleanup
    cleanup --> terminatedstate
```

---

## 4.4 Context Switch Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    runningP1["PROCESS P1 CURRENTLY RUNNING"]
    saveP1["SAVE STATE OF P1 INTO PCB P1"]
    loadP2["LOAD STATE OF P2 FROM PCB P2"]
    runningP2["PROCESS P2 NOW RUNNING"]

    pcbTable["PROCESS TABLE IN KERNEL MEMORY"]
    pcbP1["PCB OF P1"]
    pcbP2["PCB OF P2"]

    runningP1 -->|INTERRUPT OR SYSTEM CALL| saveP1
    saveP1 -->|WRITE REGISTERS PC SP| pcbP1
    pcbP1 --> pcbTable
    saveP1 --> loadP2
    loadP2 -->|READ REGISTERS PC SP| pcbP2
    pcbP2 --> pcbTable
    loadP2 --> runningP2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The context switch overhead is pure *wasted* CPU time — the OS does no useful user work during this window. KTU problems may ask you to compute effective CPU utilization accounting for context switch cost.
