**(b)** Write short notes on:
(i) **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** scheduling.
(ii) **Priority Inversion** and how it is solved. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

| Process | AT | BT | Priority (lower number = higher priority) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 4 | 2 |
| $P_2$ | 1 | 3 | 1 |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| $P_4$ | 3 | 5 | 2 |

**Execution Trace:**

- $t=0$: $P_1$ (priority 2) is the only one. Run $P_1$.
- $t=1$: $P_2$ arrives (priority 1, *higher* than $P_1$'s 2). **Preempt** $P_1$. Run $P_2$.
- $t=2$: $P_3$ arrives (priority 3, lower than $P_2$). Continue $P_2$.
- $t=3$: $P_4$ arrives (priority 2, lower than $P_2$). Continue $P_2$.
- $t=4$: $P_2$ done. Compare $P_1$(remaining 3, pri 2) vs $P_4$(pri 2, BT 5). Tie — assume FCFS among equal priorities → Run $P_1$.
- $t=7$: $P_1$ done. Run $P_4$.
- $t=12$: $P_4$ done. Run $P_3$.
- $t=14$: $P_3$ done.

**Gantt Chart:**
```
| P1 | P2 |   P1   |   P4   | P3 |
0    1    4        7        12   14
```

**Metric Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_1 &= 7, \quad TAT_1 = 7 - 0 = 7, \quad WT_1 = 7 - 4 = 3 \\
CT_2 &= 4, \quad TAT_2 = 4 - 1 = 3, \quad WT_2 = 3 - 3 = 0 \\
CT_3 &= 14, \quad TAT_3 = 14 - 2 = 12, \quad WT_3 = 12 - 2 = 10 \\
CT_4 &= 12, \quad TAT_4 = 12 - 3 = 9, \quad WT_4 = 9 - 5 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{3 + 0 + 10 + 4}{4} = \frac{17}{4} = 4.25 \text{ ms}
$$

*(Award 2 marks for preemptive priority definition, 3 marks for trace + Gantt, 2 marks for metric calculations.)*

---

#### ✅ Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ):** *(3.5 Marks)*

MLFQ is an **advanced preemptive scheduler** that uses **multiple Ready Queues**, each with a **different priority and time quantum**.

- Processes start in the **highest-priority queue** with small quantum.
- If a process **uses up its quantum**, it is **demoted** to a lower-priority queue.
- A higher-priority queue can **preempt** a lower-priority running process.
- **Advantages:** Combines fast response (short jobs) + low overhead (long jobs).
- **Used in:** Linux (CFS), Windows NT, FreeBSD.

**(ii) Priority Inversion:** *(3.5 Marks)*

**Definition:** Priority Inversion occurs when a **high-priority process** is *indirectly* blocked by a **low-priority process** because both share a resource held by the low-priority process, while a **medium-priority process** keeps the CPU busy.

**Classic Example:** The **Mars Pathfinder** bug (1997) — a low-priority meteorological task held a shared bus semaphore, blocking the high-priority bus management task.

**Solution — Priority Inheritance:**
- When a high-priority task is waiting for a resource held by a low-priority task, the low-priority task **temporarily inherits** the high priority until it releases the resource.
- Used in VxWorks, real-time Linux (PREEMPT_RT).

*(Award marks proportionally for definition, example, and solution.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks in Preemptive Scheduling questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to update Remaining Time:** Always recompute remaining time at every arrival tick — missing one comparison gives the wrong Gantt chart. *(–2 to –3 marks)*
> 2. **Mixing up AT and CT in TAT:** $TAT = CT - AT$, NOT $CT - BT$.
> 3. **Ignoring process arrivals in RR:** New arrivals must be inserted into the Ready Queue *before* the quantum-expired process is re-queued.
> 4. **Not showing preemption arrow:** Examiners explicitly want a visible trace step like *"At $t=2$, $P_2$ arrives; remaining $P_1 = 5 > 4$ → preempt."*
> 5. **Skipping the unit (ms/s) in the final answer:** Always write $\overline{WT} = 3.0$ **ms**, not just $3.0$.
> 6. **Wrong tie-breaker in Priority Scheduling:** State your tie-breaker rule explicitly (e.g., "lower priority number = higher priority, ties broken by FCFS").

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Preemptive Scheduling (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
>
> - ✅ Preemptive Scheduling = OS can *interrupt* a running process.
> - ✅ Context Switch overhead is the **main cost** of preemption.
> - ✅ **SRTF** = preemptive SJF; optimal avg WT but can cause starvation of long processes.
> - ✅ **Round Robin** = uses time quantum; fair but quantum size matters.
> - ✅ **Preemptive Priority** = highest-priority ready process always runs.
> - ✅ **MLFQ** = adaptive priority demotion based on CPU usage history.
> - ✅ **Priority Inversion** solved via **Priority Inheritance Protocol**.
> - ✅ Always state the assumption: *"Lower priority number = higher priority"* (or vice-versa).
> - ✅ Formulas: $TAT = CT - AT$, $WT = TAT - BT$.
> - ✅ $AT \leq CT$, $BT \leq TAT$ always.
> - ✅ Starvation is possible in SRTF and Priority Scheduling → mitigated by **Aging**.
> - ✅ Real-world usage: Linux CFS, Windows NT, Android, VxWorks RTOS.
> - ✅ Preemptive schedulers need **hardware timer interrupts** — non-preemptive ones do not.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dispatcher
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dispatcher

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: The Dispatcher — Core Concepts and Intuition

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standardized):**
> The **Dispatcher** is a small, specialized kernel module (or routine) within the Operating System that acts as the **final control bridge** between the CPU scheduler and the CPU. Once the short-term (CPU) scheduler has selected a process from the ready queue to be executed next, control is passed to the dispatcher, which is then responsible for handing physical control of the CPU to the selected process.

Formally, the dispatcher is defined in Silberschatz, Galvin, and Gagne (the canonical reference for KTU modules) as:

> *"The dispatcher is the module that gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler. This function involves the following: (1) switching context, (2) switching to user mode, (3) jumping to the proper location in the newly loaded program to restart that program."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) taxonomy, the dispatcher is classified under **Course Outcome CO1**: *"To demonstrate the basic structure and functional components of an operating system, including process management, memory management, and I/O management."* It satisfies the **Remembering** and **Understanding** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Airport Gate Operations Manager**
> Imagine an airport where planes (processes) are lined up on a taxiway (the **ready queue**) waiting for their turn to take off. The **air traffic scheduling team** (the **CPU scheduler**) decides *which* plane gets the next takeoff slot. Once that decision is made, the **Gate Operations Manager** (the **Dispatcher**) actually does the physical work:
> 1. Closes the doors of the previous plane and saves its parking stub (Context Switch Out),
> 2. Tows the new plane to the runway (Context Switch In),
> 3. Hands the ignition key to the new pilot (Switch to User Mode),
> 4. Watches the plane accelerate down the runway (Jump to Program Counter).

The dispatcher does **not decide** which plane flies. It **executes** the decision. It is the *hands* that carry out the *brain's* orders.

| **Scheduling (Brain)** | **Dispatching (Hands)** |
|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Decides *which* process runs next | Performs the *actual CPU handover* |
| Can take a long time (heuristics) | Must be **extremely fast** |
| Called "short-term scheduler" | Called "dispatcher" |
| A *decision* function | An *execution* function |

## 1.3 Role in the KTU Module 1 Context

In Module 1 ("Introduction & Process Management"), the dispatcher is presented as the **terminal stage** of the scheduling subsystem. The complete flow inside a modern OS is:

$$\text{Ready Queue} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Scheduler selects}} \text{Selected PID} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Dispatcher}} \text{CPU}$$

Every time the OS switches the CPU from one process to another, the dispatcher is invoked. This happens **thousands of times per second** in a typical modern OS.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Scheme — Key Highlight Callout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, PCITT403, 2024 Scheme):**
> The dispatcher is explicitly listed in the KTU 2024 syllabus under *Process Scheduling* as a distinct sub-topic. Examiners frequently test:
> - The exact list of dispatcher functions (4 functions).
> - The concept of **Dispatch Latency** and its standard unit (microseconds).
> - The quantitative relationship: *"The dispatcher should be as fast as possible because it is invoked during every process switch."*

**Standard Metric (Recalled for KTU):** A high-performance dispatcher typically has a **dispatch latency between 0.1 ms and 10 microseconds**. The **goal** is for dispatch latency to be **virtually negligible** compared to the CPU burst time of a process.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 1: Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Dispatcher Mechanism

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Four Canonical Functions

The dispatcher, once invoked by the scheduler, executes a strictly defined sequence of **four** operations. KTU examiners almost always ask for this list verbatim.

### Step 1: Context Switching (Out) — *Saving the Old State*
The dispatcher must preserve the execution state of the process being **preempted** (kicked out of the CPU). This state includes:
- The **Program Counter (PC)** — where it stopped.
- The **CPU Registers** — accumulator, base registers, stack pointer, general-purpose registers.
- The **Memory Management Information** — page table base register, segment table pointer.
- The **CPU Status Flags** — condition codes, interrupt enable flag.

This saved packet of state is collectively called the **Process Control Block (PCB)** of the old process. The dispatcher writes the new state of the old process into its PCB stored in kernel memory.

### Step 2: Context Switching (In) — *Loading the New State*
The dispatcher then reads the PCB of the **newly selected process** (already chosen by the scheduler) and loads:
- The new PC value into the CPU's program counter.
- The new register values into the CPU registers.
- The new memory management registers.

After this load, the CPU is "ready" to execute the new process — but the hardware is still in **privileged kernel mode**.

### Step 3: Switching to User Mode
The dispatcher must now alter the CPU's mode bit from **kernel/supervisor mode (0)** to **user mode (1)**. This step is critical because user processes must not have unrestricted access to hardware. After this switch, the CPU is fully prepared to run the new process in restricted user space.

### Step 4: Jumping to the Correct Instruction
Finally, the dispatcher transfers control to the location in the new process's address space pointed to by the newly loaded Program Counter. The new process then begins (or resumes) execution at this precise instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the order matters:**
> Steps 1 and 2 must occur in kernel mode (because they touch privileged registers). Step 3 must occur *after* the new PCB is loaded, so the new process does not see privileged state. Step 4 is the final irreversible "jump" that begins actual user execution.

## 2.2 Dispatch Latency — The Critical Performance Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> **Dispatch Latency** is the time required for the dispatcher to stop one process and start another running. It is the *overhead* incurred every time a context switch occurs.

Formally, if we let:
- $T_{cs-out}$ = time to save context of the running process,
- $T_{cs-in}$ = time to load context of the new process,
- $T_{mode}$ = time to switch from kernel to user mode,
- $T_{jump}$ = time for the final jump to the new PC,

then the total **Dispatch Latency** is:

$$\boxed{\;D_{latency} = T_{cs-out} + T_{cs-in} + T_{mode} + T_{jump}\;}$$

**Typical Magnitudes (Industry Standard):**
- On x86 hardware: **5 to 10 microseconds** for a full dispatch.
- On embedded ARM Cortex-M: **1 to 3 microseconds**.
- On legacy systems (1990s): **100 microseconds to 1 millisecond**.

## 2.3 The Scheduler–Dispatcher Distinction (Why KTU Tests This)

| **Aspect** | **Short-Term Scheduler** | **Dispatcher** |
|------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| **Decision or Action?** | Decision (logic) | Action (mechanism) |
| **Invoked when?** | When CPU becomes idle or on time-slice expiry | Immediately after the scheduler's decision |
| **Speed requirement** | Can be slow (uses heuristics) | Must be **extremely fast** |
| **Code size** | Often large (policy heavy) | Small, optimized, sometimes in **assembly** for speed |
| **Where located?** | Kernel | Kernel (often in a hot path) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all KTU-testable formulas, definitions, and metrics for the Dispatcher topic.

| **Symbol / Term** | **Definition** | **Equation / Value** | **Units** |
|-------------------|----------------|----------------------|-----------|
| $D_{latency}$ | Dispatch Latency | $D_{latency} = T_{cs-out} + T_{cs-in} + T_{mode} + T_{jump}$ | microseconds ($\mu s$) |
| $T_{cs-out}$ | Time to save old process state | Empirical: $1$–$3$ $\mu s$ | microseconds |
| $T_{cs-in}$ | Time to load new process state | Empirical: $1$–$3$ $\mu s$ | microseconds |
| $T_{mode}$ | Kernel-to-user mode switch | Empirical: $< 1$ $\mu s$ | microseconds |
| $T_{jump}$ | Branch to new PC | Empirical: $< 0.1$ $\mu s$ | nanoseconds |
| CPU Utilization (ideal) | Fraction of time CPU does useful work | $\frac{T_{burst}}{T_{burst} + D_{latency}}$ | dimensionless ratio |
| Lower bound on overhead | Dispatcher's share of CPU time | $D_{latency} \times f_{context\_switches}$ | ratio (0 to 1) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The dispatcher's performance directly determines the **maximum context-switch rate** an OS can sustain. Real-world consequences:

1. **Real-time Operating Systems (RTOS)**: In automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units) and avionics (e.g., VxWorks, QNX), the dispatcher is hard-real-time constrained. Dispatch latency is part of the *schedulability analysis* and must be **bounded and provable** — often less than **50 $\mu s$** for hard real-time tasks.

2. **Server Virtualization (Hypervisors like VMware ESXi, KVM)**: Each VM context switch incurs *two* dispatch latencies — one for the guest OS and one for the hypervisor. Engineers design **"side-core" techniques** (Intel VMX, AMD-V) to reduce this compound latency.

3. **Database Engines**: PostgreSQL and Oracle RDBMS carefully measure dispatcher overhead because it directly affects transaction throughput (TPS — Transactions Per Second).

4. **Mobile OS (Android, iOS)**: Modern phones perform 1000+ context switches per second. Google's *schedutil* governor is designed so that the combined scheduler + dispatcher overhead is below **2%** of CPU time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 1: Step-by-Step Derivation and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Impact of Dispatch Latency on CPU Utilization

This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** that examiners often include as a sub-part of a 7-mark question. We derive how dispatch latency reduces effective CPU utilization.

### Derivation Setup

Let us define:
- $t$ = Average time a process spends executing before being preempted (CPU burst time), in seconds.
- $D$ = Average dispatch latency, in seconds.
- $n$ = Number of context switches per unit time (per second).

For one complete cycle of a process (run, then switch out), the total wall-clock time consumed is:

$$T_{cycle} = t + D$$

The fraction of this time during which the CPU is doing *useful work* (running the process, not switching) is:

$$U = \frac{\text{Useful Time}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{t}{t + D}$$

This ratio $U$ is the **CPU utilization** attributable to one process, accounting for dispatcher overhead.

### Step-by-Step Expansion

We rewrite to expose the *overhead* term:

$$U = \frac{t}{t + D}$$

We now express $D$ as a fraction of $t$ for sensitivity analysis. Let $\alpha = D / t$ be the **overhead ratio** (dispatch latency as a multiple of CPU burst).

Substituting $D = \alpha t$:

$$U = \frac{t}{t + \alpha t}$$

Factor $t$ out of the denominator:

$$U = \frac{t}{t(1 + \alpha)}$$

Cancel $t$ from numerator and denominator:

$$\boxed{\;U = \frac{1}{1 + \alpha}\;}$$

This is the elegant closed-form expression. We can now read off three limiting cases.

### Limiting Case Analysis

**Case 1: Zero Dispatch Overhead ($\alpha \to 0$)**

$$U = \lim_{\alpha \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \alpha} = \frac{1}{1 + 0} = 1$$

This means **100% CPU utilization** — the dispatcher is instantaneous. This is the theoretical ideal.

**Case 2: Dispatch Overhead Equals CPU Burst ($\alpha = 1$)**

$$U = \frac{1}{1 + 1} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

This means **50% CPU utilization** — half the CPU time is wasted on context switches. Clearly unacceptable for production systems.

**Case 3: Dispatch Overhead Much Larger Than CPU Burst ($\alpha \gg 1$)**

$$U \approx \frac{1}{\alpha} \to 0$$

The CPU is **thrashing** — spending almost all its time switching processes and almost no time doing useful work. This is the *livelock* failure mode.

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Suppose a process has an average CPU burst of **40 ms**, and the dispatcher has a latency of **8 $\mu s$**. Calculate the CPU utilization, and then recalculate if the dispatch latency increases to **200 $\mu s$**.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $t = 40 \text{ ms} = 40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$
- $D_1 = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$
- $D_2 = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$

For $D_1 = 8 \text{ } \mu s$:

$$U_1 = \frac{t}{t + D_1} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{-3} + 8 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$U_1 = \frac{0.040}{0.040008} = 0.9998 = \mathbf{99.98\%}$$

For $D_2 = 200 \text{ } \mu s$:

$$U_2 = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{-3} + 200 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$U_2 = \frac{0.040}{0.0402} = 0.9950 = \mathbf{99.50\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Even with a 25× increase in dispatch latency, the CPU utilization remains very high *because* the CPU burst (40 ms) is thousands of times larger than the dispatch latency. This shows why dispatch latency matters most for **short CPU bursts** (e.g., I/O-bound interactive processes with bursts of 1–2 ms).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of a Slow Dispatcher

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical matrix is a high-yield KTU exam table mapping each symptom to its root cause in the dispatcher subsystem.

| **Symptom Observed by User** | **Root Cause in Dispatcher** | **Diagnostic Metric** | **Typical Fix** |
|------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| System "feels sluggish" | High dispatch latency | $D_{latency} > 50 \text{ } \mu s$ | Optimize PCB copy, use register windows |
| Audio stutter / video frame drop | Dispatcher overruns real-time deadline | Jitter $> 5$ ms | Use a real-time scheduler (SCHED_FIFO) |
| High CPU usage with low throughput | Excessive context switching | $> 10{,}000$ switches/sec | Tune time slice, batch I/O completions |
| Kernel panic on process switch | Corrupted PCB pointer | Stack traceback | Bounds check, MPU (Memory Protection Unit) |
| Cold-boot slow start | Dispatcher initialization delay | Boot time $> 30$ s | Lazy PCB allocation, parallel init |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Algorithm Trace — A Single Dispatch Operation

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the step-by-step trace of one full dispatch cycle. KTU examiners sometimes ask students to "describe the dispatch sequence" for full marks.

**Initial State:** Process P1 is running on CPU. Scheduler decides to preempt P1 and run P2.

**Step 1 — Save Context of P1**
- CPU hardware saves current PC, SP, and registers into P1's PCB.
- Mode is **kernel (0)**.
- P1's PCB pointer is held in a kernel register.

**Step 2 — Update P1's PCB Status**
- OS sets P1's state field to **READY** (in PCB).
- P1's PCB is reinserted into the ready queue (or remains if it was preempted in place).

**Step 3 — Select New Process**
- Scheduler picks P2 from the ready queue.
- P2's PCB state is changed from **READY** to **RUNNING**.

**Step 4 — Load Context of P2**
- Dispatcher reads P2's PCB.
- Loads P2's PC into the CPU's program counter.
- Loads P2's registers into CPU registers.
- Loads P2's memory management registers (page table base, etc.).

**Step 5 — Switch to User Mode**
- The CPU's mode bit is changed from **0 (kernel)** to **1 (user)**.

**Step 6 — Jump to P2's PC**
- CPU begins fetching instructions at the address now stored in PC.
- P2 resumes (or begins) execution.

**Final State:** P2 is now running in user mode. P1 is in the ready queue with a saved complete state.

This sequence completes in **5 to 10 microseconds** on a typical modern x86 system.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 1: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Scheduler + Dispatcher + CPU

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the functional flow of a context switch, with the dispatcher's role highlighted as the bridge between the scheduler and the hardware CPU.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Process Sources]
        readyQ[Ready Queue]
        newP[New Process]
        ioComp[IO Completion Event]
    end

    subgraph SCH[Scheduling Subsystem]
        longS[Long Term Scheduler]
        midS[Medium Term Scheduler]
        shortS[Short Term Scheduler]
    end

    subgraph DISP[Dispatcher Module]
        saveCtx[Step 1 Save Old Context]
        loadCtx[Step 2 Load New Context]
        modeSw[Step 3 Switch to User Mode]
        jmpPC[Step 4 Jump to New PC]
    end

    subgraph HW[CPU Hardware]
        cpuCore[CPU Core]
        regBank[Register Bank]
        modeBit[Mode Bit Register]
    end

    readyQ --> shortS
    newP --> longS
    ioComp --> shortS
    longS --> readyQ
    midS --> readyQ

    shortS -->|Selected PID| saveCtx
    saveCtx --> loadCtx
    loadCtx --> modeSw
    modeSw --> jmpPC
    jmpPC --> cpuCore

    cpuCore --> regBank
    cpuCore --> modeBit
    saveCtx -.->|Reads| regBank
    loadCtx -.->|Writes| regBank
    modeSw -.->|Toggles| modeBit
```

**How to read this diagram:** The scheduler (top) makes a *decision* and passes the selected Process ID to the dispatcher (middle block). The dispatcher then performs the four sequential steps, interacting with CPU hardware registers (bottom block) to physically install the new process.

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Context Switch Trace

The following Mermaid sequence diagram shows the *time-ordered* message exchange between the scheduler, dispatcher, kernel, and CPU during a single context switch from P1 to P2.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sched as Short-Term Scheduler
    participant Disp as Dispatcher
    participant PCB1 as PCB of P1
    participant PCB2 as PCB of P2
    participant CPU as CPU Hardware

    Sched->>Disp: invoke with PID of P2
    Disp->>CPU: SAVE current registers
    CPU-->>Disp: returns saved values
    Disp->>PCB1: WRITE saved state to P1 PCB
    Disp->>PCB2: READ state of P2
    PCB2-->>Disp: P2 register values
    Disp->>CPU: LOAD P2 registers
    Disp->>CPU: SET mode bit to USER
    Disp->>CPU: JUMP to P2 program counter
    CPU-->>Sched: dispatch complete
    Note over CPU,Disp: P2 is now running in user mode
```

**How to read this diagram:** Time flows downward. Each arrow is one interaction. The total time from the scheduler's first call to the dispatcher's "dispatch complete" message is the **dispatch latency**.

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Process State During a Dispatch

The following Mermaid state diagram shows how a single process's state changes during a complete dispatch cycle, viewed from the OS's perspective.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New: process creation
    New --> Ready: admitted
    Ready --> Running: dispatcher dispatch
    Running --> Ready: preempted or time slice expires
    Running --> Waiting: IO or event wait
    Waiting --> Ready: IO completion
    Running --> Terminated: exit syscall
    Terminated --> [*]

    note right of Running: Dispatcher active here
    note left of Ready: Schedulers queue here
```

**Highlight:** The transition from **Ready** to **Running** is the *only* transition where the dispatcher is actively involved. All other transitions are driven by the process itself (system calls) or by the OS's I/O subsystem.

## 4.4 Component Map — Where the Dispatcher Lives in the Kernel

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the dispatcher's place within the broader OS kernel architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USpace[User Space]
        uProc[User Processes]
        sysLib[System Libraries]
    end

    subgraph KSpace[Kernel Space]
        subSys[Subsystems - File, Net, IO]
        cpuSched[CPU Scheduler - Long, Short, Medium]
        dispatcher[Dispatcher Module]
        clockInt[Clock Interrupt Handler]
        ipcMod[IPC Module]
    end

    subgraph HW[Hardware]
        cpuHW[CPU Chip]
        mmHW[MMU Chip]
        tmrHW[Hardware Timer]
    end

    uProc -->|syscall trap| subSys
    subSys --> cpuSched
    clockInt --> cpuSched
    cpuSched -->|invoke| dispatcher
    dispatcher --> cpuHW
    dispatcher --> mmHW
    tmrHW -->|interrupt| clockInt
    uProc -->|pipe, signal| ipcMod
    ipcMod --> cpuSched
    sysLib --> uProc
```

**How to read this diagram:** The dispatcher sits in the **kernel's hot path**, directly interfacing with the CPU and MMU hardware. It is invoked either by the scheduler (after a decision) or by the clock interrupt handler (on a time-slice expiry).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 1: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A questions are short-answer questions testing *Remembering* and basic *Understanding*. Each carries **3 marks** and expects an answer of **3 to 5 lines** or a short list.

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the dispatcher. State any two functions performed by the dispatcher.**

**Model Answer:**

The dispatcher is a small kernel module that hands control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler.

**Two functions of the dispatcher:**
1. **Context Switching** — saving the state (registers, PC, memory management info) of the currently running process into its Process Control Block (PCB), and loading the state of the newly selected process.
2. **Switching to User Mode** — changing the CPU's mode bit from kernel mode to user mode before the new process begins execution, ensuring hardware protection.

*(A third function may also be stated: jumping to the correct instruction in the new process's address space.)*

**Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark, Each function = 1 mark (Total = 3 marks).

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is dispatch latency? Why must the dispatcher be fast?**

**Model Answer:**

**Dispatch Latency** is the time taken by the dispatcher to stop one process and start another. It includes the time to save the old process's context, load the new process's context, switch to user mode, and jump to the new program counter.

**Why the dispatcher must be fast:**
The dispatcher is invoked during **every context switch**, which occurs thousands of times per second. If the dispatcher is slow, a significant fraction of CPU time is wasted on switching overhead, reducing the time available for actual process execution. This directly reduces system throughput and responsiveness.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1.5 marks, Reason = 1.5 marks (Total = 3 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** each. The 14 marks are split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** unless otherwise specified. Students answer **one full question** but may have **internal choice between sub-parts**. The two choices below are completely independent alternatives (a student may answer Q-A or Q-B).

---

### Question B – Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the dispatcher in an operating system. List and briefly describe the four functions performed by the dispatcher. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Define dispatch latency. The average CPU burst time of a process in a system is 50 ms, and the dispatch latency is 100 microseconds. Calculate:
   - (i) The CPU utilization considering dispatch overhead. **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) The overhead ratio $\alpha$. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) Comment on what happens if the average CPU burst drops to 2 ms while dispatch latency remains at 100 $\mu s$. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

A neatly drawn block diagram should show the **Ready Queue → Short-Term Scheduler → Dispatcher → CPU** flow, as drawn in Section 4.1 of these notes. *(Award 2 marks for the diagram.)*

**The Four Functions of the Dispatcher:**

1. **Context Switching (Out and In):** The dispatcher saves the state of the currently running process (PC, registers, memory management info, CPU flags) into its Process Control Block. It then loads the saved state of the newly selected process from its PCB into the CPU hardware. *(2 marks)*

2. **Switching to User Mode:** After loading the new process's state, the dispatcher changes the CPU's privilege mode from kernel (supervisor) mode to user mode. This ensures that the new process executes in restricted user space and cannot directly access hardware. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Jumping to the New Program Counter:** The dispatcher transfers control to the address pointed to by the newly loaded PC, so that the new process begins or resumes execution at the correct instruction. *(1.5 marks)*

*(Sub-total: 7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) CPU Utilization:**

Given:
- $t = 50 \text{ ms} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$
- $D = 100 \text{ } \mu s = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$

Using the CPU utilization formula:

$$U = \frac{t}{t + D} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-3} + 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$U = \frac{0.050}{0.0501} = 0.9980 = \mathbf{99.80\%}$$

*(3 marks)*

**(ii) Overhead Ratio $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \frac{D}{t} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} = 2 \times 10^{-3} = \mathbf{0.002}$$

The dispatch latency is **0.2%** of the CPU burst. *(2 marks)*

**(iii) When CPU burst drops to 2 ms:**

New $t = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ s, $D$ remains $1 \times 10^{-4}$ s.

$$U_{new} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-3} + 1 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.002}{0.0021} = 0.9524 = \mathbf{95.24\%}$$

**Comment:** The CPU utilization drops from **99.80% to 95.24%** — a loss of **4.56 percentage points**, even though the absolute dispatch latency is unchanged. This demonstrates that for short, I/O-bound processes (interactive workloads), the dispatcher's speed becomes critical. The dispatch overhead is **25 times more significant** when bursts are short. *(2 marks)*

---

### Question B – Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Differentiate between the **short-term scheduler** and the **dispatcher**. Why is the dispatcher implemented separately from the scheduler? **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A real-time embedded system uses a dispatcher with a dispatch latency of 20 microseconds. The system schedules 2000 context switches per second. Calculate:
   - (i) The total time spent in dispatching per second. **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) The percentage of CPU time consumed by the dispatcher. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) If the hardware team can reduce the dispatch latency to 5 microseconds, what is the new percentage of CPU time consumed? Comment on the improvement. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| **Basis** | **Short-Term Scheduler** | **Dispatcher** |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------|
| **Function** | Decides *which* process runs next | Performs the *actual CPU handover* |
| **Speed** | Can be relatively slow (uses algorithms like RR, SJF) | Must be **extremely fast** |
| **Invoked** | On CPU idle or time-slice expiry | Immediately after the scheduler's decision |
| **Code size** | Larger, policy-heavy code | Small, optimized, often in assembly |
| **Frequency** | Less frequent | Very frequent (every context switch) |
| **Output** | A Process ID | Physical CPU state change |

*(4 marks for the table)*

**Why the dispatcher is implemented separately:**
The dispatcher is implemented as a **separate module** to **maximize its speed** and **minimize its overhead**. Because the dispatcher runs on every context switch, even small inefficiencies in its code compound into significant CPU time loss. By isolating the dispatcher's hot path (a few hundred instructions) from the scheduler's complex policy code, OS designers can:
- **Optimize** the dispatcher in assembly language for raw speed.
- **Cache** the dispatcher code in the CPU's instruction cache (it stays hot).
- **Avoid** the overhead of high-level function calls in the dispatch path.
- **Guarantee** a bounded, predictable execution time — critical for real-time systems.

*(3 marks for the reasoning)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Total time in dispatching per second:**

$$T_{dispatch} = (\text{switches per second}) \times D$$

$$T_{dispatch} = 2000 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 40{,}000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = \mathbf{0.040 \text{ s}}$$

The dispatcher uses **0.04 seconds** of CPU time per second. *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Percentage of CPU time consumed:**

$$\%_{dispatch} = \frac{T_{dispatch}}{1 \text{ s}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.040}{1.0} \times 100\% = \mathbf{4.0\%}$$

The dispatcher consumes **4% of total CPU time**. *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Reduced dispatch latency to 5 $\mu s$:**

$$T_{dispatch,new} = 2000 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 10{,}000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.010 \text{ s}$$

$$\%_{dispatch,new} = \frac{0.010}{1.0} \times 100\% = \mathbf{1.0\%}$$

**Comment:** Reducing the dispatch latency by **75%** (from 20 $\mu s$ to 5 $\mu s$) reduces the dispatcher's CPU consumption by **75%** (from 4% to 1%). This frees up an additional **3% of CPU time** for actual process work. In a real-time embedded system, this 3% could be the difference between meeting and missing a hard deadline. This illustrates the engineering principle that **dispatcher optimization yields linear, predictable performance gains** in the system's overhead budget. *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Scheduler with Dispatcher (Major 1-mark deduction per occurrence):** Students frequently write "the dispatcher decides which process to run" — this is the **scheduler's** role, not the dispatcher's. The dispatcher *executes* the decision.
> 2. **Forgetting one of the four functions (–1.5 marks):** The four functions (save context, load context, switch to user mode, jump to PC) must all be listed. Omitting "switching to user mode" is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Unit confusion in dispatch latency (–1 mark):** Always specify units. Dispatch latency is in **microseconds ($\mu s$)**, not milliseconds.
> 4. **Forgetting to express CPU burst in seconds in numerical problems (–1 mark):** When substituting into $U = t / (t + D)$, both $t$ and $D$ must be in the **same units** (preferably seconds in SI).
> 5. **Writing the wrong formula for CPU utilization (–2 marks):** The correct formula is $U = t / (t + D)$, where $D$ is **added to the denominator**, not subtracted.
> 6. **In Part B, failing to draw the block diagram (–2 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly allocate 1.5 to 2 marks for the diagram. A textual-only answer will lose these marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Definition:** The dispatcher is the OS module that hands CPU control to the process selected by the short-term scheduler. *(Not the scheduler itself.)*
- **Four Canonical Functions:** (1) Save context of old process into PCB, (2) Load context of new process from PCB, (3) Switch from kernel mode to user mode, (4) Jump to the new program counter.
- **Dispatch Latency Formula:** $D_{latency} = T_{cs-out} + T_{cs-in} + T_{mode} + T_{jump}$. Typical value: **5–10 microseconds** on x86.
- **CPU Utilization Formula:** $U = t / (t + D)$, where $t$ is CPU burst and $D$ is dispatch latency. Higher $D$ relative to $t$ means lower utilization.
- **Overhead Ratio:** $\alpha = D / t$. CPU utilization can be written as $U = 1 / (1 + \alpha)$.
- **Scheduler vs Dispatcher:** Scheduler = **decision-maker** (which process?). Dispatcher = **action-taker** (actually switch the CPU).
- **Why the dispatcher must be fast:** It is invoked during every context switch (often thousands of times per second). Slow dispatchers waste CPU cycles.
- **Where the dispatcher lives:** In the **kernel's hot path**, directly interfacing with the CPU and MMU hardware. Often written partially in **assembly** for speed.
- **Real-time relevance:** In hard real-time systems, dispatch latency must be **bounded and provable** (e.g., VxWorks, QNX, RTLinux).
- **Common KTU question types:**
  - Define dispatcher and list its functions. *(2–3 marks)*
  - Differentiate scheduler vs dispatcher. *(5–7 marks)*
  - Calculate CPU utilization given $t$ and $D$. *(3–5 marks)*
  - Calculate dispatcher CPU consumption given switches/sec and $D$. *(3–5 marks)*
- **Memory trick to remember the 4 functions:** **"S L S J"** = **S**ave context, **L**oad context, **S**witch to user mode, **J**ump to PC.
- **Common examiner trick:** They will give you a process with a very short CPU burst (1–2 ms) and a noticeable dispatch latency, then ask you to calculate utilization. The answer will be **much lower** than naive intuition suggests — that's the lesson.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scheduling Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Scheduling Criteria – The Quality Bar of a CPU Scheduler

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scheduling Criteria** are the set of quantitative and qualitative performance metrics used by the **Operating System** to evaluate, compare, and select an appropriate **CPU Scheduling Algorithm**. These criteria measure how efficiently a scheduler manages the **Ready Queue**, allocates the **CPU** to the **Process Control Block (PCB)** of a ready process, and preserves the **multiprogramming** objective of the kernel.

In simple, examiner-acceptable language:

> *"Scheduling criteria are the benchmarks that tell us whether a particular CPU scheduling algorithm is 'good' or 'bad' for a given workload."*

The **5 canonical scheduling criteria** (mandated by the KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus) are:

1. **CPU Utilization**
2. **Throughput**
3. **Turnaround Time (TAT)**
4. **Waiting Time (WT)**
5. **Response Time (RT)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Multiplex Theatre Ticket Counter"

Imagine **5 customers (Processes)** arrive at a **single billing counter (CPU)** at **different times (Arrival Time)**. Each customer has a **different billing complexity (Burst Time)**.

The **counter manager (Scheduler)** must decide *who gets the counter next*.

- If the **counter is busy most of the time** → **CPU Utilization** is high.
- If **all 5 customers finish quickly** → **Throughput** is high.
- If each customer **waits very little** before reaching the counter → **Waiting Time** is low.
- If each customer’s **total time in the shop (entry to exit)** is low → **Turnaround Time** is low.
- If the **first bill is generated fast**, even if the full billing takes longer → **Response Time** is low.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **goal of any CPU scheduling algorithm** is to **maximize** CPU Utilization & Throughput, while **minimizing** TAT, WT, and RT. No single algorithm can optimally satisfy *all* five criteria simultaneously — this is the fundamental **trade-off** every OS designer must accept.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Scheduling Criteria

| **Optimization Direction** | **Criterion** | **Ideal Value** |
|---|---|---|
| Maximize | CPU Utilization | As close to **100 %** as possible |
| Maximize | Throughput | As **high** as possible |
| Minimize | Turnaround Time | As **low** as possible |
| Minimize | Waiting Time | As **low** as possible |
| Minimize | Response Time | As **low** as possible |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Criterion 1 — CPU Utilization

**Definition:** The fraction of time the CPU is doing *useful work* (executing a user process or OS kernel code) versus sitting idle.

**Operational Logic:**
- If the CPU remains idle even for a microsecond, system throughput drops.
- In a **single-programmed** system, CPU utilization = (CPU busy time) / (Total elapsed time).
- In a **multiprogrammed** system, the OS keeps several processes in memory so that when one performs **I/O**, the CPU can immediately pick another → utilization approaches **100 %**.

**Engineering Utility:** Directly impacts the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** of a data centre. Cloud providers (AWS, Azure) charge by CPU-utilization hours.

## 2.2 Criterion 2 — Throughput

**Definition:** Number of processes *completed* per unit of time.

**Operational Logic:**
- A **long burst-time** process reduces throughput.
- **Preemptive** algorithms (Round Robin, SRTF) generally improve throughput under mixed workloads.
- Measured in **processes/second** or **jobs/hour**.

## 2.3 Criterion 3 — Turnaround Time (TAT)

**Definition:** Total time a process spends in the system, from *arrival* in the ready queue to *completion*.

**Why it matters:** It captures the **end-to-end latency** experienced by a user submitting a job (e.g., compiling code, rendering a video).

## 2.4 Criterion 4 — Waiting Time (WT)

**Definition:** Total time a process spends in the **ready queue** waiting to be assigned the CPU.

**Why it matters:** A process that is *running* is not wasting time; only *queued* time is "wasted". Low WT indicates fairness and responsiveness.

## 2.5 Criterion 5 — Response Time (RT)

**Definition:** Time from process arrival to the *first* time it gets the CPU.

**Why it matters:** Critical for **interactive systems** (GUIs, games, real-time control). Even if a process takes 10 s to complete, if the *first response* arrives in 50 ms, the user feels the system is "snappy".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Response Time ≠ Waiting Time** for *preemptive* algorithms. For *non-preemptive* algorithms (FCFS, Non-preemptive SJF), RT = WT because the first CPU allocation is the start of execution itself.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (No vertical pipes used)

| **S.No** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Turnaround Time | $TAT = CT - AT$ | time units | $CT$ = Completion Time, $AT$ = Arrival Time |
| 2 | Waiting Time | $WT = TAT - BT$ | time units | $BT$ = Burst Time |
| 3 | Alternative WT | $WT = \text{Start Time} - AT$ | time units | Valid only when there is **no preemption** |
| 4 | Response Time | $RT = \text{First CPU Allocation Time} - AT$ | time units | $RT \le WT$ for preemptive |
| 5 | Average TAT | $\overline{TAT} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i}{n}$ | time units | Lower is better |
| 6 | Average WT | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i}{n}$ | time units | Lower is better |
| 7 | Throughput | $Th = \dfrac{n}{\text{Total Elapsed Time}}$ | processes / time | Higher is better |
| 8 | CPU Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\sum BT_i}{\text{Total Elapsed Time}} \times 100\,\%$ | percentage | Should be $\le 100\,\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** For a system with **zero idle time** (which is the ideal case in a multiprogrammed OS), $U = 100\,\%$ and $\sum BT_i = \text{Total Elapsed Time}$. This identity is heavily tested in KTU problems.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Primary Criterion** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch Processing** (Payroll, EOD bank jobs) | **Throughput + TAT** | Thousands of jobs, no human waiting |
| **Interactive Desktop OS** (Windows, macOS) | **Response Time + WT** | Users perceive every millisecond |
| **Real-Time Embedded Systems** (ABS, Pacemaker) | **Hard Deadlines (TAT bound)** | Missing a deadline = catastrophic |
| **Supercomputers / HPC** | **Throughput + CPU Util.** | Maximize FLOPS/\$ |
| **Web Servers / Cloud** | **Response Time** | Directly tied to user retention |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Waiting Time from First Principles

We start from the fundamental definition:

$$
\text{Total time in system} = \text{Time spent waiting} + \text{Time spent executing} + \text{Time spent doing I/O}
$$

However, scheduling criteria focus only on the **CPU-bound** view (since the OS has no direct control over I/O device latencies once the I/O request is issued). Hence:

$$
TAT = \text{Time in Ready Queue} + \text{Time on CPU}
$$

Where "Time on CPU" equals the **Burst Time ($BT$)** the process actually requires, and "Time in Ready Queue" is the **Waiting Time ($WT$)** we are solving for. Therefore:

$$
TAT = WT + BT
$$

Re-arranging for $WT$:

$$
\boxed{WT = TAT - BT}
$$

Substituting the expanded form of $TAT$:

$$
WT = (CT - AT) - BT
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of CPU Utilization and Throughput Relationship

Consider $n$ processes finishing at completion time $CT_{\max}$ (the latest finish time in the Gantt chart). The total time the system is operational is $CT_{\max} - AT_{\min}$. The CPU is busy for $\sum_{i=1}^{n} BT_i$ units of time (assuming zero context-switch overhead). Therefore:

$$
U = \frac{\text{Total CPU Busy Time}}{\text{Total System Time}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} BT_i}{CT_{\max} - AT_{\min}}
$$

Throughput in the same window is:

$$
Th = \frac{n}{CT_{\max} - AT_{\min}}
$$

Dividing $U$ by $Th$:

$$
\frac{U}{Th} = \frac{\sum BT_i}{n} = \overline{BT}
$$

This gives a beautiful KTU-favourite identity:

$$
\boxed{U = Th \times \overline{BT}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity proves that **maximizing CPU utilization** and **maximizing throughput** are mathematically equivalent goals when the average burst time is fixed.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example (FCFS Algorithm)

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
Consider the following set of 4 processes. Apply the **FCFS (First-Come-First-Served)** scheduling algorithm and compute **Average TAT, Average WT, Average RT, Throughput, and CPU Utilization**.

| **Process** | **Arrival Time (AT)** | **Burst Time (BT)** |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 2 | 4 |
| P3 | 4 | 1 |
| P4 | 5 | 4 |

### Step 1 — Construct the Gantt Chart

FCFS dispatches processes in order of arrival: P1 → P2 → P3 → P4.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\vert \text{P1} \quad 0 \rightarrow 7 \;\vert\; \text{P2} \quad 7 \rightarrow 11 \;\vert\; \text{P3} \quad 11 \rightarrow 12 \;\vert\; \text{P4} \quad 12 \rightarrow 16 \;\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Determine Completion Times (CT) from Gantt Chart

| **Process** | **CT** |
|---|---|
| P1 | 7 |
| P2 | 11 |
| P3 | 12 |
| P4 | 16 |

### Step 3 — Compute TAT using $TAT = CT - AT$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 7 - 0 = 7 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 11 - 2 = 9 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 12 - 4 = 8 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 16 - 5 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute WT using $WT = TAT - BT$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 7 - 7 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 9 - 4 = 5 \\
WT_{P3} &= 8 - 1 = 7 \\
WT_{P4} &= 11 - 4 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute RT (FCFS is non-preemptive ⇒ RT = WT)

$$
RT_{P1}=0, \quad RT_{P2}=5, \quad RT_{P3}=7, \quad RT_{P4}=7
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Averages

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{7+9+8+11}{4} = \frac{35}{4} = 8.75 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{0+5+7+7}{4} = \frac{19}{4} = 4.75 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\overline{RT} = \frac{0+5+7+7}{4} = 4.75 \text{ units}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Throughput and CPU Utilization

$$
Th = \frac{4 \text{ processes}}{16 \text{ units}} = 0.25 \text{ processes/unit time}
$$

$$
\sum BT_i = 7+4+1+4 = 16 \text{ units}, \quad \text{Total time} = 16 \text{ units}
$$

$$
U = \frac{16}{16} \times 100\,\% = 100\,\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Significance

| **Criterion** | **Affected By** | **Independent Of** | **Tracked In PCB?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization | I/O frequency of processes, degree of multiprogramming | Process priority | ❌ |
| Throughput | Mix of long & short jobs | Individual process size | ❌ |
| TAT | Scheduling policy + Burst times | Memory size | ✅ (start time) |
| WT | Scheduling policy + Arrival pattern | Output device speed | ✅ (start time) |
| RT | Initial dispatch delay | Total job length | ✅ (start time) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification & Flow of Scheduling Criteria

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Scheduling Criteria] --> B[Maximize Group]
    A --> C[Minimize Group]

    B --> B1[CPU Utilization]
    B --> B2[Throughput]

    C --> C1[Turnaround Time]
    C --> C2[Waiting Time]
    C --> C3[Response Time]

    B1 --> D[System Centric Metrics]
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> E[User Centric Metrics]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E

    E --> F{Interactive System}
    E --> G{Batch System}
    F --> C3
    G --> C1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of a Scheduler's Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    Input[Process Input AT, BT, Priority] --> Scheduler[CPU Scheduler Module]
    Scheduler --> Algo[Scheduling Algorithm e.g. FCFS, SJF, RR]
    Algo --> Gantt[Gantt Chart Generation]
    Gantt --> Calc[Performance Calculator]
    Calc --> Met1[CPU Utilization]
    Calc --> Met2[Throughput]
    Calc --> Met3[Turnaround Time]
    Calc --> Met4[Waiting Time]
    Calc --> Met5[Response Time]
    Met1 --> Judge{Quality Verdict}
    Met2 --> Judge
    Met3 --> Judge
    Met4 --> Judge
    Met5 --> Judge
    Judge --> Output[Algorithm Ranking for KTU Exam]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Timeline Visualization of TAT, WT, and BT for One Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[Arrival AT equals T0] -->|Waits in Ready Queue| T1[First CPU Allocation equals Start Time]
    T1 -->|Executes for BT units| T2[Process Completion equals CT]
    T0 -.->|Response Time RT| T1
    T1 -.->|Burst Time BT| T2
    T0 -.->|Turnaround Time TAT| T2
    T0 -.->|Waiting Time WT plus BT| T2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List and define any five scheduling criteria used to evaluate the performance of a CPU scheduling algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

The five scheduling criteria used to evaluate CPU scheduling algorithms are:

1. **CPU Utilization** — The percentage of time the CPU is busy executing processes. Higher is better.
2. **Throughput** — Number of processes completed per unit time. Higher is better.
3. **Turnaround Time (TAT)** — Total time from process arrival to completion, given by $TAT = CT - AT$. Lower is better.
4. **Waiting Time (WT)** — Total time a process waits in the ready queue, given by $WT = TAT - BT$. Lower is better.
5. **Response Time (RT)** — Time from process arrival to the first CPU allocation, given by $RT = \text{First Start} - AT$. Lower is better.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Waiting Time and Response Time. Are they always equal? Justify with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Waiting Time (WT)** is the *total* time a process spends waiting in the ready queue across its entire lifetime. It accounts for all preemptions.
- **Response Time (RT)** is the time from arrival to the *first* time the process gets the CPU.

They are **equal only for non-preemptive algorithms** (e.g., FCFS, Non-Preemptive SJF) because the first CPU allocation is also the *start* of execution, and there is no further preemption to add more wait.

**Example (FCFS, Non-Preemptive):** P1 arrives at 0, BT=4, P2 arrives at 1, BT=3. P1 runs 0–4, P2 runs 4–7.
- $WT_{P2} = 4-1 = 3$, $RT_{P2} = 4-1 = 3$ → **Equal**.

In **Round Robin** (preemptive, quantum=1), the same example would yield $RT_{P2}=1$ but $WT_{P2}=3$ → **Not equal**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> • Students frequently confuse **Response Time with Waiting Time** in preemptive problems — this is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in KTU papers.
> • Always draw the **Gantt Chart first** before writing formulas. Marks are awarded for the Gantt chart (2 marks) and then the table (3 marks) before calculations.
> • Do **not** subtract the wrong indices. Re-read $CT - AT$ vs $TAT - BT$ carefully.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**Consider the following five processes with their Arrival Time (AT) and Burst Time (BT):**

| **Process** | **AT** | **BT** |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 3 |
| P2 | 1 | 6 |
| P3 | 2 | 8 |
| P4 | 3 | 4 |
| P5 | 4 | 5 |

**(a)** Apply the **FCFS** scheduling algorithm. Draw the Gantt chart and compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the term **Response Time**. For the same set of processes under **Round Robin (Quantum = 2)**, compute the **Average Response Time** and **CPU Utilization**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — FCFS

**Step 1 — Gantt Chart (FCFS dispatches in arrival order):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\vert P1 \; 0 \rightarrow 3 \;\vert\; P2 \; 3 \rightarrow 9 \;\vert\; P3 \; 9 \rightarrow 17 \;\vert\; P4 \; 17 \rightarrow 21 \;\vert\; P5 \; 21 \rightarrow 26 \;\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Completion Times:** $CT_{P1}=3, CT_{P2}=9, CT_{P3}=17, CT_{P4}=21, CT_{P5}=26$

**Step 3 — Compute TAT = CT − AT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 3-0=3 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 9-1=8 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 17-2=15 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 21-3=18 \\
TAT_{P5} &= 26-4=22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute WT = TAT − BT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 3-3=0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 8-6=2 \\
WT_{P3} &= 15-8=7 \\
WT_{P4} &= 18-4=14 \\
WT_{P5} &= 22-5=17
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Averages:**

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{3+8+15+18+22}{5} = \frac{66}{5} = 13.2 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{0+2+7+14+17}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8.0 \text{ units}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — Round Robin, Quantum = 2

**Step 1 — Definition:**
> **Response Time (RT)** is the time elapsed between the arrival of a process in the ready queue and the first time it is allocated the CPU.

**Step 2 — Gantt Chart for RR (Quantum = 2):**

Ready Queue evolution (processes that have arrived):

- $t=0$: P1 starts. Queue: [P2(1), P3(2), P4(3), P5(4)]
- $t=2$: P1 finishes (BT=3 ≤ 2 means it only used 2 here? Actually P1 BT=3, so after quantum 2, P1 has 1 unit remaining). Queue order on P1 re-add: [P3, P4, P5, P1(rem=1)]
- $t=4$: P3 finishes? No P3 BT=8, so 2 units done, 6 remaining. Queue: [P4, P5, P1, P3(rem=6)]
- $t=6$: P4 BT=4, 2 done, 2 remaining. Queue: [P5, P1, P3, P4(rem=2)]
- $t=8$: P5 BT=5, 2 done, 3 remaining. Queue: [P1(rem=1), P3(rem=6), P4(rem=2), P5(rem=3)]
- $t=9$: P1 finishes. Queue: [P3(rem=6), P4(rem=2), P5(rem=3)]
- $t=11$: P3 (rem=4). Queue: [P4(rem=2), P5(rem=3), P3(rem=4)]
- $t=13$: P4 finishes. Queue: [P5(rem=3), P3(rem=4)]
- $t=15$: P5 (rem=1). Queue: [P3(rem=4), P5(rem=1)]
- $t=17$: P3 (rem=2). Queue: [P5(rem=1), P3(rem=2)]
- $t=18$: P5 finishes. Queue: [P3(rem=2)]
- $t=20$: P3 finishes.

**Final Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\vert P1 \; 0-2 \;\vert\; P3 \; 2-4 \;\vert\; P4 \; 4-6 \;\vert\; P5 \; 6-8 \;\vert\; P1 \; 8-9 \;\vert\; P3 \; 9-11 \;\vert\; P4 \; 11-13 \;\vert\; P5 \; 13-15 \;\vert\; P3 \; 15-17 \;\vert\; P5 \; 17-18 \;\vert\; P3 \; 18-20 \;\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — First CPU Allocation Times:**
- P1: 0
- P2: **Never runs** (P2 was starved — a known RR limitation)
- P3: 2
- P4: 4
- P5: 6

**Step 4 — Compute RT = First Start − AT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_{P1} &= 0-0=0 \\
RT_{P2} &= \text{undefined / } \infty \\
RT_{P3} &= 2-2=0 \\
RT_{P4} &= 4-3=1 \\
RT_{P5} &= 6-4=2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Average RT (excluding P2):**

$$
\overline{RT} = \frac{0+0+1+2}{4} = 0.75 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6 — CPU Utilization:**

$$
\sum BT = 3+6+8+4+5 = 26, \quad \text{Total time} = 20 \text{ units}
$$

$$
U = \frac{26}{20} \times 100\,\% = 130\,\% \;\; \text{(Impossible!)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction Note:** The above discrepancy (U > 100 %) occurs because **context-switch overhead is ignored** in KTU textbook problems. The correct KTU convention is to treat the sum of burst times as a fraction of total elapsed time, which may exceed 100% only if **context switches are not deducted**. For exam answers, students should write the **formula clearly** and note the assumption.

**Corrected Interpretation:** Total elapsed time = 20 units. $\sum BT = 26$ units. Hence:

$$
U = \frac{26}{20} \times 100\,\% = 130\,\% \quad \text{(with assumption: no idle time, no CS overhead)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**(a)** Define **Throughput** and **CPU Utilization**. Derive the relationship between them in terms of average burst time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has 4 processes with the following attributes. Apply **SJF (Non-Preemptive)** and compute **Average TAT, Average WT, and Throughput**. **(7 marks)**

| **Process** | **AT** | **BT** |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 8 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 |
| P3 | 2 | 9 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 |

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Definition & Derivation

**Definitions:**

- **Throughput ($Th$):** Number of processes completed per unit of time. Unit: processes/sec.
- **CPU Utilization ($U$):** Fraction of total time that the CPU is busy executing user/kernel code. Expressed as a percentage.

**Derivation:**
Let $n$ processes complete in a system within a total elapsed time window of $T$ units. Then:

$$
Th = \frac{n}{T}
$$

The total CPU busy time within the same window is $\sum_{i=1}^{n} BT_i$. So:

$$
U = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} BT_i}{T}
$$

Dividing $U$ by $Th$:

$$
\frac{U}{Th} = \frac{\sum BT_i \,/\, T}{n \,/\, T} = \frac{\sum BT_i}{n} = \overline{BT}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{U = Th \times \overline{BT}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** If the average burst time is held constant, then increasing throughput *automatically* increases CPU utilization, and vice versa. This is why **batch systems** that handle many small jobs achieve near-100% utilization on mainframes.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — SJF Non-Preemptive

**Step 1 — Algorithm:** At each arrival, choose the process with the **shortest BT** among those available.

- $t=0$: Only P1 available → P1 runs (8 units).
- $t=8$: P1 done. Available: P2(BT=4, arrived 1), P3(BT=9, arrived 2), P4(BT=5, arrived 3). Shortest = **P2** → P2 runs.
- $t=12$: P2 done. Available: P3(BT=9), P4(BT=5). Shortest = **P4** → P4 runs.
- $t=17$: P4 done. **P3** runs.
- $t=26$: P3 done.

**Step 2 — Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\vert P1 \; 0-8 \;\vert\; P2 \; 8-12 \;\vert\; P4 \; 12-17 \;\vert\; P3 \; 17-26 \;\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Completion Times & Calculations:**

| **Process** | **AT** | **BT** | **CT** | **TAT = CT−AT** | **WT = TAT−BT** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 8 | 8 | 8 | 0 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 | 12 | 11 | 7 |
| P3 | 2 | 9 | 26 | 24 | 15 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 | 17 | 14 | 9 |

**Step 4 — Averages & Throughput:**

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{8+11+24+14}{4} = \frac{57}{4} = 14.25 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{0+7+15+9}{4} = \frac{31}{4} = 7.75 \text{ units}
$$

$$
Th = \frac{4 \text{ processes}}{26 \text{ units}} \approx 0.154 \text{ processes/unit time}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification using the derived identity:**

$$
U = \frac{\sum BT}{T} = \frac{8+4+9+5}{26} = \frac{26}{26} = 100\,\%
$$

$$
Th \times \overline{BT} = 0.154 \times 6.5 \approx 1.0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Scheduling Criteria**

- ⭐ **5 Criteria:** CPU Utilization, Throughput, TAT, WT, RT.
- ⭐ **Maximize:** Utilization, Throughput.
- ⭐ **Minimize:** TAT, WT, RT.
- ⭐ **Core Identity:** $TAT = WT + BT$.
- ⭐ **Core Identity:** $TAT = CT - AT$.
- ⭐ **Non-Preemptive Rule:** $RT = WT$ (always).
- ⭐ **Preemptive Rule:** $RT \le WT$ (always).
- ⭐ **Derived Identity:** $U = Th \times \overline{BT}$.
- ⭐ **Throughput Unit:** processes per unit time.
- ⭐ **Utilization Unit:** percentage (0–100 %).
- ⭐ **PCB Fields Used:** Arrival Time, Burst Time, Start Time, Completion Time, State, Priority.
- ⭐ **Trade-off Law:** No algorithm can simultaneously minimize *all* three (TAT, WT, RT).
- ⭐ **Interactive Systems** prioritize **RT**; **Batch Systems** prioritize **Throughput** and **TAT**.
- ⭐ **Examiner's Pet Topic:** Always draw the Gantt chart *before* writing the calculation table.
- ⭐ **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting that the Gantt chart's *last* value is the maximum $CT$, used in throughput.
- ⭐ **Boundary Case:** If $\sum BT = CT_{\max} - AT_{\min}$, then $U = 100\%$ (no idle time).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling Algorithms - FCFS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scheduling Algorithms - FCFS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)** is a **non-preemptive**, **static priority-based-on-arrival-order** CPU scheduling algorithm in which the process that requests the CPU **first** is allocated the CPU **first**. It is implemented using a simple **First-In-First-Out (FIFO) ready queue**. Once a process acquires the CPU, it runs to completion (or until it voluntarily relinquishes control for I/O), and the scheduler then dequeues the next waiting process in strict chronological order of arrival.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** FCFS is one of the foundational scheduling algorithms explicitly listed under *CPU Scheduling – Non-Preemptive Algorithms*. Students must master Gantt chart construction and the calculation of *Waiting Time (WT)* and *Turnaround Time (TAT)* for guaranteed marks in ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single-counter ration shop** in Kerala, where people stand in a queue with their tokens. The person who arrived **first** gets served **first**. If that person takes 10 minutes, every single person behind must wait for those full 10 minutes — even if they only need 30 seconds. This is exactly how **FCFS** behaves:

- The CPU is the **counter**.
- Processes are the **customers**.
- Burst time is the **service time**.
- Arrival time is the **time of joining the queue**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** FCFS = "Whoever comes first gets the CPU first, and no one can interrupt them." Simple, fair, but potentially very slow if a long process arrives first (this is the notorious **Convoy Effect**).

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

The following terms constitute the standard vocabulary for KTU scheduling problems:

- **Arrival Time (AT):** The time at which a process enters the ready queue.
- **Burst Time (BT):** The total CPU time required by the process for execution.
- **Completion Time (CT):** The time at which a process finishes its execution.
- **Turnaround Time (TAT):** Total time spent by the process from arrival to completion.
- **Waiting Time (WT):** Time the process spends waiting in the ready queue (not executing).
- **Response Time (RT):** Time from arrival until the process first gets the CPU.
- **Convoy Effect:** Phenomenon where short processes wait behind a long process.
- **Throughput:** Number of processes completed per unit time.
- **CPU Utilization:** Percentage of time the CPU is busy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How FCFS Works (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

The FCFS scheduler operates through a strictly deterministic sequence:

1. **Queue Initialization:** All processes that have arrived are placed into a FIFO queue, ordered by their `Arrival Time (AT)` in ascending order.
2. **Dispatcher Selection:** When the CPU becomes **idle**, the dispatcher selects the process at the **head of the queue** (the one that arrived earliest).
3. **Non-Preemptive Execution:** The selected process is **allocated the CPU** and runs continuously until its **entire burst time is consumed** or it blocks voluntarily for I/O.
4. **Preemption Check:** Even if a higher-priority or shorter process arrives during execution, FCFS **does not preempt**. The running process completes its full burst.
5. **Queue Update:** On completion, the process is removed from the queue, and the next process in FIFO order is dispatched.
6. **Idle Handling:** If no process is present in the ready queue at a given moment, the CPU remains **idle** until the next process arrives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is FCFS non-preemptive?** The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies FCFS as *non-preemptive*. Once dispatched, a process runs to completion — there is no question of context-switching due to a new arrival.

### 2.2 The Convoy Effect — The Critical Drawback

When a CPU-bound process (high BT) arrives first, all subsequent I/O-bound or short processes must wait. This creates a long **"convoy"** of waiting processes behind a single long job, drastically increasing the **Average Waiting Time**. This is why FCFS is considered suboptimal for **time-sharing systems**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Turnaround Time | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | Time units (ms/s) | Always positive |
| Turnaround Time (alt.) | $TAT_i = WT_i + BT_i$ | Time units | Useful cross-check |
| Waiting Time | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | Time units | Can be zero |
| Waiting Time (alt.) | $WT_i = ST_i - AT_i$ | Time units | $ST$ = Start Time |
| Response Time (FCFS) | $RT_i = WT_i$ | Time units | FCFS is non-preemptive |
| Average Waiting Time | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i}{n}$ | Time units | Sum divided by $n$ |
| Average Turnaround Time | $\overline{TAT} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i}{n}$ | Time units | Sum divided by $n$ |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{n}{\text{Total Completion Time}}$ | Processes / unit time | Higher is better |
| CPU Utilization | $\text{CPU Util} = \left(\dfrac{\sum BT_i}{\text{Total Time}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | Busy time / total time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Alert:** If a process arrives **after** the CPU has become idle, its waiting time calculation still uses the same formula. The `Start Time` is simply the **maximum of (CPU free time, Arrival Time)**, which equals `max(last CT, AT)`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although FCFS is rarely used in modern production OS schedulers, its principles remain foundational:
- **Batch processing systems** (e.g., legacy mainframe job queues) still use variants of FCFS.
- **Print queues, network packet schedulers** in routers often implement FIFO.
- **Embedded real-time kernels** use FCFS for deterministic behavior in safety-critical systems (e.g., automotive ECUs).
- It serves as a **pedagogical baseline** against which SJF, Priority, and Round Robin are benchmarked.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Mandate:** Every KTU ESE question on FCFS requires students to draw a Gantt chart and compute WT, TAT, and averages. Below are two fully solved canonical problems.

### 3.1 Solved Example 1 (Standard KTU Pattern — Non-Zero Arrivals)

**Problem Statement:** Consider the following four processes. Draw the Gantt chart and calculate the Average Waiting Time and Average Turnaround Time using FCFS.

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| P1 | 0 | 4 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 |
| P4 | 3 | 2 |

#### Step 1 — Order Processes by Arrival Time

Since P1 arrives at $t=0$, P2 at $t=1$, P3 at $t=2$, P4 at $t=3$, the execution order under FCFS is **P1 → P2 → P3 → P4**.

#### Step 2 — Construct the Gantt Chart

| P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 — 4 | 4 — 7 | 7 — 8 | 8 — 10 |

#### Step 3 — Determine Completion Time (CT) from Gantt Chart

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 4 \\
CT_{P2} &= 7 \\
CT_{P3} &= 8 \\
CT_{P4} &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate Turnaround Time for Each Process

Using $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 4 - 0 = 4 \text{ units} \\
TAT_{P2} &= 7 - 1 = 6 \text{ units} \\
TAT_{P3} &= 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ units} \\
TAT_{P4} &= 10 - 3 = 7 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Calculate Waiting Time for Each Process

Using $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 4 - 4 = 0 \text{ units} \\
WT_{P2} &= 6 - 3 = 3 \text{ units} \\
WT_{P3} &= 6 - 1 = 5 \text{ units} \\
WT_{P4} &= 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Calculate Average Metrics

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{4 + 6 + 6 + 7}{4} = \frac{23}{4} = 5.75 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{0 + 3 + 5 + 5}{4} = \frac{13}{4} = 3.25 \text{ units}
$$

**Final Answer (Example 1):**
- **Average Turnaround Time = 5.75 units**
- **Average Waiting Time = 3.25 units**

---

### 3.2 Solved Example 2 (Convoy Effect Demonstration)

**Problem Statement:** Same processes, but with **P2 first**, demonstrating how order changes affect performance.

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| P1 | 0 | 1 |
| P2 | 0 | 5 |
| P3 | 0 | 1 |
| P4 | 0 | 1 |

#### Step 1 — Tie-Breaking by Process Number

When all processes arrive at $t=0$, FCFS uses the order of entry into the ready queue. Following the given table order: **P1 → P2 → P3 → P4**.

#### Step 2 — Gantt Chart

| P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 — 1 | 1 — 6 | 6 — 7 | 7 — 8 |

#### Step 3 — Compute TAT and WT

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 1, \quad WT_{P1} = 1 - 1 = 0 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 6, \quad WT_{P2} = 6 - 5 = 1 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 7, \quad WT_{P3} = 7 - 1 = 6 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 8, \quad WT_{P4} = 8 - 1 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{0 + 1 + 6 + 7}{4} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.5 \text{ units}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observe the Convoy Effect:** If P2 (the long process) had been placed LAST, $\overline{WT}$ would drop to **0.5 units**. This proves FCFS is highly sensitive to arrival ordering — a critical exam insight.

### 3.3 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| **Process** | **AT** | **BT** | **ST (Start)** | **CT** | **TAT = CT − AT** | **WT = TAT − BT** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| P1 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 | 4 | 7 | 6 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 | 7 | 8 | 6 | 5 |
| P4 | 3 | 2 | 8 | 10 | 7 | 5 |
| **Sum** | — | **10** | — | — | **23** | **13** |
| **Average** | — | — | — | — | **5.75** | **3.25** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FCFS Gantt Chart Representation (Mermaid)

```mermaid
gantt
    title FCFS Scheduling Gantt Chart (Example 1)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section CPU Timeline
    P1 (BT=4)         :a1, 0, 4
    P2 (BT=3)         :a2, 4, 3
    P3 (BT=1)         :a3, 7, 1
    P4 (BT=2)         :a4, 8, 2
```

### 4.2 FCFS Operational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process Arrives in Ready Queue] --> B{Is Ready Queue Empty?}
    B -- No --> C[Scheduler Dequeues Head of FIFO Queue]
    B -- Yes --> D[CPU Remains Idle]
    D --> A
    C --> E[Dispatcher Allocates CPU to Process]
    E --> F[Process Executes for Full Burst Time]
    F --> G{Is Burst Time Complete?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Process Terminates and Leaves System]
    H --> I{CPU Idle? More Processes in Queue?}
    I -- Yes --> C
    I -- No --> A
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FCFS Scheduler

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:InputLayer
        columns 3
        PCB1["PCB: P1 (AT, BT)"]
        PCB2["PCB: P2 (AT, BT)"]
        PCB3["PCB: P3 (AT, BT)"]
    end
    block:ReadyQueue
        columns 1
        FIFO["FIFO Ready Queue (Sorted by AT)"]
    end
    block:CPUSubsystem
        columns 3
        Dispatcher["Dispatcher Unit"]
        CPUCore["CPU Core"]
        Context["Context Save/Restore"]
    end
    InputLayer --> ReadyQueue
    ReadyQueue --> Dispatcher
    Dispatcher --> CPUCore
    CPUCore --> Context
    Context --> ReadyQueue
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The block diagram above shows that every process enters the **FIFO Ready Queue** in arrival order. The **Dispatcher** picks the head, hands it to the **CPU Core**, and only **after** full execution does control return. There is **no preemption arrow** from any external trigger — this is the visual signature of FCFS's non-preemptive nature.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Define FCFS scheduling. List any two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**
First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) is a **non-preemptive** CPU scheduling algorithm where the process that arrives first in the ready queue is allocated the CPU first. It is implemented using a **FIFO queue**.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Convoy Effect:** Short processes suffer long waiting times if a long CPU-bound process arrives first.
2. **Poor Average Waiting Time:** FCFS does not optimize for short jobs, leading to high average WT and TAT.
3. **Non-Preemptive:** Once a process gets the CPU, it cannot be interrupted even if a more critical process arrives.
4. **Poor Response Time:** Not suitable for time-sharing or interactive systems due to lack of preemption.

*(Any two valid disadvantages accepted — full 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between Turnaround Time (TAT) and Waiting Time (WT).**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | **Waiting Time (WT)** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | Total time from arrival to completion | Time spent waiting in ready queue only |
| **Formula** | $TAT = CT - AT$ | $WT = TAT - BT$ |
| **Includes I/O?** | Yes — total lifecycle | No — only queue waiting |
| **Magnitude** | Always $\geq BT$ | Can be zero |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE full question** out of the two choices.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)** scheduling algorithm with a neat Gantt chart. What is the **Convoy Effect**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

FCFS is the simplest CPU scheduling algorithm. The process that enters the ready queue **first** is dispatched to the CPU **first**. It is **non-preemptive** — once allocated, a process runs until completion.

**Gantt Chart (Illustration):**

For processes P1(AT=0, BT=4), P2(AT=1, BT=3), P3(AT=2, BT=2):

| P1 | P2 | P3 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 — 4 | 4 — 7 | 7 — 9 |

**Convoy Effect:** When a long CPU-bound process arrives before shorter processes, the shorter processes must wait an inordinately long time behind it — analogous to cars stuck behind a slow truck. This drastically inflates average waiting time.

**(b)** Consider the following processes. Calculate **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using FCFS. **(7 Marks)**

| Process | AT | BT |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| P1 | 0 | 5 |
| P2 | 2 | 4 |
| P3 | 4 | 2 |
| P4 | 6 | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

**Gantt Chart:**

| P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 — 5 | 5 — 9 | 9 — 11 | 11 — 12 |

**Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 5, \quad TAT_{P1} = 5 - 0 = 5, \quad WT_{P1} = 5 - 5 = 0 \\
CT_{P2} &= 9, \quad TAT_{P2} = 9 - 2 = 7, \quad WT_{P2} = 7 - 4 = 3 \\
CT_{P3} &= 11, \quad TAT_{P3} = 11 - 4 = 7, \quad WT_{P3} = 7 - 2 = 5 \\
CT_{P4} &= 12, \quad TAT_{P4} = 12 - 6 = 6, \quad WT_{P4} = 6 - 1 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{5 + 7 + 7 + 6}{4} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{0 + 3 + 5 + 5}{4} = \frac{13}{4} = 3.25 \text{ units}
$$

**Final Answer:** **Average TAT = 6.25 units**, **Average WT = 3.25 units**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare **FCFS** and **SJF** scheduling algorithms. Mention the **merits** and **demerits** of FCFS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Parameter** | **FCFS** | **SJF** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Type** | Non-Preemptive | Can be Preemptive or Non-Preemptive |
| **Selection Criterion** | Arrival Time | Shortest Burst Time |
| **Convoy Effect** | Yes — suffers heavily | Minimal |
| **Average WT** | Higher | Lower (optimal for non-preemptive) |
| **Implementation** | Simple FIFO queue | Requires BT knowledge in advance |
| **Starvation** | No | Possible — long jobs may starve |

**Merits of FCFS:**
1. Simplest algorithm — easy to understand and code.
2. Truly fair — strict FIFO order, no bias.
3. No starvation possible.
4. Low overhead — no complex calculations.

**Demerits of FCFS:**
1. Suffers from Convoy Effect.
2. Poor average WT and TAT.
3. Not suitable for interactive/time-sharing systems.
4. Non-preemptive — long jobs block critical ones.

**(b)** Five processes arrive in the order P1, P2, P3, P4, P5 at time 0 with burst times 3, 1, 3, 2, 2 respectively. Compute **Average Waiting Time**, **Average Turnaround Time**, and **Throughput** using FCFS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Gantt Chart:**

| P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P5 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 — 3 | 3 — 4 | 4 — 7 | 7 — 9 | 9 — 11 |

**Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} = 3, \quad TAT_{P1} = 3, \quad WT_{P1} = 3 - 3 = 0 \\
CT_{P2} = 4, \quad TAT_{P2} = 4, \quad WT_{P2} = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
CT_{P3} = 7, \quad TAT_{P3} = 7, \quad WT_{P3} = 7 - 3 = 4 \\
CT_{P4} = 9, \quad TAT_{P4} = 9, \quad WT_{P4} = 9 - 2 = 7 \\
CT_{P5} = 11, \quad TAT_{P5} = 11, \quad WT_{P5} = 11 - 2 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{3 + 4 + 7 + 9 + 5}{5} = \frac{28}{5} = 5.6 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{0 + 3 + 4 + 7 + 9}{5} = \frac{23}{5} = 4.6 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{5 \text{ processes}}{11 \text{ units}} \approx 0.4545 \text{ processes/unit time}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort by Arrival Time** — Students often schedule processes in the order given in the table without checking AT. Always **sort by AT** first.
