- $P_4$: Need $\langle 2,1,1,0 \rangle \leq \langle 5,2,4,3 \rangle$. **Select $P_4$.**

**Step 4: Safe Sequence:**

$$\langle P_1, P_2, P_0, P_3, P_4 \rangle$$

The system is in a **safe state**. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Confusing the direction of edges:** Many students draw assignment edges from process to resource. **Penalty: −2 marks.** Always remember: $P_i \to R_j$ = request; $R_j \to P_i$ = assignment.
> 2. **Forgetting to update Work vector:** When applying Banker's Algorithm, students often forget to add the allocation back to Work. **Penalty: −3 marks** (entire conclusion becomes wrong).
> 3. **Component-wise comparison error:** Need $\leq$ Work must be checked **element-wise**, not by summing the components. A single failing component disqualifies the process.
> 4. **Misapplying the cycle condition:** Stating that "a cycle means deadlock" without specifying *single-instance* resources leads to **−2 marks** deduction.
> 5. **Not labeling the safe sequence:** The final answer must be a specific ordering of processes, e.g., $\langle P_1, P_2, P_0 \rangle$, not just "the system is safe."

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RAG = (V, E)** where $V = P \cup R$ and edges are either *request* or *assignment*.
- **Vertices:** Processes = circles; Resources = rectangles with dots showing instance count.
- **Edges:** $P_i \to R_j$ means request; $R_j \to P_i$ means allocation.
- **Single-Instance Resources:** Cycle $\iff$ Deadlock (necessary and sufficient).
- **Multi-Instance Resources:** Cycle $\implies$ possibly deadlock (necessary only). Use Banker's Algorithm to confirm.
- **Wait-For Graph:** Derived from RAG by collapsing resources; contains only process vertices.
- **Banker's Safety Algorithm:** Compute Need = Max − Allocation. Repeatedly find a process with Need $\leq$ Work, mark it, and update Work = Work + Allocation.
- **Deadlock Recovery Strategies:** Kill a process, roll back to a safe checkpoint, or preempt resources (only if the resource is preemptable).
- **Time Complexity of Cycle Detection:** $O(V + E)$ using DFS — efficient for large systems.
- **Key Data Structures to Memorize:** Max, Allocation, Need, Available, Work, Finish (boolean array).
- **Common Mistake:** A safe state guarantees freedom from deadlock only in the *current* allocation; future requests can move the system into an unsafe state.
- **Engineering Connection:** RAG principles are used in real-time systems (RTOS), database concurrency control, and distributed lock managers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Banker's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Banker's Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
The **Banker's Algorithm** is a classic **deadlock avoidance** algorithm proposed by **Edsger W. Dijkstra (1965)** that models resource allocation in an operating system as analogous to a banker extending credit lines to customers. Before granting any resource request, the algorithm simulates the allocation to ensure the system will **never enter an unsafe state** (a state where deadlock becomes a possibility). It achieves this by maintaining four key data structures:

- **Available Vector** — a 1D array of length $m$ (number of resource types) indicating the count of free instances of each resource.
- **Max Matrix** — an $n \times m$ matrix where $n$ is the number of processes; $\text{Max}[i][j]$ is the *maximum* demand of process $P_i$ for resource type $R_j$.
- **Allocation Matrix** — an $n \times m$ matrix where $\text{Allocation}[i][j]$ is the *currently held* count of $R_j$ by $P_i$.
- **Need Matrix** — an $n \times m$ matrix where $\text{Need}[i][j]$ represents the *remaining* maximum claim, derived as $\text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The Banker's Algorithm belongs to the **Deadlock Avoidance** paradigm. It differs from *Deadlock Prevention* (which structurally eliminates one of the 4 Coffman conditions) and *Deadlock Detection* (which allows deadlocks and recovers later). The Banker's Algorithm proactively sidesteps deadlocks by only granting requests that preserve system **safety**.

> [!NOTE]
> **System States in Deadlock Theory**
> - **Safe State:** A state from which there exists *at least one* sequence of process completion (a **safe sequence**) such that every process can obtain its maximum required resources without deadlock.
> - **Unsafe State:** A state with *no guaranteed* safe sequence. An unsafe state is *not* a deadlock, but it carries the risk of one.
> - **Deadlocked State:** A state where two or more processes are permanently blocked, each waiting for a resource held by the other.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Banking Metaphor

Imagine you are a **banker** managing a finite pool of **loanable capital** (system resources). Several **business owners** (processes) wish to borrow capital. Each owner has:

1. A **declared maximum borrowing limit** (the $\text{Max}$ matrix — their credit ceiling).
2. A **currently utilized loan amount** (the $\text{Allocation}$ matrix).
3. A **remaining credit headroom** (the $\text{Need}$ matrix = $\text{Max} - \text{Allocation}$).

You, as the banker, must decide whether to approve a *new loan request* without ever running out of money to satisfy *all* remaining customers. The Banker's Algorithm is precisely this logic: **simulate** the loan disbursement, recalculate the new capital pool, and verify whether a safe order of loan repayments (process completions) still exists. If yes, approve; if no, deny the request and let the customer wait. This is the essence of **deadlock avoidance**.

### 1.3 Core Symbols & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **$n$** — number of processes in the system.
- **$m$** — number of distinct resource types.
- **$r_j$** — total number of instances of resource type $R_j$ (system-wide total).
- **Available$[j]$** — number of *free* instances of $R_j$ at the current moment.
- **Safe Sequence** — a permutation $\langle P_{s_1}, P_{s_2}, \ldots, P_{s_n} \rangle$ of all processes such that for each $k$, the resources required by $P_{s_k}$ are satisfied by **Available $+ \sum_{i<k} \text{Allocation}[s_i]$**.
- **Work** — a working copy of the Available vector used inside the safety check; it grows as processes "complete" during the simulation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the Banker's Algorithm

The Banker's Algorithm is delivered as **two coupled sub-algorithms**:

#### A. Safety Algorithm
A diagnostic routine that, given the current system state, answers the binary question: *Is the system in a safe state?* If yes, it also produces one such **safe sequence**.

#### B. Resource-Request Algorithm
A runtime routine invoked whenever process $P_i$ asks for additional resources. It evaluates the request against $\text{Need}$ and $\text{Available}$, performs a hypothetical allocation, and invokes the **Safety Algorithm** as a subroutine on the post-allocation state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two sub-algorithms?** The Safety Algorithm is the *core evaluator*; the Request Algorithm is the *decision gate*. In KTU 2024 scheme questions, marks are often split — 7 marks for executing the Safety Algorithm on a given snapshot, and 7 marks for processing a specific resource request. Treat them as siblings, not alternatives.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

#### Safety Algorithm (Pseudo-formal)
1. **Initialize:**
   - Set $\text{Work} = \text{Available}$.
   - Set $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{false}$ for all $i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\}$.
2. **Find a candidate:** Locate an index $i$ such that $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{false}$ AND $\text{Need}[i] \leq \text{Work}$ (component-wise). If no such $i$ exists, **jump to Step 4**.
3. **Hypothetically complete $P_i$:**
   - $\text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}[i]$.
   - $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{true}$.
   - **Go back to Step 2**.
4. **Terminate & report:**
   - If $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{true}$ for **all** $i$, the system is in a **safe state**, and the order in which processes were marked $\text{Finish} = \text{true}$ is the **safe sequence**.
   - Else, the system is in an **unsafe state**.

#### Resource-Request Algorithm
When process $P_i$ issues $\text{Request}_i$ (a vector of length $m$):

1. **Bounded Demand Check:** If $\text{Request}_i[j] \leq \text{Need}[i][j]$ for all $j$, proceed; else, raise **error** (process has claimed more than its declared maximum).
2. **Availability Check:** If $\text{Request}_i[j] \leq \text{Available}[j]$ for all $j$, proceed to Step 3; else, $P_i$ must **wait** (resources not free now).
3. **Hypothetical Allocation (Pretend to Allocate):**
   - $\text{Available} = \text{Available} - \text{Request}_i$.
   - $\text{Allocation}[i] = \text{Allocation}[i] + \text{Request}_i$.
   - $\text{Need}[i] = \text{Need}[i] - \text{Request}_i$.
4. **Safety Re-evaluation:** Run the Safety Algorithm on the **newly simulated state**.
   - If **safe** → the OS commits the allocation (real allocation).
   - If **unsafe** → the OS rolls back to the previous safe state and $P_i$ must **wait**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j]$ | Remaining maximum claim | Derive the Need matrix (compulsory first step in any KTU question) |
| $\text{Work} := \text{Available}$ | Initialization of Safety Algorithm | Every safety check starts here |
| $\text{Work} := \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}[i]$ | Simulated completion of $P_i$ | Grows the work vector as we "run" the safe sequence |
| $\text{Need}[i] \leq \text{Work}$ (component-wise) | Feasibility test for $P_i$ | A process can run only if its remaining need fits in the current Work |
| $\text{Request}_i \leq \text{Need}_i$ (component-wise) | Demand boundedness | Prevents over-claim errors |
| $\text{Request}_i \leq \text{Available}$ (component-wise) | Instant availability | Otherwise, the requesting process must wait |
| $\text{Available} := \text{Available} - \text{Request}_i$ | Tentative de-allocation | Performed only in the **pretend allocation** phase |
| Safe $\Leftrightarrow \forall i,\ \text{Finish}[i] = \text{true}$ | Safe-state verdict | Concluding condition of the Safety Algorithm |
| **Number of safe sequences** $\leq n!$ (worst case) | Combinatorial upper bound | Useful sanity check; $n=5 \Rightarrow 120$ candidates max |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison with Other Deadlock Strategies (High-Yield KTU Point)**
> - *Banker's Algorithm* is **deadlock avoidance** — runs the **Safety Algorithm** *before* granting resources.
> - *Deadlock Prevention* negates one Coffman condition (Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait) — *structural* fix.
> - *Deadlock Detection* — lets the system run; periodically invokes a **Wait-For Graph** or **Resource Allocation Graph** to detect cycles, then recovers.
> - *Ostrich Algorithm* — ignores deadlocks entirely (used in UNIX for certain resources, e.g., terminals).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

While the Banker's Algorithm is rarely used in full form in modern general-purpose OS (because it requires a *priori* declaration of maximum resource needs, which is impractical for unpredictable workloads), the **safety-check philosophy** underpins:

- **Database transaction managers** (lock ordering & two-phase locking decisions).
- **Embedded / Real-Time Systems** (RTOS scheduling where resource sets are pre-declared).
- **Cloud resource orchestrators** (Kubernetes scheduler avoiding resource starvation).
- **Verifiable safety kernels** in avionics and railway signaling software.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (5 Processes, 3 Resource Types)

Consider a system with $n = 5$ processes $\{P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\}$ and $m = 3$ resource types $A, B, C$. The system totals are $A = 10$, $B = 5$, $C = 7$ instances.

**Given Data:**

| Process | Allocation (A B C) | Max (A B C) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_0$ | 0 1 0 | 7 5 3 |
| $P_1$ | 2 0 0 | 3 2 2 |
| $P_2$ | 3 0 2 | 9 0 2 |
| $P_3$ | 2 1 1 | 2 2 2 |
| $P_4$ | 0 0 2 | 4 3 3 |

**Available** = (10, 5, 7) — the totals minus the column sums of Allocation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Used} &= (0+2+3+2+0,\ 1+0+0+1+0,\ 0+0+2+1+2) = (7, 2, 5) \\
\text{Available} &= (10-7,\ 5-2,\ 7-5) = (3, 3, 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Examiners often present only Allocation, Max, and Available. Always **first verify the consistency** by recomputing $\text{Available} = \text{Total} - \sum \text{Allocation}$. If you skip this and use a wrong Available, every subsequent step loses marks.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Need Matrix

Using the identity $\text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Need}[P_0] &= (7, 5, 3) - (0, 1, 0) = (7, 4, 3) \\
\text{Need}[P_1] &= (3, 2, 2) - (2, 0, 0) = (1, 2, 2) \\
\text{Need}[P_2] &= (9, 0, 2) - (3, 0, 2) = (6, 0, 0) \\
\text{Need}[P_3] &= (2, 2, 2) - (2, 1, 1) = (0, 1, 1) \\
\text{Need}[P_4] &= (4, 3, 3) - (0, 0, 2) = (4, 3, 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compiled Need Matrix:**

| Process | Need (A B C) |
|---|---|
| $P_0$ | 7 4 3 |
| $P_1$ | 1 2 2 |
| $P_2$ | 6 0 0 |
| $P_3$ | 0 1 1 |
| $P_4$ | 4 3 1 |

### 3.3 Executing the Safety Algorithm

**Step 0 — Initialize:**
$$
\text{Work} = (3, 3, 2), \quad \text{Finish} = [F, F, F, F, F]
$$

**Iteration 1:** Scan for $i$ with $\text{Finish}[i]=F$ and $\text{Need}[i] \leq \text{Work}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0:\ (7, 4, 3) &\not\leq (3, 3, 2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{cannot run} \\
P_1:\ (1, 2, 2) &\leq (3, 3, 2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{can run (smallest match)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Select $P_1$. Simulate completion:

$$
\text{Work} = (3, 3, 2) + (2, 0, 0) = (5, 3, 2)
$$

Update $\text{Finish}[1] = T$. Sequence prefix: $\langle P_1 \rangle$.

**Iteration 2:** $\text{Work} = (5, 3, 2)$, scan remaining.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0:\ (7, 4, 3) &\not\leq (5, 3, 2) \\
P_2:\ (6, 0, 0) &\not\leq (5, 3, 2) \quad (\text{first component } 6 > 5) \\
P_3:\ (0, 1, 1) &\leq (5, 3, 2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{select } P_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Simulate completion:

$$
\text{Work} = (5, 3, 2) + (2, 1, 1) = (7, 4, 3)
$$

$\text{Finish}[3] = T$. Sequence: $\langle P_1, P_3 \rangle$.

**Iteration 3:** $\text{Work} = (7, 4, 3)$, scan remaining.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0:\ (7, 4, 3) &\leq (7, 4, 3) \quad \Rightarrow \text{select } P_0 \\
P_2:\ (6, 0, 0) &\leq (7, 4, 3) \quad (\text{also eligible}) \\
P_4:\ (4, 3, 1) &\leq (7, 4, 3) \quad (\text{also eligible})
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiple candidates exist. Following the standard convention (smallest index), select $P_0$:

$$
\text{Work} = (7, 4, 3) + (0, 1, 0) = (7, 5, 3)
$$

$\text{Finish}[0] = T$. Sequence: $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0 \rangle$.

**Iteration 4:** $\text{Work} = (7, 5, 3)$, scan remaining.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2:\ (6, 0, 0) &\leq (7, 5, 3) \quad \Rightarrow \text{select } P_2 \\
P_4:\ (4, 3, 1) &\leq (7, 5, 3) \quad (\text{also eligible})
\end{aligned}
$$

Select $P_2$:

$$
\text{Work} = (7, 5, 3) + (3, 0, 2) = (10, 5, 5)
$$

$\text{Finish}[2] = T$. Sequence: $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2 \rangle$.

**Iteration 5:** $\text{Work} = (10, 5, 5)$, only $P_4$ remains.

$$
P_4:\ (4, 3, 1) \leq (10, 5, 5) \quad \Rightarrow \text{select } P_4
$$

$$
\text{Work} = (10, 5, 5) + (0, 0, 2) = (10, 5, 7)
$$

$\text{Finish}[4] = T$. Sequence: $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle$.

**Final Verdict:** All $\text{Finish}[i] = T$. System is **SAFE**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Safe Sequence} = \langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Uniqueness of Safe Sequence:** Multiple safe sequences often exist (e.g., $P_1 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to P_0 \to P_2$ is also valid). Any one valid safe sequence suffices to declare the system safe. The **first-found** deterministic order (by process index) is the KTU-preferred answer.

### 3.4 Executing a Resource-Request (e.g., $P_1$ requests $(1, 0, 2)$)

Suppose after the above state, $P_1$ issues $\text{Request}_1 = (1, 0, 2)$.

**Step 1 — Bounded Demand Check:**

$$
(1, 0, 2) \leq \text{Need}[P_1] = (1, 2, 2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{PASS}
$$

**Step 2 — Availability Check:**

$$
(1, 0, 2) \leq \text{Available} = (3, 3, 2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{PASS}
$$

**Step 3 — Pretend Allocation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available}^{\text{new}} &= (3, 3, 2) - (1, 0, 2) = (2, 3, 0) \\
\text{Allocation}^{\text{new}}[P_1] &= (2, 0, 0) + (1, 0, 2) = (3, 0, 2) \\
\text{Need}^{\text{new}}[P_1] &= (1, 2, 2) - (1, 0, 2) = (0, 2, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Re-run Safety Algorithm on new state:**

$$
\text{Work} = (2, 3, 0)
$$

- $P_1$ (need $(0,2,0)$) $\leq$ $(2,3,0)$ ✓ → run, $\text{Work} = (2,3,0) + (3,0,2) = (5, 3, 2)$.
- $P_3$ (need $(0,1,1)$) $\leq$ $(5,3,2)$ ✓ → run, $\text{Work} = (5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7, 4, 3)$.
- $P_0$ (need $(7,4,3)$) $\leq$ $(7,4,3)$ ✓ → run, $\text{Work} = (7,4,3) + (0,1,0) = (7, 5, 3)$.
- $P_2$ (need $(6,0,0)$) $\leq$ $(7,5,3)$ ✓ → run, $\text{Work} = (7,5,3) + (3,0,2) = (10, 5, 5)$.
- $P_4$ (need $(4,3,1)$) $\leq$ $(10,5,5)$ ✓ → run, $\text{Work} = (10,5,5) + (0,0,2) = (10, 5, 7)$.

Safe sequence preserved: $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle$. **Request APPROVED**, OS commits allocation.

### 3.5 Resource-Request Counter-Example (Unsafe Outcome)

Suppose $P_4$ requests $\text{Request}_4 = (3, 3, 0)$ against the *original* state with $\text{Available} = (3, 3, 2)$.

- $(3, 3, 0) \leq \text{Need}[P_4] = (4, 3, 1)$ ✓
- $(3, 3, 0) \leq (3, 3, 2)$ ✓
- Pretend: $\text{Available} = (0, 0, 2)$, $\text{Need}[P_4] = (1, 0, 1)$.

Re-run safety: $\text{Work} = (0, 0, 2)$. No process has $\text{Need} \leq (0, 0, 2)$ (smallest nonzero need is $P_2$'s $(6, 0, 0)$). All $\text{Finish} = F$ — **UNSAFE**. Request **DENIED**; rollback to prior state; $P_4$ must wait.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Banker's Algorithm Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Process P_i issues Resource Request vector"] --> B{"Step 1: Request_i <= Need_i ?"}
    B -- "NO - Overclaim" --> ERR["Raise Error: Process exceeded declared maximum"]
    B -- "YES" --> C{"Step 2: Request_i <= Available ?"}
    C -- "NO" --> WAIT["P_i must WAIT until resources free"]
    C -- "YES" --> D["Pretend Allocation: Update Available, Allocation, Need"]
    D --> E["Invoke Safety Algorithm on new state"]
    E --> F{"Is the new state SAFE ?"}
    F -- "YES - Safe sequence exists" --> COMMIT["COMMIT the allocation to real state"]
    F -- "NO - Unsafe state detected" --> ROLLBACK["ROLLBACK to pre-request state, P_i WAITS"]
    E --> G["Safety Subroutine: Initialize Work = Available, Finish = false"]
    G --> H{"Find process i with Finish false AND Need_i <= Work"}
    H -- "Found such i" --> I["Simulate: Work = Work + Allocation_i; Finish_i = true"]
    I --> H
    H -- "No such i exists" --> J{"All Finish true ?"}
    J -- "YES" --> SAFE["Report SAFE state + safe sequence"]
    J -- "NO" --> UNSAFE["Report UNSAFE state"]
    SAFE --> F
    UNSAFE --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The outer flow is the **Resource-Request Algorithm** (the decision gate). The inner cycle (`G → H → I → H`) is the **Safety Algorithm** (the evaluator). The two merge at the safety check `F`, which feeds back into the original request decision.

### 4.2 State-Space Venn Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["All System States (Universe)"] --> B["Safe States"]
    A --> C["Unsafe States"]
    B -.->|"No Deadlock"| D["Guaranteed Deadlock-Free"]
    C --> E["Deadlocked States"]
    C --> F["Deadlock-Prone but Not Deadlocked"]
    E --> G["All 4 Coffman Conditions Hold"]
    F --> H["Risk of Future Deadlock"]
```

**Insight:** The Banker's Algorithm ensures the system **never leaves the Safe States region** by rejecting any request that would push it into Unsafe States. It does *not* enter the Deadlocked region at all.

### 4.3 Mermaid Safety-Algorithm Trace Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start: Work = Available, Finish = false for all"] --> S1["Iteration: scan i where Finish false and Need_i <= Work"]
    S1 --> S2{"Eligible i found ?"}
    S2 -- "Yes" --> S3["Work = Work + Allocation_i; Finish_i = true"]
    S3 --> S1
    S2 -- "No" --> S4{"All Finish true ?"}
    S4 -- "Yes" --> S5["Report: SAFE, sequence = order of Finish = true"]
    S4 -- "No" --> S6["Report: UNSAFE, deadlock possible"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q1. Define a "safe state" in the context of the Banker's Algorithm. Why is it important to maintain the system in a safe state?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A system is in a **safe state** if there exists a *safe sequence* — an ordering of all processes $\langle P_{s_1}, P_{s_2}, \ldots, P_{s_n} \rangle$ such that for every $P_{s_k}$, the resources it may still request (bounded by its $\text{Need}$) are available from the currently free resources **plus** the resources held by all previously completed processes in the sequence. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> Maintaining a safe state is important because it **guarantees that the system can avoid deadlock** for any future sequence of resource requests up to each process's declared $\text{Max}$. In a safe state, the OS has a *future-proof execution plan*; in an unsafe state, no such guarantee exists, although a deadlock is not yet inevitable. *(1.5 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between the Need matrix and the Max matrix in the Banker's Algorithm. What is the invariant relation between them?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Max matrix** specifies, *a priori*, the **maximum** number of instances of each resource type that a process may *ever* request during its lifetime. The **Need matrix** specifies, at the *current* moment, the **remaining** resources a process still requires to finish its execution. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> The invariant relation is $\text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j]$, where $\text{Allocation}$ holds what the process is *currently* using. Consequently, the Need matrix is dynamic (changes with allocation/release) whereas the Max matrix is static (declared at process start). *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Banker's Algorithm – 14 Marks]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> Consider the following snapshot of a system with 5 processes and 4 resource types $A, B, C, D$.
>
> | Process | Allocation (A B C D) | Max (A B C D) |
> |---|---|---|
> | $P_0$ | 0 0 1 2 | 0 0 1 2 |
> | $P_1$ | 2 0 0 0 | 2 7 5 0 |
> | $P_2$ | 0 0 3 4 | 6 6 5 6 |
> | $P_3$ | 1 0 0 0 | 4 3 1 1 |
> | $P_4$ | 0 0 1 4 | 0 6 5 6 |
>
> **Available** = (2, 1, 0, 0). Answer the following:
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Compute the **Need matrix** and determine whether the system is currently in a **safe state** using the Banker's Safety Algorithm. If safe, give one safe sequence.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Now suppose process $P_1$ issues a request vector $\text{Request}_1 = (0, 4, 2, 0)$. Apply the **Resource-Request Algorithm** step-by-step and state whether the request can be granted immediately.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute the Need Matrix** [1 mark]

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Need}[P_0] &= (0,0,1,2) - (0,0,1,2) = (0,0,0,0) \\
\text{Need}[P_1] &= (2,7,5,0) - (2,0,0,0) = (0,7,5,0) \\
\text{Need}[P_2] &= (6,6,5,6) - (0,0,3,4) = (6,6,2,2) \\
\text{Need}[P_3] &= (4,3,1,1) - (1,0,0,0) = (3,3,1,1) \\
\text{Need}[P_4] &= (0,6,5,6) - (0,0,1,4) = (0,6,4,2)
\end{aligned}
$$

| Process | Need (A B C D) |
|---|---|
| $P_0$ | 0 0 0 0 |
| $P_1$ | 0 7 5 0 |
| $P_2$ | 6 6 2 2 |
| $P_3$ | 3 3 1 1 |
| $P_4$ | 0 6 4 2 |

**Step 2: Run Safety Algorithm** [5 marks]

Initialize: $\text{Work} = (2, 1, 0, 0)$, $\text{Finish} = [F, F, F, F, F]$.

- **Iteration 1:** $P_0$ has $\text{Need} = (0,0,0,0) \leq (2,1,0,0)$ ✓. Run $P_0$:
  - $\text{Work} = (2,1,0,0) + (0,0,1,2) = (2,1,1,2)$.
  - $\text{Finish}[0] = T$. Sequence: $\langle P_0 \rangle$.
- **Iteration 2:** Scan $P_1$–$P_4$ against $\text{Work} = (2,1,1,2)$.
  - $P_1$: $(0,7,5,0) \not\leq (2,1,1,2)$ ✗.
  - $P_2$: $(6,6,2,2) \not\leq (2,1,1,2)$ ✗.
  - $P_3$: $(3,3,1,1) \not\leq (2,1,1,2)$ ✗ (first component $3 > 2$).
  - $P_4$: $(0,6,4,2) \not\leq (2,1,1,2)$ ✗ (third component $4 > 1$).
  - **No eligible process found.**
- **Termination:** Not all $\text{Finish}[i] = T$.

**Verdict:** The system is in an **UNSAFE state**. [1 mark]

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When the system is **unsafe** at the start, students often still try to force a "safe sequence." Do not. If even a single iteration ends with no eligible process and some $\text{Finish} = F$, the system is unsafe. State this clearly to earn the final mark.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

Process $P_1$ requests $\text{Request}_1 = (0, 4, 2, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Bounded Demand Check** [1 mark]

$$
(0, 4, 2, 0) \leq \text{Need}[P_1] = (0, 7, 5, 0) \quad \Rightarrow \text{PASS (component-wise)}
$$

**Step 2 — Availability Check** [1 mark]

$$
(0, 4, 2, 0) \leq \text{Available} = (2, 1, 0, 0)
$$

Check component-wise: $0 \leq 2$ ✓, $4 \leq 1$ ✗. **FAILS** at component $B$.

**Verdict:** The request **cannot be granted immediately**. Process $P_1$ **must wait** until at least $4$ instances of resource $B$ are free. [1 mark — stating the final decision clearly; remaining 4 marks are for the detailed steps above]

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always show *which component* caused the failure. Examiners award partial marks for correctly identifying the bottleneck component even if the final verdict is "wait."

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> A system has **4 processes** and **3 resource types** $A, B, C$. Totals: $A = 6, B = 7, C = 8$.
>
> | Process | Allocation (A B C) | Max (A B C) |
> |---|---|---|
> | $P_0$ | 1 2 1 | 3 4 2 |
> | $P_1$ | 2 1 3 | 4 3 5 |
> | $P_2$ | 0 1 0 | 2 2 2 |
> | $P_3$ | 2 1 1 | 5 4 4 |
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Compute the Need matrix, compute the Available vector, and determine whether the system is in a **safe state**. If safe, give one **safe sequence**.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Now $P_3$ requests $(0, 1, 1)$. Use the Resource-Request Algorithm to decide if the request can be granted safely.

#### **Model Solution — Question B, Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Available** [1 mark]
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \text{Allocation} &= (1+2+0+2,\ 2+1+1+1,\ 1+3+0+1) = (5, 5, 5) \\
\text{Available} &= (6-5,\ 7-5,\ 8-5) = (1, 2, 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Need Matrix** [1 mark]
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Need}[P_0] &= (3,4,2) - (1,2,1) = (2,2,1) \\
\text{Need}[P_1] &= (4,3,5) - (2,1,3) = (2,2,2) \\
\text{Need}[P_2] &= (2,2,2) - (0,1,0) = (2,1,2) \\
\text{Need}[P_3] &= (5,4,4) - (2,1,1) = (3,3,3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Safety Algorithm** [4 marks]

$\text{Work} = (1, 2, 3)$.

- **Iter 1:** Scan — $P_0$ need $(2,2,1) \not\leq (1,2,3)$ ✗. $P_1$ need $(2,2,2) \not\leq (1,2,3)$ ✗. $P_2$ need $(2,1,2) \not\leq (1,2,3)$ ✗ (first component). $P_3$ need $(3,3,3) \not\leq (1,2,3)$ ✗. No eligible process — **UNSAFE**.

**Verdict:** System is **NOT in a safe state** [1 mark].

#### **Model Solution — Question B, Part (b) [7 marks]**

$P_3$ requests $(0, 1, 1)$.

**Step 1:** $(0,1,1) \leq \text{Need}[P_3] = (3,3,3)$ ✓
**Step 2:** $(0,1,1) \leq \text{Available} = (1,2,3)$ ✓
**Step 3: Pretend allocation** [2 marks]
$$
\text{Available}^{\text{new}} = (1,1,2),\quad \text{Need}^{\text{new}}[P_3] = (3,2,2),\quad \text{Allocation}^{\text{new}}[P_3] = (2,2,2)
$$

**Step 4: Re-run Safety** [3 marks] $\text{Work} = (1, 1, 2)$.
- $P_0$ need $(2,2,1) \not\leq (1,1,2)$ ✗
- $P_1$ need $(2,2,2) \not\leq (1,1,2)$ ✗
- $P_2$ need $(2,1,2) \not\leq (1,1,2)$ ✗ (first component $2 > 1$)
- $P_3$ need $(3,2,2) \not\leq (1,1,2)$ ✗

**No process can run — UNSAFE.** Request **DENIED**. Roll back; $P_3$ waits. [2 marks]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to derive Available**: Many students use whatever Available is given, without verifying. If the question gives totals, *recompute Available* from $\text{Total} - \sum \text{Allocation}$ — partial credit depends on it.
> 2. **Component-wise comparison slips**: "$\text{Need} \leq \text{Work}$" means **every** component, not just a single one. A single larger component fails the test. Always show the comparison component-by-component.
> 3. **Not updating Work after each pseudo-completion**: $\text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}[i]$ is the most-omitted step. Without it, the safety check degenerates into a static one and the safe sequence is wrong.
> 4. **Declaring a system unsafe prematurely**: If a process has $\text{Need} = (0,0,0)$, it can *always* run — even with $\text{Work} = (0,0,0)$. Look for zero-Need rows first.
> 5. **Committing an unsafe allocation**: The Request Algorithm only *commits* when the post-pretend safety check passes. Many students "grant" the request before re-running the Safety Algorithm — this is the most common 2-to-3 mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Banker's Algorithm = Deadlock Avoidance**, *not* prevention or detection.
- **Four data structures**: $\text{Available}$ (vector), $\text{Max}$ (matrix), $\text{Allocation}$ (matrix), $\text{Need}$ (matrix).
- **Identity:** $\text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j]$.
- **Two sub-algorithms:** *Safety Algorithm* (evaluator) + *Resource-Request Algorithm* (decision gate).
- **Safe state $\neq$ Deadlock-free forever; it is "deadlock-free for some known sequence."** Unsafe state $\neq$ Deadlocked; it is "deadlock is *possible*."
- **Safety Algorithm steps:** $\text{Work} = \text{Available}$ → find eligible $P_i$ with $\text{Need}_i \leq \text{Work}$ → $\text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}_i$ → repeat. If all $\text{Finish}[i] = T$, **safe**; else, **unsafe**.
- **Request Algorithm:** Check $\text{Request} \leq \text{Need}$ → check $\text{Request} \leq \text{Available}$ → pretend-allocate → re-run Safety Algorithm. **Grant only if safe.**
- **Practical limit:** The Banker's Algorithm assumes a *priori* declaration of Max and a *fixed* number of processes. Modern OS prefer *Ostrich* (ignore) or *Detection + Recovery* (RAG, wait-for graph) due to this rigidity.
- **Counting safety check marks (KTU pattern):** ~1 mark for Need matrix, ~1 mark for Available, ~4–5 marks for the step-by-step Safety Algorithm trace with Work updates, ~1 mark for final verdict + safe sequence.
- **For Request problems:** ~1 mark each for the two checks, ~1 mark for pretend-allocated matrices, ~2–3 marks for the safety re-evaluation, ~1 mark for the final grant/wait decision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadlock Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Deadlock Detection ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Deadlock Detection** is a non-preventive technique in Operating Systems whereby the system employs a dedicated algorithm to **periodically inspect the state of the system** to determine whether a **deadlock has actually occurred**, and if so, to **identify the specific processes and resources** involved in the cyclic wait. Unlike deadlock prevention or avoidance (which act *before* deadlock occurs by restricting resource requests), detection allows deadlocks to form freely and simply *observes* the system. The detection algorithm runs at predefined intervals, or when CPU utilization drops below a threshold, to maintain an accurate snapshot of the system's state. **Formal Components of the Detection Problem:** - **System State Representation**: Captured via four data structures — `Available`, `Allocation`, `Request`, and the derived `Work` vector. - **Deadlock Existence Condition**: A deadlock exists if and only if the wait-for graph contains a cycle (single resource instance) or if the detection algorithm returns `False` (i.e., cannot find a safe sequence) for multi-instance resources. - **Recovery Obligation**: Once detected, the OS must invoke a recovery scheme (process termination or resource preemption). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Roundabout > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy: Four Cars at a Single-Lane Roundabout** > > Imagine four cars (P1, P2, P3, P4) entering a one-lane roundabout from four different exits, each needing to exit through the entry of the car behind it. Car P1 is blocking P2, P2 is blocking P3, P3 is blocking P4, and P4 is blocking P1. **No car can move forward.** > > - **Prevention** would be: *Don't let all four cars enter at once* (restrict entry — like preventing deadlock by ordering resource requests). > - **Avoidance** would be: *Before each car enters, check if it will block an exit* (like Banker's algorithm checking safe states). > - **Detection** would be: *A traffic camera (algorithm) monitors the roundabout. The moment it sees a circular jam, it alerts the police to break the deadlock (recovery).* This illustrates the **post-hoc** nature of detection — the system tolerates the problem, finds it, and then fixes it. ### 1.3 When is Detection Used? Detection is preferred in environments where: 1. Deadlocks are **rare** but expensive when they occur. 2. **Prevention/avoidance** is too restrictive (e.g., real-time or transactional database systems). 3. The OS has **runtime algorithms** to identify and recover from deadlocks. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Detection algorithms differ based on resource instance types: > - **One instance of each resource type** → Use a **Wait-for Graph** (variant of Resource Allocation Graph). > - **Multiple instances of each resource type** → Use a **Matrix-based Detection Algorithm** (similar in structure to the Banker's safety algorithm). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Data Structures Used in Detection For a system with **n** processes (P1 ... Pn) and **m** resource types (R1 ... Rm): | Symbol | Type | Meaning | Size | |---|---|---|---| | **Available** | Vector | Free instances of each resource type | m | | **Allocation** | Matrix | Resources currently held by each process | n × m | | **Request** | Matrix | Outstanding requests still pending | n × m | | **Work** | Vector | Working copy of Available (used by algo) | m | | **Finish** | Vector | Indicates whether Pi can finish | n | | **Work = Work + Allocation[i]** | Step | Simulates release after Pi finishes | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Initial Setup Rule**: > - `Work = Available` (initially) > - `Finish[i] = false` for all `i = 1..n` > - Find an index `i` such that: `Finish[i] == false` AND `Request[i] ≤ Work` (component-wise). > - If found: `Work = Work + Allocation[i]`; `Finish[i] = true`; **repeat**. > - If no such `i` exists, the algorithm **terminates**. ### 2.2 Detection Algorithm — Operational Logic Steps **Step 1 — Initialization** - Set `Work = Available` - Set `Finish[i] = false` for `i = 0, 1, ..., n-1` - If `Allocation[i] ≠ 0` for any `i`, set `Finish[i] = false` (a process holding resources is still potentially active). **Step 2 — Find a Process to Complete** - Search for an index `i` such that: $$\text{Finish}[i] = \text{false} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Request}_i \leq \text{Work}$$ - If **no such i** exists, jump to **Step 4**. **Step 3 — Simulate Process Completion** - Once such an `i` is found: $$\text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}_i$$ $$\text{Finish}[i] = \text{true}$$ - Return to **Step 2**. **Step 4 — Termination Condition** - If `Finish[i] == false` for **some** `i`, it implies that **process Pi is deadlocked**. - The set of deadlocked processes is: `{ Pi | Finish[i] == false }`. ### 2.3 Wait-for Graph (Single Instance Per Resource) For systems with **one instance of each resource type**, detection is simplified. - Remove resource nodes from the Resource Allocation Graph. - Draw an edge `Pi → Pj` if process `Pi` is waiting for `Pj` to release a resource. - A **deadlock exists ⟺ the wait-for graph contains a cycle**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does cycle ⟺ deadlock?** > Because with a single instance of each resource, the cycle in the wait-for graph corresponds exactly to a circular chain of resource holdings and requests — the precise definition of deadlock. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Condition | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | Safety check | $\text{Request}_i \leq \text{Work}$ | Pi's pending request can be satisfied | | Process completion | $\text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}_i$ | Released resources become available | | Deadlock exists | $\exists i : \text{Finish}[i] = \text{false}$ at termination | Pi cannot complete → deadlocked | | Wait-for cycle | $\exists$ cycle in WFG | Deadlock (single instance) | | Cycle detection cost | $O(n^2)$ or $O(V+E)$ | Depth-first search-based | | Matrix detection cost | $O(m \cdot n^2)$ | n processes, m resource types | | Recovery by termination | $\min(\text{cost}(P_i))$ | Abort cheapest process | | Recovery by preemption | $\min(\text{rollback} + \text{starvation cost})$ | Roll back and reassign resource | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Database Systems (DBMS)**: Transaction managers use cycle detection in wait-for graphs to abort deadlocked transactions (e.g., MySQL InnoDB lock manager). - **Distributed Systems**: Global deadlock detection in distributed DBs uses **edge-chasing algorithms** (e.g., Chandy-Misra-Haas algorithm). - **Embedded/Real-time OS**: VxWorks, FreeRTOS use lightweight detection for predictable recovery. - **Cloud Resource Managers**: Kubernetes uses leader-election timeouts to detect distributed deadlocks. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Multi-Instance Detection Algorithm > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Practice Problem Style**: This mirrors the standard university exam problem. **System Configuration (5 processes, 3 resource types A, B, C):** $$\text{Allocation} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{Request} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{Available} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Question:** Is the system deadlocked? If yes, identify the deadlocked processes. --- #### Step 1 — Initialization $$\text{Work} = \text{Available} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\text{Finish} = [\text{false}, \text{false}, \text{false}, \text{false}, \text{false}]$$ --- #### Step 2 — Search for Process Where `Finish[i] = false` AND `Request[i] ≤ Work` | Iteration | Process i | Finish[i] | Request_i | Work | Request_i ≤ Work? | Action | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | P0 | false | (0,0,0) | (0,0,0) | ✓ Yes | Execute P0 | | 2 | P1 | false | (2,0,2) | (0,0,0) | ✗ No | Skip | | 2 | P2 | false | (0,0,0) | (0,0,0) | ✓ Yes | Execute P2 | | 2 | P3 | false | (1,0,0) | (0,0,0) | ✗ No | Skip | | 2 | P4 | false | (0,0,2) | (0,0,0) | ✗ No | Skip | --- #### Step 3 — Simulate Process Completion **Execute P0:** $$\text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\text{Finish}[0] = \text{true}$$ **Execute P2:** $$\text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\text{Finish}[2] = \text{true}$$ --- #### Step 4 — Re-examine (Iteration 3) Now, with `Work = (3, 1, 3)`: | Process i | Finish[i] | Request_i | Work | Request_i ≤ Work? | Action | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | P1 | false | (2,0,2) | (3,1,3) | ✓ Yes | Execute P1 | | P3 | false | (1,0,0) | (3,1,3) | ✓ Yes | Execute P3 | | P4 | false | (0,0,2) | (3,1,3) | ✓ Yes | Execute P4 | **Execute P1:** $$\text{Work} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \quad ; \quad \text{Finish}[1] = \text{true}$$ **Execute P3:** $$\text{Work} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \quad ; \quad \text{Finish}[3] = \text{true}$$ **Execute P4:** $$\text{Work} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \quad ; \quad \text{Finish}[4] = \text{true}$$ --- #### Step 5 — Final Termination Check $$\text{Finish} = [\text{true}, \text{true}, \text{true}, \text{true}, \text{true}]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion**: All processes completed. The system is **NOT deadlocked**. A safe completion order exists: P0 → P2 → P1 → P3 → P4. --- ### 3.2 Detection Algorithm — Formal Correctness Derivation **Theorem (KTU Standard Proof)**: The detection algorithm correctly identifies all deadlocked processes. **Proof Sketch:** 1. **Soundness**: If the algorithm declares Pi deadlocked (`Finish[i] = false`), then no safe sequence can include Pi. This is because `Request[i] > Work` at the point when all other completable processes are exhausted — meaning Pi is permanently blocked. 2. **Completeness**: If Pi is truly deadlocked, then there exists no sequence that can release enough resources to satisfy Pi's request. The algorithm will exhaust all possible completions and leave `Finish[i] = false`. The algorithm has time complexity $O(m \cdot n^2)$ because for each of the **n** processes, we scan the **m** resource types in both the `Request` check and the `Work` update. ### 3.3 Detection Frequency Trade-off > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Trade-off in KTU Answers**: > - **High detection frequency** → Early recovery, but high overhead. > - **Low detection frequency** → Low overhead, but many processes may be in deadlock before recovery. Optimal: invoke detection when **CPU utilization drops below 40%** (Silberschatz convention). ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Detection Algorithm Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start Detection Algorithm]) --> initB[Initialize Work = Available] initB --> initC[Set Finish i = false for all i] initC --> searchD{Find process i where Finish i = false AND Request i <= Work} searchD -->|Found| execE[Work = Work + Allocation i] execE --> setF[Finish i = true] setF --> searchD searchD -->|Not Found| checkG{Any Finish i = false?} checkG -->|Yes| deadlockH[Processes with Finish i = false are DEADLOCKED] checkG -->|No| safeI[System is in SAFE State - No Deadlock] deadlockH --> recoverJ[Invoke Recovery Scheme] recoverJ --> endK([End]) safeI --> endK ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Wait-for Graph Example ```mermaid flowchart LR P1node([P1]) -->|waits for| P2node([P2]) P2node -->|waits for| P3node([P3]) P3node -->|waits for| P4node([P4]) P4node -->|waits for| P1node subgraph cycleX[Circular Wait Detected] P1node P2node P3node P4node end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation**: The cycle P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P1 represents a **deadlock**. Each process holds one resource instance that the next process in the cycle is requesting. ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Resource Allocation Graph to Wait-for Graph Reduction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ragA[Resource Allocation Graph RAG] P1a([P1]) -->|request| R1a((R1)) R2a((R2)) -->|allocated to| P1a P2a([P2]) -->|request| R2a R1a -->|allocated to| P2a end ragA -->|Remove resource nodes
Collapse edges| wfgB subgraph wfgB[Wait-For Graph WFG] P1b([P1]) -->|waits for| P2b([P2]) end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reduction Rule**: When a resource node has exactly one outgoing allocation edge and one incoming request edge (for the same instance), it can be eliminated by directly connecting the two processes. The resulting edge `P1 → P2` means "P1 is waiting for P2 to release a resource." ### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Recovery Options ```mermaid flowchart TD detectA([Deadlock Detected]) --> optB{Recovery Strategy} optB -->|Option 1| termC[Process Termination] optC --> c1[Abort all deadlocked processes] optC --> c2[Abort one at a time until cycle eliminated] optC --> c3[Select process with minimum cost] optB -->|Option 2| preemptD[Resource Preemption] optD --> d1[Select victim process] optD --> d2[Rollback to safe state] optD --> d3[Starvation prevention via cost factor] termC --> doneE([System Recovered]) preemptD --> doneE ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — 3 Mark Questions #### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between deadlock detection and deadlock avoidance.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Deadlock Detection | Deadlock Avoidance | |---|---|---| | **Timing** | Runs *after* deadlock may have formed | Runs *before* granting each request | | **Resource policy** | Allows all requests; observes system | Restricts requests via safe-state check | | **Algorithm** | Wait-for graph / detection matrix | Banker's algorithm (safe sequence) | | **Overhead** | Lower runtime overhead | Higher per-request overhead | | **Failure mode** | May need to recover (abort/rollback) | Denies unsafe requests | | **Use case** | DBMS, distributed systems | Real-time systems, predictable loads | --- #### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **When is a system said to be in a safe state according to the deadlock detection algorithm?** **Model Answer:** A system is in a **safe state** if there exists an **ordering of all processes** (Pπ1, Pπ2, ..., Pπn) such that for each process Pπi in the sequence, its outstanding `Request` can be satisfied by the currently available resources plus all resources held by processes earlier in the sequence. Formally, the detection algorithm returns `Finish[i] = true` for every `i`. This does *not* guarantee that a deadlock will not occur in the future — it only confirms that the **current state is recoverable**. --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) #### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | ESE Module 2 Choice A]` **(a)** Explain the **deadlock detection algorithm** for a system with **multiple instances of each resource type** with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the construction of a **wait-for graph** and explain how it is used to detect deadlock in a system with **single instances of each resource type**. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Q.A (a) **Introduction (1 mark):** The detection algorithm for multi-instance resources is a matrix-based algorithm that determines whether the current allocation state can lead all processes to completion. **Data Structures (2 marks):** - `Available[m]` — free resources vector - `Allocation[n × m]` — currently held resources - `Request[n × m]` — pending requests **Algorithm Steps (3 marks):** 1. Initialize `Work = Available`; `Finish[i] = false` for all i. 2. Find `i` such that `Finish[i] = false` and `Request[i] ≤ Work`. 3. If found, set `Work = Work + Allocation[i]` and `Finish[i] = true`; go to step 2. 4. If no such i exists, terminate. 5. If `Finish[i] = false` for some i, then Pi is deadlocked. **Example (1 mark):** Work through the 5-process / 3-resource example from Section 3.1, showing Work vector evolution: - `(0,0,0) → (0,1,0) → (3,1,3) → (5,1,3) → (7,2,4) → (7,2,6)` with all Finish becoming true. --- ##### Model Solution — Q.A (b) **Wait-for Graph Construction (3 marks):** - Start with the Resource Allocation Graph (RAG). - **Remove all resource nodes**. - For every edge `Pi → Rj → Pk` (Pi requests Rj, which is allocated to Pk), add a direct edge `Pi → Pk` in the wait-for graph. - The wait-for graph has only process nodes and directed edges. **Deadlock Detection (3 marks):** - A deadlock exists **if and only if** the wait-for graph contains a **cycle**. - Cycle detection is performed using **DFS (Depth-First Search)** with $O(V + E)$ complexity. - If multiple cycles exist, all processes in any cycle are deadlocked. **Example (1 mark):** If P1 → P2 → P3 → P1 forms a cycle, all three processes are deadlocked. --- #### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | ESE Module 2 Choice B]` **(a)** Explain **recovery from deadlock** through (i) process termination and (ii) resource preemption. Compare both approaches. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write and explain the **deadlock detection algorithm**. Given the following system state, determine whether the system is deadlocked: $$\text{Allocation} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{Request} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{Available} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Q.B (a) **Process Termination (3.5 marks):** - **Abort all deadlocked processes**: Crude but guarantees deadlock resolution. High cost — all work lost. - **Abort one at a time**: After each abort, run the detection algorithm again to check if remaining deadlocks are resolved. Lower cost. - **Selection criteria**: Choose victim with minimum cost, considering priority, computation time, resources held, etc. **Resource Preemption (3.5 marks):** - **Select a victim** holding a needed resource. - **Rollback** the victim to a safe state (requires saving state at checkpoints). - **Starvation** may occur if a process is repeatedly selected as victim → use a rollback count to prevent starvation. **Comparison (Bonus - included for completeness):** | Aspect | Termination | Preemption | |---|---|---| | Complexity | Simple | Complex (state save/restore) | | Data loss | High (work lost) | Moderate (rollback) | | Starvation risk | Low | High (if not mitigated) | --- ##### Model Solution — Q.B (b) **Algorithm (2 marks):** As described in Section 2.2. **Worked Problem (5 marks):** **Initial State:** - `Work = (0, 0, 0)` - `Finish = [false, false, false]` **Iteration 1:** - P0: `Request[0] = (0,1,0)`, `Work = (0,0,0)` → `(0,1,0) ≤ (0,0,0)`? **No** (1 > 0) - P1: `Request[1] = (1,0,0)`, `Work = (0,0,0)` → `(1,0,0) ≤ (0,0,0)`? **No** (1 > 0) - P2: `Request[2] = (0,0,1)`, `Work = (0,0,0)` → `(0,0,1) ≤ (0,0,0)`? **No** (1 > 0) **Termination:** No process can proceed. $$\text{Finish} = [\text{false}, \text{false}, \text{false}]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion**: The system **IS deadlocked**. All three processes (P0, P1, P2) are deadlocked. The available resources `(0,0,0)` are insufficient to satisfy any pending request. **Recovery Suggestion (Implicit in marks):** Abort P1 (lowest cost in this case) to break the cycle and re-run detection. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting to update Work after process completion** — Most students mark `Finish[i] = true` but forget `Work = Work + Allocation[i]`. This loses **2-3 marks** instantly. > 2. **Component-wise comparison misuse** — Students often write `Request[i] ≤ Work` as a scalar comparison. Always clarify it is **component-wise**: `Request[i][j] ≤ Work[j]` for all j. > 3. **Confusing detection with avoidance** — Detection finds an *existing* deadlock; avoidance prevents a *future* one. Mixing up definitions costs full CO marks. > 4. **Not identifying ALL deadlocked processes** — If multiple processes are stuck, only the first is sometimes listed. Always list the **set** `{Pi | Finish[i] = false}`. > 5. **In wait-for graph questions, forgetting to remove resource nodes** — A direct copy of RAG is not a wait-for graph. Process-only graph is mandatory. > 6. **Skipping the `Work` initialization in handwriting** — Examiners deduct for unstated initial conditions. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deadlock Detection** > > ✅ **Detection ≠ Prevention ≠ Avoidance** — Detection is post-hoc; prevention is restrictive; avoidance (Banker's) pre-validates. > > ✅ **Single instance** → Use **Wait-for Graph**; cycle = deadlock. > > ✅ **Multiple instances** → Use **Matrix Detection Algorithm** with `Available`, `Allocation`, `Request`. > > ✅ **Algorithm rule**: Find `i` with `Finish[i] = false` AND `Request[i] ≤ Work` (component-wise); set `Work = Work + Allocation[i]`; set `Finish[i] = true`. > > ✅ **Safe state**: All `Finish[i] = true` at termination. > > ✅ **Deadlock exists**: Some `Finish[i] = false` at termination; the set of such i are deadlocked. > > ✅ **Complexity**: $O(m \cdot n^2)$ for matrix algorithm; $O(V + E)$ for wait-for graph DFS. > > ✅ **Recovery methods**: (1) Process termination (all or one-by-one) (2) Resource preemption (with rollback, with starvation prevention). > > ✅ **Detection frequency trade-off**: Too frequent = overhead; too rare = wasted work. Use CPU utilization drop as trigger. > > ✅ **KTU Favorite Question Pattern**: Always expect a **5-process / 3-resource** matrix problem with a "find if deadlocked + identify processes" sub-question. > > ✅ **Recovery cost function** $C = f(\text{priority}, \text{resources held}, \text{time consumed})$ — select process minimizing C.

topics.title: Recovery from Deadlock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recovery from Deadlock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Deadlock Recovery** is the set of algorithmic and system-level procedures invoked by the Operating System after a deadlock has been *detected* by a deadlock detection algorithm, with the goal of breaking the circular-wait condition and restoring the system to a safe, consistent state from which computation can continue.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, deadlock recovery sits at the final stage of the **Deadlock Handling Strategy** spectrum:

$$\text{Deadlock Handling} = \{\text{Prevention},\ \text{Avoidance},\ \text{Detection},\ \text{Recovery}\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCITT403 – Module 2):**
> Recovery is **post-incident** action. It is chosen when the system designer has *deliberately* allowed deadlocks to occur (using a Detection algorithm like the **Wait-For Graph** or the **Banker's Algorithm matrix**) and accepted the cost of occasional recovery in exchange for higher resource utilization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Five-Way Traffic Circle

Imagine **five cars** entering a small roundabout at the same time, one from each road. Each car needs the road segment currently occupied by another car. No one can move forward – a perfect **circular wait**.

How do we break the jam?

| Recovery Strategy (OS Equivalent) | Real-World Action |
|---|---|
| **Abort all processes** | Tow away *every* car in the circle. |
| **Abort one process at a time** | Tow away *one* car at a time until the circle unclogs. |
| **Resource Preemption** | Physically *lift* one car over another (roll-back + restart). |
| **Starvation handling** | Ensure no single car is repeatedly towed. |

The OS faces the exact same dilemma: *something must give up so the rest can proceed.*

### 1.3 When is Recovery Triggered?

The recovery subsystem is activated **only if** all of the following hold:

1. A **Deadlock Detection Algorithm** (e.g., Wait-For Graph cycle detection, or Banker's reduction algorithm) has reported a cycle.
2. The system is **not using Prevention or Avoidance** strategies that would have prevented the deadlock in the first place.
3. The expected **cost of recovery < cost of letting the system remain deadlocked**.

> [!NOTE]
> The OS maintains a `deadlock_detected = TRUE` flag. When set, the recovery module is dispatched with the list of deadlocked processes $P = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}$ and their held/waiting resources.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

There are **two principal recovery philosophies** taught in the KTU syllabus. Let us dissect each one in detail.

### 2.1 Strategy A – Process Termination (Abort and Restart)

The OS forcibly terminates one or more deadlocked processes, releasing **all** their resources back to the free pool. This is the **simplest and most commonly tested** approach.

#### 2.1.1 Sub-Approach A.1 – Abort All Deadlocked Processes

* **Mechanism:** Terminate every process $P_i \in \text{Deadlocked Set}$.
* **Pros:** Definitively breaks the cycle in **one step**; no further detection needed.
* **Cons:** Extremely expensive – partial computations are lost, open files may become inconsistent, kernel state is corrupted.

#### 2.1.2 Sub-Approach A.2 – Abort One Process at a Time

* **Mechanism:** Terminate a single process, reclaim its resources, and **re-run the detection algorithm** to see if the cycle is broken. If not, terminate another.
* **Pros:** Minimally destructive – only the strictly necessary processes are killed.
* **Cons:** High **overhead**; the detection algorithm may be invoked $O(n)$ times in the worst case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When a process is aborted, the OS must guarantee that its termination is **orderly** – releasing all locks, flushing I/O buffers, and writing a `core dump` if configured. Otherwise, secondary deadlocks or inconsistent file states may emerge.

### 2.2 Strategy B – Resource Preemption (Rollback and Restart)

Instead of killing processes, the OS **forcibly takes resources** away from a chosen process and grants them to another so the latter can complete and release resources naturally. This is the **more sophisticated** approach.

#### 2.2.1 The Three Classical Problems of Preemption

| # | Problem | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Selecting a Victim** | Which process should be stripped of its resources? |
| 2 | **Rollback** | If a process loses a resource mid-computation, can we safely roll it back to a safe state? |
| 3 | **Starvation** | How do we ensure the same process is not repeatedly chosen as the victim? |

### 2.3 Victim Selection Criteria (Cost Function)

The OS computes a **victim cost** for each deadlocked process. The process with the **lowest cost** is preempted first. The KTU textbook lists the following parameters:

$$\text{Cost}(P_i) = w_1 \cdot \text{CPU}_{\text{used}} + w_2 \cdot \text{R}_{\text{held}} + w_3 \cdot \text{Priority} + w_4 \cdot \text{Unfinished\ Work}$$

Where:

* $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ are tunable weights chosen by the OS designer.
* $\text{CPU}_{\text{used}}$ is the amount of CPU time $P_i$ has already consumed.
* $\text{R}_{\text{held}}$ is the number of resources currently held by $P_i$.
* $\text{Priority}$ is the external priority assigned to the process.
* $\text{Unfinished Work}$ is the estimated remaining computation.

> [!TIP]
> The heuristic is to **minimize the loss** – the process that has done the *least* work and holds the *fewest* resources is typically chosen.

### 2.4 Handling Rollback

When a process $P_j$ is preempted of a resource, it must be **rolled back** to a state where it does not hold that resource. The OS must maintain a **checkpoint** mechanism.

* **Minimum rollback:** Roll $P_j$ back only as far as needed to break the wait-for cycle.
* **Total rollback:** Abort $P_j$ and restart it from scratch (more conservative).

The rollback distance is bounded by:

$$\text{Rollback Distance}(P_j) = \text{Checkpoint}_{\text{safe}} - \text{Current State}(P_j)$$

### 2.5 Starvation Prevention

If the system always picks the *cheapest* victim, the same low-priority process may be repeatedly preempted and **never finish**. To prevent this, the OS adds a **roll-back count**:

$$\text{Priority}_{\text{effective}}(P_i) = \text{Priority}_{\text{base}}(P_i) + \alpha \cdot \text{RollbackCount}(P_i)$$

Where $\alpha$ is a small constant. This guarantees **bounded waiting** – no process can be starved indefinitely.

### 2.6 KTU Formula / Criteria Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Notation | KTU Significance |
|-----------|----------|------------------|
| Number of deadlocked processes | $n$ | Upper bound on abort iterations |
| Cost of aborting process $P_i$ | $C(P_i)$ | Used to pick minimum-cost victim |
| Rollback counter for $P_i$ | $RC(P_i)$ | Prevents starvation |
| Number of resources held by $P_i$ | $\vert R_i \vert$ | Use $\mid R_i \mid$ notation in exam |
| Total resources in system | $m$ | Total preemptable units |
| Resources needed to break cycle | $k$ | Minimum preemption set |
| Detection algorithm complexity (Wait-For) | $O(n^2)$ | Re-run after each abort |
| Detection algorithm complexity (Banker) | $O(m \cdot n^2)$ | Re-run after each abort |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU valuation key, examiners will check whether you have **mentioned the cost of the chosen recovery method**. Always state: *"The recovery method chosen has cost C, justified by the following parameters..."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Procedures

Since this topic is algorithmic rather than numerical, the "derivations" here take the form of **exhaustive decision procedures** that a KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark answer.

### 3.1 Decision Procedure – Process Termination Approach

Given a deadlocked set $D = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}$ and resource allocation matrix $A$ and request matrix $Q$:

**Step 1 – Initial Assessment**

$$\text{If } n = 1 \text{ or } n = 2, \text{ abort all processes in } D$$
$$\text{Else, proceed to Step 2}$$

**Step 2 – Sort by Cost**

Compute $C(P_i)$ for each $P_i \in D$ using the cost function from Section 2.3. Sort the processes in **ascending order of cost**:

$$\text{Sorted} = \{P_{\sigma(1)}, P_{\sigma(2)}, \ldots, P_{\sigma(n)}\}, \quad C(P_{\sigma(1)}) \leq C(P_{\sigma(2)}) \leq \cdots \leq C(P_{\sigma(n)})$$

**Step 3 – Iterative Abort**

For $k = 1$ to $n$:

1. Abort process $P_{\sigma(k)}$.
2. Update allocation matrix: $A[P_{\sigma(k)}] \leftarrow \mathbf{0}$, release all held resources to the free vector $\mathbf{F} \leftarrow \mathbf{F} + A_{\text{old}}[P_{\sigma(k)}]$.
3. Re-run the **deadlock detection algorithm** on the remaining $(n-k)$ processes.
4. If the cycle is **broken** (no cycle in the Wait-For graph), stop. Otherwise, increment $k$.

**Step 4 – Termination Guarantee**

In the worst case, the loop runs to $k = n$, which is equivalent to *Abort All*. The algorithm **always terminates** with the system in a deadlock-free state.

### 3.2 Decision Procedure – Resource Preemption Approach

**Step 1 – Identify the Cycle**

Using the Wait-For graph $G = (V, E)$, locate the strongly connected component $S$ that contains the cycle. All processes in $S$ are candidates for preemption.

**Step 2 – Compute Victim Cost**

For each $P_i \in S$, compute $\text{Cost}(P_i)$ as defined in Section 2.3.

**Step 3 – Select Minimum-Cost Victim**

$$P_{\text{victim}} = \arg\min_{P_i \in S} \text{Cost}(P_i)$$

**Step 4 – Checkpoint the Victim**

Save a safe state of $P_{\text{victim}}$ so that rollback is possible. Define:

$$S_{\text{safe}} = \text{checkpoint}(P_{\text{victim}})$$

**Step 5 – Preempt Resources**

Identify the set of resources $R_{\text{preempt}}$ held by $P_{\text{victim}}$ that are needed by another process in the cycle. The minimum required set is:

$$R_{\text{preempt}} = \{r_j \mid r_j \in \text{Held}(P_{\text{victim}}) \land \exists P_k \in S, P_k \text{ waits for } r_j\}$$

**Step 6 – Rollback and Restart**

Roll $P_{\text{victim}}$ back to $S_{\text{safe}}$ and update its state to reflect that it no longer holds $R_{\text{preempt}}$. Add $R_{\text{preempt}}$ to the free pool $\mathbf{F}$.

**Step 7 – Re-detect**

Run the detection algorithm. If a cycle persists, repeat from Step 2; otherwise, exit.

**Step 8 – Update Starvation Counter**

$$RC(P_{\text{victim}}) \leftarrow RC(P_{\text{victim}}) + 1$$

If $RC(P_i)$ exceeds a threshold $T_{\text{max}}$, elevate $P_i$'s priority using the formula in Section 2.5.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU 14-Mark Questions)

| Comparison Axis | Process Termination | Resource Preemption |
|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Implementation complexity** | Low | High |
| **Data loss** | High (all work lost for aborted process) | Low (only partial rollback) |
| **Recovery time** | Fast (one-shot) | Slow (iterative) |
| **Overhead** | Low | High (checkpointing) |
| **Risk of starvation** | Low | High (needs explicit counter) |
| **State consistency** | Hard to maintain | Easier (gradual) |
| **Best used when** | Processes are short-lived / batch | Processes are long-lived / interactive |
| **KTU textbook page weight** | Moderate | Heavy (exam favorite) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** If the question does **not** specify the type of application, mention that *Process Termination is preferred for batch systems* and *Resource Preemption is preferred for interactive / real-time systems*. This single sentence is worth **2 marks** in a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Recovery Strategy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Deadlock Detected by Detection Algorithm]) --> condA{Deadlock Still Exists?}
    condA -- No --> exitA([Resume Normal Execution])
    condA -- Yes --> stratA{Choose Recovery Strategy}

    stratA -- "Abort All" --> abortAll[Terminate All Deadlocked Processes]
    abortAll --> relAll[Release All Resources to Free Pool]
    relAll --> exitA

    stratA -- "Abort One by One" --> pickOne[Pick Lowest-Cost Process]
    pickOne --> termOne[Abort Selected Process]
    termOne --> relOne[Release Its Resources]
    relOne --> redetectA[Re-run Detection Algorithm]
    redetectA --> condA

    stratA -- "Resource Preemption" --> pickVictim[Select Minimum-Cost Victim]
    pickVictim --> checkPt[Checkpoint Victim to Safe State]
    checkPt --> preemptR[Preempt Required Resources]
    preemptR --> rollBack[Rollback Victim to Checkpoint]
    rollBack --> redetectB[Re-run Detection Algorithm]
    redetectB --> condA
```

### 4.2 Victim Selection Decision Tree (Cost-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Candidate Process Pi from Deadlock Set]) --> q1{Number of Resources Held by Pi}
    q1 -- "Few" --> q2{Priority of Pi}
    q1 -- "Many" --> q3[High Preemption Cost - Avoid Selecting]
    q2 -- "Low" --> q4[Compute Cost Function]
    q2 -- "High" --> q3
    q4 --> q5{CPU Time Used by Pi}
    q5 -- "Little" --> q6[Low Cost - Strong Candidate for Victim]
    q5 -- "Much" --> q3
    q6 --> q7{Rollback Count of Pi}
    q7 -- "Below Threshold" --> victim[Select Pi as Victim]
    q7 -- "Above Threshold" --> starve[Increment Effective Priority - Avoid Starvation]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture of Recovery Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input Module"]
        detIn[Detection Flag TRUE]
        setIn[Deadlocked Process Set D]
        resIn[Resource Matrices A and Q]
    end

    subgraph Decision["Decision Engine"]
        strat[Strategy Selector]
        costFn[Cost Function Evaluator]
        victimSel[Victim Selector]
    end

    subgraph Exec["Execution Layer"]
        abortA[Abort All Handler]
        abortB[One-by-One Abort Handler]
        preemptA[Checkpoint Manager]
        preemptB[Rollback Engine]
        preemptC[Resource Reclaimer]
    end

    subgraph Output["Output Module"]
        freeRes[Updated Free Resource Vector]
        safeState[Safe State Confirmation]
        redetect[Re-detection Trigger]
    end

    detIn --> strat
    setIn --> costFn
    resIn --> costFn
    costFn --> victimSel
    strat --> abortA
    strat --> abortB
    strat --> preemptA
    victimSel --> abortB
    victimSel --> preemptA
    abortA --> freeRes
    abortB --> freeRes
    preemptA --> preemptB
    preemptB --> preemptC
    preemptC --> freeRes
    freeRes --> safeState
    safeState --> redetect
    redetect --> detIn
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly explain the two main approaches used by an Operating System to recover from a deadlock.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The two main approaches for deadlock recovery are:

1. **Process Termination (1 Mark):** The OS forcibly aborts one or more deadlocked processes, releasing all their allocated resources back to the free pool. The processes may either be terminated *all at once* or *one at a time*, with the detection algorithm re-run between each termination.

2. **Resource Preemption (1 Mark):** The OS *temporarily takes* resources away from a selected process (the *victim*), rolls it back to a previous safe checkpoint, and reassigns those resources to a waiting process. The preempted process is later restarted. (1 Mark for the brief explanation of issues like victim selection, rollback, and starvation.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is starvation, and how does it arise in the context of deadlock recovery via resource preemption? Suggest a solution.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

* **Starvation (1 Mark):** A situation in which a process is **perpetually denied** the resources it needs to make progress, often because it is repeatedly selected as the victim during repeated deadlock recoveries.
* **How it arises (1 Mark):** When the victim selection cost function always favours the *cheapest* process, a low-priority process with little CPU usage will be repeatedly preempted, while high-cost processes are never chosen.
* **Solution (1 Mark):** Maintain a **rollback counter** $RC(P_i)$ for each process. When $RC(P_i)$ exceeds a threshold, the OS artificially raises $P_i$'s effective priority, ensuring it cannot be chosen as a victim indefinitely.

---

### Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Notice:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Process Termination** approach to deadlock recovery in detail. Discuss both variants – aborting *all* deadlocked processes and aborting *one at a time* – and state which is preferred under what circumstances. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system has 4 deadlocked processes $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ with the following parameters:

| Process | CPU Time Used (ms) | Resources Held | Priority | Unfinished Work (ms) |
|---------|-------------------:|---------------:|---------:|---------------------:|
| $P_1$   | 100                | 2              | Low      | 50                   |
| $P_2$   | 200                | 4              | High     | 100                  |
| $P_3$   | 50                 | 1              | Medium   | 30                   |
| $P_4$   | 150                | 3              | Medium   | 80                   |

Using the cost function $C(P_i) = 0.2 \cdot \text{CPU} + 0.3 \cdot \text{Resources} + 0.3 \cdot \text{Priority Score} + 0.2 \cdot \text{Unfinished Work}$ (where Low = 1, Medium = 2, High = 3), identify the order in which the processes should be aborted in the *one-at-a-time* approach. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) – Process Termination Approach (7 Marks):**

Process Termination is the **simplest** deadlock recovery technique. The OS forcibly kills one or more processes involved in the deadlock, automatically releasing all their held resources. **(1 Mark)**

**Variant 1 – Abort All Deadlocked Processes (2 Marks):**
In this variant, the OS terminates *every* process in the deadlocked set in a single step. This guarantees that the cycle is broken and the system returns to a safe state with **one operation**. However, the cost is severe – all partially completed work is lost, open file descriptors may leak, and any inter-process communication channels become orphaned. This approach is generally preferred in **batch systems** where the cost of recomputation is low and recovery speed is critical.

**Variant 2 – Abort One Process at a Time (3 Marks):**
In this variant, the OS picks a single process, aborts it, releases its resources, and re-runs the **deadlock detection algorithm** on the remaining processes. If the cycle persists, another process is chosen. The selection is based on a **cost function** that considers CPU time used, resources held, priority, and remaining work. This approach is more **conservative** and minimizes data loss, but its overhead is high because the detection algorithm may be invoked up to $n$ times in the worst case. It is preferred in **interactive systems** where user data and long computations must be preserved.

**Comparison Summary (1 Mark):**
| Criterion | Abort All | One at a Time |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| Speed | Fast | Slow |
| Data loss | Maximum | Minimum |
| Detection calls | 1 | Up to $n$ |
| Best for | Batch | Interactive |

---

**Part (b) – Cost Computation (7 Marks):**

We compute $C(P_i) = 0.2 \cdot \text{CPU} + 0.3 \cdot \text{Resources} + 0.3 \cdot \text{Priority} + 0.2 \cdot \text{Unfinished}$ for each process.

**For $P_1$ (Priority Low = 1):**

$$C(P_1) = (0.2 \times 100) + (0.3 \times 2) + (0.3 \times 1) + (0.2 \times 50)$$
$$C(P_1) = 20 + 0.6 + 0.3 + 10 = \mathbf{30.9}$$

**For $P_2$ (Priority High = 3):**

$$C(P_2) = (0.2 \times 200) + (0.3 \times 4) + (0.3 \times 3) + (0.2 \times 100)$$
$$C(P_2) = 40 + 1.2 + 0.9 + 20 = \mathbf{62.1}$$

**For $P_3$ (Priority Medium = 2):**

$$C(P_3) = (0.2 \times 50) + (0.3 \times 1) + (0.3 \times 2) + (0.2 \times 30)$$
$$C(P_3) = 10 + 0.3 + 0.6 + 6 = \mathbf{16.9}$$

**For $P_4$ (Priority Medium = 2):**

$$C(P_4) = (0.2 \times 150) + (0.3 \times 3) + (0.3 \times 2) + (0.2 \times 80)$$
$$C(P_4) = 30 + 0.9 + 0.6 + 16 = \mathbf{47.5}$$

**Sorting in ascending order of cost:**

$$C(P_3) = 16.9 < C(P_1) = 30.9 < C(P_4) = 47.5 < C(P_2) = 62.1$$

**Order of Abortation:** $P_3 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow P_4 \rightarrow P_2$ **(1 Mark for the order)**

**Verification and Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The lowest-cost process $P_3$ holds the fewest resources and has done the least work, making it the ideal first victim. The highest-cost process $P_2$ would only be aborted as a last resort (i.e., if all others fail to break the cycle), since it has consumed significant CPU time and holds the most resources.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Resource Preemption** approach to deadlock recovery. Explain the three major issues – *victim selection*, *rollback*, and *starvation* – with appropriate solutions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system has 3 processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ in deadlock. The rollback counts so far are $RC(P_1) = 0$, $RC(P_2) = 2$, $RC(P_3) = 1$. The starvation threshold is $T = 3$, and the priority boosting constant is $\alpha = 0.5$. Base priorities are $P_1 = 5$, $P_2 = 5$, $P_3 = 5$. If the cost function selects $P_2$ again as the lowest-cost victim, demonstrate using the formula $\text{Priority}_{\text{effective}} = \text{Priority}_{\text{base}} + \alpha \cdot RC(P_i)$ whether the system prevents starvation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) – Resource Preemption Approach (7 Marks):**

In Resource Preemption, the OS forcibly *takes away* a resource from a process and grants it to another, so that the latter can complete and release resources in due course. **(1 Mark for definition)**

**Issue 1 – Victim Selection (2 Marks):**
The OS must decide *which* process to preempt. The selection is driven by a **cost function** that considers CPU time consumed, number of resources held, process priority, and amount of unfinished work. The process with the **minimum cost** is preempted because the goal is to minimize the overall loss to the system.

**Issue 2 – Rollback (2 Marks):**
When a process loses a resource, its state becomes invalid for the resources it was using. The OS must therefore **roll the process back** to a previously saved *safe state* (a checkpoint) and restart it from there. The minimum rollback distance is the smallest number of state transitions required to break the cycle. Some systems perform a *total rollback* (restart from scratch) for safety.

**Issue 3 – Starvation (2 Marks):**
If the cost function continually selects the same process as the victim, that process is perpetually rolled back and never completes – a classic starvation problem. The solution is to maintain a **rollback counter** $RC(P_i)$ and to artificially boost the effective priority of any process whose $RC$ exceeds a threshold $T$. This guarantees that no process can be starved indefinitely.

---

**Part (b) – Starvation Prevention Demonstration (7 Marks):**

We are given:
* $\alpha = 0.5$
* $T = 3$
* $RC(P_1) = 0, RC(P_2) = 2, RC(P_3) = 1$
* Base priorities: $\text{Pri}_{\text{base}}(P_1) = \text{Pri}_{\text{base}}(P_2) = \text{Pri}_{\text{base}}(P_3) = 5$

**Step 1 – Compute effective priorities before the new selection:**

$$\text{Pri}_{\text{eff}}(P_1) = 5 + (0.5 \times 0) = 5$$
$$\text{Pri}_{\text{eff}}(P_2) = 5 + (0.5 \times 2) = 5 + 1 = \mathbf{6}$$
$$\text{Pri}_{\text{eff}}(P_3) = 5 + (0.5 \times 1) = 5 + 0.5 = \mathbf{5.5}$$

**Step 2 – Check the threshold:** No process has yet reached $T = 3$, so no immediate priority boost is triggered.

**Step 3 – Hypothetical selection of $P_2$ again:**

If the cost function (ignoring priority) selects $P_2$ as the lowest-cost victim once more, the system must update $P_2$'s rollback counter:

$$RC(P_2) \leftarrow 2 + 1 = 3$$

**Step 4 – Now recompute $P_2$'s effective priority:**

$$\text{Pri}_{\text{eff}}(P_2) = 5 + (0.5 \times 3) = 5 + 1.5 = \mathbf{6.5}$$

**Step 5 – Check starvation trigger:** $RC(P_2) = 3 = T$. The process has been preempted exactly $T$ times. At this point, the OS may **boost $P_2$'s priority to a level where it is no longer the minimum-cost victim** in subsequent selections, thereby preventing it from being picked again.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
This demonstrates the **starvation prevention mechanism**: the effective priority monotonically increases with each rollback, ensuring that no process can be selected as the victim indefinitely. The threshold $T$ serves as a tunable parameter for the OS designer to balance fairness against recovery overhead.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write "kill the process" – the textbook terminology is **"abort"** or **"terminate"**. Using imprecise language costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Do NOT** forget to mention the *re-running* of the detection algorithm after each abort/preemption in the one-at-a-time approach. This is a **favourite 1-mark question** in KTU.
> 3. **Do NOT** skip the starvation discussion in the Preemption approach – examiners specifically allocate 2 marks for it.
