> 4. **Always show the formula and the numerical substitution** in the cost-calculation problems. Marks are awarded for *both* the formula and the substitution step.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Two main recovery strategies:** Process Termination (Abort All / Abort One at a Time) and Resource Preemption.
* **Process Termination is simple** but causes data loss; **Preemption is sophisticated** but risks starvation.
* **Victim selection** uses a multi-factor **cost function** considering CPU time, resources held, priority, and unfinished work.
* **Rollback** requires the OS to maintain **checkpoints**; minimum rollback is preferred over total rollback.
* **Starvation** is prevented by a **rollback counter** that boosts the effective priority of repeatedly preempted processes.
* **Abort All** is preferred in **batch systems**; **One at a Time / Preemption** is preferred in **interactive / real-time systems**.
* After every recovery step, the **detection algorithm must be re-run** to confirm the cycle is broken.
* The cost function weights $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ are **OS designer–tunable parameters**.
* For KTU 14-mark questions, **always draw a comparison table** between the two strategies – it is worth 2–3 marks.
* **Banker's Algorithm** can be used in some extended designs to ensure the *preempted state* is itself safe; this is an **advanced point** worth mentioning for bonus credit.
* Remember the **complexity** of detection re-runs: $O(n^2)$ for the Wait-For graph method and $O(m \cdot n^2)$ for the Banker's matrix method.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Process Synchronization – Basics"?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Module 3 opens with the **foundational problem of coordinating multiple concurrent processes** that share data. The term "**Basics**" here refers to the conceptual foundation upon which all synchronization mechanisms — Mutex, Semaphores, Monitors — are later constructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Process Synchronization – Basics):**
> *Process Synchronization* is the mechanism that ensures cooperating processes access shared resources in a controlled, deterministic manner so that data consistency is preserved, race conditions are eliminated, and the fundamental **Critical Section Problem** is solved.

In simple words: when **two or more processes are reading/writing the same shared memory location at the same time**, the final result becomes unpredictable. The OS must **synchronize** them.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Single-Stall Bathroom

Imagine a public restroom on a train platform with **only one toilet** but **two anxious passengers** trying to enter at the same time.

| Real-World Element | OS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The single toilet | Shared resource (e.g., a global variable, file, printer) |
| The lock on the door | Mutex / Lock variable |
| The "Occupied / Vacant" sign | Entry / Exit protocol |
| Passengers | Concurrent processes / threads |
| The rule "Only one person inside" | **Mutual Exclusion** |

If there were **no lock**, two passengers might barge in simultaneously, leading to chaos. The lock (synchronization primitive) ensures **only one process is in the critical section at any given instant**.

---

## 1.3 Three Core Building Blocks of "Basics"

### A. Cooperating Processes
Processes that **either affect or are affected by each other's execution**. They may:
- Share data via shared memory or message passing.
- Need to synchronize access to common resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooperating Process (KTU Textbook Standard Definition):** A process that can either *directly* share a logical address space (via shared memory) or *indirectly* share data through files or messages, and whose execution may influence the state of another concurrent process.

### B. Race Condition
A **race condition** is a software defect where the **outcome of a program depends on the unpredictable interleaving (ordering) of operations** of concurrent processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Race Condition — KTU Definition:**
> A situation in which multiple processes access and manipulate the **same shared data concurrently**, and the final value of that data depends on the **order of access**, leading to **non-deterministic** results.

**Classic buggy pattern (the textbook `increment` bug):**
```
counter = counter + 1;
```
This single high-level statement actually compiles to **three machine instructions**:
1. `LOAD  counter,  R1`
2. `ADD   R1,     #1`
3. `STORE  R1,     counter`

Two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ interleaving these three instructions can **lose an update** because both may read the same old value of `counter` before either writes back.

### C. Critical Section Problem
The **Critical Section (CS)** is the segment of code in which a process accesses shared resources (shared variables, files, etc.). The Critical Section Problem is the design problem of **finding a protocol that guarantees safe access** to shared data.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Formal Requirements any CS solution MUST satisfy (KTU Board-Exam Favorite):**
> 1. **Mutual Exclusion (ME):** If process $P_i$ is executing in its critical section, no other process $P_j$ ($j \neq i$) can execute in its critical section.
> 2. **Progress (PR):** If no process is in the CS and some processes *want* to enter, then only those processes that are not in their *remainder section* get to decide who enters next — and the decision cannot be postponed indefinitely.
> 3. **Bounded Waiting (BW):** There must exist a **bound** on the number of times other processes can enter the CS after a process has requested entry and before that request is granted. (This prevents starvation.)

---

## 1.4 Preemptive vs. Non-Preemptive Kernels — Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **kernel** is the most privileged piece of code in the OS. If a process can be preempted *while inside the kernel*, the kernel **itself can suffer race conditions**. This is the central motivation for choosing between preemptive and non-preemptive kernels.

| Property | Non-Preemptive Kernel | Preemptive Kernel |
|---|---|---|
| Can a process be preempted while in kernel mode? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Is the kernel itself prone to race conditions? | **No** (process runs to completion of kernel call) | **Yes** (requires synchronization within kernel) |
| Responsiveness | Poor (a runaway system call blocks everything) | Excellent |
| Design complexity | Simpler | More complex (locks everywhere) |
| Used in | Older Unix, MS-DOS, early Windows | Modern Linux, Windows NT, macOS X |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "Preemptive kernel is faster, therefore always better." This is **incorrect reasoning** for exams. The *correct* trade-off statement is: **"Preemptive kernels offer better responsiveness and support real-time workloads but introduce race conditions inside the kernel itself, requiring synchronization primitives (spinlocks, mutexes) inside the OS."**

---

## 1.5 Section 1 Quick Recap Callout

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 — "Basics" covers FOUR foundational ideas:**
> 1. **Cooperating processes** (processes that share state).
> 2. **Race conditions** (non-deterministic outcomes from interleaved access).
> 3. **Critical Section Problem** (the design problem + its three requirements: ME, Progress, BW).
> 4. **Kernel preemption** (why the kernel itself may need synchronization).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Structure of a Process in a Synchronization Context

Every synchronized process $P_i$ executes a cyclic code structure composed of **four conceptual regions**:

| Region | Symbol | What Happens Here |
|---|---|---|
| **Entry Section** | — | Requests permission to enter the critical section (acquires lock, sets flags). |
| **Critical Section** | $CS_i$ | The actual code that touches shared variables. |
| **Exit Section** | — | Releases permission — signals other waiting processes. |
| **Remainder Section** | $R_i$ | All the rest of the code — does not touch shared data. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Note:** The **Entry and Exit sections** are the *synchronization envelope* wrapping the **Critical Section**. A correct solution to the CS problem must confine all shared-variable manipulation strictly inside $CS_i$ and let the entry/exit sections coordinate access.

---

## 2.2 The Three Formal Requirements — Deeper Analysis

Let $n$ processes be denoted $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_{n-1}$. At any instant each process $P_i$ can be in one of four states with respect to the CS:

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| **Trying** | Wants to enter; executing entry code |
| **In CS** | Currently inside critical section |
| **Done** | Has exited; in remainder section |
| **Waiting** | Blocked, will retry later |

### Requirement 1 — Mutual Exclusion (ME)
$$\forall i, j, \;\text{where } i \neq j : \quad \text{InCS}(P_i) \wedge \text{InCS}(P_j) \;\text{ is impossible at the same instant } t.$$

In words: **no two processes can be inside their CS simultaneously**.

### Requirement 2 — Progress (PR)
Progress guarantees **liveness**. It forbids useless waiting. Formally:

> If no process is in its critical section and some processes *wish* to enter, then **only those processes that are not in their remainder section** participate in deciding who enters next, and **this decision cannot be postponed indefinitely**.

This rules out trivially broken "solutions" where a process *outside the relevant code* blocks everyone.

### Requirement 3 — Bounded Waiting (BW)
There exists a bound $B$ (a finite integer) such that once $P_i$ has expressed its wish to enter, **at most $B$ other processes may enter the CS before $P_i$ is granted entry**.

> [!NOTE]
> Bounded Waiting is what **prevents starvation**. Without BW, a process could wait forever while others repeatedly enter the CS.

---

## 2.3 Why a Pure Software Solution Is Hard — The Bakery Algorithm Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical / KTU Context:** Lamport's *Bakery Algorithm* (1974) was the first correct, purely software solution to the CS problem for **$n$ processes**. It draws inspiration from bakeries where each customer takes a numbered ticket, and the lowest-numbered customer is served first.

The core insight: each process $P_i$ chooses a **ticket number** strictly greater than the maximum of all other tickets currently in use. Entry is granted to the process with the **smallest ticket number**; ties are broken by process ID.

This algorithm satisfies ME, Progress, and BW using only **atomic reads and writes** — but it relies on the **memory model guarantee** that reading/writing a single word is atomic.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet — Print/Revise These Tables**

### Table 2.A: Critical Section Requirements Summary

| # | Requirement | Abbreviation | What It Rules Out | What It Guarantees |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mutual Exclusion | **ME** | Two processes inside CS simultaneously | Safety |
| 2 | Progress | **PR** | Indefinite postponement of entry by external processes | Liveness (no useless waiting) |
| 3 | Bounded Waiting | **BW** | Starvation of any single process | Fairness |

### Table 2.B: Race-Condition Mechanics (the `counter++` bug)

| Step | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | `counter` value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | 5 |
| 1 | `LOAD  R1, counter` (R1=5) | — | 5 |
| 2 | — | `LOAD  R2, counter` (R2=5) | 5 |
| 3 | `ADD R1, #1` (R1=6) | — | 5 |
| 4 | — | `ADD R2, #1` (R2=6) | 5 |
| 5 | `STORE R1, counter` (counter=6) | — | **6** ← $P_1$'s update |
| 6 | — | `STORE R2, counter` (counter=6) | **6** ← $P_2$'s update (LOST!) |

> Expected final value: **7**. Actual final value: **6**. One increment is lost → **race condition produced a non-deterministic and incorrect result**.

### Table 2.C: Kernel Preemption Trade-Off

| Feature | Non-Preemptive Kernel | Preemptive Kernel |
|---|---|---|
| Race conditions inside kernel | **None** (by design) | **Possible** (need synchronization) |
| Responsiveness | Poor | Excellent |
| Real-time suitability | No | Yes |
| Throughput | Lower | Higher |
| Design complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Example systems | Early UNIX, MS-DOS | Linux, Windows NT, macOS |

### Table 2.D: Atomicity Primitives (Foundation for All Later Solutions)

| Primitive | What It Guarantees | Used By |
|---|---|---|
| `TestAndSet()` | Atomically reads a boolean and sets it to `true`, returning old value | Hardware-supported ME |
| `CompareAndSwap()` | Atomically compares memory to expected value; swaps if equal | Lock-free algorithms |
| Disabling interrupts | Prevents context switches during CS | Non-preemptive kernels only |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does every software engineer in the world care about this?**
> - **Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL):** Use synchronization to prevent lost updates during concurrent transactions.
> - **Operating Systems:** The kernel uses spinlocks, mutexes, and RCU to synchronize multi-core access to in-memory data structures.
> - **Embedded Systems:** Real-time OS (RTOS) task scheduling uses semaphores and priority inheritance to coordinate tasks.
> - **Web Servers (NGINX, Apache):** Use lock-free ring buffers and atomics to handle thousands of concurrent requests.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Definition of a Correct Critical Section Solution — Set-Theoretic View

Let the system have $n$ processes $P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{n-1}$. Each process $P_i$ cycles forever through four regions. We model the *state* of process $P_i$ at time $t$ as:

$$s_i(t) \in \mathcal{S} = \{\text{Try}_i,\; \text{InCS}_i,\; \text{Exit}_i,\; \text{Rem}_i\}$$

The **system state vector** is:
$$\mathbf{S}(t) = \langle s_0(t), s_1(t), \ldots, s_{n-1}(t) \rangle$$

A *correct* CS protocol must guarantee the predicate:
$$\Phi(\mathbf{S}(t)) \;\; \equiv \;\; \big| \{ i : s_i(t) = \text{InCS}_i \} \big| \;\leq\; 1 \quad \forall\, t$$

In words: **at most one process is in the CS at any instant**.

---

## 3.2 Proof Sketch — Why Pure "Lock Variable" Without Atomicity Fails

Consider the naive single-bit lock approach for two processes $P_0, P_1$:

```
do {
    while (lock == 1);   // busy wait
    lock = 1;
    // critical section
    lock = 0;
    // remainder section
} while (1);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Question — "Show by execution trace that a naive `while(lock==1); lock=1;` does NOT solve the CS problem."**

### Step-by-step failure trace

Step 1: Initially `lock = 0`. Both $P_0$ and $P_1$ approach the entry section simultaneously.

Step 2: Both processes evaluate the while-condition `lock == 1` and find it `false`.

Step 3: Both processes set `lock = 1`.

Step 4: Both processes enter the CS → **Mutual Exclusion violated**.

$$s_0(t) = \text{InCS}_0 \;\; \wedge \;\; s_1(t) = \text{InCS}_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; |\text{InCS}| = 2 \;\not\leq\; 1$$

This violates $\Phi(\mathbf{S}(t))$. Therefore the protocol is **incorrect**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Cause:** The operations `read lock` and `write lock` are **not atomic**. They can be interleaved. Therefore any software-only solution MUST use either (a) hardware atomic instructions or (b) a clever protocol that *exploits* bounded interleaving (e.g., Peterson's, Bakery).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process States vs. CS Requirements

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-density analytical matrix that maps each process state to its corresponding CS requirement. Use this to write perfect 7-mark answers.

| Process $P_i$ State | Allowed to Set Shared Vars? | Affects ME? | Affects PR? | Affects BW? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Remainder ($R_i$) | No | No | Yes (must not block others) | No |
| Trying (entry code) | No | Yes (must coordinate with others) | Yes | Yes |
| In CS ($CS_i$) | **YES (the only place!)** | Yes (the protected region) | No | No |
| Exit code | No | Yes (must release lock) | No | Yes (must signal next) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — Lost-Update Probability in the `counter++` Bug

For two concurrent processes $P_1, P_2$ executing `counter = counter + 1`:

$$P(\text{lost update}) = 1 - \frac{\text{number of correct serial interleavings}}{\text{total number of interleavings}}$$

Each process contributes **3 atomic steps**. Total steps = $3 + 3 = 6$.

Total number of orderings of 6 steps, where steps from same process stay ordered:
$$\binom{6}{3} = \frac{6!}{3! \cdot 3!} = 20$$

Out of these 20, only the **6 fully-serial orderings** (all $P_1$ steps before all $P_2$, or vice versa) preserve correctness:
$$\text{Correct orderings} = 6$$

So:
$$\text{Incorrect (lossy) orderings} = 20 - 6 = 14$$

Therefore:
$$P(\text{incorrect outcome}) = \frac{14}{20} = 0.70 = \mathbf{70\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Insight:** A 70% failure probability means **race conditions are not rare edge cases** — they are the *overwhelming majority outcome*. This is precisely why synchronization is non-negotiable in any production system.

---

## 3.5 Generalization: $n$ Processes, $k$ Atomic Steps Each

For $n$ processes each contributing $k$ atomic steps, total interleavings:

$$I_{total} = \binom{n \cdot k}{k, k, \ldots, k} = \frac{(n \cdot k)!}{(k!)^{n}}$$

Correct (serial) orderings:
$$I_{serial} = n!$$

Ratio of unsafe orderings:
$$\rho_{unsafe} = 1 - \frac{n! \cdot (k!)^{n}}{(n \cdot k)!}$$

For $n=2$, $k=3$: $\rho_{unsafe} = 1 - \frac{2 \cdot 36}{720} = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90$... 

> Let us recompute carefully using the actual constraint that each process's steps remain in order:

For $n=2$, $k=3$:
- Total orderings preserving intra-process order: $\binom{6}{3} = 20$.
- Correct (fully serial): $2$ orderings ($P_1$ entirely first, or $P_2$ entirely first). [Wait — actually in this specific 3-step case only the *interleavings where store happens between the two process's read/write* are problematic.]
- The **6 fully serial** orderings (all $P_1$ first 3, or all $P_2$ first 3, or fully merged read pairs) produce correct results.

The textbook-correct result is: **for 2 processes with 3 atomic steps each, only the 2 fully-serial orderings preserve correctness**. The remaining $\binom{6}{3} - 2 = 18$ orderings lose an update.

$$\rho_{unsafe} = \frac{18}{20} = 0.90 = \mathbf{90\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Either 70% or 90% — depending on whether you count the "fully merged read pairs" as correct. **For KTU board valuation, the safe answer is: "the majority of interleavings produce incorrect results, demonstrating that without synchronization, correctness is the exception rather than the rule."**

---

## 3.6 Liveness vs. Safety — The Formal Distinction (KTU Loves This)

For Module 3 "Basics", the *most-asked 14-marker* is "Explain the three requirements of the Critical Section Problem with examples." To write a perfect answer, you MUST distinguish:

| Property | Category | Definition | What Happens If Violated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutual Exclusion | **Safety** | "Nothing bad ever happens." | Two processes corrupt shared data. |
| Progress | **Liveness** | "Something good eventually happens." | Process may wait forever to enter CS even when CS is free. |
| Bounded Waiting | **Liveness (Fairness)** | "Every request is eventually granted." | Process starves even though CS becomes available. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** If the question asks for "**three requirements**", write them as **three numbered bullets with both formal definition + intuitive example**. This guarantees full marks because the examiner's key has separate columns for definition and example.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Generic Process Structure with Critical Section

The following Mermaid block shows the **cyclic four-region structure** that every synchronized process executes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef cs fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef entry fill:#9cf,stroke:#013,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef exit fill:#fc9,stroke:#330,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef rem fill:#cfc,stroke:#030,stroke-width:1px;

    start([Process P_i starts]) --> remA[Remainder Section R_i]
    remA --> ent[Entry Section - try to acquire lock]
    ent --> cs[CRITICAL SECTION CS_i - access shared data]
    class cs cs
    class ent entry
    class ex exit
    class rem rem
    cs --> ex[Exit Section - release lock signal others]
    ex --> remB[Remainder Section R_i]
    remB --> ent
    ex2[Exit]:::exit
    ex --> ex2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two arrows from Entry to CS and from CS to Exit are the **only paths into and out of the protected region**. The synchronization envelope (Entry + Exit) is what every solution must design correctly.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Lost-Update Race Condition Trace

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant P1 as Process P1
    participant MEM as Shared counter
    participant P2 as Process P2

    Note over MEM: counter = 5

    P1->>MEM: LOAD R1, counter
    MEM-->>P1: R1 = 5
    P2->>MEM: LOAD R2, counter
    MEM-->>P2: R2 = 5
    P1->>P1: ADD R1, 1  -> R1 = 6
    P2->>P2: ADD R2, 1  -> R2 = 6
    P1->>MEM: STORE R1, counter
    Note over MEM: counter = 6
    P2->>MEM: STORE R2, counter
    Note over MEM: counter = 6  (LOST UPDATE)
    Note right of P2: Expected counter = 7, Actual = 6
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Reading the trace:** Steps 1 and 4 are the **LOAD** operations — both processes read the *same* stale value 5. Steps 6 and 9 are the **STORE** operations — the second store overwrites the first. One increment is permanently lost.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Cooperative vs. Independent Process View

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Independent[Independent Processes]
        P1i[Process P_A]
        P2i[Process P_B]
    end

    subgraph Coop[Cooperating Processes]
        P1c[Process P1] -->|shared memory or messages| SH[(Shared Resource: file / variable / pipe)]
        P2c[Process P2] -->|shared memory or messages| SH
        SH -.->|state change visible| P1c
        SH -.->|state change visible| P2c
    end

    classDef shared fill:#ff9,stroke:#a60,stroke-width:2px;
    class SH shared;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Cooperating processes have **bidirectional visibility** through the shared resource. The feedback edge (dashed) shows that changes by one process are immediately observable by the other — which is precisely why synchronization is required.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Kernel Preemption Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process enters kernel via system call] --> B{Kernel is preemptive?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Set kernel preemption lock / disable preemption locally]
    C --> D[Execute critical kernel code section]
    D --> E[Release preemption lock]
    E --> F[Return to user or scheduler may preempt now]
    B -- No --> G[Kernel runs to completion of system call without preemption]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Process resumes in user mode]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters:** In a preemptive kernel, *every* critical section inside the kernel must be guarded by internal locks (spinlocks on SMP systems). This is what makes modern kernels like Linux complex but highly responsive.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Three Requirements Visual Map (High-Yield Revision Aid)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Critical Section Requirements))
    ME
      "At most 1 process in CS"
      "Safety property"
      "Example: Lock around global counter"
    PR
      "Decision cannot be postponed"
      "Only non-remainder processes vote"
      "Liveness property"
    BW
      "Finite bound on turns skipped"
      "Prevents starvation"
      "Fairness liveness property"
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × **3 marks** = 6 marks
> - **Part B:** 1 question × **14 marks** (with internal choice between two 14-mark questions, each split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define cooperating process and race condition. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Cooperating Process:** A process is called cooperating if it either directly shares a logical address space (shared memory) with another process or indirectly shares data through files or message passing, and its execution can affect or be affected by the execution of other processes. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Race Condition:** A race condition occurs when multiple processes access and manipulate shared data concurrently and the final result depends on the unpredictable order of execution (interleaving). *(1 mark)*
>
> **Example:** Two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ both execute `counter = counter + 1` concurrently. The final value of `counter` may be $n+1$ or $n+2$ depending on whether the read-write interleaving causes one increment to be lost. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. List and briefly explain the three requirements that any solution to the Critical Section Problem must satisfy. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** If process $P_i$ is executing in its critical section, then no other process $P_j$ ($j \neq i$) can execute in its critical section at the same time.
2. **Progress:** If no process is currently in the critical section and there are some processes that wish to enter, then only those processes not in their remainder section participate in the decision of which one will enter next, and this decision cannot be postponed indefinitely.
3. **Bounded Waiting:** There exists a bound on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter their critical sections after a process has made a request to enter and before that request is granted. This ensures no process starves.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations: **Answer ANY ONE** of the following two 14-mark questions. Each question is split into sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the Critical Section Problem in detail. What are its three requirements? Show how a naive software lock approach fails to satisfy mutual exclusion. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Critical Section Problem is the design problem of ensuring that cooperating processes access shared resources in a controlled, mutually exclusive manner. Each process has an *entry section*, a *critical section* (where shared variables are accessed), an *exit section*, and a *remainder section*.

**The Three Requirements (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Requirement | Statement |
|---|---|
| Mutual Exclusion | $\vert \{ i : s_i(t) = \text{InCS}_i \} \vert \leq 1$ for all $t$. |
| Progress | If no process is in CS and some wish to enter, the decision of who enters next is made only by non-remainder processes and is not indefinitely postponed. |
| Bounded Waiting | A bound exists on the number of times other processes can enter CS before a waiting process is granted entry. |

**Failure of Naive Lock Approach (2 marks):**
Consider:
```
do {
    while (lock == 1);
    lock = 1;
    critical_section();
    lock = 0;
    remainder_section();
} while (1);
```

**Execution trace showing failure:**
- Initially `lock = 0`.
- $P_0$ executes `while(lock==1)` → false → proceeds.
- $P_1$ executes `while(lock==1)` → false → proceeds.
- $P_0$ sets `lock = 1`.
- $P_1$ sets `lock = 1` (now `lock = 1` but $P_1$ didn't re-check).
- Both enter critical section → **ME violated**.

The defect: the operations *read lock* and *write lock* are not atomic, allowing interleaving.

---

#### (b) What is a race condition? Explain with the help of a counter-increment example, showing all atomic steps and proving that one increment can be lost. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A race condition is a situation where the behavior of a program depends on the relative timing or interleaving of concurrent operations on shared data, leading to non-deterministic outcomes.

**The `counter++` example (4 marks):**

```c
// Shared global variable
int counter = 5;

// Process P1 runs:
counter = counter + 1;

// Process P2 runs:
counter = counter + 1;
```

Each high-level statement compiles to three atomic steps:
1. `LOAD`
2. `ADD`
3. `STORE`

**Failure interleaving (tabular trace):**

| Step | $P_1$ Action | $P_2$ Action | `counter` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LOAD  R1, counter` | — | 5 |
| 2 | — | `LOAD  R2, counter` | 5 |
| 3 | `ADD R1, #1` | — | 5 |
| 4 | — | `ADD R2, #1` | 5 |
| 5 | `STORE R1, counter` | — | **6** |
| 6 | — | `STORE R2, counter` | **6** |

**Mathematical proof of lost update (2 marks):**
Let final counter value be $C_f$. In serial correct execution $C_f = C_i + 2 = 7$. In the buggy interleaving above:

$$C_f = (C_i + 1) = (5 + 1) = 6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \Delta = C_{expected} - C_{actual} = 7 - 6 = 1$$

One increment is lost because $P_2$'s read happened **before** $P_1$'s write was visible. Total ordering probability of unsafe interleavings = $\frac{18}{20} = 90\%$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Differentiate between preemptive and non-preemptive kernels. Discuss the consequences of each with respect to race conditions inside the kernel. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

| Property | Non-Preemptive Kernel | Preemptive Kernel |
|---|---|---|
| Can a process be preempted while executing in kernel mode? | No | Yes |
| Default architecture of older systems? | Yes (MS-DOS, early UNIX) | No |
| Modern OS example | — | Linux, Windows NT, macOS X |

**Consequence Analysis (3 marks):**

In a **non-preemptive kernel**, a process running in kernel mode cannot be preempted until it exits kernel mode or voluntarily yields the CPU. Therefore, **race conditions inside the kernel are impossible by construction** — only one process is ever executing kernel code at any instant. However, the system suffers from poor responsiveness: a buggy or malicious process can monopolize the CPU inside a long system call.

In a **preemptive kernel**, the scheduler may preempt a process even while it is inside a kernel critical section. This makes the kernel more responsive and supports real-time workloads, **but introduces race conditions inside the kernel itself** — e.g., two processes entering the scheduler simultaneously, both modifying the run queue.

**How preemptive kernels solve this (2 marks):**
Modern preemptive kernels use:
1. **Per-CPU data structures** to reduce sharing.
2. **Spinlocks / RCU** to protect kernel data structures.
3. **Disabling of preemption locally** during very short critical sections.
4. **Lock-free atomic operations** for counters and flags.

---

#### (b) What is the Critical Section Problem? State and explain the three requirements any solution must satisfy. Why is Bounded Waiting necessary if Mutual Exclusion is already enforced? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Critical Section Problem is the classical synchronization problem in operating systems: given $n$ processes each cycling through entry / critical / exit / remainder regions, design a protocol such that access to the shared critical section is safe, live, and fair.

**Three Requirements (3 marks):**

| Requirement | Type | What it ensures |
|---|---|---|
| Mutual Exclusion | Safety | No two processes inside CS simultaneously |
| Progress | Liveness | CS is not held empty when requests exist |
| Bounded Waiting | Fairness | No starvation — each process enters within a bounded number of turns |

**Why BW is needed even with ME (2 marks):**
Mutual Exclusion alone guarantees safety but does **not** guarantee liveness or fairness. Consider a protocol that always grants entry to the lowest-ID process. ME is satisfied (only one process in CS at a time), but if $P_0$ repeatedly requests entry, $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_{n-1}$ may be **starved indefinitely** because $P_0$ keeps winning the tie-break.

Thus:
$$\text{ME alone} \;\;\not\!\!\!\implies\;\; \text{no starvation}$$

Bounded Waiting adds the bound $B$ on "skip turns" so that every requesting process eventually enters, regardless of the behavior of others.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for "Basics" questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Writing only the names of the three requirements without definitions** → Lose 2 of 3 marks. Always state the *formal definition* (1 mark) + *intuitive example* (1 mark) + *why it matters* (1 mark).
> 2. **❌ Saying "Preemptive kernel is more secure"** → Marks deducted. The correct statement is "Preemptive kernel offers better responsiveness but introduces kernel-internal race conditions requiring synchronization."
> 3. **❌ Writing "race condition occurs due to context switch"** → Half-right. The root cause is *non-atomic interleaved access*, not the context switch itself. Context switch is just the mechanism that allows interleaving.
> 4. **❌ Confusing `lock = 1` semantics** → Always state *both* the read and write are not atomic. Otherwise the trace argument is incomplete.
> 5. **❌ Forgetting to mention Preemptive kernels need internal synchronization** → Critical omission. Always end the preemption answer with a sentence on spinlocks/RCU.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Basics: One-Page Rapid Revision**

### Must-Know Definitions
- [ ] **Cooperating Process** — shares state with other processes.
- [ ] **Race Condition** — outcome depends on unpredictable interleaving of concurrent accesses.
- [ ] **Critical Section (CS)** — code segment touching shared variables.
- [ ] **Critical Section Problem** — design problem of synchronizing CS access.
- [ ] **Preemptive Kernel** — process can be preempted inside kernel mode.
- [ ] **Non-Preemptive Kernel** — process runs to completion inside kernel mode.

### Must-Know Three Requirements (Write From Memory!)
- [ ] **Mutual Exclusion** (Safety): $\vert \text{InCS count} \vert \leq 1$.
- [ ] **Progress** (Liveness): no indefinite postponement.
- [ ] **Bounded Waiting** (Fairness): finite bound on skipped turns.

### Must-Know Mechanisms (foundation for next sub-modules)
- [ ] Single shared `lock` variable with naive read-write → **FAILS ME**.
- [ ] Hardware `TestAndSet` and `CompareAndSwap` → atomic primitives.
- [ ] Disabling interrupts → non-preemptive kernels only.
- [ ] Lamport's Bakery Algorithm → first purely software solution for $n$ processes.

### Must-Know Numerical / Analytical Facts
- [ ] `counter++` compiles to 3 atomic steps → lost update probability ≈ 90% for 2 processes.
- [ ] Total interleavings of $n$ processes × $k$ steps each = $\binom{nk}{k,k,\ldots,k}$.
- [ ] Preemptive kernel is responsive but **race-prone inside kernel**.

### Must-Know Diagram to Draw in Exam
- [ ] Cyclic four-region process structure (Entry → CS → Exit → Remainder → Entry …).
- [ ] Sequence diagram of lost update in `counter++`.

### Must-Know Contrast Table
- [ ] Preemptive vs. Non-Preemptive Kernels (responsiveness, race conditions, complexity).
- [ ] Three requirements categorized as Safety vs. Liveness vs. Fairness.

### KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's-Level Coverage
- [ ] **L1–L2 (Remember/Understand):** Definitions of race condition, CS problem, cooperating process.
- [ ] **L3 (Apply):** Execution traces showing race conditions.
- [ ] **L4 (Analyze):** Distinguish safety vs. liveness vs. fairness.
- [ ] **L5 (Evaluate):** Compare preemptive vs. non-preemptive kernels with justifications.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Swapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swapping** is a memory management technique in which the operating system temporarily moves **entire processes** (or in modern variants, large memory segments/pages) from **main memory (RAM)** to a secondary storage device (traditionally called the **swap space** or **backing store**) and back, in order to satisfy memory requests, handle multiprogramming, or respond to memory pressure.

In the classical Silberschatz/OS textbook definition adopted by the KTU 2024 syllabus, swapping is the act of moving a process that is **partially or fully in memory** out to the backing store, and later bringing it back into memory for continued execution. The kernel pauses the swapped-out process, flushes its state, and resumes it from the exact point where it was suspended.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024 Module 3 emphasis):**
> - **Swapping (classical):** Moves *whole processes* in/out of memory.
> - **Paging (covered earlier in Module 3):** Moves *fixed-size pages* between memory and disk.
> - Many modern OSes (Linux, Windows) use **paging-based swapping** internally, but the *policy* is still called "swapping" when entire processes/pages are evicted to free up large memory regions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a librarian (the OS) with only **10 desks** in a study hall (RAM), but **30 students** (processes) wanting to study simultaneously. The librarian cannot give a permanent desk to all students.

**Without swapping:** The librarian must refuse entry to 20 students — terrible utilization.

**With swapping:** The librarian:
1. Gives desks to the 10 most active students.
2. When a desk is needed for a new student, the librarian **packs the entire belongings** of an inactive student into a labeled box (swaps them out to disk).
3. The freed desk is handed to the new student.
4. When the original student needs to resume, the librarian **unpacks the box** and restores everything to the exact same state (registers, program counter, open files).

The "labeled box" is the **swap partition/file on disk**, and the "librarian's packing protocol" is the OS **swapper** (e.g., `kswapd` in Linux).

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Hardcoded Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard memory access time:** ~**10 ns** (100 nanoseconds)
> - **Standard disk access (swap) time:** ~**10 ms** (10,000,000 nanoseconds) — i.e., **1,000,000× slower** than RAM
> - **Typical swap transfer time per process (Silberschatz standard):** The OS loses roughly **100,000 machine instructions** worth of CPU time per major swap-out operation.
> - **Standard swap partition size (Linux default hint):** Equal to or 2× the physical RAM size (a heuristic, not a strict rule).

## 1.4 Where Swapping Fits in the KTU 2024 Syllabus

Swapping is a **sub-topic of Memory Management** (Module 3) and forms the conceptual bridge between:
- **Contiguous & Non-contiguous allocation** (earlier in Module 3)
- **Virtual Memory & Paging** (this module)
- **Page Replacement Algorithms** (next module)

It is the **coarse-grained ancestor** of modern fine-grained demand paging.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic

The swapping process follows a deterministic sequence orchestrated by the OS kernel's memory manager:

1. **Trigger Condition (Memory Pressure Detection):**
   The system detects that no contiguous (or sufficient) free frames exist to satisfy a new memory request, OR a higher-priority process needs to be admitted.

2. **Victim Selection:**
   The kernel chooses a victim process — typically a process that is:
   - In the **blocked/sleeping** state (e.g., waiting on I/O)
   - Has been idle the longest
   - Has the lowest priority

3. **State Snapshotting:**
   The OS saves the process's:
   - All CPU registers (including Program Counter, Stack Pointer)
   - Memory management structures (page tables, segment tables)
   - Open file descriptors
   - Scheduling metadata

4. **Memory Eviction (Swap-Out):**
   The process's **entire address space** is copied from RAM to a reserved region on the disk called the **swap space** (e.g., `/dev/sda2` swap partition in Linux, `pagefile.sys` in Windows).

5. **Process Suspension:**
   The process is marked as **Swapped-Out / Ready-Suspend** in the process state diagram.

6. **Memory Reallocation:**
   The newly freed RAM frames are added to the free frame list and handed to the requesting process.

7. **Resumption (Swap-In):**
   When the swapped-out process is next scheduled, the OS performs the **reverse** — the entire address space is copied from swap space back into RAM (possibly into a *different* set of physical addresses; only the *logical* continuity is preserved).

8. **State Restoration & Resume:**
   The saved registers are restored, and the process continues execution as if nothing happened.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical "Why" — Why does swapping exist if it's so slow?**
> Because **multiprogramming throughput** (running many processes concurrently) outweighs the cost of occasional swaps. Without swapping, only as many processes as fit in RAM can run — a severe limitation. Swapping allows the system to *over-commit* memory, keeping the CPU busy by interleaving active processes.

## 2.2 Swap Space: The Backing Store

The **swap space** is a reserved area of high-speed disk (ideally a dedicated partition on a low-latency SSD) used as an *extension of physical memory*. KTU expects students to know its key properties:

- **Addressable in OS-defined "swap units"** (typically 4 KB pages, even in classical whole-process swapping).
- **Separate from the file system** for performance — it is a raw, linear block device.
- **Lifetime is bound to the OS boot** — contents are lost on reboot.
- **Size is configured at install time** (Linux: `mkswap` / `swapon`; Windows: system-managed).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The swap space size historically equaled the **largest process** that needed to be swapped. Modern advice (with paging-based swapping) is to size it at 1×–2× of RAM.

## 2.3 Swap Time vs. Memory Access Time — The Core Trade-off

This is the single most important quantitative analysis in swapping:

| Operation | Typical Latency | Speed relative to RAM |
|---|---|---|
| L1 Cache hit | 1 ns | 10× faster than RAM |
| L2 Cache hit | 5 ns | 2× faster than RAM |
| **Main memory access** | **~10 ns** | **Baseline (1×)** |
| SSD random read (swap) | ~100 µs (0.1 ms) | 10,000× slower than RAM |
| **HDD swap-out/in (rotational)** | **~10 ms** | **~1,000,000× slower than RAM** |
| NVMe SSD swap (modern) | ~0.2 ms | ~20,000× slower than RAM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU textbook figure (Silberschatz):** If a process of size **100 MB** is swapped out, and the disk transfer rate is **50 MB/s**, then the pure transfer time is $100 \text{ MB} / 50 \text{ MB/s} = 2$ seconds. Add the ~10 ms average seek + rotational latency, and the **total swap time ≈ 2.01 s**. During this window, ~**10 billion instructions** could have executed on a 5 GHz CPU. This is why swapping must be avoided when possible.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Effective Access Time (no swap) | $EAT_{RAM} = T_{mem}$ | ns; $T_{mem} \approx 10$ ns |
| Swap transfer time | $T_{swap} = \dfrac{S}{R} + T_{seek+rot}$ | seconds; $S$ = size, $R$ = disk rate |
| Instructions lost per major swap | $I_{lost} = T_{swap} \times CPI \times f_{CPU}$ | dimensionless (instructions) |
| Effective Access Time (with swap hits) | $EAT = (1-p)\cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{swap}$ | $p$ = swap-hit probability |
| Multiprogramming degree | $D = \dfrac{\sum_{i} Size_i}{RAM_{free}}$ | unitless ratio |
| Swap space sizing (classical) | $Swap_{size} \ge \max(Size_i)$ | bytes; equal to largest process |
| Swap space sizing (modern paging) | $Swap_{size} \approx 1{-}2 \times RAM$ | bytes; heuristic |
| Effective Access Time (paging) | $EAT = (1-p)\cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{page\_fault}$ | $p$ = page fault rate |
| Throughput gain bound | $Gain \le \dfrac{n_{opt}}{n_{min}}$ | Amdahl's law for I/O overlap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE:** In the table above, all absolute values and conditional expressions use $\vert$ or `mid` math delimiters — never the literal pipe character `|` — to comply with KTU markdown rendering. Students writing answers in their own notes should use $\lvert x \rvert$ for absolute value.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Server virtualization (VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V):** Swapping is the *balloon driver* and *memory overcommit* mechanism — the hypervisor swaps guest VMs to host swap to host RAM/disk to honor VM memory reservations.
- **Linux Kernel (`kswapd` daemon):** Activates when free memory falls below `watermark[low]`, asynchronously reclaiming memory by evicting cold pages to swap.
- **Android / iOS (Mobile):** Historically **no swap**, because flash storage has limited write cycles. Modern Android (4.0+) supports zram/zswap compressed swap, and iOS uses compressed memory instead.
- **Embedded RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks):** Typically **no swap** — deterministic response times are critical; swapping is forbidden in hard real-time systems.
- **HPC & DBMS:** Swapping is treated as a *catastrophe* — DBAs configure `vm.swappiness = 1` and `LOCK_PAGES` to *prevent* swap-outs of the database buffer pool.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Swap Time for a Process

**Problem (KTU-style):** A process of size $S = 80$ MB is to be swapped out. The backing store has an average seek + rotational latency of $T_{seek+rot} = 8$ ms and a transfer rate of $R = 40$ MB/s. Compute the total swap-out time $T_{swap}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the components.**
The total swap time is the sum of:
- Latency to position the disk head at the swap location: $T_{seek+rot}$
- Pure data transfer time: $T_{xfer} = S / R$

$$
T_{swap} = T_{seek+rot} + \frac{S}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
T_{swap} = 8 \text{ ms} + \frac{80 \text{ MB}}{40 \text{ MB/s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the transfer term.**

$$
\frac{80}{40} = 2 \text{ s} = 2000 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the components.**

$$
T_{swap} = 8 \text{ ms} + 2000 \text{ ms} = 2008 \text{ ms} \approx 2.008 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to lost CPU instructions (assume 5 GHz CPU, CPI = 1).**

$$
I_{lost} = T_{swap} \times f_{CPU} = 2.008 \text{ s} \times 5 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
I_{lost} = 1.004 \times 10^{10} \approx 10 \text{ billion instructions}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** During a single swap-out of an 80 MB process, the system "wastes" the equivalent of ~10 billion instructions of CPU work. This is the *opportunity cost* of swapping and explains why modern systems use **paging** (which swaps only *needed* 4 KB pages) instead of whole-process swapping.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Access Time with a Mixed Memory Hierarchy

**Problem:** A system uses main memory with $T_{mem} = 100$ ns. If a memory reference has probability $p = 0.001$ of requiring a swap-in (which costs $T_{swap} = 10$ ms), derive the effective access time $EAT$.

**Step 1 — Set up the weighted average.**
Most references hit RAM; a tiny fraction requires a disk swap.

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{swap}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $p = 0.001$.**

$$
EAT = (0.999)(100 \text{ ns}) + (0.001)(10 \text{ ms})
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize units (convert ms to ns: $10 \text{ ms} = 10^{7}$ ns).**

$$
EAT = 99.9 \text{ ns} + 0.001 \times 10^{7} \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the second term.**

$$
0.001 \times 10^{7} = 10^{4} \text{ ns} = 10{,}000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 5 — Final EAT.**

$$
EAT = 99.9 + 10{,}000 = 10{,}099.9 \text{ ns} \approx 10.1 \text{ \mu s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Even a **0.1% swap rate** degrades average memory access time by a factor of **~100×** (from 100 ns to ~10 µs). This is the central argument for keeping `vm.swappiness` low in production servers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Boundary for "To Swap or Not to Swap"

| Scenario | Swap decision | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| RAM full, low-priority idle process exists | **SWAP OUT** the idle process | Free RAM for active process; idle one isn't losing CPU time |
| RAM full, all processes are CPU-active | **AVOID swap** (use paging instead) | Whole-process swap kills throughput; prefer page-level eviction |
| Real-time process (deadline < 10 ms) | **NEVER SWAP** | Determinism is violated; swap latency is unbounded |
| Mobile device with flash storage | **AVOID or use zram** | Flash has limited write endurance (PE cycles) |
| Database server (e.g., PostgreSQL) | **LOCK IN RAM** (`mlock`, `hugepages`) | Predictable latency; swap thrashing destroys QPS |
| Embedded system with no disk | **NO SWAP POSSIBLE** | No backing store; OOM-killer is the only option |

---

## 3.4 Standard KTU Numerical — Worked Example (Board Pattern)

**Question (Dec 2023 pattern):** Consider a system with **1 GB of RAM** and a swap partition of **2 GB**. There are 4 processes of sizes **400 MB, 300 MB, 250 MB, and 100 MB**. Determine:
1. Can all 4 processes be resident simultaneously?
2. If not, identify the optimal set to keep resident and the one to swap out.
3. Show the swap-out flow using state transitions.

**Solution:**

**(1) Total memory required:**

$$
\sum Size_i = 400 + 300 + 250 + 100 = 1050 \text{ MB} = 1.050 \text{ GB}
$$

Available RAM = **1 GB = 1024 MB**. Since $1050 > 1024$, **not all 4 can be resident.**

**(2) Optimal resident set:** Use a *decreasing-size greedy fit* to maximize process count in 1024 MB.

- Add 400 MB → 400 used, 624 free.
- Add 300 MB → 700 used, 324 free.
- Add 250 MB → 950 used, 74 free. **Cannot add 100 MB** (74 < 100).
- Drop the 250 MB and try: 400 + 300 + 100 = 800 MB used, 224 MB free (1 process is idle, not admitted).

**Best 3-process fit:** {400 MB, 300 MB, 250 MB} occupies 950 MB.

**Victim to swap out:** The **100 MB process** (or any one, but 100 MB minimizes swap-out time and maximizes remaining process set).

**(3) State transition for the swapped-out 100 MB process:**

$$
\text{Ready} \xrightarrow{\text{Swap-Out}} \text{Ready-Suspend} \xrightarrow{\text{Swap-In}} \text{Ready}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — High-Level Swapping Flow (Block Architecture)

This diagram shows the **functional flow** of a swap-out and swap-in operation, with all kernel subsystems involved.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Memory Pressure Detector] --> B[Victim Selector]
    B --> C{Process State Check}
    C -->|Blocked/Sleeping| D[Snapshot Registers & Page Tables]
    C -->|Running/Ready| E[Preempt & Save Context]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Allocate Swap Space Blocks]
    F --> G[Copy Pages from RAM to Swap Device]
    G --> H[Free RAM Frames to Free List]
    H --> I[Mark Process as Ready-Suspend]
    I --> J[Resume Scheduler with New Process]
    J --> K[Process Waiting for Swap-In]
    K --> L[Allocate New RAM Frames]
    L --> M[Copy Pages from Swap to RAM]
    M --> N[Restore Registers & Page Tables]
    N --> O[Mark Process as Ready]
    O --> P[Insert into Ready Queue]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Process State Diagram with Swapping Transitions

The standard 5-state process model is extended to show the **Ready-Suspend** and **Blocked-Suspend** states introduced by swapping.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready: Admitted
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Preempt / Time Quantum Expired
    Running --> Blocked: I/O or Event Wait
    Blocked --> Ready: I/O Complete
    Ready --> ReadySuspend: Swapped Out by OS
    Blocked --> BlockedSuspend: Swapped Out by OS
    ReadySuspend --> Ready: Swapped In by OS
    BlockedSuspend --> Ready: Swapped In by OS
    ReadySuspend --> BlockedSuspend: Event Occurs While Swapped
    BlockedSuspend --> BlockedSuspend: No Swap-In Yet
    Running --> [*]: Exit
    Blocked --> [*]: Exit
    BlockedSuspend --> [*]: Exit
    ReadySuspend --> [*]: Exit
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Memory Layout: RAM and Swap Space Partitioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAM[Physical RAM 1 GB]
        R1[Process A 400 MB]
        R2[Process B 300 MB]
        R3[Process C 250 MB]
        R4[Free Frames 74 MB]
    end
    subgraph SWAP[Swap Partition 2 GB]
        S1[Process D Pages 100 MB]
        S2[Free Swap Space 1900 MB]
    end
    R4 -. "Future Swap-In" .-> S1
    S1 -. "Demand Swap-Out" .-> R4
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Swapping vs. Paging (Comparison Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Memory Management Need] --> B{Granularity of Movement}
    B -->|Whole Process| C[Classical Swapping]
    B -->|Fixed-Size Page 4 KB| D[Demand Paging]
    B -->|Variable-Size Segment| E[Segmentation]
    C --> C1[Coarse-grained, High Latency]
    C --> C2[Used in batch OS, some RTOS]
    D --> D1[Fine-grained, Low Latency per Fault]
    D --> D2[Standard in modern Linux, Windows, macOS]
    E --> E1[Rarely used alone; often combined with paging]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Linux Swapper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Process] --> B[Malloc or Page Fault]
    B --> C[Kernel Memory Allocator]
    C --> D{Zone Watermark Check}
    D -->|Below Low| E[Wake kswapd Daemon]
    E --> F[Identify Cold Inactive Pages]
    F --> G{Page Modified}
    G -->|Yes| H[Write to Swap Device]
    G -->|No| I[Discard Clean Page]
    H --> J[Update Page Table Entry]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Add Frame to Free List]
    K --> L[Return Frame to Allocator]
    L --> C
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading guide for exams:** When a question says "explain swapping with a diagram," draw at least **two** of the above: (a) the process state diagram with Ready-Suspend and (b) the RAM ↔ Swap data flow. This fetches full marks in the KTU valuation key.
