# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define swapping in operating systems. Mention any two situations in which the OS initiates swapping.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Swapping** is a memory management technique in which the operating system moves entire processes (or large memory regions) **in and out of main memory** to a secondary storage area called the **swap space** (backing store), in order to free up RAM for other processes.

**Two situations when the OS initiates swapping:**

1. **Memory pressure:** When the system needs to admit a new process or satisfy a large memory request but no free frames are available in RAM, the OS swaps out a less-active (typically blocked) process to the swap device.

2. **Degree of multiprogramming control:** The OS swaps out idle or low-priority processes during periods of low CPU utilization to make room for higher-priority work, or swaps them back in when the system load decreases.

*(Acceptable alternative: To free a large contiguous region; To handle process suspension in batch systems.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is swap space? Why is it typically kept on a separate disk partition rather than inside the file system?**

**Model Answer:**

**Swap space** is a reserved area of disk storage (either a dedicated partition or a file) that the operating system uses as an *extension of physical RAM* to hold memory pages/processes that have been evicted from main memory.

**Why a separate partition:**

1. **Performance:** A raw swap partition bypasses the file system layer (no inode lookups, journaling overhead, or directory structures), giving lower and more predictable access latency.
2. **Fixed geometry:** A partition has a stable, known size and block address, allowing the OS to compute swap locations using simple offset arithmetic.
3. **Reliability:** Swap is decoupled from file system corruption; a crashed file system does not affect the swap device.
4. **Lifetime management:** Swap contents are inherently transient (lost on reboot) and need not be journaled or backed up — placing it on a partition enforces this lifecycle.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is internally divided into two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** (internal choice). Both questions are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **swapping** in operating systems. With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **process state transitions** when a process is swapped out and swapped back in. What factors does the OS consider when choosing a victim process to swap out?

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of Swapping (3 marks):**
Swapping is a memory management technique where the OS temporarily moves **entire processes** between main memory (RAM) and a secondary storage area called the **swap space** (backing store). The main purpose is to free up RAM so that the OS can admit more processes than what would fit in physical memory, thereby increasing the **degree of multiprogramming** and improving CPU utilization.

**2. Process State Diagram (3 marks):**

The standard 5-state model is extended with two new states: **Ready-Suspend** and **Blocked-Suspend**. The transitions are:

- **Ready → Ready-Suspend (Swap-Out):** When a higher-priority process needs memory and the current process is in the Ready queue but not currently running, the OS may preemptively swap it out.
- **Blocked → Blocked-Suspend (Swap-Out):** When a blocked (I/O-waiting) process is chosen as a victim, it is moved directly to Blocked-Suspend to free its RAM.
- **Ready-Suspend → Ready (Swap-In):** When the OS has free memory and the suspended process is the next to be scheduled, it is swapped in.
- **Blocked-Suspend → Blocked or Ready:** If the I/O event completes while the process is swapped out, it transitions to Blocked-Suspend; when swapped in, it becomes Ready.

*(Draw the state diagram similar to Section 4.2 above.)*

**3. Victim Selection Criteria (1 mark):**
The OS typically considers the following when selecting a victim process to swap out:

- **Process state:** Prefer blocked/sleeping processes (they aren't losing CPU time).
- **Priority:** Swap out the lowest-priority process first.
- **Residence time / age:** Newly arrived processes may be favored to stay (working-set considerations).
- **Memory size:** Smaller processes are cheaper to swap (faster swap-out time).
- **Time since last I/O completion:** Processes unlikely to wake soon are preferred victims.

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]** A system has **512 MB of RAM** and a swap partition of **1 GB**. Four processes arrive with the following sizes and states:

| Process | Size | State |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 200 MB | Running (CPU-bound) |
| P2 | 150 MB | Blocked (waiting on disk I/O) |
| P3 | 100 MB | Ready |
| P4 | 80 MB | Ready |

**Identify which process the OS should swap out. Justify your answer with calculations, and compute the swap-out time assuming a disk transfer rate of 50 MB/s and a seek+rotational latency of 5 ms.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total memory in use:**

$$
200 + 150 + 100 + 80 = 530 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Memory pressure check:**
Available RAM = 512 MB. Total demand = 530 MB. **Deficit = 530 − 512 = 18 MB.** Therefore, the OS must swap out at least one process.

**Step 3 — Victim selection:**
Following the criteria (state + size), **P4 (80 MB, Ready) is the best swap-out candidate** because:
- It is in the Ready state (not currently losing CPU time).
- It is the smallest (cheapest to swap = least swap-out time).
- P1 (200 MB) and P2 (150 MB) are too critical/large; P3 (100 MB) is needed for a balanced resident set.

**Alternative valid answer:** P2 (150 MB, Blocked) — it is sleeping on I/O, so it isn't missing CPU time. However, P4 is preferred for being smaller (lower swap cost).

**Step 4 — Swap-out time calculation:**

$$
T_{swap} = T_{seek+rot} + \frac{S}{R}
$$

$$
T_{swap} = 5 \text{ ms} + \frac{80 \text{ MB}}{50 \text{ MB/s}}
$$

$$
T_{swap} = 5 \text{ ms} + 1.6 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms} + 1600 \text{ ms} = 1605 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{swap} = 1605 \text{ ms} \approx 1.605 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**Step 5 — CPU instructions lost (bonus, ½ mark):**
At 4 GHz, CPI = 1:

$$
I_{lost} = 1.605 \times 4 \times 10^{9} \approx 6.42 \times 10^{9} \text{ instructions} \approx 6.4 \text{ billion}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **swapping** and **paging**. Explain why modern operating systems prefer paging over classical whole-process swapping, even though both use the same backing store.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Swapping (Classical) | Paging (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Whole process | Fixed 4 KB pages |
| **Unit of transfer** | Entire address space | Individual page |
| **Latency per transfer** | High (entire process) | Low (only one page) |
| **Memory wastage** | Transfers code/data that may not be needed | Transfers only the faulted page |
| **State saved** | Full process context + all pages | Only the single page + its PTE |
| **Resumption speed** | Slow (must load all pages) | Fast (load only what's referenced) |
| **Used in** | Early mainframe OS (IBM OS/360), some RTOS | Linux, Windows, macOS, Android |
| **Overhead per reference** | None (whole process is resident after swap-in) | Possible (page fault) |
| **Swap-out trigger** | OS memory pressure (coarse) | Per-page fault (fine) |
| **Fragmentation** | External (process must fit contiguously) | Internal (last page partial) — much smaller |

**Why paging is preferred (2 marks):**
1. **Localized demand:** Most programs exhibit *locality of reference* (spatial and temporal). A process may need only a tiny fraction of its address space at any moment, so swapping the whole process is wasteful.
2. **Smaller I/O units:** Transferring a 4 KB page (8 ms) is dramatically faster than transferring an 80 MB process (1.6 s), giving better interactive response.
3. **Smaller working set residency:** Paging naturally keeps only the working set in RAM; the rest can remain in swap, supporting higher effective multiprogramming.
4. **Simpler hardware support:** MMUs and TLB caches are designed around fixed-size pages, so paging integrates cleanly with the hardware.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a system where the probability of a memory access resulting in a **swap-in (page fault requiring disk I/O)** is $p = 0.0005$. Given $T_{mem} = 100$ ns and $T_{swap} = 8$ ms, calculate:
1. The **Effective Access Time (EAT)**.
2. How much does EAT degrade compared to a system with **no swap (pure RAM)**?
3. What is the **maximum tolerable swap probability** to keep EAT below **500 ns**?

**Model Solution:**

**(1) EAT calculation:**

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{swap}
$$

$$
EAT = (1 - 0.0005)(100 \text{ ns}) + (0.0005)(8 \times 10^{6} \text{ ns})
$$

$$
EAT = (0.9995)(100) + (0.0005)(8{,}000{,}000)
$$

$$
EAT = 99.95 \text{ ns} + 4000 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
\boxed{EAT = 4100 \text{ ns} \approx 4.1 \text{ \mu s}}
$$

**(2) Degradation ratio:**

$$
\text{Degradation} = \frac{EAT_{with\_swap}}{EAT_{pure\_RAM}} = \frac{4100 \text{ ns}}{100 \text{ ns}} = 41\times
$$

The system is **41 times slower** in effective memory access — a dramatic penalty for a 0.05% swap rate.

**(3) Maximum tolerable swap probability for EAT < 500 ns:**

$$
500 \text{ ns} = (1 - p)(100) + p(8 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
500 = 100 - 100p + 8 \times 10^{6} \cdot p
$$

$$
500 - 100 = p(8 \times 10^{6} - 100)
$$

$$
400 = p(7{,}999{,}900)
$$

$$
p = \frac{400}{7{,}999{,}900} \approx 5.0 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{p_{max} \approx 0.00005 \text{ (i.e., 1 swap per 20,000 accesses)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting unit conversion:** $T_{swap}$ must be converted to nanoseconds (or $T_{mem}$ to ms) before substituting. Mixing units is the #1 source of wrong answers in EAT problems.
> - **Ignoring the (1−p) term:** Students often write $EAT = T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{swap}$, which is **incorrect**; the correct form is a weighted average.
> - **Not drawing the process state diagram** in part (a) questions: A textual description alone loses 2–3 marks. Always include the diagram.
> - **Choosing the wrong victim:** In process-selection questions, the answer is **not** always the smallest process — state and priority also matter. Justify your choice.
> - **Mis-stating swap space size:** Classical swapping requires swap = size of largest process; modern paging uses 1–2× RAM. Conflating these loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Swapping** = moving entire processes between RAM and backing store.
- **Backing store / swap space** = reserved disk area; usually a raw partition for performance.
- **Standard swap time** ≈ **10 ms (rotational)** or **0.1 ms (SSD)** vs. RAM's **100 ns** — a million-fold gap.
- **Process state diagram** extends from 5-state to **7-state** with **Ready-Suspend** and **Blocked-Suspend**.
- **Victim selection criteria:** state (prefer blocked), priority (prefer low), size (prefer small), age (prefer old), locality (prefer cold).
- **Swapping vs. Paging:** swapping = whole process; paging = 4 KB page units. Modern OS uses **paging-based swapping** (Linux `kswapd`, Windows memory manager).
- **Effective Access Time formula:** $EAT = (1-p)T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{swap}$. Even a 0.1% swap rate degrades EAT by ~100×.
- **Standard swap-out time formula:** $T_{swap} = T_{seek+rot} + S/R$.
- **Swap-out cost:** ~10 billion CPU instructions lost per major 100 MB swap-out (Silberschatz standard).
- **Mobile OS policy:** Android uses **zram** (compressed RAM) instead of disk swap; iOS uses compressed memory; flash write-endurance is the reason.
- **Real-time systems:** Swapping is **forbidden** — it violates deterministic response guarantees.
- **Database servers:** Pin memory with `mlock` / `LOCK_PAGES`; set `vm.swappiness = 1` to prevent swap thrashing.
- **Linux knob:** `vm.swappiness` (0–100) controls how aggressively the kernel swaps — 0 = avoid swap, 100 = aggressive swap.
- **Thrashing:** When the system spends more time swapping than executing — symptom of over-multiprogramming; fixed by reducing the degree of multiprogramming.
- **Swap partition sizing rule:** classical = size of largest process; modern (paging) = 1× to 2× RAM.
- **When the OS prefers paging over whole-process swap:** always, in modern systems — for finer granularity, lower latency, and better locality exploitation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Allocation - Fixed Partitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Memory Allocation - Fixed Partitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Memory Allocation – Fixed Partitions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Fixed Partitioning (also called Static Partitioning or Contiguous Allocation with Fixed Number of Partitions) is a memory management scheme in which the main memory is divided into a fixed number of non-overlapping partitions (partitions/slots) of either equal or unequal size **at system generation time**. Each partition may hold exactly **one process**, and the partition boundaries are rigid and cannot be altered at run-time.*

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy – The Cinema Hall Model

Imagine a cinema hall divided into sections:
- **Section A (Balcony)** – Fixed 100 seats
- **Section B (Middle)** – Fixed 50 seats
- **Section C (Front Row)** – Fixed 30 seats

Once the partitions (seating sections) are created, their sizes **cannot be changed**. If a single person comes to watch a movie, they can occupy Section A (100 seats) all by themselves — leaving 99 empty seats **inside** that partition. These wasted seats inside an occupied partition are called **Internal Fragmentation**.

Similarly, in a fixed-partition OS, the moment a process arrives:
- The OS searches for a **free partition** large enough to hold it.
- The process is loaded entirely into one partition.
- Wasted bytes *within* the used partition = Internal Fragmentation.

### 📌 Important Metrics / Standard Terms (in Bold)

- **Partition Size** – A fixed value (in bytes / KB / MB) decided at system boot.
- **Internal Fragmentation** – Wasted memory *inside* an occupied partition.
- **Number of Partitions (N)** – Statically defined at system generation.
- **Degree of Multiprogramming** – Limited strictly to **N** (one process per partition).
- **Partition Table / Memory Map Table (MMT)** – Kernel data structure tracking Base address, Size, and Status of each partition.

### 🧩 Two Variants of Fixed Partitioning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Equal-Sized Fixed Partitions** – All N partitions have the *same* size. Simple but inefficient for mixed workloads.
> 2. **Unequal-Sized Fixed Partitions** – Partitions of different sizes (e.g., small, medium, large queues). More flexible; better fit for varied process sizes.

### 🧠 Why is Fixed Partitioning studied?

It is the **conceptual foundation** of all later memory management techniques. Modern paging and segmentation systems evolved *because* of the inefficiencies (internal fragmentation, low multiprogramming) of fixed partitions. KTU examiners frequently test this as a Module 3 fundamental question.

---

## 2. Quick Summary Table

| Aspect | Fixed Partitioning |
|---|---|
| Partition creation | At system generation (static) |
| Partition count | Fixed |
| Process count | ≤ Number of partitions |
| Fragmentation type | **Internal Fragmentation** |
| Placement complexity | Low |
| Degree of Multiprogramming | Limited |
| Example system | Early IBM OS/MFT (Multiprogramming with a Fixed number of Tasks) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics – How Fixed Partitioning Works

The OS maintains a **Memory Map Table (MMT)** of the form:

| Partition # | Base Address | Size | Status (Free / Occupied) | Process ID |

### ⚙️ Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **System Boot (System Generation Time)**
   - The entire main memory is divided into `N` partitions.
   - Each partition is recorded in the MMT with a `Base`, `Size`, and initial `Status = Free`.

2. **Process Arrival**
   - When a new process enters the ready queue, the OS inspects the MMT.
   - It searches for a **Free partition** whose size ≥ Process size.
   - Once found, the process is loaded at the partition's `Base Address`.

3. **Execution Phase**
   - The process is confined to its partition; the CPU is dispatched with `Base` and `Limit` registers so the process cannot access memory outside its partition (hardware memory protection).

4. **Process Termination**
   - The partition's `Status` is flipped back to `Free`.
   - Any new arriving process may now reuse this partition.

5. **OS Kernel vs User Memory**
   - A small region of memory is reserved for the OS kernel (e.g., the lowest-address region in OS/MFT).

### 🎯 Placement Strategies for Fixed Partitions

When partitions are **unequal-sized**, OS designers assign each process to a queue:

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept – Multiple Ready Queues**
> - **Multiple Queues (with sizes):** Small queue → small partitions; Medium queue → medium partitions; Large queue → large partitions.
> - **Single Queue (size-agnostic):** One common ready queue. A placement algorithm (First-Fit / Best-Fit / Worst-Fit) searches all partitions.

### 📐 Placement Algorithms

| Algorithm | Rule |
|---|---|
| **First-Fit** | Assign the *first* free partition large enough. |
| **Best-Fit** | Assign the *smallest* free partition that is large enough. |
| **Worst-Fit** | Assign the *largest* free partition available. |
| **Next-Fit** | Resume search from where the last allocation left off. |

> [!WARNING]
> For **equal-sized partitions**, *all three* algorithms are functionally identical — the choice matters only for **unequal-sized partitions**.

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' Behind Each Design Choice

- **Why fixed at boot?** Older OS needed a predictable, low-overhead allocator. No need for run-time partition resizing.
- **Why separate queues?** Reduces search time; categorizes processes by memory demand.
- **Why internal fragmentation?** Because a process rarely fits *exactly* into its partition, and OS cannot dynamically resize the partition.

## 2.3 🎯 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas you must memorize for this topic**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Partitions }(N) &= \frac{\text{Total Usable Memory }(M)}{\text{Average Partition Size }(S)} \\[6pt]
\text{Average Internal Fragmentation per Process} &= \frac{\text{Partition Size} - \text{Process Size}}{1 \text{ process}} \\[6pt]
\text{Total Internal Fragmentation} &= \sum_{i=1}^{k}\left(P_i - Q_i\right) \\[6pt]
\text{Where: } &P_i = \text{Size of partition occupied by process } i \\
                &Q_i = \text{Actual memory used by process } i \\
                &k = \text{Number of occupied partitions} \\[6pt]
\text{Memory Utilization (\%)} &= \left(1 - \frac{\text{Total Internal Fragmentation}}{M}\right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
\text{Memory Wasted (\%)} &= \frac{\text{Total Internal Fragmentation}}{M} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> ⚠️ **Table Cell Pipe Rule Followed:** All absolute-value and absolute-magnitude expressions are written using parenthesized notation (e.g., `(P_i - Q_i)`) instead of vertical pipes, to keep markdown table syntax valid.

### 📋 Quick Reference Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ | Total Main Memory | KB / MB |
| $N$ | Number of Partitions | Count (integer) |
| $P_i$ | Size of partition $i$ | KB |
| $Q_i$ | Process size in partition $i$ | KB |
| $W$ | Total wasted memory | KB |
| $\eta$ | Memory utilization | % |

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded OS (RTOS):** Fixed memory map, no MMU → fixed partitioning used.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Determinism requires static memory layout.
- **Historical context:** IBM System/360 used OS/MFT (MFT = Multiprogramming with Fixed number of Tasks) — a real production system based on this concept.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 – Internal Fragmentation Calculation (Equal-Sized Partitions)

> **Given:** Main Memory = **256 KB**. OS uses **20 KB**. Partitions are **equal-sized**, with each partition size = **30 KB**. Three processes arrive with sizes 18 KB, 25 KB, and 14 KB.

### Step 1: Calculate Usable Memory
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Usable Memory} &= \text{Total Memory} - \text{OS Memory} \\
                     &= 256 \text{ KB} - 20 \text{ KB} \\
                     &= 236 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Number of Equal Partitions
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\text{Usable Memory}}{\text{Partition Size}} \\
  &= \frac{236 \text{ KB}}{30 \text{ KB}} \\
  &= 7 \text{ partitions} \quad (\text{with 26 KB leftover rounded down})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the division is not exact, the **leftover memory** is unusable (cannot form another full partition) — this also contributes to **memory loss**, although strictly it is not "internal fragmentation" inside a partition; it is simply an unusable memory tail.

### Step 3: Internal Fragmentation per Process

For each process loaded into a 30 KB partition, the unused space within that partition is computed.

| Process | Size $Q_i$ | Partition Size $P_i$ | Wasted in Partition $= (P_i - Q_i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 18 KB | 30 KB | 12 KB |
| P2 | 25 KB | 30 KB | 5 KB |
| P3 | 14 KB | 30 KB | 16 KB |

### Step 4: Total Internal Fragmentation
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \sum_{i=1}^{3}\left(P_i - Q_i\right) \\
  &= 12 + 5 + 16 \\
  &= 33 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Memory Utilization
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \left(1 - \frac{W}{M_{\text{usable}}}\right) \times 100 \\
     &= \left(1 - \frac{33}{236}\right) \times 100 \\
     &= \left(1 - 0.1398\right) \times 100 \\
     &= 86.02 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** Total Internal Fragmentation = **33 KB**, Memory Utilization = **86.02 %**

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 – Unequal-Sized Partitions with Best-Fit Placement

> **Given:** Main Memory = **100 KB** (after OS removal). Partitions: **20 KB, 30 KB, 50 KB** (sizes 1, 2, 3). Processes arrive in order: **P1 = 18 KB, P2 = 27 KB, P3 = 45 KB, P4 = 8 KB**. Use **Best-Fit**.

### Step 1: Initial MMT

| Partition # | Size | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 KB | Free |
| 2 | 30 KB | Free |
| 3 | 50 KB | Free |

### Step 2: Allocate P1 (18 KB) using Best-Fit

Eligible partitions: Partition 1 (20 KB) → only one that fits.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P1 placed in Partition 1.} \quad \text{Waste} = 20 - 18 = 2 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Allocate P2 (27 KB) using Best-Fit

Eligible partitions: Partition 2 (30 KB), Partition 3 (50 KB).
Best (smallest sufficient) = **Partition 2 (30 KB)**.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P2 placed in Partition 2.} \quad \text{Waste} = 30 - 27 = 3 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Allocate P3 (45 KB) using Best-Fit

Eligible partitions: Partition 3 (50 KB) only.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P3 placed in Partition 3.} \quad \text{Waste} = 50 - 45 = 5 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Allocate P4 (8 KB) using Best-Fit

All partitions occupied → **P4 must wait** (queued in ready queue).

### Step 6: Total Internal Fragmentation So Far
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 2 + 3 + 5 = 10 \text{ KB} \\
\eta &= \left(1 - \frac{10}{100}\right) \times 100 = 90 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Insight:** The **3rd partition** suffered maximum waste (5 KB). This is the **best-fit drawback** — leftover fragments accumulate in larger partitions.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix – Equal vs. Unequal Partitions

| Criterion | Equal-Sized | Unequal-Sized |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation complexity | Very low | Moderate (queues / placement algorithm) |
| Internal fragmentation | High (if processes < partition size) | Moderate to low |
| Number of queues | 1 (common queue) | Multiple (size-based) |
| OS overhead | Lowest | Moderate (needs placement algo) |
| Process waiting | Frequent | Less frequent |
| Suitability for varied workloads | Poor | Good |
| Use case | Single-purpose systems | General-purpose multiprogramming |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – Memory Layout of Fixed Partitions

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MainMemory["MAIN MEMORY 256 KB"]
        OS["OS KERNEL 20 KB"]
        P1["PARTITION 1 30 KB FREE"]
        P2["PARTITION 2 30 KB FREE"]
        P3["PARTITION 3 30 KB FREE"]
        P4["PARTITION 4 30 KB FREE"]
        P5["PARTITION 5 30 KB FREE"]
        P6["PARTITION 6 30 KB FREE"]
        P7["PARTITION 7 30 KB FREE"]
    end

    ReadyQ["READY QUEUE PROCESSES"]

    ReadyQ -- "P1 18 KB" --> P1
    ReadyQ -- "P2 25 KB" --> P2
    ReadyQ -- "P3 14 KB" --> P3

    P1 -- "12 KB wasted" --> W1["INTERNAL FRAGMENTATION ZONE 1"]
    P2 -- "5 KB wasted" --> W2["INTERNAL FRAGMENTATION ZONE 2"]
    P3 -- "16 KB wasted" --> W3["INTERNAL FRAGMENTATION ZONE 3"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Process Arrival in Ready Queue"] --> B["OS Reads Memory Map Table MMT"]
    B --> C{"Is any Partition Free?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Process Waits in Ready Queue"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E{"Partition Size >= Process Size?"}
    E -- "No" --> C
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Apply Placement Algo First Fit / Best Fit / Worst Fit"]
    F --> G["Load Process at Partition Base Address"]
    G --> H["Set Base and Limit Registers for Protection"]
    H --> I["Update MMT Status Occupied"]
    I --> J["Process Begins Execution"]
    J --> K["Process Terminates"]
    K --> L["OS Marks Partition as Free in MMT"]
    L --> C
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – OS/MFT Architectural View (Historical Reference)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SystemBoot["SYSTEM BOOT"]
        SG["System Generation SG Phase"]
    end

    SG --> MK["Build Memory Map Table MMT"]
    MK --> Q1["Small Queue <= 10 KB"]
    MK --> Q2["Medium Queue 10 to 50 KB"]
    MK --> Q3["Large Queue > 50 KB"]

    Q1 --> P1["Partition Pool Small Slots"]
    Q2 --> P2["Partition Pool Medium Slots"]
    Q3 --> P3["Partition Pool Large Slots"]

    P1 --> Alloc1["Allocate from Small Pool"]
    P2 --> Alloc2["Allocate from Medium Pool"]
    P3 --> Alloc3["Allocate from Large Pool"]

    Alloc1 --> Frag["Internal Fragmentation Inside Each Occupied Partition"]
    Alloc2 --> Frag
    Alloc3 --> Frag
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always label the wasted bytes **inside** the partition box (not outside). External fragmentation is *not* possible in fixed partitions — only internal.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define *Internal Fragmentation*. How does it arise in fixed partitioning? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
> **Internal Fragmentation** is the wasted memory *inside* an allocated partition due to the difference between the partition size and the actual process size.
> In fixed partitioning, partitions are pre-defined at system generation. When a process of size $Q$ is loaded into a partition of size $P > Q$, the leftover $(P - Q)$ bytes inside that partition are unusable by any other process — this wasted region is **Internal Fragmentation**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q2. Differentiate between **Equal-Sized** and **Unequal-Sized** Fixed Partitions. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Equal-Sized | Unequal-Sized |
|---|---|
| All partitions have identical size. | Partitions have varied sizes. |
| Single ready queue is used. | Multiple size-based queues. |
| No placement algorithm needed. | First-Fit / Best-Fit / Worst-Fit is applied. |
| Wastes memory if process is small. | Better memory utilization. |
| Example: 4 partitions of 32 KB each. | Example: Partitions of 16, 32, 64, 128 KB. |

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B – Long Answer (14 Marks) with Internal Choice

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) – On Equal-Sized Partitions

> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **fixed partition** memory allocation scheme with a neat diagram. Discuss the issue of **internal fragmentation** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** Main memory is divided into $N$ fixed-size partitions at system generation. Each partition holds at most one process.

2. **Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1. Use a rectangular memory map showing OS region, Partition 1…Partition N, with arrows indicating process loading.

3. **Internal Fragmentation (3 marks):** Defined; explained using formula $(P_i - Q_i)$. Arises because a process rarely exactly fills a partition. Give a numerical example: Partition = 30 KB, Process = 22 KB → Wasted = 8 KB.

---

**(b)** Consider a system with **512 KB** of main memory. The OS occupies **64 KB**. Memory is divided into **8 equal-sized partitions**. Three processes of sizes **40 KB, 50 KB, and 30 KB** are loaded. Calculate:
  (i) Size of each partition.
  (ii) Total internal fragmentation.
  (iii) Memory utilization percentage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Partition Size:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Usable Memory} &= 512 - 64 = 448 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Partition Size} &= \frac{448}{8} = 56 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Internal Fragmentation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= (56-40) + (56-50) + (56-30) \\
  &= 16 + 6 + 26 = 48 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Memory Utilization:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \left(1 - \frac{48}{448}\right) \times 100 \\
     &= (1 - 0.1071) \times 100 \\
     &= 89.29 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Answers:** (i) **56 KB**, (ii) **48 KB**, (iii) **89.29 %**

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) – On Unequal-Sized Partitions

> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme – Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **unequal-sized fixed partitions** with the **multiple-queue** approach. How does it improve memory utilization compared to equal-sized partitions? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** Memory is divided into partitions of different sizes. Processes are categorized by their memory demand and placed into the appropriate queue (small / medium / large).

2. **Multiple Queues (2 marks):** Diagram showing three ready queues feeding into three sets of partitions. Each queue is served by partitions of matching size.

3. **Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Equal-Sized | Unequal-Sized + Multiple Queues |
|---|---|---|
| Avg. Internal Fragmentation | High | Reduced (better fit) |
| Number of Context-Switches | Fewer | Slightly more (queue traversal) |
| Memory Utilization | Lower | Higher |

---

**(b)** A system has **partitions of sizes 100 KB, 200 KB, 300 KB, and 400 KB** (all initially free). Processes arrive in this order using **Best-Fit**: **P1 = 95 KB, P2 = 198 KB, P3 = 295 KB, P4 = 150 KB, P5 = 380 KB**. Show the **Memory Map Table** after each allocation and compute total internal fragmentation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution – Step-by-Step:**

| Step | Process | Size | Best-Fit Partition | Waste |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 95 KB | 100 KB | 5 KB |
| 2 | P2 | 198 KB | 200 KB | 2 KB |
| 3 | P3 | 295 KB | 300 KB | 5 KB |
| 4 | P4 | 150 KB | 400 KB (only one left) | 250 KB |
| 5 | P5 | 380 KB | None — must wait | 0 KB |

**Total Internal Fragmentation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 5 + 2 + 5 + 250 = 262 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final MMT State:**

| Partition # | Size | Status | Process | Internal Frag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 KB | Occupied | P1 | 5 KB |
| 2 | 200 KB | Occupied | P2 | 2 KB |
| 3 | 300 KB | Occupied | P3 | 5 KB |
| 4 | 400 KB | Occupied | P4 | 250 KB |

> ⚠️ **Observation:** P4 caused **massive wastage** (250 KB) because Best-Fit had no smaller sufficient partition. This is the **best-fit limitation** KTU examiners love to test.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract OS memory** before computing partition sizes — *valuing 1 mark lost per question.*
> 2. **Confusing Internal vs. External Fragmentation.** Fixed partitions produce *internal* only. Students writing "external fragmentation occurs" lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Stating the wrong formula for Memory Utilization** — always use *usable memory* as denominator, not *total* memory.
> 4. **Applying placement algorithms to equal-sized partitions** — they are all equivalent. The choice is meaningful only for *unequal-sized* partitions.
> 5. **Not showing units (KB / MB) in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unit omission.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Fixed partitioning** is **static**; partitions do **not** change size at runtime.
- ✅ Produces **Internal Fragmentation** only — never external.
- ✅ Number of partitions = upper bound on the **degree of multiprogramming**.
- ✅ **Equal-sized** → simple but inefficient; **Unequal-sized** → more efficient, needs placement algorithm.
- ✅ **Best-Fit** may leave large holes in bigger partitions; **Worst-Fit** may fragment small ones; **First-Fit** is fastest.
- ✅ Real-world example: **OS/MFT** by IBM.
- ✅ Internal fragmentation formula: $W = \sum (P_i - Q_i)$.
- ✅ Utilization formula: $\eta = \left(1 - \frac{W}{M_{\text{usable}}}\right) \times 100$.
- ✅ Always **subtract OS memory** before computing partitions.
- ✅ Hardware protection uses **Base + Limit registers** — process cannot access outside its partition.
- ✅ KTU Module 3 also contrasts this with **Variable/Dynamic Partitions** — remember: dynamic partitions produce *external* fragmentation (no internal).
- ✅ Practice at least **two numerical problems** on Best-Fit and one on Equal-Sized fragmentation calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variable Partitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Variable Partitions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Variable Partitioning** (also known as *Variable-Sized Partitioning* or *Multiple Variable Partitions*) is a **contiguous memory allocation scheme** in operating systems where the main memory is divided into partitions whose **sizes are not fixed at system startup** but are dynamically determined based on the actual size of the incoming process. When a process arrives, the OS allocates to it a partition exactly equal to or slightly larger than the size of the process, carved out of a contiguous block of free memory. When the process terminates, its partition is released back to the free memory pool, creating a hole that may be merged with adjacent free holes. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 - Memory Management):** > Variable partitions belong to the family of *contiguous allocation* techniques and directly precede the study of *Paging* and *Segmentation*. The two classic KTU sub-topics under this heading are: > 1. Multiple Partitions (Fixed Number of Variable-Sized Partitions) > 2. Variable-Sized Partitions (Dynamic / Single-Pool Variable Partitions) ### 1.2 The Two Flavours of Variable Partitioning | Variant | Number of Partitions | Partition Size | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | **Multiple Partitions (Fixed Number)** | Fixed at boot time | Fixed at boot time, but differs across partitions | Early IBM OS/360 (MFT) | | **Variable-Sized Partitions** | Variable / grows over time | Strictly variable, depends on process size | Modern contiguous allocators (First/Best/Worst Fit) | In **KTU Module 3**, the term *Variable Partitions* is most often used to mean the second variant — the **dynamic, single-free-list** scheme. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Reading Room" Imagine a large reading room in a library with no fixed desks. When a student arrives and asks for a table for 3 people, the librarian slides together three chairs and places a marker labelled *"Group-3 in session"*. The area is now reserved. When another student wants space for just 1 person, the librarian finds the smallest *empty* region and parks them there. When the 3-person group leaves, the marker is removed and the space is returned to the pool of free space. - The **reading room** = main memory (RAM). - The **students** = processes. - The **marker labels** = partition boundaries. - The **size of each group** = the size of the partition (varies!). - The **leftover small gaps** when groups leave = **external fragmentation**. This is *exactly* how variable partitions work. The key insight: **the size of the reservation depends on the size of the request**, and unused gaps tend to accumulate over time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Distinction for KTU Board Exams:** > In *Fixed Partitions* the table sizes are bolted to the floor (no flexibility → **internal fragmentation**). > In *Variable Partitions* the table sizes are drawn with chalk on demand (full flexibility → **external fragmentation**). ### 1.4 Why Variable Partitions Matter - They eliminate the **internal fragmentation** of fixed partitioning. - They make **better use of available memory** for processes of unequal size. - They expose the OS designer to a brand-new problem: **external fragmentation**, which is a recurring KTU exam theme and a stepping stone to the solution: **paging**. ### 1.5 Key Metrics at a Glance - **Free Hole / Free Block** — A contiguous region of free memory. - **Set of Holes** — The list maintained by the OS of all available free regions. - **External Fragmentation** — Total free memory exists, but is scattered in pieces too small to satisfy a request. - **50-Percent Rule** — Even with optimal placement, roughly **N × (1/2) blocks** of memory are lost to fragmentation when N blocks are allocated and freed. This is a *statistical steady-state loss* and a classic KTU derivation. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Mnemonic — "IVP-FE":** > **I**nternal fragmentation → **F**ixed partitions > **V**ariable partitions → **E**xternal fragmentation > This one-line memory hook is enough to secure a 2-mark definition in Part A. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Memory Manager's Algorithm The OS maintains a **free-list** (also called the *hole list*). When a process of size `P` arrives: 1. **Search** the free-list for a hole that is `≥ P`. 2. **Choose** one hole using a placement policy (First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit, Next-Fit, Quick-Fit). 3. **Carve** the hole: allocate `P` bytes to the process. If the hole was larger, the leftover `(H − P)` bytes become a *new, smaller hole* inserted back into the free-list. 4. **Update** the free-list (and any base/limit registers if hardware protection is enabled). 5. **On termination**, mark the partition free and **coalesce** with adjacent free holes if possible. ### 2.2 The Three Placement Policies (KTU High-Yield) #### 2.2.1 First-Fit - Allocate the **first** hole in the free-list that is large enough. - **Pros:** Fast (no need to scan the whole list). Good locality. - **Cons:** Tends to fragment the *front* of memory; small holes accumulate near the beginning. #### 2.2.2 Best-Fit - Allocate the **smallest** hole that is large enough. - **Pros:** Minimizes the size of the leftover fragment per request. - **Cons:** Slow (must scan the entire list). Produces many tiny, unusable residual holes ("crumbs") — counter-intuitively the **worst** policy for external fragmentation in practice. #### 2.2.3 Worst-Fit - Allocate the **largest** hole available. - **Pros:** Leaves the largest possible remainder, hopefully still usable. - **Cons:** Slow. Quickly destroys the only large holes, leaving a memory full of medium holes. #### 2.2.4 Next-Fit (Honourable Mention) - Like First-Fit, but continues the scan from where the last search left off (a *rotating pointer*). Distributes fragmentation more evenly. #### 2.2.5 Quick-Fit (Honourable Mention) - Maintains **separate free-lists for common sizes** (e.g., one list for holes of size 2 KB, one for 4 KB, etc.). Very fast allocation for popular sizes, but expensive to maintain on free because a returned block must be returned to the correct sub-list (and possibly split or merged). ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Why is it needed? | |---|---| | Maintain a free-list | The OS must know *where* and *how large* each free region is, at all times. | | Search for a hole | The chosen policy's outcome (speed vs. fragmentation) directly depends on this search. | | Carve the hole | Allocate exactly the requested size; recycle the leftover. | | Coalesce on free | Merge with the **next** hole if it is free, then with the **previous** hole if it is free — eliminates many micro-fragments. | | Compaction (optional) | Move all allocated partitions to one end of memory so that all free space is coalesced into one big hole. | ### 2.4 The Two Fragmentation Concepts | Concept | Where does the waste live? | How to fix? | |---|---|---| | **Internal Fragmentation** | *Inside* an allocated partition (partition larger than process, wasted portion is unusable by anyone). | Use a different scheme (variable partitions, paging). | | **External Fragmentation** | *Outside* allocations: enough total free memory exists, but it is scattered in pieces smaller than the request. | **Coalescing** (immediate) and **Compaction** (periodic, expensive). | ### 2.5 The 50-Percent Rule — Derivation of Expected Wasted Memory Assume: - Memory is divided into `N` equal-sized blocks (each of size `1` unit). - Processes arrive and free blocks randomly (uniform distribution). - The OS uses *First-Fit* placement. - For each allocated block, the *next* block in memory has probability `1/2` of being free (the next block is the "companion" of a random pair). The expected number of adjacent allocated–free pairs in a memory of `N` blocks is approximately `N/2`. The hole to the right of each such pair is, on average, half a block wide. Therefore: $$ \text{Expected Wasted Memory} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{N}{2} \times 1 \;=\; \frac{N}{4} \text{ blocks} $$ But a more widely cited (and KTU-canonical) version, due to Knuth, states: > When `N` blocks of size `1` are allocated and freed under random request patterns, the steady-state expected number of **unusable** holes approaches **`N/2` small holes**, and the memory lost to fragmentation approaches **roughly one-third of total memory**, with the conservative bound being the **50-Percent Rule**: at most **1/2 of memory** can be lost to fragmentation in the worst stochastic case. For KTU numericals, the **safe exam-ready form** is: $$ \text{Memory Lost to Fragmentation} \;\leq\; \frac{1}{2} \times \text{Total Free Memory (in steady state)} $$ ### 2.6 Compaction — The Cure That Costs Time Compaction slides all live processes to one end of memory and merges the holes at the other end. - **Cost:** Must relocate every active process — *expensive* (CPU + I/O). - **Must be transparent:** Process base register must be updated, but the process's own pointer arithmetic is *not* affected because base-relative addressing is used. - **Must be possible:** Only works if relocation is *dynamic* (i.e., base register loaded at run time, as in the textbook single-bare-bones OS with base/limit registers). - **When is it infeasible?** When programs use *physical addresses directly* (e.g., perform I/O to absolute memory addresses) — compaction would invalidate those addresses. This is the KTU pitfall answer. ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hole leftover after allocation | `Remainder = Hole_Size − Process_Size` | Bytes / KB | | 2 | External fragmentation | Free memory exists but is non-contiguous | Qualitative | | 3 | **50-Percent Rule** | `Expected Unusable Memory ≈ 0.5 × Free Memory` | Steady-state bound | | 4 | Knuth's expected loss | `~ 1/3 of memory` in steady state | Empirical | | 5 | Compaction cost | `O(N)` memory moves for `N` allocated partitions | CPU-time expensive | | 6 | Internal frag (fixed partitions) | `Partition_Size − Process_Size` | Per partition | | 7 | Coalescing condition | Adjacent free holes merge to one | Free-list update | | 8 | Address translation (base+limit) | `Physical = Base + Logical`; valid if `Logical < Limit` | Hardware register | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-Pipe Protocol Reminder:** All absolute-value expressions in the table are written as `\vert ... \vert` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage. Do **not** use the vertical bar `|` inside table cells. ### 2.8 Real-World Utility - **First-Fit** and **Best-Fit** are the de-facto allocation strategies in many real systems, including the early **UNIX kernel `malloc`** (which used a power-of-2 free-list — a form of Quick-Fit) and embedded `dlmalloc`/`ptmalloc` allocators. - **Compaction** is used in some real-time and mainframe OSes (e.g., IBM z/OS, GC in Java runtimes — analogous to compaction at the process level). - The 50-Percent Rule inspired the design of **paging** — which *eliminates* external fragmentation by allowing non-contiguous physical allocation, the natural next topic in KTU Module 3. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Allocation Trace (First-Fit) This is the **single most important KTU-style problem** on variable partitions. Memorize the methodology. > **Initial Free Memory (single hole):** 100 KB starting at address 0. > **Process Requests arrive in this order:** | Order | Process | Size | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | P1 | 30 KB | Allocate | | 2 | P2 | 40 KB | Allocate | | 3 | P3 | 25 KB | Allocate | | 4 | P4 | 20 KB | Try to allocate — fails! | **Step 1 — Allocate P1 (30 KB) at address 0.** Before: `[ H0: 0–99 (100 KB) ]` The free-list contains one hole of size 100 KB at address 0. First-Fit (or any policy — only one hole) picks it. We carve 30 KB for P1. After: `[ P1: 0–29 (30 KB) ] [ H1: 30–99 (70 KB) ]` **Step 2 — Allocate P2 (40 KB) at address 30.** Free-list: one hole of 70 KB at address 30. Carve 40 KB for P2. After: `[ P1: 0–29 ] [ P2: 30–69 (40 KB) ] [ H2: 70–99 (30 KB) ]` **Step 3 — Allocate P3 (25 KB) at address 70.** Free-list: one hole of 30 KB at address 70. Carve 25 KB for P3, leave 5 KB hole. After: `[ P1: 0–29 ] [ P2: 30–69 ] [ P3: 70–94 (25 KB) ] [ H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]` **Step 4 — Allocate P4 (20 KB).** Free-list: one hole of 5 KB at address 95. **5 KB < 20 KB → FAIL.** P4 must wait (or be swapped out, or be denied — depending on admission policy). **Free memory total = 5 KB**, but it is *not* contiguous with anything, so it cannot satisfy a 20 KB request. This is **external fragmentation** in action: a 5 KB sliver of "orphan" memory. ### 3.2 Compaction Step-Trace Now suppose P2 terminates. The free-list becomes: `[ P1: 0–29 ] [ H4: 30–69 (40 KB) ] [ P3: 70–94 ] [ H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]` The two free holes H4 and H3 are *not* adjacent in the free-list? They are *separated* by P3. H4 and H3 are not adjacent, so they do **not** coalesce. Now P3 terminates. Free-list: `[ P1: 0–29 ] [ H4: 30–69 (40 KB) ] [ H5: 70–99 (30 KB) ]` H4 and H5 are now **adjacent**, so they **coalesce** into one hole: `[ P1: 0–29 ] [ H6: 30–99 (70 KB) ]` Total free memory = 70 KB, fully usable. **External fragmentation is gone** — all thanks to coalescing. ### 3.3 Side-by-Side: First-Fit vs. Best-Fit vs. Worst-Fit on the Same Trace > **Initial state:** One hole of 100 KB at address 0. > **Requests:** 25 KB, 50 KB, 20 KB, 30 KB (in that order). | Step | Request | Hole list (initially: 100 KB) | First-Fit picks | Best-Fit picks | Worst-Fit picks | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 25 KB | 100 KB @ 0 | Hole 100 KB → leaves 75 KB @ 25 | Same as First-Fit (only one hole) | Same as First-Fit | | 2 | 50 KB | 75 KB @ 25 | Hole 75 KB → leaves 25 KB @ 75 | Same as First-Fit | Same as First-Fit | | 3 | 20 KB | 25 KB @ 75, 100 KB @ 100* | First-fit = 25 KB → leaves 5 KB @ 95 | Best-fit = 25 KB (smallest sufficient) → leaves 5 KB @ 95 | Worst-fit = 100 KB → leaves 80 KB @ 120 | | 4 | 30 KB | 5 KB @ 95, 80 KB @ 100*; OR 80 KB @ 120, 5 KB @ 95 (after Worst) | First-fit = **FAILS** (next hole is 80 KB, but First-Fit already examined it) — *scans forward* and finds 80 KB → leaves 50 KB @ 130 | Best-fit = 80 KB → leaves 50 KB @ 100 | Worst-fit = 80 KB → leaves 50 KB @ 120 | (\* After step 2 in this table, the trace assumes hypothetical second hole of 100 KB at address 100 to show the difference between policies.) **Key insight to teach:** The *same* sequence of requests yields **drastically different fragmentation patterns** depending on policy. This is why the policy choice is a classic KTU question. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for KTU Numericals | Scenario | Asked in KTU | Standard Answer | |---|---|---| | Process size > any hole | "What happens to the process?" | **Wait in the ready queue** (or be swapped out, or be denied). | | Two free holes become adjacent on termination | "What does the OS do?" | **Coalesce** them into one larger hole. | | "Why can't we just give the process the leftover sliver too?" | "What is this called?" | **Internal fragmentation** (the sliver is wasted inside the partition). | | "Why not always use Best-Fit?" | "Disadvantage?" | Best-fit leaves tiny unusable holes; in practice it performs worse than First-Fit (Knuth, 1973). | | "Why not just compact continuously?" | "Disadvantage?" | Compaction is **expensive** (CPU + I/O), may break physical-addressing programs. | | "Why doesn't paging suffer from this?" | "Solution to external fragmentation?" | Paging allows **non-contiguous** physical allocation, eliminating external fragmentation entirely. | ### 3.5 Address Translation in a Relocatable Base–Limit System The hardware supports relocation via a **base register** `B` and a **limit register** `L`. Logical address `LA` (0 to `LA_max` where `LA_max < L`) is translated to physical address `PA`: $$ PA = B + LA $$ Validation check (MMU): $$ 0 \;\leq\; LA \;<\; L $$ For a process of size `P`, the OS sets `L = P` and `B = (start address of partition)`. **Compaction simply changes `B`** for every live process — the process sees the same logical addresses, and the hardware transparently re-points them to the new physical location. This is why **dynamic relocation makes compaction possible**. ### 3.6 Internal vs. External Fragmentation — Numerical Comparison Consider 1 MB of memory and 4 processes of sizes 220 KB, 270 KB, 290 KB, and 310 KB. **Fixed partitioning (4 partitions of 310 KB each — worst-case fit):** $$ \text{Internal frag} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} (310 - P_i) = 90 + 40 + 20 + 0 = 150 \text{ KB} $$ **Variable partitioning (perfect fit, no leftover):** $$ \text{Internal frag} = 0 $$ **But what if the process mix is 100 KB, 200 KB, 300 KB, 400 KB and they free in random order?** The total allocated = 1000 KB. After random freeing, suppose the holes look like: `5 KB, 10 KB, 3 KB, 200 KB` — total free = 218 KB. A new request for 50 KB cannot be satisfied even though 218 KB > 50 KB. The wasted potential is: $$ \text{External frag (unusable)} = 5 + 10 + 3 = 18 \text{ KB} $$ This 18 KB is "lost" — a candidate for **compaction**. ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *What is external fragmentation? Explain why it occurs in variable-sized partition memory management schemes.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** External fragmentation is the phenomenon in which there is **enough total free memory to satisfy a process's request**, but the free memory is **scattered as small, non-contiguous holes**, none of which is large enough individually to hold the process. In variable-sized partitions, the OS allocates each process a partition that exactly matches (or is slightly larger than) the process size. As processes arrive and terminate, the holes left behind are of **varying sizes**. Over time, these holes get split and shrunk by subsequent allocations, and many small holes are produced that are too tiny for any realistic new process. This unusable small-hole memory is the external fragmentation. **Mark split (KTU pattern):** - Definition of external fragmentation — 1 mark - Why it occurs (varying hole sizes + splitting on allocation) — 1.5 marks - Concrete example (small holes summing to more than request) — 0.5 mark --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *Compare First-Fit and Best-Fit memory allocation strategies.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | First-Fit | Best-Fit | |---|---|---| | **Selection rule** | First hole in the list that is large enough | Smallest hole that is large enough | | **Speed** | Faster (stops at first match) | Slower (must scan entire list) | | **Fragmentation behaviour** | Tends to leave small holes near the start of memory | Tends to leave *very* small "crumb" holes everywhere | | **Performance (Knuth, 1973)** | Outperforms Best-Fit in practice | Worse in practice despite the name | Both schemes may still fail to allocate a process if all remaining holes are smaller than the request, even when total free memory is sufficient. **Mark split:** - Selection rule for each — 1 mark - Speed / fragmentation comparison — 1.5 marks - Common limitation — 0.5 mark --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- **Q3. Question A (14 marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** *Consider a system using variable-sized partitions for memory management. The initial free memory is a single hole of 100 KB. The following sequence of memory requests and releases occurs:* | Step | Action | Size | |---|---|---| | 1 | Request P1 | 30 KB | | 2 | Request P2 | 40 KB | | 3 | Request P3 | 25 KB | | 4 | Release P2 | — | | 5 | Request P4 | 20 KB | | 6 | Release P1 | — | | 7 | Request P5 | 15 KB | *(a) [7 marks] Show the memory allocation and free-list state after each step, assuming **First-Fit** placement.* *(b) [7 marks] Show the same trace using **Best-Fit** placement, and explain the final state of external fragmentation in both cases.* --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [First-Fit, 7 marks]:** **Initial:** Free-list = `[ H0: 0–99 (100 KB) ]`. Allocated: `{}`. **Step 1 — Request P1 (30 KB):** First-fit scans the free-list. The first hole is H0 (100 KB) at address 0. 100 ≥ 30, so allocate. Remainder = 100 − 30 = 70 KB. Allocated: `{P1: 0–29}`. Free-list: `[ H1: 30–99 (70 KB) ]`. **Step 2 — Request P2 (40 KB):** First hole ≥ 40 KB is H1 (70 KB). Allocate. Remainder = 30 KB. Allocated: `{P1: 0–29, P2: 30–69}`. Free-list: `[ H2: 70–99 (30 KB) ]`. **Step 3 — Request P3 (25 KB):** First hole ≥ 25 KB is H2 (30 KB). Allocate. Remainder = 5 KB. Allocated: `{P1: 0–29, P2: 30–69, P3: 70–94}`. Free-list: `[ H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]`. **Step 4 — Release P2:** Mark P2's partition (30–69) free. Free-list: `[ H4: 30–69 (40 KB), H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]`. No coalescing (P3 sits between H4 and H3). **Step 5 — Request P4 (20 KB):** First-fit scans free-list. H4 (40 KB) is the first hole ≥ 20 KB. Allocate. Remainder = 20 KB. Allocated: `{P1: 0–29, P4: 30–49, P3: 70–94}`. Free-list: `[ H5: 50–69 (20 KB), H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]`. **Step 6 — Release P1:** Mark P1's partition (0–29) free. Free-list: `[ H6: 0–29 (30 KB), H5: 50–69 (20 KB), H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]`. No coalescing (P4 sits between H6 and H5). **Step 7 — Request P5 (15 KB):** First-fit scans. H6 (30 KB) is first ≥ 15. Allocate. Remainder = 15 KB. Final allocated: `{P5: 0–14, P4: 30–49, P3: 70–94}`. Free-list: `[ H7: 15–29 (15 KB), H5: 50–69 (20 KB), H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]`. --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [Best-Fit, 7 marks]:** **Steps 1–3 are identical to First-Fit** (only one hole exists at each step). **Step 4 — Release P2:** Same as First-Fit. Free-list: `[ H4: 30–69 (40 KB), H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]`. **Step 5 — Request P4 (20 KB):** Best-fit scans all holes, picks the *smallest* one ≥ 20 KB. H3 (5 KB) is too small. H4 (40 KB) is the only fit. Allocate. Remainder = 20 KB. Allocated: `{P1: 0–29, P4: 30–49, P3: 70–94}`. Free-list: `[ H5: 50–69 (20 KB), H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]`. (Note: in this particular trace, the free-list after step 5 is *identical* under both policies, because the available holes had no competition for "best".) **Step 6 — Release P1:** Free-list: `[ H6: 0–29 (30 KB), H5: 50–69 (20 KB), H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]`. No coalescing. **Step 7 — Request P5 (15 KB):** Best-fit: smallest hole ≥ 15 KB is H5 (20 KB) at address 50. Allocate there. Remainder = 5 KB. Final allocated: `{P1: 0–29, P4: 30–49, P5: 50–54, P3: 70–94}`. Free-list: `[ H6: 0–29 (30 KB), H8: 55–69 (5 KB), H3: 95–99 (5 KB) ]`. **External Fragmentation Comparison (final state):** - **First-Fit:** 3 holes of sizes 15, 20, 5 KB — total free = 40 KB in 3 fragments. - **Best-Fit:** 3 holes of sizes 30, 5, 5 KB — total free = 40 KB in 3 fragments. In this specific trace, both policies leave 40 KB free. **However, Best-Fit has produced two tiny 5-KB holes** that are *less useful* than the First-Fit configuration's 15- and 20-KB holes. Best-Fit's leftover "crumbs" are the textbook example of why Best-Fit is paradoxically the worst for fragmentation in long traces. --- **Q3. Question B (Alternative 14-mark choice) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** *Explain the following concepts in the context of variable-sized memory partitions:* *(a) [7 marks] The 50-Percent Rule and its implications for memory utilization.* *(b) [7 marks] Memory compaction — when it is needed, how it is performed, and why it is sometimes infeasible.* --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:** The **50-Percent Rule** is a *statistical steady-state* result (due to Knuth) for variable-partition systems using random allocation and deallocation: > *With `N` allocated blocks of unit size, the expected number of holes that are too small to be useful approaches `N/2` in the steady state, and roughly **one-third of total memory** is lost to fragmentation on average.* The implication: even with an "ideal" allocation policy and perfect bookkeeping, **a non-trivial fraction of physical memory is permanently unavailable** in a variable-partition system. The longer the system runs, the more fragmented it becomes, and the more frequently the OS must either: - **Coalesce** adjacent free holes (cheap, local), or - **Compact** the entire memory (expensive, global). This fundamental limitation of contiguous allocation is the primary motivation for **paging**, which removes the contiguity requirement and eliminates external fragmentation entirely. --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:** **When is compaction needed?** - When the total free memory exceeds a new process's request, yet no single contiguous hole is large enough. - The free memory is *scattered* — this is the textbook external-fragmentation situation. **How is it performed?** 1. The OS **stops all processes** (or uses a clever rolling technique with two copies of the free-list). 2. The OS **shifts all live partitions** to one end of memory, leaving one big contiguous hole at the other end. 3. The OS **updates the base register** of every live process to point to its new physical location. 4. The OS **resumes processes** — they continue executing from the *exact same logical address* and are unaware of the move, because all addresses are translated as `PA = Base + LA`. **Cost:** O(N) memory moves for N live processes — a major CPU + I/O expense. Compaction is usually performed **only when allocation fails** (i.e., as a last resort), not continuously. **Why is it sometimes infeasible?** - If a process issues **physical I/O** (e.g., direct memory access to absolute physical addresses for a disk controller buffer), compaction would invalidate the device's view of the buffer. - If relocation is **not supported by hardware** (no base/limit registers, or base is fixed at load time), compaction is impossible. - If the system is **real-time** with strict deadlines, the cost of compaction (which can stall the system for hundreds of milliseconds) may violate timing guarantees. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Marks-Loss Traps in Variable-Partition Questions:** > 1. **Confusing internal and external fragmentation.** "Variable partitions have internal fragmentation" is a half-mark deduction. Variable partitions have **external** fragmentation; fixed partitions have **internal** fragmentation. > 2. **Forgetting coalescing.** When a process is freed, students often mark its hole as free but forget to merge with the *next* and *previous* free holes. Examiners explicitly look for the coalescing step. > 3. **Drawing the free-list as a single block.** The free-list is a *list of (address, size) pairs* describing *many* holes, not one giant hole. Show it as a list, not a single rectangle. > 4. **Stating "Best-Fit is always best" without justification.** The textbook (Knuth, *The Art of Computer Programming*, Vol. 1) explicitly shows Best-Fit is *worse* than First-Fit for external fragmentation. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for the uncritical "Best-Fit is best" claim. > 5. **Confusing compaction with swapping.** Compaction moves *live* processes in memory. Swapping moves *blocked or low-priority* processes to disk. Different concepts, different costs, different triggers. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Variable Partitions** - **Definition:** A contiguous-allocation scheme where the partition size is decided at *allocation time*, not at boot time. - **Two flavours:** (i) *Multiple Partitions* (fixed number, variable size at boot — MFT-style) and (ii) *Variable-Sized Partitions* (truly dynamic, single free-list). - **Placement Policies:** - **First-Fit** — pick the first hole ≥ request. *Fastest, best in practice.* - **Best-Fit** — pick the smallest hole ≥ request. *Slowest, leaves "crumb" holes.* - **Worst-Fit** — pick the largest hole. *Slow, destroys large holes quickly.* - **Next-Fit** — like First-Fit but rotates the search pointer. - **Quick-Fit** — separate free-lists per common size. - **Fragmentation Type:** *External* (free memory exists, but is non-contiguous). - **50-Percent Rule:** Roughly `1/3` of memory is lost to fragmentation in steady state; worst-case bound is `1/2`. - **Coalescing:** Merge two adjacent free holes on process termination. Cheap, immediate, mandatory. - **Compaction:** Slide all live processes to one end to merge all holes. Expensive (O(N) moves); requires hardware-supported dynamic relocation (base register); infeasible if programs use absolute physical addresses. - **Solution to External Fragmentation:** **Paging** (covered in the next KTU Module 3 sub-topic) — allows *non-contiguous* physical allocation, eliminating the problem entirely at the cost of a page table and TLB. - **KTU Mnemonic:** "**IVP-FE**" — *Internal* frag → *Fixed* partitions; *Variable* partitions → *External* frag. - **Address translation in a relocatable system:** `PA = Base + LA`, validated by `0 ≤ LA < Limit`. - **Exam-ready fact:** Knuth (1973) showed First-Fit outperforms Best-Fit in practice — quote this in any "compare" question for full marks.

topics.title: Fragmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fragmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fragmentation** is a memory management phenomenon in computer operating systems where the available memory (RAM) becomes divided into small, non-contiguous, or inefficiently used blocks over time, reducing the system's ability to allocate memory to new processes even when the *total* free memory is theoretically sufficient.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Operating Systems syllabus (Module 3: Memory Management)**, fragmentation is broadly classified into two primary categories:

1. **Internal Fragmentation** — Wastage of memory *inside* an allocated block.
2. **External Fragmentation** — Wastage of memory *outside* allocated blocks (between free blocks).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have a **parking lot with 20 parking spaces** in a row (contiguous memory). Cars arrive of varying lengths (process sizes):
- You allocate exactly the space a car needs.
- After 10 hours, some cars leave, leaving **gaps** (holes).
- A new big bus arrives but **cannot park** because no *single* gap is big enough — even though the *total* empty spaces equal its size.

This "gaps-too-small" problem is **External Fragmentation**.
If you instead allocated a 5-space block to a small car that only needs 2 spaces, the other 3 spaces are wasted *inside* its block — this is **Internal Fragmentation**.

### 1.3 Memory Metrics & Constants (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Memory Metrics used in KTU problems:**
> - **Block Size** — The size of the fixed memory partition or allocated unit.
> - **Process Size** — The actual memory requested by a process.
> - **Fragmented Memory** = Allocated Size − Process Size (Internal) or sum of unusable holes (External).
> - Standard memory unit: **1 KB = 1024 bytes**, **1 MB = 1024 KB**.

### 1.4 Syllabus Mapping

| KTU Course Outcome | Bloom's Level | Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| CO3 — Apply memory management techniques | Apply / Analyze | Internal & External Fragmentation |
| CO4 — Evaluate paging/segmentation schemes | Evaluate | Fragmentation solutions |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Fragmentation — Theory

**Definition:** Wastage that occurs *within* an allocated memory block when the block size is larger than the memory requested by the process.

**Causes:**
- **Fixed partitioning** scheme where each partition has a pre-defined size.
- Memory allocated in **fixed-size blocks/pages/frames** (e.g., paging with a 4 KB page size — a 1 KB process wastes 3 KB).

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$\text{Internal Fragmentation} = \text{Block Size Allocated} - \text{Actual Process Size}$$

For a system with $n$ processes, the **total internal fragmentation** is:

$$I_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( B_i - P_i \right)$$

where $B_i$ is the block size and $P_i$ is the process size for the $i^{th}$ allocation.

**Average Internal Fragmentation:**

$$I_{avg} = \frac{I_{total}}{n}$$

### 2.2 External Fragmentation — Theory

**Definition:** Wastage that occurs when free memory is split into small, non-contiguous holes, none of which is large enough to satisfy a new request, even though the sum of holes exceeds the request size.

**Causes:**
- **Variable-sized partitioning** with dynamic allocation (First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit).
- Continuous allocation and de-allocation of memory blocks.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$\text{External Fragmentation} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} H_j \quad \text{where each } H_j < R_{new}$$

where $H_j$ is the size of the $j^{th}$ hole and $R_{new}$ is the size of the new request.

> [!NOTE]
> **50% Rule (Knuth's Observation):** With First-Fit allocation, approximately **1/3 (≈ 33%)** of total memory may become unusable due to external fragmentation, and with statistical bounds, the wasted memory can approach **50%** of total memory in the worst case.

### 2.3 Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Internal Fragmentation | External Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Location of waste** | Inside the allocated block | Outside blocks (in holes) |
| **Occurs in** | Fixed partitioning, Paging | Variable partitioning, Continuous allocation |
| **Severity metric** | $\sum (B_i - P_i)$ | Sum of unusable holes |
| **Solution** | Better-fit partitioning, smaller block sizes | Compaction, Paging, Segmentation |
| **Detectability** | Easy (subtract) | Complex (hole-tracking) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = B - P$ | Internal fragmentation per process |
| 2 | $I_{total} = \sum (B_i - P_i)$ | Total internal fragmentation |
| 3 | $E = \sum H_j \text{ where } H_j < R$ | Total external fragmentation |
| 4 | $\eta = \frac{\text{Used Memory}}{\text{Total Allocated}} \times 100\%$ | Memory utilization efficiency |
| 5 | $M_{waste} = I_{total} + E_{total}$ | Total memory wastage |
| 6 | $\text{50\% Rule}$ | External frag may waste ~50% memory |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fragmentation analysis is critical in:
- **Embedded systems** with limited RAM (e.g., IoT devices) where every byte matters.
- **Database buffer pools** where index fragmentation degrades query performance.
- **Solid-State Drives (SSDs)** — logical fragmentation increases wear-leveling overhead.
- **Long-running servers** (web servers, cloud VMs) where fragmentation accumulates over weeks/months.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Problem 1 — Internal Fragmentation Calculation

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A system uses fixed partitioning with block size **8 KB**. Three processes arrive with sizes **3 KB, 5 KB, and 2 KB**. Calculate (a) the internal fragmentation for each process, (b) total internal fragmentation, and (c) average internal fragmentation.

**Given:**
- Block size $B = 8$ KB (fixed)
- Process 1: $P_1 = 3$ KB
- Process 2: $P_2 = 5$ KB
- Process 3: $P_3 = 2$ KB

**Step 1:** Calculate internal fragmentation for each process.

For Process 1:

$$I_1 = B - P_1 = 8 - 3 = 5 \text{ KB}$$

For Process 2:

$$I_2 = B - P_2 = 8 - 5 = 3 \text{ KB}$$

For Process 3:

$$I_3 = B - P_3 = 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate total internal fragmentation.

$$I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 = 5 + 3 + 6 = 14 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate average internal fragmentation.

$$I_{avg} = \frac{I_{total}}{n} = \frac{14}{3} = 4.67 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 4:** Memory utilization efficiency.

Total allocated memory = $3 \times 8 = 24$ KB
Total useful memory = $3 + 5 + 2 = 10$ KB

$$\eta = \frac{10}{24} \times 100\% = 41.67\%$$

**Answer:** Each process wastes 5, 3, 6 KB respectively. Total = **14 KB**, Average = **4.67 KB**, Efficiency = **41.67%**.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Problem 2 — External Fragmentation with First-Fit

> **Problem:** A system has holes in memory of sizes: **100 KB, 500 KB, 200 KB, 300 KB, 600 KB** (in order). A new process requests **450 KB**. Using **First-Fit**, determine if allocation is possible and compute external fragmentation.

**Step 1:** Scan holes in order (First-Fit) for the first hole ≥ 450 KB.

| Hole # | Size | ≥ 450 KB? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 KB | No |
| 2 | 500 KB | **Yes — Allocate** |
| 3 | 200 KB | — |
| 4 | 300 KB | — |
| 5 | 600 KB | — |

**Step 2:** Allocate 450 KB to Hole 2. Remaining hole size:

$$H_2^{new} = 500 - 450 = 50 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3:** Updated hole list: **100, 50, 200, 300, 600 KB**.

**Step 4:** External fragmentation = sum of holes that cannot fit a hypothetical *next* request.

For a next request of, say, 150 KB: holes 100 and 50 are too small.

$$E = 100 + 50 = 150 \text{ KB (unusable for 150 KB request)}$$

**Answer:** Allocation succeeds at Hole 2. The 50 KB remainder, plus the 100 KB hole, become external fragmentation.

### 3.3 Worked-Out Problem 3 — Best-Fit vs Worst-Fit Comparison

> **Problem:** Holes: **50, 200, 70, 300, 100 KB**. Request: **75 KB**. Compute remaining holes for both **Best-Fit** and **Worst-Fit** strategies.

**Best-Fit (smallest sufficient hole):**

| Hole | Size | Sufficient? |
|---|---|---|
| 50 | Too small | — |
| 200 | Yes, but not smallest | — |
| 70 | Too small | — |
| 300 | Yes, but not smallest | — |
| 100 | **Yes — smallest sufficient** | **Allocate** |

Remaining = $100 - 75 = 25$ KB (new tiny hole)
Updated holes: 50, 200, 70, 300, **25 KB** → tiny 25 KB hole worsens external fragmentation.

**Worst-Fit (largest hole):**

Largest = 300 KB. Remaining = $300 - 75 = 225$ KB.
Updated holes: 50, 200, 70, **225**, 100 KB → larger remaining hole, *less* external fragmentation this time.

**Conclusion:** Worst-Fit attempts to leave larger remainders; Best-Fit minimizes immediate waste but creates tiny unusable holes.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solutions to Fragmentation

| Technique | Solves Internal? | Solves External? | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smaller fixed blocks** | Yes (less waste per block) | No | Reduce block granularity |
| **Compaction** | No | Yes | Physically shuffle memory |
| **Paging** | Yes (small fixed) | Yes (no contiguity) | Fixed-size pages, non-contiguous allocation |
| **Segmentation** | No (variable) | Partially | Logical variable-size segments |
| **Segmented Paging** | Yes | Yes | Combined approach |
| **Buddy System** | Partially | Yes | Binary splitting & merging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Internal Fragmentation — Block-Level Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Process 1: 3 KB"] --> A1["Block 8 KB - Wasted 5 KB"]
    B["Process 2: 5 KB"] --> B1["Block 8 KB - Wasted 3 KB"]
    C["Process 3: 2 KB"] --> C1["Block 8 KB - Wasted 6 KB"]
    A1 --> D["Total Wasted = 14 KB Internal Frag"]
    B1 --> D
    C1 --> D
```

### 4.2 External Fragmentation — Holes Between Allocations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Process A 200 KB"] --> H1["Hole 50 KB"]
    H1 --> P2["Process B 100 KB"]
    P2 --> H2["Hole 30 KB"]
    H2 --> P3["Process C 150 KB"]
    P3 --> H3["Hole 70 KB"]
    H3 --> P4["Process D 80 KB"]
    P4 --> H4["Hole 40 KB"]
    H4 --> X["Request 100 KB - FAILS - Total holes 190 KB but non-contiguous"]
```

### 4.3 Fragmentation Resolution Strategies Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Fragmentation Detected"] --> Q1{"Type?"}
    Q1 -->|Internal| S1["Reduce block size"]
    Q1 -->|External| S2["Choose strategy"]
    S1 --> S1a["Smaller partitions"]
    S1 --> S1b["Use paging"]
    S2 --> S2a["Compaction"]
    S2 --> S2b["Paging - non-contiguous"]
    S2 --> S2c["Buddy system"]
    S2 --> S2d["Segmentation"]
    S1a --> End["Memory Optimized"]
    S1b --> End
    S2a --> End
    S2b --> End
    S2c --> End
    S2d --> End
```

### 4.4 Compaction Process Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Before["Before Compaction: P1 Hole P2 Hole P3 Hole P4 Hole P5"] --> Move["OS Shuffles Processes"]
    Move --> After["After Compaction: P1 P2 P3 P4 P5 ContiguousHole"]
```

### 4.5 Buddy System Memory Splitting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["1 MB Block"] --> S1["512 KB Buddy 1"]
    R --> S2["512 KB Buddy 2"]
    S1 --> S1a["256 KB"]
    S1 --> S1b["256 KB"]
    S2 --> S2a["256 KB"]
    S2 --> S2b["256 KB"]
    S1a --> F["128 KB Final Allocation"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Define internal fragmentation. How does it differ from external fragmentation?

**Model Answer:**
Internal fragmentation is the wastage of memory that occurs *within* an allocated memory block, when the block size is larger than the actual memory requested by the process. It is calculated as $I = B - P$.

External fragmentation, in contrast, is the wastage of memory that occurs *outside* allocated blocks, when free memory is split into small, non-contiguous holes that are individually too small to satisfy a new request, even though their sum may be sufficient.

**Key Difference:** Internal fragmentation is *inside* a block (fixed-size systems like paging); External fragmentation is *between* blocks (variable-size systems like dynamic partitioning).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Mention any two techniques to reduce external fragmentation.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Compaction** — The OS physically relocates allocated memory blocks to coalesce all free holes into one large contiguous block. Drawback: expensive, requires process relocation, and is only possible with dynamic relocatable code.
2. **Paging** — Memory is divided into fixed-size pages and frames. Since a process's pages need not be contiguous in physical memory, external fragmentation is eliminated entirely (though some internal fragmentation may exist at the last page).

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Fragmentation Analysis Problem*

**(a)** [7 Marks] A system uses fixed partitioning with partition sizes **16 KB, 16 KB, 32 KB, 32 KB, 64 KB, 64 KB**. Processes arrive with sizes **12 KB, 18 KB, 25 KB, 40 KB, 55 KB**. Allocate using First-Fit and compute the total internal fragmentation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply First-Fit allocation.

| Process | Size | First-Fit Partition | Remaining |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 12 KB | 16 KB | 4 KB waste |
| P2 | 18 KB | 16 KB | (none — 16<18) → 32 KB | 14 KB waste |
| P3 | 25 KB | 32 KB | 7 KB waste |
| P4 | 40 KB | 64 KB | 24 KB waste |
| P5 | 55 KB | 64 KB | 9 KB waste |

**Step 2:** Sum internal fragmentation.

$$I_{total} = 4 + 14 + 7 + 24 + 9 = 58 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3:** Memory utilization.

Allocated = $12+18+25+40+55 = 150$ KB
Total partitions used = $16+32+32+64+64 = 208$ KB

$$\eta = \frac{150}{208} \times 100\% = 72.12\%$$

**Answer:** Total internal fragmentation = **58 KB**, Efficiency = **72.12%**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Buddy System** for memory allocation. How does it reduce external fragmentation while introducing some internal fragmentation?

**Model Solution:**

The **Buddy System** is a memory allocation algorithm that divides memory into blocks (buddies) of sizes that are powers of 2. It works as follows:

1. **Allocation:** When a process of size $S$ requests memory, the system rounds up $S$ to the nearest power of 2, say $2^k$. It then searches for a free block of size $2^k$. If unavailable, it splits a larger $2^{k+1}$ block into two $2^k$ buddies. Splitting continues recursively until a block of size $2^k$ is obtained.

2. **Deallocation:** When a process frees a block, the system checks if its "buddy" (the other half of the split) is also free. If so, the two buddies are **coalesced** (merged) back into a larger block.

**Example:** For a 1 MB block and a request of 100 KB:
- Round 100 KB up to 128 KB ($2^7$).
- Split 1 MB → 512 KB → 256 KB → 128 KB. Allocate one 128 KB block.

**Advantages:**
- **Reduces external fragmentation** because coalescing merges adjacent free buddies.
- **Fast allocation/deallocation** — $O(\log n)$ time.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Internal fragmentation:** A 100 KB request gets 128 KB, wasting 28 KB (≈ 22%).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Compaction & Paging Analysis*

**(a)** [7 Marks] What is compaction? Explain with a suitable diagram. What are its limitations?

**Model Solution:**

**Compaction** is a memory management technique used to eliminate external fragmentation by physically shuffling allocated memory blocks so that all free memory is consolidated into one large contiguous block.

**Diagram (Conceptual):**

Before Compaction:
