$$\underbrace{P_1}_{200} \; \underbrace{\text{Hole}}_{50} \; \underbrace{P_2}_{100} \; \underbrace{\text{Hole}}_{30} \; \underbrace{P_3}_{150}$$

After Compaction:
$$\underbrace{P_1 \; P_2 \; P_3}_{450} \; \underbrace{\text{Contiguous Hole}}_{80}$$

**Step-by-step process:**
1. The OS halts process execution (or performs live migration).
2. Processes are shifted downward (or upward) to eliminate holes.
3. Base registers of all affected processes are updated to reflect new starting addresses.
4. Free memory is now a single large block, ready for new allocation.

**Limitations:**
- **Time-consuming** — copying large memory regions is expensive.
- **Requires dynamic relocation** — only works if base register-based addressing is in use.
- **CPU-bound overhead** — processes must be paused during compaction.
- **Not feasible in real-time systems** due to unpredictable delays.

**(b)** [7 Marks] How does paging eliminate external fragmentation? Show with a numerical example.

**Model Solution:**

**Paging** eliminates external fragmentation by dividing both logical memory (process) and physical memory (RAM) into **fixed-size** units called **pages** (logical) and **frames** (physical). A process's pages can be placed in *any* available frames, requiring no contiguity.

**Numerical Example:**

- Logical memory: Process of **40 KB** divided into pages of size **4 KB** → **10 pages**.
- Physical memory: **60 KB** available in frames of **4 KB** → **15 frames**.
- Some frames are already in use: 5 frames occupied by OS, leaving 10 free.

**Allocation via Page Table:**

| Page # | Frame # |
|---|---|
| 0 | 7 |
| 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 11 |
| 3 | 5 |
| ... | ... |
| 9 | 14 |

**Key Observations:**
- **No external fragmentation** — any free frame can hold any page.
- **Internal fragmentation** — only in the *last* page of the process. Here, if the process were 39 KB, the last page wastes 1 KB.
- Maximum internal fragmentation per process = 1 page size − 1 byte.

**Conclusion:** Paging solves external fragmentation completely at the cost of negligible internal fragmentation (bounded by one page size per process).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Confusing fragmentation types:** Students often mix up internal and external. Remember: *Internal = inside the block*, *External = between blocks (holes)*.
> - **Forgetting the 50% Rule:** For full marks in best-fit/worst-fit questions, mention the statistical bound.
> - **Ignoring the last-page internal fragmentation in paging:** A common 2-mark loss in problems on paging.
> - **Not stating time complexity of Buddy System:** Examiners look for $O(\log n)$ mention.
> - **Skipping diagrams in compaction answers:** A 7-mark compaction question without a clear before/after diagram typically loses 2–3 marks.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Internal Fragmentation Formula:** $I = B - P$ (Block size − Process size)
- **External Fragmentation:** Sum of unusable holes, where each hole is smaller than the new request.
- **50% Rule (Knuth):** First-Fit can waste up to ~50% of memory via external fragmentation; best-fit often performs worse.
- **Paging eliminates external fragmentation** but introduces small internal fragmentation (≤ 1 page per process).
- **Segmentation reduces external fragmentation** (logical, not physical) but variable-size segments can still cause it.
- **Compaction** consolidates holes but is expensive and requires relocatable code.
- **Buddy System** uses power-of-2 blocks — fast $O(\log n)$ allocation/deallocation with limited internal fragmentation.
- **Memory utilization efficiency** $\eta = \frac{\text{Useful memory}}{\text{Total allocated}} \times 100\%$ is the key exam metric.
- **Page Table** is essential for translating logical page numbers to physical frame numbers in paging.
- **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** is the hardware cache that speeds up paging in real systems (often asked in subsequent KTU modules).
- **Static vs Dynamic partitioning:** Static causes internal fragmentation; Dynamic causes external fragmentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Paging # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **PAGING — Canonical KTU Definition** > **Paging** is a **non-contiguous memory allocation scheme** used by the Operating System that divides a process's **logical (virtual) address space** into fixed-size blocks called **Pages**, and divides the **physical memory (RAM)** into fixed-size blocks called **Frames** (or Page Frames). A hardware-supported data structure known as the **Page Table** is maintained by the OS for every process to translate a **logical address (page number + offset)** into a **physical address (frame number + offset)** at run-time. This translation is performed by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, paging is taught as the **principal solution to the problem of external fragmentation** that plagued earlier schemes such as **contiguous allocation** and **static/dynamic partitioning**. ### 1.2 Key Terminology Table (KTU High-Yield Glossary) | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Page** | — | Fixed-size block of **Logical (Virtual) Memory** | | **Frame** | — | Fixed-size block of **Physical Memory (RAM)** | | **Page Number (p)** | *p* | Index into the Page Table (high-order bits) | | **Page Offset (d)** | *d* | Displacement within a Page/Frame (low-order bits) | | **Page Table** | PT | Per-process mapping from Pages → Frames | | **Page Table Entry (PTE)** | — | One row in the Page Table | | **PTE bits** | — | Valid/Invalid bit, Frame number, Protection bits, etc. | | **MMU** | — | Hardware unit that performs address translation | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Book Analogy > [!NOTE] > **📚 Intuition: Think of a Massive Library** > Imagine you are writing a 500-page research report (your **process**). The university library is your **RAM** (physical memory), but the shelves are scattered across multiple wings and floors. Instead of reserving one continuous floor for your report (contiguous allocation — wastes space and may not fit), the librarian: > > 1. Tears your report into **fixed-size chapters of exactly 20 pages each** → these are your **PAGES**. > 2. Distributes each chapter into a different shelf pocket, each pocket holding **exactly 20 pages** → these are the **FRAMES**. > 3. Maintains a **borrowing card (Page Table)** that says "Chapter 1 is in Shelf B-12, Chapter 2 is in Shelf A-04, …" > 4. Whenever you want to read Chapter 7, Page 5, the librarian looks at the card, flies to Shelf D-09, and fetches the **5th page from there**. > > This is **PAGING** — non-contiguous in physical space, fully contiguous in logical space, with a translation card (Page Table) bridging the two. > [!TIP] > **Why is this beautiful?** The student (process) is *unaware* of the physical layout. It still thinks its chapters are in a perfect, contiguous order 1→2→3→4→5. The **MMU (librarian)** hides the messiness of the shelves. ### 1.4 Why Paging? — The Motivation | Problem in Contiguous Allocation | How Paging Solves It | |---|---| | **External Fragmentation** | Eliminated — any free frame can hold any page | | **Difficult allocation of variable-sized blocks** | Solved — every page is the **same fixed size** | | **Process must fit in one contiguous block** | Solved — a process can occupy **scattered frames** | | **Compaction is slow** | Not needed at all | | **Sharing code/data is hard** | Trivial — point two page tables to the same frames | > [!WARNING] > **Trade-off:** Paging does **NOT** eliminate **Internal Fragmentation** in the very last page of a process. Average internal fragmentation is **≈ Page Size / 2**. ### 1.5 Physical & Standard Metrics (Memorize for KTU) - **Page size** is always a **power of 2** → typically **4 KB**, **8 KB**, or **2 MB (huge pages)**. - Logical address size: **32-bit** (typical) or **64-bit** (modern). - Page Table Entry size: typically **4 bytes (32 bits)** on a 32-bit system. - Valid/Invalid bit: **1 bit**. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Logical Address Architecture A logical address generated by the CPU is split into two logical parts by the **MMU**: $$\text{Logical Address} = \underbrace{\text{Page Number (p)}}_{\text{high-order bits}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Page Offset (d)}}_{\text{low-order bits}}$$ | Component | Bits | Range | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Page Number (p)** | *n − d* | $0$ to $2^{(n-d)} - 1$ | Index into the Page Table | | **Page Offset (d)** | *d* | $0$ to $2^d - 1$ | Displacement within a Page | where **n** = total number of bits in the logical address. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Translation Logic 1. **CPU generates a logical address** of *n* bits. 2. **MMU extracts the page number** *p* (high-order *n − d* bits). 3. **MMU indexes the Page Table** at row *p* → reads the **Frame number** *f*. 4. **MMU replaces *p* with *f*** and **appends the offset** *d* unchanged. 5. **The constructed physical address** is sent to RAM to fetch the data/word. ## 2.3 The Translation Formula (Core Equation) $$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Frame Number } f) \times 2^{d} + \text{Page Offset}$$ $$f = \text{PageTable}[p]$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This!) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize Before Exam** | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Logical address split** | $n = (n-d) + d$ | *n* = total bits | | 2 | **Number of pages** | $N_{pages} = 2^{(n-d)}$ | = size of Page Table | | 3 | **Number of frames** | $N_{frames} = 2^{(m-d)}$ | *m* = physical address bits | | 4 | **Page size** (bytes) | $S_{page} = 2^{d}$ | Always a power of 2 | | 5 | **Logical address space** (bytes) | $LAS = 2^{n}$ | Maximum process size | | 6 | **Physical address space** (bytes) | $PAS = 2^{m}$ | Maximum RAM size | | 7 | **Page Table size** (bytes) | $PT_{size} = N_{pages} \times PTE_{size}$ | $PTE_{size} \in \{4, 8\}$ bytes | | 8 | **EAT (no TLB)** | $EAT = 2 \times t_{mem}$ | One access for PT, one for data | | 9 | **EAT (with TLB)** | $EAT = h(t_{TLB}+t_{mem}) + (1-h)(t_{TLB}+2t_{mem})$ | *h* = hit ratio | | 10 | **Avg. Internal Fragmentation** | $\approx S_{page} / 2$ | Last page only | | 11 | **Multi-level paging (2-level) PT size** | $\text{Outer} \times \text{Inner PTE}$ | Used when PT > page size | > [!TIP] > **The pipe character** `$\vert$` in formulas is replaced by `$\mid$` in the KTU Formula Sheet to preserve markdown table integrity. The expression $\vert$ or $\mid$ represents the **magnitude / absolute value** symbol. ## 2.5 Page Table Entry (PTE) — Anatomy A typical 32-bit PTE looks like this: | Bits 31 … 12 | Bit 11 | Bit 10 | Bit 9 | Bit 8 | Bit 7 | Bit 6 … 1 | Bit 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Frame Number (20 bits)** | Reserved | **D (Dirty)** | **A (Accessed)** | **PCD** | **PWT** | **Protection / User-Supervisor / R-W-X** | **V/I (Valid/Invalid)** | - **Valid Bit (V) = 1** → page is in a valid frame in RAM. - **Valid Bit (V) = 0** → page is **NOT** in RAM (either not allocated or swapped to disk). - **Protection bits** → read / write / execute permissions. - **Reference (Accessed) bit** → used by page replacement algorithms. - **Dirty (Modified) bit** → page has been written to. ## 2.6 Page Table — Conceptual Structure | Page # (p) | Frame # (f) | Valid Bit | Other Bits | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 5 | 1 | … | | 1 | — | 0 | … | | 2 | 2 | 1 | … | | 3 | 8 | 1 | … | | 4 | — | 0 | … | | 5 | 9 | 1 | … | | 6 | 1 | 1 | … | | 7 | — | 0 | … | ## 2.7 Why We Need Hardware Support — The MMU Software-only translation is **too slow** for every memory reference. Therefore, paging is implemented with **hardware assistance**: 1. **MMU chip** does the translation in 1 cycle. 2. **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** caches recent page table lookups → makes translation ≈ 1 memory access in the best case. 3. **Page Table Base Register (PTBR)** holds the starting address of the process's page table. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Paging | |---|---| | **Virtual Memory** | Demand Paging, Page Replacement, Swapping | | **Database Systems** | Buffer-pool management mirrors paging | | **Embedded / RTOS** | Fixed-size page tables for deterministic memory | | **Cloud Servers** | Huge pages (2 MB / 1 GB) reduce TLB misses | | **Security (Intel/ARM)** | Page tables enable user/kernel isolation, ASLR | # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Worked Example #1 — Address Translation (Karnataka / KTU Board Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A system has a **logical address space of 16 pages**, each page being **2 KB** in size. The **physical memory has 8 frames**. Given a logical address **0x512A**, find the **physical address**. ### Step 1: Determine the page size in bytes $$S_{page} = 2 \, \text{KB} = 2 \times 2^{10} = 2^{11} \, \text{bytes}$$ So, **offset bits** $d = 11$. ### Step 2: Find the number of bits in the page number $$N_{pages} = 16 = 2^{n-d} \implies n - d = 4$$ So the page number uses **4 bits**. ### Step 3: Total logical address bits $$n = (n-d) + d = 4 + 11 = 15 \text{ bits}$$ ### Step 4: Convert logical address 0x512A to binary $$\begin{aligned} 0x512A &= 0101\,0001\,0010\,1010_{(2)} \\ &= \underbrace{0101}_{\text{Page \# } = 5} \;\;\underbrace{0001\,0010\,1010}_{\text{Offset}} \end{aligned}$$ > Note: We need only **15 bits** of the 16-bit hex value, so we use the lower 15 bits. ### Step 5: Look up the Page Table (assumed mapping) Suppose the page table is: | Page # | Frame # | |---|---| | 0 | 7 | | 1 | 3 | | 2 | 5 | | 3 | 2 | | 4 | — (invalid) | | 5 | 6 | | 6 | 0 | | 7 | 4 | Page 5 maps to **Frame 6**. ### Step 6: Construct the physical address $$\begin{aligned} \text{Physical Address} &= f \times 2^{d} + \text{offset} \\ &= 6 \times 2^{11} + 0x012A \\ &= 6 \times 2048 + 298 \\ &= 12288 + 298 \\ &= 12586 \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Physical Address} = 12586_{10} = 0x312A}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example #2 — Effective Access Time (EAT) Derivation (⭐ KTU Favourite) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A system uses paging with a TLB. TLB access time = **20 ns**, RAM access time = **100 ns**, TLB hit ratio = **80%**. Compute the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. ### Step 1: Identify the cases - **TLB Hit** (probability *h* = 0.8): only **1** memory access needed (after TLB). - **TLB Miss** (probability *1 − h* = 0.2): **2** memory accesses needed (one for page table entry, one for data). ### Step 2: Write the EAT equation $$EAT = h \times (t_{TLB} + t_{mem}) + (1-h) \times (t_{TLB} + 2 \cdot t_{mem})$$ ### Step 3: Plug in values $$\begin{aligned} EAT &= 0.8 \times (20 + 100) + 0.2 \times (20 + 2 \times 100) \\ &= 0.8 \times 120 + 0.2 \times (20 + 200) \\ &= 0.8 \times 120 + 0.2 \times 220 \\ &= 96 + 44 \\ &= 140 \; \text{ns} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{EAT = 140 \text{ ns}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example #3 — Page Table Size Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A process has a **virtual address space of 4 GB**, **page size is 4 KB**, and **each PTE is 4 bytes**. Find the **Page Table size**. ### Step 1: Calculate the number of pages $$N_{pages} = \frac{\text{Virtual Address Space}}{\text{Page Size}} = \frac{4 \text{ GB}}{4 \text{ KB}} = \frac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{20} \text{ pages}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate the Page Table size $$PT_{size} = N_{pages} \times PTE_{size} = 2^{20} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** A 4 MB page table **per process** is huge! If 100 processes are running → 400 MB of RAM is wasted on page tables. This motivates **Hierarchical (Multi-level) Paging** and **Inverted Page Tables**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example #4 — Multi-Level Paging (Two-Level) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A 32-bit system with a **4 KB page size** uses a **2-level page table**. The **outer page table fits in one page**. How many entries does each level have? ### Step 1: Compute offset bits $$d = \log_2(4\,\text{KB}) = \log_2(2^{12}) = 12 \; \text{bits}$$ ### Step 2: Compute remaining bits for page numbers $$n - d = 32 - 12 = 20 \; \text{bits}$$ ### Step 3: Split 20 bits into two levels equally $$\text{Outer-level bits} = \text{Inner-level bits} = 10$$ ### Step 4: Entries per level $$N_{entries} = 2^{10} = 1024 \; \text{entries}$$ **Each level** = **1024 entries × 4 bytes = 4 KB = 1 page** ✓ (matches our assumption) --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Variants of Paging | Variant | How It Works | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---| | **Simple (Single-Level) Paging** | One flat Page Table | Simple | PT size = process size, can be huge | | **Two-Level Paging** | Page Table split into outer/inner | Allocates PT memory on demand | Two memory accesses if no TLB | | **Inverted Page Table** | One global PT indexed by frame | PT size ∝ RAM, not process | Slower lookup; hashing needed | | **Hashed Page Tables** | Hash of (pid, page#) → entry | Good for >32-bit spaces | Hash collisions | | **Segmented Paging** | Segment + Page + Offset | Adds logical grouping | More complex MMU | | **64-bit Multi-Level (4 or 5 level)** | Used in x86-64 / ARMv8 | Scales to huge VA | Many TLB misses | ## 3.6 Trade-off Curve — Page Size Analysis | Page Size | Internal Fragmentation | Page Table Size | Disk I/O Efficiency | TLB Reach | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Small (e.g., 4 KB)** | ↓ Less wasted memory | ↑ Huge PT (many entries) | ↓ More I/O for big files | ↓ Few pages in TLB | | **Large (e.g., 4 MB)** | ↑ More wasted memory | ↓ Small PT | ↑ Big I/O efficient | ↑ Many pages in TLB | | **Huge (2 MB / 1 GB)** | ↑↑ Severe | ↓↓ Tiny PT | ↑↑ Excellent | ↑↑ Best TLB reach | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention the **page table itself** as a candidate to be paged (multi-level paging) and the **TLB reach problem** when discussing page-size trade-offs. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Paging Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["CPU Generates Logical Address
n bits total"] SPLIT["Address Splitter inside MMU
Page Number p = n - d bits
Offset d = lower d bits"] PTBR["Page Table Base Register
holds start address of Page Table"] TLBcache["TLB Cache
fast associative lookup
stores recent p to f mappings"] PTmain["Main Memory Page Table
one entry per virtual page
PTE size = 4 or 8 bytes"] PTEread["Read PTE
extract Frame Number f
check Valid bit and Protection bits"] FCOMBINE["Frame Offset Combiner
Physical Address = f x 2 to the d + offset"] RAM["Physical Memory RAM
Frames of size 2 to the d bytes"] DATA["Data Word Returned to CPU"] CPU --> SPLIT SPLIT --> TLBcache TLBcache -->|TLB Hit| FCOMBINE TLBcache -->|TLB Miss| PTBR PTBR --> PTmain PTmain --> PTEread PTEread --> FCOMBINE FCOMBINE --> RAM RAM --> DATA ``` ## 4.2 Logical ↔ Physical Address Mapping Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LAS["LOGICAL ADDRESS SPACE OF PROCESS"] direction TB P0["Page 0
size = 2 to d"] P1["Page 1
size = 2 to d"] P2["Page 2
size = 2 to d"] P3["Page 3
size = 2 to d"] P4["Page 4
size = 2 to d"] P5["Page 5
size = 2 to d"] P6["Page 6
size = 2 to d"] P7["Page 7
size = 2 to d"] end subgraph PT["PAGE TABLE"] direction TB PT0["0 to F5"] PT1["1 to INVALID"] PT2["2 to F2"] PT3["3 to F8"] PT4["4 to INVALID"] PT5["5 to F6"] PT6["6 to F1"] PT7["7 to F0"] end subgraph PAS["PHYSICAL MEMORY RAM FRAMES"] direction TB F0["Frame 0"] F1["Frame 1"] F2["Frame 2"] F3["Frame 3"] F4["Frame 4"] F5["Frame 5"] F6["Frame 6"] F7["Frame 7"] F8["Frame 8"] end P0 -.-> PT0 -.-> F5 P2 -.-> PT2 -.-> F2 P3 -.-> PT3 -.-> F8 P5 -.-> PT5 -.-> F6 P6 -.-> PT6 -.-> F1 P7 -.-> PT7 -.-> F0 ``` ## 4.3 TLB Lookup Flowchart (Hit / Miss Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TB START["CPU generates logical address p plus d"] PARSE["Parse p = page number
d = page offset"] TLBCK["Search TLB for p"] Q1{"TLB Hit?"} TLBH["Use stored frame f from TLB
Physical Address = f x 2 to d plus offset"] PTACC["Access Page Table in RAM at index p
read PTE to get f"] Q2{"Valid bit = 1?"} PHYACC["Physical Address = f x 2 to d plus offset"] UPDTLB["Update TLB with new p to f mapping"] FAULT["Page Fault
OS loads page from disk
update PT and TLB
retry access"] DONE["Memory returns data to CPU"] START --> PARSE PARSE --> TLBCK TLBCK --> Q1 Q1 -->|Yes| TLBH --> DONE Q1 -->|No| PTACC --> Q2 Q2 -->|Yes| UPDTLB --> PHYACC --> DONE Q2 -->|No| FAULT --> DONE ``` ## 4.4 Two-Level Page Table Hierarchy (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB LA["32-bit Logical Address"] SP["Split into
p1 = 10 bits outer index
p2 = 10 bits inner index
d = 12 bits offset"] OUTER["Outer Page Table
1024 entries x 4 bytes = 4 KB
stored in one page"] INNER["Inner Page Table
1024 entries x 4 bytes = 4 KB
allocated on demand"] PHY["Physical Frame Number f
combined with 12-bit offset to form PA"] RAM["Physical Memory"] LA --> SP SP --> OUTER OUTER --> INNER INNER --> PHY PHY --> RAM ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each rectangle in the block diagrams is a **functional unit**, not a literal memory cell. The arrows represent the **flow of address bits** and the **sequence of operations** performed by the MMU + TLB + RAM during a single memory reference. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is paging? Why is it called a non-contiguous memory allocation scheme?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Paging** is a memory management technique in which the **logical address space of a process** is divided into **fixed-size blocks called pages**, and the **physical memory (RAM)** is divided into **fixed-size blocks called frames** of the same size. A **page table** maintained by the OS maps each page to a (possibly non-adjacent) frame. > > It is called **non-contiguous** because a process's pages can be loaded into **scattered, non-adjacent frames** in physical memory. The process perceives its address space as contiguous, but the physical placement is not. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between internal and external fragmentation in the context of paging.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Aspect | Internal Fragmentation | External Fragmentation | > |---|---|---| > | **When it occurs** | In the **last page** of a process (unused space inside the page) | In **contiguous allocation** schemes (free holes scattered in RAM) | > | **Size of wasted memory** | Average ≈ **Page Size / 2** | Depends on the pattern of allocation/deallocation | > | **Presence in Paging** | **YES** (always possible in last page) | **NO** (eliminated by paging) | > | **Solution** | Better page size choice; compaction of pages | Paging itself, or compaction / coalescing | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Rule:** Every Part-B question carries **14 marks**, usually split as **7 + 7** in two sub-parts. Students must answer **ONE full question** from the choice provided. --- ### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **basic concept of paging** with a neat diagram. Discuss how a **logical address** is translated to a **physical address** using a page table. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A system uses a **32-bit logical address space** with a **page size of 4 KB**. Each **Page Table Entry (PTE)** is **4 bytes**. Compute: 1. The **number of pages** in the process. 2. The **size of the single-level page table**. 3. The **size of the outer page table** if a **two-level paging** scheme is used (split equally). 4. The **size of each inner page table**. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — 7 marks** 1. **Definition of Paging** (2 marks): Paging is a memory management scheme that divides the **logical address space** into **fixed-size pages** and **physical memory** into **fixed-size frames** of the same size. The OS maintains a **page table** for each process to perform the mapping. 2. **Diagram** (2 marks): A clean block diagram showing **Logical Memory → Page Table → Physical Memory**, with arrows indicating mapping of pages 0, 1, 2, 3, … to scattered frames. 3. **Translation Steps** (3 marks): - CPU generates a logical address of *n* bits. - Top *p = n − d* bits = **Page Number**; bottom *d* bits = **Offset**. - MMU looks up Page Table at index *p* → gets Frame Number *f*. - Physical address = $f \times 2^{d} + \text{offset}$. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** Given: $n = 32$ bits, $S_{page} = 4 \text{ KB} = 2^{12}$ bytes, $PTE_{size} = 4$ bytes. **(1) Number of pages** (2 marks): $$\begin{aligned} N_{pages} &= \frac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pages} \end{aligned}$$ **(2) Page table size** (2 marks): $$PT_{size} = 2^{20} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 2^{22} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}$$ **(3) Outer page table size** (1.5 marks): $$p - d = 32 - 12 = 20 \text{ bits for page number}; \text{split into } 10 + 10$$ $$\text{Outer PT size} = 2^{10} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB}$$ **(4) Inner page table size** (1.5 marks): $$\text{Inner PT size} = 2^{10} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB (per inner table)}$$ --- ### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE* **[KTU University Exam – Model / Sample Paper Pattern]** **(a)** What is a **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)**? Explain its **role in paging** with the help of the **EAT (Effective Access Time)** formula. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A system with paging has the following parameters: - TLB access time = **10 ns** - Memory access time = **100 ns** - TLB hit ratio = **90%** - Page fault service time = **20 ms** Compute: 1. The **Effective Access Time (EAT)** with TLB and **no page faults**. 2. The **EAT** if the **page fault rate** is **1 in 1000 accesses** (hit ratio still 90%). **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Definition of TLB (2 marks):** A **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, fast, fully-associative **hardware cache** inside the MMU that stores recently used **Page Table Entries (PTEs)**. Each TLB entry contains a **(Page Number, Frame Number, Protection bits)** tuple. **Role in Paging (3 marks):** - Eliminates the need to consult the **main-memory page table** for every memory access. - On a **TLB hit**, the frame number is retrieved in 1 cycle → physical address formed in **one** memory access. - On a **TLB miss**, the main-memory page table is consulted → 2 memory accesses, and the TLB is updated. **EAT Formula (2 marks):** $$EAT = h(t_{TLB} + t_{mem}) + (1-h)(t_{TLB} + 2 t_{mem})$$ where $h$ = TLB hit ratio, $t_{TLB}$ = TLB access time, $t_{mem}$ = RAM access time. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Given:** $t_{TLB} = 10$ ns, $t_{mem} = 100$ ns, $h = 0.9$, $t_{pf} = 20 \text{ ms} = 20 \times 10^{6}$ ns. **(1) EAT with TLB, no page faults** (3 marks): $$\begin{aligned} EAT &= 0.9 \times (10 + 100) + 0.1 \times (10 + 200) \\ &= 0.9 \times 110 + 0.1 \times 210 \\ &= 99 + 21 = 110 \text{ ns} \end{aligned}$$ **(2) EAT with 1-in-1000 page fault rate** (4 marks): $$\begin{aligned} EAT &= (1 - p) \times \big[h(t_{TLB}+t_{mem}) + (1-h)(t_{TLB}+2t_{mem})\big] \\ &\quad + p \times t_{pf} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} EAT &= \left(\frac{999}{1000}\right) \times 110 + \left(\frac{1}{1000}\right) \times 20 \times 10^{6} \\ &= 0.999 \times 110 + 20{,}000 \\ &= 109.89 + 20{,}000 \\ &\approx 20{,}000.11 \text{ ns} \\ &\approx 20 \;\mu s \end{aligned}$$ > **Observation:** Even a tiny page fault rate of 0.1% **explodes the EAT** from 110 ns to ~20 µs — this is why **demand paging** is the single most expensive operation in modern OS. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — Common Mark-Deduction Zones** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** in EAT problems: $t_{pf} = 20$ ms must become $20 \times 10^{6}$ ns before arithmetic. **(-1 mark)** > 2. **Mixing up page number and frame number bits:** $p = n - d$, not $p = d$. **(-1 mark)** > 3. **Drawing the page table as a "block of data"** without showing the **valid/invalid bits** and **frame number** columns → examiner may deduct 1–2 marks. > 4. **Forgetting internal fragmentation** when asked "advantages/disadvantages of paging." Paging only **kills external** fragmentation. **(-1 mark)** > 5. **Confusing TLB with cache:** TLB caches **page numbers → frame numbers**, not actual data. **(-1 mark)** > 6. **Writing EAT formula without derivation:** Show at least the **two cases** (hit + miss) — don't just quote the equation. **(-1 mark)** --- ## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **Save this section — read it 30 minutes before the exam.** - [x] Paging = **non-contiguous allocation** using **fixed-size pages and frames**. - [x] Page size = $2^{d}$ bytes; page number bits = $n - d$. - [x] Translation: $f = PT[p]$; $\text{PA} = f \times 2^{d} + \text{offset}$. - [x] Page Table stored in main memory; base held in **PTBR**. - [x] PTE contains **frame number, valid bit, protection bits, accessed, dirty**. - [x] **TLB** = small, fast, associative cache of PTEs → reduces EAT. - [x] **EAT with TLB**: $EAT = h(t_{TLB}+t_{mem}) + (1-h)(t_{TLB}+2t_{mem})$. - [x] **EAT with page faults**: add $p \times t_{pf}$ term. - [x] **Internal fragmentation** exists in the last page (avg ≈ page size / 2); **external fragmentation is ZERO**. - [x] Page table can be **huge** → solved by **multi-level paging** and **inverted page tables**. - [x] **Two-level paging** splits page number into two indices, e.g., 10 + 10 for a 32-bit system with 4 KB pages. - [x] **Inverted page table**: one global PT indexed by frame, with $(pid, p)$ as key. - [x] Page size trade-off: small → ↓ fragmentation, ↑ PT size; large → ↑ fragmentation, ↓ PT size. - [x] **TLB reach** = (TLB entries) × (page size) → bigger pages give better TLB reach. - [x] Paging **enables sharing**: two PTEs pointing to the same frame → shared code. - [x] Paging **enables protection**: every access is checked against the PTE.

topics.title: Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Segmentation** is a non-contiguous memory management scheme in which the programmer's logical address space is divided into **variable-sized blocks called segments**, where each segment represents a logically meaningful division of a program (such as the *code segment*, *data segment*, *stack segment*, and *heap segment*). Each logical address is expressed as a two-dimensional tuple `<segment-number, offset>`, and the OS maintains a **Segment Table** to map each segment to its physical base address and its size (limit).

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PCITT403 – Operating Systems, Module 3: Memory Management), segmentation is studied as the **logical counterpart to paging**. While paging chops memory into equal-sized pieces (pages/frames) without regard to program logic, **segmentation respects the programmer's view of memory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A typical KTU question on segmentation expects the student to explain (i) the logical-to-physical address translation, (ii) the role of the Segment Table, (iii) protection/sharing mechanisms, and (iv) comparison with paging.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library and Books" View

Imagine you walk into a massive library where the librarian does **not** organize books by fixed shelf size. Instead, the librarian groups the entire collection by **subject categories** (Physics, Mathematics, Computer Science, History, Novels…). Each subject is a *segment* of the library.

- Each **subject category** → a **segment** (variable-sized, because the number of Physics books differs from the number of Novels).
- Each **book within a category** → an **offset** from the start of that category.
- When you say *"give me the 4th book in the Computer Science section"*, the librarian finds the *starting shelf of CS* and then counts 4 books from there.

Similarly, in a program, the user says *"byte at offset 540 in the data segment"*. The Memory Management Unit (MMU) finds the *starting physical address of the data segment* and then adds 540 to it.

## 1.3 Core Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Logical Address** | The two-part address `<s, d>` issued by the CPU |
| **Segment Number (s)** | Index into the Segment Table |
| **Offset (d)** | Displacement within the segment |
| **Segment Table** | OS data structure holding `base` and `limit` for every segment |
| **STBR (Segment Table Base Register)** | Points to the start of the segment table in memory |
| **STLR (Segment Table Length Register)** | Number of segments a process is allowed |
| **Base** | Starting physical address of the segment |
| **Limit** | Length of the segment (boundary check) |
| **Segment Fault** | Generated when `offset ≥ limit` (illegal access) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Tip for Exams:** Always remember the magic number **2 parts** in a segmentation address — `<segment, offset>`. The same address becomes **3 parts** when combined with paging (`<segment, page, offset>`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Segmentation? — The Motivating 'Why'

Paging treats a program as a flat, uniform sequence of bytes. But programmers don't *think* in flat memory. A C programmer writes into a `data[]` array, calls a function in `code`, and pushes into a `stack`. These are **logically distinct entities**, and they have very different access patterns:

- **Code segment** → read-only, executable, shared.
- **Data segment** → read-write, fixed size at compile time.
- **Stack segment** → read-write, grows downward dynamically.
- **Heap segment** → read-write, grows upward dynamically.

Segmentation mirrors **this natural visibility** to the hardware, which enables four benefits no flat scheme can provide: **protection, sharing, dynamic growth, and logical view of memory**.

## 2.2 The Segment Table — Heart of the Hardware Support

The OS keeps one segment table per process. Each entry (one row per segment) contains at least:

| Field | Width (typical) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Base** | 16–24 bits | Starting physical address of the segment |
| **Limit** | 16–20 bits | Length of the segment (in bytes) |
| **Protection bits** | 2–3 bits | Read / Write / Execute permissions |
| **Valid bit** | 1 bit | 1 = segment in memory, 0 = swapped out |
| **Modified (Dirty) bit** | 1 bit | Has the segment been written to? |
| **Reference bit** | 1 bit | Used by LRU/working-set algorithms |

## 2.3 The Address Translation Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

When the CPU generates a logical address `<s, d>`:

1. **Bound Check on `s`:** Compare `s < STLR`. If false → **trap** to OS (illegal segment access).
2. **Fetch Segment Entry:** Use `STBR + s × entry_size` to read the segment table entry.
3. **Bound Check on `d`:** Compare `d < limit`. If false → **segment fault** (trap to OS).
4. **Compute Physical Address:** `physical = base + d`.
5. **Permission Check:** Verify that the requested operation (R/W/X) is allowed by the protection bits.
6. **Issue the physical address** to the memory bus.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Logical Address | $L = \langle s, d \rangle$ | `s` = segment, `d` = offset |
| 2 | Physical Address | $P = \text{base}(s) + d$ | Add base + offset |
| 3 | Maximum Segments | $N_{\max} = 2^{k}$ | where `k` = bits allocated to `s` |
| 4 | Maximum Segment Size | $L_{\max} = 2^{m}$ | where `m` = bits allocated to `d` |
| 5 | Total Logical Space | $V_{\text{logical}} = N_{\max} \times L_{\max}$ | bytes |
| 6 | Bound Check (offset) | $0 \le d < \text{limit}(s)$ | else **segment fault** |
| 7 | Bound Check (segment) | $0 \le s < \text{STLR}$ | else **trap** |
| 8 | Internal Fragmentation | $\mathbf{0}$ bytes (no internal frag.) | segments are exact-fit |
| 9 | External Fragmentation | Possible (variable-sized segments) | dynamic relocation needed |
| 10 | STBR indexing | $\text{entry addr} = \text{STBR} + s \times E$ | `E` = entry size in bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** With `k` bits for the segment number and `m` bits for the offset, the *logical address space* is $2^{k+m}$ bytes, but a single process can only access $2^{k}$ segments, each of which may be up to $2^{m}$ bytes long. This is **not** the same as a flat $2^{k+m}$-byte space.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Segmentation is **not just a textbook concept**. It is the actual architecture used in x86 real mode (16-bit `CS:DS:SS:ES` segment registers with a 20-bit physical address = segment×16 + offset). Modern x86-64 in long mode largely abandons segmentation but retains the GDT/LDT mechanism for privilege protection. Embedded controllers and security-critical microcontrollers (ARM TrustZone, certain DSPs) use segmentation-like memory regions with base+limit to enforce hardware isolation between trusted and untrusted firmware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Walkthrough — Address Translation (Worked Example)

This is the **canonical KTU-style numerical**. We will solve it end-to-end with every intermediate step shown.

### **Problem Statement**

A system uses segmentation. A process has a segment table with 4 entries given below. The CPU generates the logical address `<2, 530>`. Compute the physical address. Also determine what happens if the offset is changed to 900.

| Segment # | Base (decimal) | Limit (decimal) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1400 | 1000 |
| 1 | 6300 | 400 |
| 2 | 4300 | 400 |
| 3 | 3200 | 1100 |

### **Solution (Step 1 — Identify inputs)**

The CPU gives us the logical address:

$$
L = \langle s, d \rangle = \langle 2, 530 \rangle
$$

So segment number `s = 2` and offset `d = 530`.

### **Solution (Step 2 — Fetch the segment table entry for s = 2)**

From the table, for segment `2`:

$$
\text{base}(2) = 4300, \quad \text{limit}(2) = 400
$$

### **Solution (Step 3 — Apply offset bound check)**

The hardware checks whether the offset lies within the segment length:

$$
0 \le d < \text{limit}(2) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; 0 \le 530 < 400
$$

Since $530 \not< 400$, the condition **fails**. The MMU raises a **segmentation fault (trap to OS)**. The process is terminated or the offending instruction is aborted.

### **Solution (Step 4 — Try a valid offset: d = 100 instead)**

Suppose the CPU had issued `<2, 100>`. Then:

$$
0 \le 100 < 400 \quad\checkmark \;\;\text{(passes bound check)}
$$

The physical address is computed as:

$$
P = \text{base}(2) + d = 4300 + 100
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 4400}
$$

### **Solution (Step 5 — Trace the access for segment 0 with d = 900)**

Now take `<0, 900>`. From the table, `base(0) = 1400` and `limit(0) = 1000`.

$$
0 \le 900 < 1000 \quad\checkmark \;\;\text{(valid access)}
$$

$$
P = 1400 + 900 = 2300
$$

### **3.2 Analytical Matrix — All 4 Address Translations**

| Logical Address $\langle s, d \rangle$ | Base | Limit | Bound Check | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `<0, 900>` | 1400 | 1000 | $900 < 1000$ ✅ | Physical = $1400 + 900 = 2300$ |
| `<1, 200>` | 6300 | 400 | $200 < 400$ ✅ | Physical = $6300 + 200 = 6500$ |
| `<2, 530>` | 4300 | 400 | $530 \not< 400$ ❌ | **Segment Fault** |
| `<3, 1050>` | 3200 | 1100 | $1050 < 1100$ ✅ | Physical = $3200 + 1050 = 4250$ |

## 3.3 Derivation — Maximum Addressable Space

Given an addressing scheme with `k` bits for the segment number and `m` bits for the offset:

The total number of distinct segments a process may reference is:

$$
N_{\text{seg}} = 2^{k}
$$

The maximum length of any one segment is:

$$
L_{\max} = 2^{m} \;\;\text{bytes}
$$

The **total logical address space** is therefore:

$$
V_{\text{logical}} = 2^{k} \times 2^{m} = 2^{k+m} \;\;\text{bytes}
$$

> **Numerical application:** If the logical address has `k = 3` segment bits and `m = 12` offset bits, then:
> - Maximum segments = $2^{3} = 8$
> - Maximum segment size = $2^{12} = 4096$ bytes = 4 KB
> - Total logical space = $2^{3+12} = 2^{15} = 32,768$ bytes = 32 KB

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Segmentation vs. Paging

| Property | Segmentation | Paging |
|---|---|---|
| Division basis | Logical (programmer's view) | Physical (fixed size) |
| Block size | **Variable** | **Fixed** (e.g., 4 KB) |
| Address format | `<segment, offset>` | `<page, offset>` |
| Internal fragmentation | **None** | Possible (last page) |
| External fragmentation | **Yes** | **None** |
| Protection | Per segment | Per page |
| Sharing | Whole segments | Page-level |
| Hardware support | Base + Limit registers | Page Table + TLB |
| Visibility to programmer | Yes (logical entities) | No (transparent) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical View — Program as Multiple Segments

The following diagram shows how a programmer views a process: as a set of named logical segments.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PROGRAMMERS LOGICAL VIEW OF MEMORY] --> B[Segment 0: Code]
    A --> C[Segment 1: Data]
    A --> D[Segment 2: Stack]
    A --> E[Segment 3: Heap]
    A --> F[Segment 4: Symbol Table]

    B --> B1[Read Only Executable]
    C --> C1[Read Write Static Globals]
    D --> D1[Read Write Grows Downward]
    E --> E1[Read Write Grows Upward]
    F --> F1[Read Only Debug Info]

    style A fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c82333,color:#000
    style E fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style F fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#5a3d8a,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Physical View — Segments Scattered in Physical Memory

Notice how segments may be placed **non-contiguously** anywhere in RAM.

```mermaid
graph LR
    PM[PHYSICAL MEMORY] --> F0[Frame 0 KB to 4 KB: Free]
    PM --> F1[Segment 1 DATA placed at 4 KB]
    PM --> F2[Frame 4 KB to 12 KB: Free]
    PM --> F3[Segment 0 CODE placed at 12 KB]
    PM --> F4[Frame 20 KB to 28 KB: Free]
    PM --> F5[Segment 2 STACK placed at 28 KB]
    PM --> F6[Frame 36 KB to 50 KB: Free]
    PM --> F7[Segment 3 HEAP placed at 50 KB]
    PM --> F8[Frame 60 KB onwards: Free]

    style PM fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style F3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style F5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c82333,color:#000
    style F7 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Address Translation Flow (MMU Pipeline)

This is the **MUST-KNOW** block diagram for any KTU segmentation question.

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[CPU generates logical address s comma d] --> CHK1{Is s less than STLR ?}
    CHK1 -->|No| TRAP1[Trap: Illegal Segment Number]
    CHK1 -->|Yes| FETCH[Fetch STBR plus s times E from Segment Table]
    FETCH --> SEGENTRY[Segment Table Entry: base, limit, protection]
    SEGENTRY --> CHK2{Is d less than limit ?}
    CHK2 -->|No| TRAP2[Segment Fault: Offset out of range]
    CHK2 -->|Yes| CHK3{R W X permission OK ?}
    CHK3 -->|No| TRAP3[Trap: Protection Violation]
    CHK3 -->|Yes| ADD[Physical Address = base + d]
    ADD --> MEM[Send to Physical Memory]
    MEM --> DONE[Data returned to CPU]

    style CPU fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style SEGENTRY fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style ADD fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style TRAP1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c82333,color:#000
    style TRAP2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c82333,color:#000
    style TRAP3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c82333,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Segment Sharing — Block-Level Functional Architecture

Two processes share a single read-only code segment. This illustrates why segmentation is powerful for **shared libraries and re-entrant code**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[PROCESS P1 Segment Table] --> P1S0[Segment 0: Private Data base=1000 limit=500]
    P1 --> P1S1[Segment 1: SHARED CODE base=8000 limit=2000]
    P2[PROCESS P2 Segment Table] --> P2S0[Segment 0: Private Data base=3000 limit=500]
    P2 --> P2S1[Segment 1: SHARED CODE base=8000 limit=2000]

    P1S1 -. points to same physical region .-> SHARED[Shared Code Segment in Physical Memory at 8000 to 9999]
    P2S1 -. points to same physical region .-> SHARED

    style SHARED fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#5a3d8a,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**Define segmentation. What is a segment table?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Segmentation is a memory management technique that divides a program's logical address space into **variable-sized blocks called segments**, each representing a logically meaningful entity such as code, data, or stack **(1 mark)**.
> A **segment table** is a per-process data structure maintained by the OS that stores, for each segment, its **base address** (starting physical location) and its **limit** (length of the segment) **(1 mark)**. Optionally, it also stores protection bits, a valid bit, and a modified bit **(1 mark)**.

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`
**Differentiate between internal and external fragmentation. Which one occurs in segmentation?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Internal fragmentation** is the wasted space *inside* an allocated block when the block is larger than what was actually requested **(1 mark)**. **External fragmentation** is the wasted space *between* allocated blocks in physical memory, where free holes are too small to satisfy a request **(1 mark)**.
> Segmentation produces **external fragmentation** (because segments are variable-sized and may leave small unusable gaps), but **no internal fragmentation** since each segment is allocated exactly its required size **(1 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> The KTU 2024 scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) allows the student to answer **EITHER** full Question A **OR** full Question B from each module. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**, summing to **14 marks**.

---

### ❓ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

#### **(a)** Explain the segmentation-based address translation mechanism with the help of a neat block diagram. Clearly state the role of **STBR** and **STLR**. **[7 marks]**

#### **(b)** A system uses segmentation. The segment table is:

| Segment | Base | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 200 | 500 |
| 1 | 1500 | 200 |
| 2 | 900 | 100 |
| 3 | 1200 | 400 |

Translate the following logical addresses. State clearly what happens if an address is invalid.
- (i) `<0, 430>`
- (ii) `<1, 198>`
- (iii) `<2, 100>`
- (iv) `<3, 399>` **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Q.A(a)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Segmentation translates a 2-part logical address `<s, d>` (segment number `s`, offset `d`) into a 1-part physical address using a per-process **Segment Table**. Each segment table entry contains a **base** (starting physical address) and a **limit** (segment length).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **STBR (Segment Table Base Register)** holds the physical address of the *start* of the segment table for the current process. **STLR (Segment Table Length Register)** holds the *number of segments* the process is allowed to use. STBR enables the MMU to locate the table; STLR enables a fast bound check on the segment number itself.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Translation flow (as drawn in SECTION 4.3):

1. CPU issues `<s, d>`.
2. MMU checks `s < STLR`. If false → **trap** (illegal segment).
3. MMU reads entry at `STBR + s × E` where `E` is entry size.
4. MMU checks `d < limit`. If false → **segment fault**.
5. MMU checks protection bits (R/W/X).
6. MMU computes **Physical Address = base + d** and sends to bus.

> **Exam Tip:** Use the exact phrase *"segment fault due to offset exceeding limit"* to score full marks.

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Q.A(b)**

**Address `<0, 430>`:**
- `base(0) = 200`, `limit(0) = 500`
- Check: $430 < 500$ ✅
- Physical = $200 + 430 = 630$

**Address `<1, 198>`:**
- `base(1) = 1500`, `limit(1) = 200`
- Check: $198 < 200$ ✅
- Physical = $1500 + 198 = 1698$

**Address `<2, 100>`:**
- `base(2) = 900`, `limit(2) = 100`
- Check: $100 < 100$ ❌ (offset must be **strictly less** than limit)
- Result: **Segment Fault**

**Address `<3, 399>`:**
- `base(3) = 1200`, `limit(3) = 400`
- Check: $399 < 400$ ✅
- Physical = $1200 + 399 = 1599$

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A *very common* error is writing the bound check as $d \le \text{limit}$. The correct check is **strictly less than** ($d < \text{limit}$). Also, students often confuse `base` and `limit` in the segment table. Memorize: **Base = Begin, Limit = Length**.

---

### ❓ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`**

#### **(a)** Compare paging and segmentation. State at least **five** points of difference. Why is segmentation said to provide a *"logical view"* of memory? **[7 marks]**

#### **(b)** With the aid of a diagram, explain how two processes can share a common code segment using segmentation. Mention **two** benefits of segment-level sharing. **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Q.B(a)**

| S.No. | Paging | Segmentation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Divides memory into **fixed-size** pages/frames | Divides memory into **variable-size** segments |
| 2 | Invisible to programmer | **Visible** to programmer (logical entities) |
| 3 | Suffers **internal** fragmentation | Suffers **external** fragmentation |
| 4 | No external fragmentation | No internal fragmentation |
| 5 | Address = `<page, offset>` | Address = `<segment, offset>` |
| 6 | Protection is page-level | Protection is segment-level (coarser, easier to set) |
| 7 | Sharing done at page granularity | Sharing done at whole-segment granularity (more meaningful) |

**Why "logical view" (2 marks):** The programmer references memory by *named* logical entities (code, data, stack, heap) rather than by raw physical addresses. The hardware/OS hides where these entities are physically placed; the programmer sees a clean, named, hierarchical memory model.

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Q.B(b)**

**Diagram (3 marks):** See **SECTION 4.4** for the Mermaid block diagram. Both Process P1 and Process P2 have a segment table entry for *Segment 1* that points to the **same physical base address 8000** (with the same limit). Thus both processes execute the same code from the same physical region.

**Mechanism (2 marks):** Sharing is achieved simply by giving two different segment-table entries (one in each process's table) the **same base and limit** values. The protection bits are set to *read-execute only*, preventing either process from corrupting the shared code.

**Two benefits (2 marks):**
1. **Memory economy** — only one copy of common code (e.g., `printf`, `libc`) is kept in RAM even if 100 processes use it.
2. **Faster startup & updates** — shared segments are mapped into a new process's address space simply by copying one entry into its segment table, with no copying of code.
3. *(Optional 3rd benefit)* **Security** — read-only protection ensures processes cannot tamper with shared code.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Q.B):** Students often draw the sharing diagram with the **same segment table** for both processes, which is **wrong** — each process has its **own private** segment table; only the segment *contents* (base/limit) coincide. Also, do not confuse segment sharing with *page sharing* — they are different granularities and the diagrams differ.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Segmentation** divides a program into **variable-sized logical blocks** (segments).
- [x] Logical address = `<segment-number, offset>` — exactly **2 components**.
- [x] **Segment Table** is per-process; entries contain **base + limit + protection**.
- [x] **STBR** → start of segment table; **STLR** → number of valid segments.
- [x] **Physical address** = `base + offset` after two bound checks pass.
- [x] Bound check 1: `s < STLR` (else illegal segment).
- [x] Bound check 2: `d < limit` (else **segment fault**).
- [x] **Zero internal fragmentation**; **external fragmentation possible**.
- [x] Enables **hardware-level protection** (R/W/X bits) and **segment sharing**.
- [x] Real-world: x86 real mode, ARM TrustZone memory regions.
- [x] Combined with paging: `<segment, page, offset>` — used in MULTICS and modern x86 protected mode.
- [x] Strict-less-than rule: $d < \text{limit}$ (not $d \le \text{limit}$).
- [x] Paging = **physical** view, fixed size; Segmentation = **logical** view, variable size.
- [x] Common 14-mark topics: (a) address translation with diagram, (b) numerical on segment table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Memory Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual Memory Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Virtual Memory** is a memory management technique implemented by modern operating systems (OS) that creates an illusion of a large, contiguous, private logical address space for each running process, even when the process does not physically reside entirely in the main memory (RAM) at any given moment. It achieves this by decoupling the *logical addresses* generated by the CPU from the *physical addresses* in actual RAM, and by utilizing a portion of the secondary storage (typically a Hard Disk Drive or SSD, configured as the **swap space** or **page file**) as an extension of the main memory.

The three fundamental properties of Virtual Memory, as defined by the KTU 2024 syllabus, are:

1. **Logical Address Space Abstraction:** Every process believes it owns the entire address range from `0` to `2^n - 1`.
2. **Demand Paging:** Only the pages actually required by the CPU are loaded into RAM. Unused pages remain on disk until demanded.
3. **Address Translation & Swapping:** The Memory Management Unit (MMU), with the help of page tables, translates virtual addresses to physical addresses, transparently swapping pages between disk and RAM when a *page fault* occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Virtual Memory is NOT a separate physical memory. It is a *technique* that uses a combination of hardware (MMU, TLB) and software (OS kernel) to provide the abstraction of a larger memory than what is physically installed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Study Desk Library

Imagine you are a B.Tech student preparing for the KTU exam with a very **small study desk** (which represents your **Physical RAM**, say 8 GB). However, your entire syllabus consists of **20 thick textbooks** (which represent the **entire virtual address space** of all running processes, say 256 GB worth).

You obviously cannot keep all 20 books open on your small desk simultaneously. So, what do you do?

- You keep a **bookshelf in another room** (the **Hard Disk / Swap Space**) where ALL 20 books are stored.
- On your desk, you only keep the **one or two books** you are currently studying (this is the concept of **Demand Paging** — only what is *demanded* is in main memory).
- Your friend (the **Operating System / MMU**) acts as a librarian. When you need a chapter from a book on the bookshelf, you ask the librarian. The librarian brings that specific chapter to your desk, and if necessary, places the currently open chapter back on the shelf to make room (this is called **Page Swap-Out / Page-In**).
- You, the student, never realize that the books are being moved around. You simply have the *illusion* that all 20 books are within your reach at all times. This illusion is **Virtual Memory**.

## 1.3 Core Components of Virtual Memory

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to recognize the following architectural components:

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Entities in Virtual Memory:**
>
> | Component | Role |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | **Virtual Address (Logical Address)** | Address generated by the CPU during program execution. |
> | **Physical Address** | Actual address in the RAM hardware memory chips. |
> | **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** | Hardware component that performs address translation at runtime. |
> | **Page Table** | Kernel-managed data structure (per-process) storing VA-to-PA mappings. |
> | **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** | A small, fast hardware cache inside the MMU holding recent translations. |
> | **Swap Space / Page File** | Reserved area on the secondary storage (HDD/SSD) used to hold evicted pages. |
> | **Page Fault Handler** | OS kernel routine that services a trap when a required page is not in RAM. |

## 1.4 Why Virtual Memory? — The Driving Motivation

- **Process Isolation & Protection:** Each process operates in its own private address space; one process cannot corrupt another's memory.
- **Efficient Memory Utilization:** The CPU does not waste RAM storing pages that will never be accessed (sparse address spaces, large binaries).
- **Ability to Run Programs Larger than Physical RAM:** A 16 GB game can run on an 8 GB laptop by swapping pages in and out on demand.
- **Simplified Programmer Model:** Programmers can write code assuming a large, contiguous memory layout without worrying about physical fragmentation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism of Demand Paging

**Demand Paging** is the most common implementation of virtual memory. Under this scheme, pages of a process are loaded into RAM *only when they are demanded* by the CPU — not all at once at process start.

The operational logic proceeds as follows:

- **Step 1 — Process Creation:** When a process is loaded, the OS does NOT load the entire executable into RAM. Instead, it records in the page table that *all pages* are currently in the **swap space on disk** (marked as **invalid** in the valid/invalid bit).
- **Step 2 — First Memory Access:** The CPU generates a virtual address. The MMU looks up the page table. Finding the **valid/invalid bit = invalid**, the MMU raises a **Page Fault Trap** to the OS.
- **Step 3 — Page Fault Service Routine:** The OS kernel is invoked. It locates the required page on the disk in the swap space.
- **Step 4 — Free Frame Search:** The OS checks for a free frame in RAM. If none is free, it invokes a **page replacement algorithm** (e.g., FIFO, LRU, Optimal) to select a victim page to evict.
- **Step 5 — Disk-to-RAM Transfer:** The desired page is read from disk into the chosen free frame (this is a slow I/O operation).
- **Step 6 — Page Table Update:** The OS updates the page table entry, setting the **valid/invalid bit = valid**, and updates the **frame number**.
- **Step 7 — Instruction Restart:** The originally faulting instruction is restarted. The process now continues seamlessly, as if no fault had occurred.

## 2.2 The Effective Access Time (EAT) — Core KTU Concept

The Effective Access Time (EAT) is the single most important formula KTU examiners test in this module. It quantifies the *average memory access time* considering the probability of a page fault.

### Definitions of Variables

- $p$ = **Page Fault Rate** (probability that a given memory access results in a page fault). Range: $0 \le p \le 1$.
- $1 - p$ = Probability that the page is already in RAM (a *hit*).
- $ma$ = Memory Access time (time to access RAM). Usually given as **100 nanoseconds** in KTU problems.
- $pft$ = Page Fault Service Time (total time to service a page fault: I/O + OS overhead + restart). Usually given as **10 milliseconds** in KTU problems.

### The Core Formula

$$
\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (\text{Page Fault Service Time})
$$

When we expand the Page Fault Service Time to include a memory access step:

$$
\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (ma + pft)
$$

Or, the most common textbook expansion:

$$
\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot pft
$$

(The simpler form assumes the page fault service time already accounts for the initial failed access.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When the problem gives you the EAT, the page fault rate, and the memory access time, and asks you to find the page fault service time — use the formula in reverse. Always show the substitution step explicitly to earn full marks.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Unit / Range | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Effective Access Time (EAT) | $(1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot pft$ | Nanoseconds (ns) or Microseconds (µs) | Average memory access latency |
| Page Fault Rate | $p = \dfrac{\text{Number of Page Faults}}{\text{Total Memory References}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le p \le 1$ | Quantifies paging efficiency |
| Hit Ratio | $h = 1 - p$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le h \le 1$ | Probability of RAM access success |
| Address Space Size | $2^{\text{virtual address bits}}$ | Bytes, KB, MB, GB | Total logical memory size |
| Physical Memory Size | $2^{\text{physical address bits}}$ | Bytes, KB, MB, GB | Total installed RAM size |
| Page Size | $2^{\text{offset bits}}$ | Bytes (typically 4 KB) | Granularity of paging |
| Number of Pages | $\dfrac{\text{Virtual Address Space}}{\text{Page Size}}$ | Pure number | Entries in page table |
| Number of Frames | $\dfrac{\text{Physical Memory Size}}{\text{Page Size}}$ | Pure number | Available physical slots |
| Page Table Size | $\text{Number of Pages} \times \text{Size of PTE}$ | Bytes | Memory overhead of paging |
| Swap Space Required | Total virtual memory of all resident processes | Bytes, GB | Disk reserved for swapping |
| EAT with TLB hit | $h_{tlb} \cdot (t_{tlb} + ma) + (1 - h_{tlb}) \cdot (t_{tlb} + 2 \cdot ma)$ | Nanoseconds | Performance with Translation Lookaside Buffer |
| Memory Access with TLB Miss | $2 \cdot ma$ | Nanoseconds | Accessing page table then RAM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Default Values:** When a problem does not explicitly state $ma$ or $pft$, assume $ma = 100$ ns and $pft = 10$ ms = $10,000,000$ ns.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems & Mobile OS (Android):** Modern smartphones use *zRAM* and *zSwap* (compressed virtual memory) to extend effective RAM without disk-based swapping.
- **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure):** Hypervisors like VMware and KVM over-commit physical memory using virtual memory, allowing more virtual machines than available RAM.
- **Database Systems (Oracle, PostgreSQL):** Buffer pool managers use the same demand paging principles to load only the requested data blocks.
- **Graphics Processing Units (GPUs):** Use virtual memory to map large textures into limited GPU VRAM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Access Time (EAT)

### The Logical Premise

When a CPU executes a memory reference instruction, exactly one of two outcomes occurs:

- **Outcome A (Probability = $1 - p$):** The required page is **in RAM**. The MMU translates the address, and the memory read takes $ma$ nanoseconds.
- **Outcome B (Probability = $p$):** The required page is **NOT in RAM** (page fault). The OS must fetch the page from disk, which takes $pft$ nanoseconds, and then the memory is accessed taking $ma$ more nanoseconds.

### The Mathematical Derivation

The Expected Value (statistical average) of a memory access time is the sum of each outcome's probability multiplied by its time cost.

$$
\text{EAT} = (\text{Prob of Hit}) \cdot (\text{Time for Hit}) + (\text{Prob of Miss}) \cdot (\text{Time for Miss})
$$

Substituting the variables defined in Section 2:

$$
\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (ma + pft)
$$

Expanding the parenthesis:

$$
\text{EAT} = ma - p \cdot ma + p \cdot ma + p \cdot pft
$$

The two middle terms, $- p \cdot ma$ and $+ p \cdot ma$, **cancel each other out**:

$$
\text{EAT} = ma + p \cdot pft
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **cleanest, most exam-friendly form** of the EAT equation that KTU students should memorize. It clearly shows that the average access time is the *normal memory access time* plus an *additional penalty proportional to the page fault rate*.

### Boundary Condition Verification

- **If $p = 0$** (no page faults ever occur): $\text{EAT} = ma + 0 = ma$. This makes sense: every access is a pure RAM hit.
- **If $p = 1$** (every access is a fault): $\text{EAT} = ma + pft$. This makes sense: every access must include the page fault service penalty.
- **As $p \to 0$**, the OS achieves near-native performance, which is the engineering goal of any virtual memory system.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Standard KTU Problem

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU 2024 Style):**

A system uses demand paging. The page fault service time is **8 ms**. The memory access time is **200 ns**. The page fault rate is observed to be **1 in 1000 memory references**. Calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT) and the percentage slowdown due to paging.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify and convert all variables to a consistent unit. Convert $pft$ from milliseconds to nanoseconds.

$$
pft = 8 \text{ ms} = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ ns} = 8{,}000{,}000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the page fault rate $p$.

$$
p = \frac{1}{1000} = 0.001
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the memory access time $ma$.

$$
ma = 200 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the EAT formula.

$$
\text{EAT} = ma + p \cdot pft
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = 200 + (0.001) \cdot (8{,}000{,}000)
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = 200 + 8000
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = 8200 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the percentage slowdown. The slowdown factor is the ratio of EAT to the baseline $ma$.

$$
\text{Slowdown} = \frac{\text{EAT}}{ma} = \frac{8200}{200} = 41
$$

$$
\text{Percentage Slowdown} = (41 - 1) \times 100\% = 4000\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion:** Even a tiny page fault rate of just **0.1%** can degrade performance by **40 times**! This demonstrates why good page replacement algorithms and sufficient RAM are critical in OS design.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Reverse Calculation

**Problem Statement:**

In a demand-paged system, the effective access time is **205 ns**. The memory access time is **100 ns** and the page fault service time is **10 ms**. What is the maximum acceptable page fault rate?

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Convert $pft$ to nanoseconds.

$$
pft = 10 \text{ ms} = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ ns} = 10{,}000{,}000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the EAT formula.

$$
\text{EAT} = ma + p \cdot pft
$$

$$
205 = 100 + p \cdot (10{,}000{,}000)
$$

**Step 3:** Isolate the term containing $p$.

$$
205 - 100 = p \cdot 10{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
105 = p \cdot 10{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $p$.

$$
p = \frac{105}{10{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
p = 1.05 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
p = 0.0000105 \text{ (or about 1 page fault per 95,238 accesses)}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Form

| Scenario | Formula to Use | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Given $ma$, $pft$, and $p$, find EAT | $\text{EAT} = ma + p \cdot pft$ | Standard forward computation. |
| Given EAT, $ma$, $pft$, find $p$ | $p = \dfrac{\text{EAT} - ma}{pft}$ | Inverse algebraic manipulation. |
| With TLB, given hit ratio $h_{tlb}$ | $\text{EAT} = h_{tlb} \cdot (t_{tlb} + ma) + (1 - h_{tlb}) \cdot (t_{tlb} + 2 \cdot ma)$ | Two-level memory access. |
| TLB lookup + page table + RAM | $t_{tlb} + 2 \cdot ma$ (on miss) | Sequential access through MMU. |
| Multi-level page table | Multiply access times by number of levels | Each level requires one RAM access. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Virtual Memory Translation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete end-to-end flow of how a Virtual Address (VA) generated by the CPU is translated into a Physical Address (PA), including the handling of a page fault.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Generates Virtual Address VA] --> B[MMU Receives VA]
    B --> C{Search TLB Cache}
    C -- TLB Hit --> D[Get Frame Number from TLB]
    C -- TLB Miss --> E[Consult Page Table in RAM]
    E --> F{Valid Invalid Bit Check}
    F -- Valid Equals 1 --> G[Read Frame Number from PTE]
    F -- Valid Equals 0 Page Fault --> H[OS Page Fault Handler Invoked]
    H --> I[Locate Page in Swap Space on Disk]
    I --> J{Free Frame Available in RAM}
    J -- Yes --> K[Load Page from Disk to Free Frame]
    J -- No --> L[Run Page Replacement Algorithm e.g., LRU]
    L --> M[Evict Victim Page to Disk]
    M --> K
    K --> N[Update Page Table Entry PTE]
    N --> O[Set Valid Bit to 1]
    O --> P[Restart Faulting Instruction]
    G --> Q[Combine Frame Number with Page Offset]
    D --> Q
    Q --> R[Generate Physical Address PA]
    R --> S[Access Physical RAM at PA]
    S --> T[Return Data to CPU]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Virtual Memory Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USERSPACE[User Process Layer]
        Proc1[Process 1 VA Space 0 to MAX1]
        Proc2[Process 2 VA Space 0 to MAX2]
        Proc3[Process 3 VA Space 0 to MAX3]
    end
    subgraph OSKERNEL[Operating System Kernel Layer]
        Scheduler[Process Scheduler]
        Pager[Demand Pager]
        Replacer[Page Replacement Module]
        PTMgr[Page Table Manager]
    end
    subgraph HARDWARE[Hardware Layer]
        MMU[Memory Management Unit MMU]
        TLB[Translation Lookaside Buffer TLB]
        RAM[Physical RAM Frames]
        DISK[Secondary Storage Swap Space]
    end
    Proc1 --> MMU
    Proc2 --> MMU
    Proc3 --> MMU
    MMU --> TLB
    TLB --> PTMgr
    PTMgr --> RAM
    MMU -- Page Fault Trap --> Pager
    Pager --> Replacer
    Replacer --> RAM
    Replacer --> DISK
    Pager --> DISK
    Scheduler --> Pager
```

## 4.3 Page Fault Handling Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU
    participant MMU
    participant PT as Page Table
    participant OS as OS Kernel
    participant DISK as Swap Disk
    participant RAM as Physical Memory
    CPU->>MMU: Send Virtual Address
    MMU->>PT: Lookup Page Table Entry
    PT-->>MMU: Return Invalid Bit = 0
    MMU->>OS: Trap Page Fault
    OS->>DISK: Locate Required Page
    DISK-->>OS: Page Found at Disk Block X
    OS->>RAM: Check for Free Frame
    alt Free Frame Exists
        OS->>DISK: Read Page from Disk
        DISK->>RAM: Load Page into Free Frame
    else No Free Frame
        OS->>RAM: Select Victim Frame via LRU
        OS->>DISK: Write Victim Page to Disk
        OS->>DISK: Read Required Page from Disk
        DISK->>RAM: Load Required Page into Frame
    end
    OS->>PT: Update PTE with Frame Number and Valid = 1
    OS->>CPU: Return from Trap Ready to Restart
    CPU->>MMU: Restart Instruction with Same VA
    MMU->>RAM: Translate and Access Physical Address
    RAM-->>CPU: Return Requested Data
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Virtual Memory. Explain how it creates an illusion of a larger memory space for the user.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Virtual Memory is a memory management technique implemented by the operating system that provides an abstraction of a logical address space to each process, independent of the actual physical memory available in the system. The OS achieves this illusion by combining the limited physical RAM with a designated area on the secondary storage device (called the swap space). Through demand paging and address translation performed by the MMU and page tables, the OS loads only the actively used pages into RAM and keeps the remaining pages on the disk. When a process accesses a page that is not currently in RAM, a page fault occurs, and the OS transparently fetches the required page from the disk into RAM. This mechanism creates the illusion that the process has access to a memory space much larger than the physical RAM installed on the system. (3 marks)

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a Page Fault? Differentiate between a valid page fault and an invalid page fault.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A page fault is a type of hardware exception (trap) raised by the Memory Management Unit when a running process attempts to access a page that is marked as **invalid** in its page table — meaning the page is not currently present in the physical RAM.

- **Valid Page Fault:** Occurs when the accessed virtual address is within the legal range of the process's address space, but the corresponding page has been swapped out to the disk. The OS can service this fault by loading the page from the swap space. The process continues normally.
- **Invalid Page Fault (Segmentation Fault):** Occurs when the accessed virtual address lies outside the legal range of the process (e.g., accessing an unallocated memory region or attempting a NULL pointer dereference). The OS cannot service this fault and must terminate the offending process with a segmentation fault signal. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to convert milliseconds to nanoseconds in EAT calculations. **1 mark is deducted** for unit inconsistency.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** When asked for "percentage degradation," students calculate the wrong ratio. Use $(\text{EAT} - ma) / ma \times 100\%$, NOT $\text{EAT} / pft$.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In page fault service time problems, students often omit the initial memory access. Read the problem carefully — some questions include it, others do not.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing the EAT formula without defining variables loses **1 mark**. Always state: "Let $p$ = page fault rate, $ma$ = memory access time, $pft$ = page fault service time."

---

### Question B-A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of demand paging with a neat diagram. Discuss the steps involved in handling a page fault. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Consider a demand-paged system where the page fault service time is **12 ms**, the memory access time is **150 ns**, and the page fault rate is **1 in 500,000 memory references**. Calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT) and the percentage performance degradation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Demand paging is a memory management scheme in which pages of a process are loaded into the physical RAM only when they are demanded (referenced) by the CPU, rather than loading the entire process at once. The OS keeps the page table entries initially marked as **invalid**, and a page fault trap is raised whenever the CPU accesses an invalid page.

**Step-by-Step Page Fault Handling (5 marks):**

1. **Trap Generation:** The CPU issues a virtual address. The MMU checks the page table and finds the **valid/invalid bit = 0**. A page fault trap is raised to the OS. (1 mark)
2. **Save Process State:** The OS saves the state of the faulting process, including its program counter and registers, in its PCB. (1 mark)
3. **Locate Page on Disk:** The OS searches the swap space on the disk to find the required page. (1 mark)
4. **Find a Free Frame:** The OS checks the free-frame list in RAM. If empty, a page replacement algorithm (e.g., LRU) is invoked to select a victim page for eviction. (1 mark)
5. **Issue Disk I/O:** The OS schedules a disk I/O request to read the required page into the selected free frame. The process is context-switched out, allowing the CPU to run other processes while the I/O completes. (1 mark)
6. **Update Page Table:** Upon completion of I/O, the OS updates the page table entry with the new frame number and sets the valid bit to 1. The faulting instruction is restarted, and the process resumes execution. (1 mark — distributed across the above)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Given data extraction. (1 mark)

$$
ma = 150 \text{ ns}, \quad pft = 12 \text{ ms}, \quad p = \frac{1}{500{,}000}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert $pft$ to nanoseconds for unit consistency. (1 mark)

$$
pft = 12 \times 10^{6} \text{ ns} = 12{,}000{,}000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $p$ in decimal form. (1 mark)

$$
p = \frac{1}{500{,}000} = 2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Effective Access Time formula. (2 marks)

$$
\text{EAT} = ma + p \cdot pft
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = 150 + (2 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (12{,}000{,}000)
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = 150 + 24
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = 174 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the percentage degradation. (2 marks)

$$
\text{Degradation \%} = \frac{\text{EAT} - ma}{ma} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Degradation \%} = \frac{174 - 150}{150} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Degradation \%} = \frac{24}{150} \times 100\% = 16\%
$$

**Final Conclusion:** The EAT is **174 ns** and the system suffers a **16% performance degradation** due to paging.

---

### Question B-B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a)** Define Virtual Memory. Explain the role of the Memory Management Unit (MMU) and the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) in virtual address translation. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A system has a memory access time of **80 ns**. The TLB access time is **20 ns** with a TLB hit ratio of **85%**. The page fault service time is **8 ms** and the page fault rate is **1 in 1,000,000 references**. Compute the Effective Access Time considering both TLB and page faults. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition of Virtual Memory (2 marks):** Virtual Memory is a storage allocation scheme in which the OS uses both hardware and software to allow programs to address memory locations beyond the physical RAM by mapping virtual addresses to physical addresses through page tables. The OS employs the secondary storage as an extension of RAM.

**Role of the MMU (3 marks):** The Memory Management Unit is a dedicated hardware component inside the CPU that performs **runtime address translation** from virtual to physical addresses. For every memory reference issued by the CPU, the MMU splits the virtual address into a **page number** and a **page offset**. It consults the page table to find the corresponding frame number in physical RAM, combines the frame number with the offset, and generates the physical address. The MMU also checks the valid/invalid bit and raises a page fault trap if necessary.

**Role of the TLB (2 marks):** The Translation Lookaside Buffer is a small, fully-associative, high-speed hardware cache located inside the MMU.
