| Disadvantage | Small processes waste memory; large processes thrash | Small processes may still get too few frames; ignores priority |
| Computation | $a_i = a / n$ | $a_i = a \cdot (S_i / \sum S_j)$ |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### Question A: Comprehensive 14-Mark Problem `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q3 (a)** Consider a paging system with **32-bit logical address space**, **4 KB page size**, and **64 MB physical memory**. A process $P_1$ uses **2 MB** of logical address space, and process $P_2$ uses **14 MB**. If the OS uses **Proportional Allocation**, compute the number of frames each process gets, assuming 4 frames are reserved for the OS. **(7 marks)**

> **Q3 (b)** Explain *Global Replacement* and *Local Replacement* strategies. With a neat diagram, show how a high-priority process can starve a low-priority process under global replacement. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Compute page size in bytes.**

Page size = 4 KB = $2^{12}$ bytes.

**Step 2: Compute the number of physical frames.**

Physical memory = 64 MB = $2^{26}$ bytes.

Number of physical frames = $2^{26} / 2^{12} = 2^{14} = 16384$ frames.

**Step 3: Compute frames available for user processes.**

$a = 16384 - 4 = 16380$ frames.

**Step 4: Compute virtual sizes of processes.**

$P_1 = 2$ MB, $P_2 = 14$ MB, Total $= 16$ MB.

**Step 5: Compute proportional shares.**

$a_1 = 16380 \times \frac{2}{16} = 16380 \times 0.125 = 2047.5 \rightarrow \lfloor 2047.5 \rfloor = 2047$ frames.

$a_2 = 16380 \times \frac{14}{16} = 16380 \times 0.875 = 14332.5 \rightarrow \lfloor 14332.5 \rfloor = 14332$ frames.

**Step 6: Verify.**

Total allocated = $2047 + 14332 = 16379$. Leftover = $16380 - 16379 = 1$ frame.

**Final Answer:** $P_1$ gets **2047 frames**; $P_2$ gets **14332 frames**; **1 frame** remains with the OS.

##### Model Solution for (b):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Replacement:** When a process incurs a page fault, the OS may select a victim frame from *any* process's frame pool — even one belonging to a lower-priority process. This maximizes system-wide throughput but creates a starvation risk for low-priority processes.
>
> **Local Replacement:** A process can only evict pages from its *own* allocated frame set. This isolates each process, ensuring that one process's memory pressure cannot directly harm another. However, it sacrifices global optimization.

**Starvation Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Priority Process - Page Fault] -->|Steals from| B[Low Priority Process Frame]
    A -->|Steals more| C[Low Priority Process Frame 2]
    A -->|Steals more| D[Low Priority Process Frame 3]
    B -.->|Eventually Depleted| E[Low Priority Process Starved]
```

**Explanation:** Under global replacement, a high-priority process (e.g., a real-time trading app) can repeatedly steal frames from a low-priority process (e.g., a backup job) until the low-priority process has no resident pages left, causing its execution to halt or thrash uncontrollably.

---

#### Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Problem `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q4 (a)** Discuss the three major frame allocation algorithms with suitable formulas. State one situation where each algorithm is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

> **Q4 (b)** A system has **5 processes** and **100 frames** of physical memory. The OS uses *priority allocation* with the following priorities:
> - $P_1$: priority 5, needs 20 frames
> - $P_2$: priority 1, needs 10 frames
> - $P_3$: priority 4, needs 35 frames
> - $P_4$: priority 2, needs 25 frames
> - $P_5$: priority 3, needs 10 frames
>
> The OS reserves 10 frames. Compute the proportional allocation and determine which process is *most* under-allocated. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Frame Allocation Algorithms:**
>
> 1. **Equal Allocation:** Each process gets an equal share. Best when all processes have similar memory footprints and uniform priority.
> 2. **Proportional Allocation:** Distribution is proportional to process size. Best when processes have varied virtual memory sizes.
> 3. **Priority Allocation:** Distribution favors high-priority processes, often coupled with global replacement. Best in real-time systems where some processes are mission-critical.

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Compute available frames.**

$a = 100 - 10 = 90$ frames.

**Step 2: Sum the priorities.**

$\sum P_i = 5 + 1 + 4 + 2 + 3 = 15$.

**Step 3: Compute proportional allocation for each process.**

$a_1 = 90 \times \frac{5}{15} = 30$ frames (needs 20, surplus = 10).

$a_2 = 90 \times \frac{1}{15} = 6$ frames (needs 10, deficit = 4).

$a_3 = 90 \times \frac{4}{15} = 24$ frames (needs 35, deficit = 11).

$a_4 = 90 \times \frac{2}{15} = 12$ frames (needs 25, deficit = 13).

$a_5 = 90 \times \frac{3}{15} = 18$ frames (needs 10, surplus = 8).

**Step 4: Identify the most under-allocated process.**

$P_4$ has the **largest deficit of 13 frames** → most under-allocated.

**Final Answer:** $P_1 = 30$, $P_2 = 6$, $P_3 = 24$, $P_4 = 12$, $P_5 = 18$ frames. **$P_4$ is the most under-allocated process.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract OS-reserved frames** before computing the share is the #1 cause of full-mark loss. Always compute $a = m - n_{OS}$ first.
> 2. **Skipping the floor function** in proportional allocation loses 1 mark. The floor symbol `\lfloor \rfloor` is mandatory.
> 3. **Confusing page size with frame size**: In standard paging, they are equal by design, but stating this explicitly in your answer earns a 1-mark bonus.
> 4. **For proportional allocation, remember**: It is a function of *virtual memory size*, NOT of the process's *current page fault rate*. Students often confuse this with working-set allocation.
> 5. **For priority allocation**: Do NOT use page fault rate or CPU usage as "priority" — that would be a different policy (working-set or page-fault-frequency allocation).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Allocation of Frames**

- **Frame Allocation** = deciding *how many* physical frames each process gets in RAM.
- The **minimum number of frames** is dictated by the **ISA** (instruction set architecture).
- The **three classic algorithms** are: **Equal**, **Proportional**, and **Priority**.
- **Equal Allocation** formula: $a_i = a / n$, where $a$ is available frames and $n$ is process count.
- **Proportional Allocation** formula: $a_i = \lfloor a \cdot S_i / \sum S_j \rfloor$, where $S_i$ is virtual size of process $i$.
- **Priority Allocation** formula: $a_i = \lfloor a \cdot P_i / \sum P_j \rfloor$, where $P_i$ is priority.
- Always **apply the floor function** when allocating discrete frame counts.
- Always **subtract OS-reserved frames** from total physical frames *before* any allocation calculation.
- **Local Replacement** = evict own pages; **Global Replacement** = evict any process's pages.
- **Global Replacement** yields higher throughput but introduces **starvation** risk for low-priority processes.
- **Local Replacement** provides **isolation** between processes but lowers system-wide throughput.
- **Thrashing** occurs when the system spends more time swapping pages than executing instructions.
- The most common exam pitfall: forgetting the floor function and the OS reserve deduction.
- Real-world analog: Hotel room allocation where each guest is a process and rooms are frames.
- Key engineering applications: **DBMS buffer pools**, **cloud VM scheduling**, **mobile OS low-memory killers**.
- For **multiprogramming level** balancing, the OS uses **working set models** and **page fault frequency** as feedback to dynamically adjust frame counts.
- **KTU 2024 Tag**: Frame allocation falls under **Module 3 - Memory Management (Virtual Memory)** and is a guaranteed 7-14 mark question in the End Semester Exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thrashing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thrashing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thrashing** is a critical performance degradation phenomenon in operating systems where the system spends a disproportionately large amount of time swapping pages **in and out** of memory rather than executing actual processes, resulting in a sharp drop in CPU utilization.

In formal terms, thrashing occurs when a system is engaged in **continuous paging** — a state where the operating system is constantly transferring pages between the **RAM (main memory)** and the **disk (secondary storage)** because the working set of active processes exceeds the available physical memory frames.

> [!NOTE]
> **Denning's Working Set Model (1968)** formally described thrashing as the condition where the **sum of working sets of all running processes exceeds the total available physical memory**, causing frequent page faults.

Mathematically, the condition for thrashing is expressed as:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} WSS_i > \text{Total Available Frames}
$$

where $WSS_i$ is the **Working Set Size** of process $P_i$ and $n$ is the total number of active processes in the ready queue.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cluttered Desk" Metaphor

Imagine you are a student preparing for **5 different exams** simultaneously, but your study desk is only large enough to hold **2 textbooks at a time**.

1. You open Book A and read a page — but you need a formula from Book B.
2. You put Book A on the floor (swap out) and bring Book B onto the desk (swap in).
3. While reading Book B, you realize you need a diagram from Book C.
4. You swap B for C — but then A is still relevant...
5. You end up **spending 90% of your time shuffling books** and only **10% actually studying**.

This is **EXACTLY** what happens in a thrashing computer system:
- **Desk** = Physical RAM (limited frames)
- **Books** = Pages belonging to active processes
- **Shuffling** = Disk I/O paging activity
- **Actual studying** = Useful CPU computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CPU utilization drops to near 0%** because the CPU spends most of its idle cycles waiting for the disk to complete paging operations — ironically called "working" but actually doing nothing productive.

---

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary Anchors (Board Exam Favorites)

| Term | Standard Definition | KTU Tag |
|------|--------------------|---------|
| **Page Fault** | Interrupt raised when a referenced page is not in RAM | Core |
| **Swap-In** | Transfer of a page from disk → main memory | I/O Term |
| **Swap-Out** | Transfer of a page from main memory → disk | I/O Term |
| **Working Set** | Set of pages referenced by a process in a fixed time window $\Delta$ | Denning's Model |
| **Degree of Multiprogramming** | Number of processes resident in memory at once | Scheduler Param |
| **Page Fault Rate** $p$ | Number of page faults per memory reference | Performance Metric |

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU board answer, always define thrashing using **three keywords**: **high page-fault rate**, **low CPU utilization**, and **excessive disk I/O**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thrashing Vicious Cycle — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The phenomenon of thrashing is fundamentally a **positive feedback loop**. Let us dissect each stage:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Understanding the *cause-effect chain* below is essential — KTU board questions frequently ask students to **"explain how thrashing occurs"** for 7 marks.

### Stage 1: Over-Subscription of Memory
The **long-term scheduler** admits too many processes into the ready queue, each demanding its own page table and resident set.

- **Effect:** Available free frames per process shrinks dramatically.
- **Metric:** Effective frames per process $\approx \dfrac{\text{Total Frames}}{n}$ where $n$ is the multiprogramming level.

### Stage 2: Page Fault Frequency Spikes
Because each process now holds only a few frames, the probability that a referenced page is *not* in memory increases sharply.

$$
p_{\text{fault}} \uparrow \uparrow
$$

- **Effect:** Process is blocked; OS must invoke the page replacement algorithm.

### Stage 3: Pager Becomes Overwhelmed
The OS paging daemon (or replacement routine) must constantly evict pages and fetch new ones from the disk.

- **Effect:** Disk I/O queue length grows; **Ready Queue** becomes empty of runnable processes.

### Stage 4: CPU Waits Idle
With no runnable processes in the ready queue, the **CPU enters an idle state**, even though there are processes in the system.

- **Critical Insight:** This idle time is **NOT productive** — it is a symptom, not a cause.

### Stage 5: OS Misinterprets the Symptom
The OS sees **low CPU utilization** and — *fatal mistake* — assumes the system is underloaded. It admits **MORE processes** into memory, worsening the situation.

- **Effect:** Loop closes → thrashing intensifies.

### Stage 6: System Collapse
At this point, **disk throughput saturates**, response time becomes seconds-to-minutes, and the system effectively becomes unusable.

---

## 2.2 Root Cause: Why Multiprogramming Has an Optimal Point

This is best visualized using the **CPU Utilization vs. Multiprogramming Level** curve:

| Multiprogramming Level | CPU Utilization | Page Fault Rate |
|:----------------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|
| 1 Process | Moderate | Low |
| Optimal Range (k processes) | **Maximum (peak)** | Moderate |
| Beyond k | Decreasing | Rising rapidly |
| Far beyond k | **Near 0% (Thrashing)** | **Saturating** |

The intuition: **diminishing returns** — adding one more process beyond the optimal point *reduces* the resources available to all others.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every formula you must memorize for thrashing-related problems in KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $W(t,\Delta) = \{ p_i \mid p_i \text{ referenced in } [t-\Delta+1, t] \}$ | **Working Set Definition** (Denning) | Set of pages |
| 2 | $WSS_i = \mid W(t,\Delta) \mid$ | Working Set Size (cardinality) | Pages |
| 3 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} WSS_i > M$ | **Thrashing Condition** | Boolean |
| 4 | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} WSS_i$ | Total Demand for Frames | Frames |
| 5 | $p = \dfrac{\text{Page Faults}}{\text{Total References}}$ | **Page Fault Rate** (0 to 1) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $EAT = (1-p) \times \text{memory access} + p \times \text{page fault service time}$ | **Effective Access Time** | Time units |
| 7 | $\text{Optimal Multiprogramming Level} = k^{\star}$ | Peak CPU utilization point | Integer |
| 8 | $\text{Frames per Process} = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Average allocation | Frames |
| 9 | $\Delta$ (Delta) | Working Set Window Size | References / Time |
| 10 | $\text{PFF} > \text{Threshold} \Rightarrow \text{Suspicion of Thrashing}$ | Page Fault Frequency Heuristic | Faults/sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In your answer booklet, always use $\mid WSS \mid$ or $\lvert WSS \rvert$ — never the bare pipe symbol — when writing working set cardinality inside markdown tables, as the pipe `|` breaks table parsing.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Thrashing mitigation is at the heart of **cloud computing resource management**.

- **AWS EC2 / Azure VMs**: Hypervisors use working-set estimation to dynamically provision memory to virtual machines. Thrashing on a guest VM is detected via PFF monitoring and triggers live migration.
- **Database Systems (Oracle, PostgreSQL)**: The **buffer pool manager** uses LRU-K and similar algorithms to prevent buffer thrashing under high query concurrency.
- **Mobile OS (Android, iOS)**: The Low Memory Killer (LMK) daemon uses PFF thresholds to reclaim memory before thrashing occurs on devices with constrained RAM.
- **Compilers and IDEs**: Memory paging issues during large C++ template instantiation or Java IDE indexing are classic real-world thrashing scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Thrashing Condition

We will now rigorously derive the mathematical condition under which thrashing occurs, step by step. This derivation is a **favorite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE.

### Setup

Let:
- $M$ = Total physical memory (number of frames available)
- $n$ = Number of active processes in memory
- $WSS_i$ = Working Set Size of process $P_i$ (pages required to avoid frequent faults)
- $\Delta$ = Window of recent page references

### Step 1 — Define Memory Demand

Each process $P_i$ requires **at least $WSS_i$ frames** to maintain an acceptable page fault rate. The total memory demand is:

$$
D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} WSS_i
$$

> **Logic Row:** Summation across all $n$ processes because each has independent working set requirements.

### Step 2 — Compare Demand with Supply

For the system to operate without thrashing, supply must meet or exceed demand:

$$
D \leq M
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} WSS_i \leq M
$$

> **Logic Row:** This is the **stable operating condition**.

### Step 3 — Establish the Thrashing Condition

By contrapositive, thrashing occurs when the inequality is **violated**:

$$
\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} WSS_i > M}
$$

> **Logic Row:** Excess demand forces constant page replacement, which is the definition of thrashing.

### Step 4 — Substitute Approximation

If we approximate each process's working set as roughly equal (homogeneous assumption), then:

$$
n \cdot \overline{WSS} > M
$$

where $\overline{WSS}$ is the **average working set size**. Solving for the safe multiprogramming level:

$$
n_{\text{max}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{M}{\overline{WSS}} \right\rfloor
$$

> **Logic Row:** This gives the **maximum number of processes** the system can safely admit.

### Step 5 — Express Effective Access Time (EAT) Degradation

Now we connect the working set model to performance. Let:
- $a$ = memory access time (e.g., **100 ns**)
- $p$ = page fault rate (probability)
- $T_s$ = page fault service time (e.g., **25 ms = 25,000,000 ns**)

The **Effective Access Time** is:

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot a \; + \; p \cdot T_s
$$

Substituting typical values $a = 100$ ns and $T_s = 25 \times 10^6$ ns:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - p)(100) + p(25 \times 10^6) \\
&= 100 - 100p + 25 \times 10^6 \cdot p \\
&= 100 + p(25 \times 10^6 - 100) \\
&= 100 + p \cdot (24{,}999{,}900)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Row:** Even a tiny page fault rate (e.g., $p = 0.001$) catastrophically inflates access time.

Numerical check at $p = 0.001$:

$$
EAT = 100 + 0.001 \times 24{,}999{,}900 = 100 + 24{,}999.9 = 25{,}099.9 \text{ ns} \approx 25.1 \; \mu s
$$

> **Insight:** This is a **251× slowdown** — the hallmark of thrashing.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical textbook problem** — practice it until it becomes reflexive.

### Problem Statement
A system has **$M = 5$ frames**. Three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ have working set sizes $WSS_1 = 2$, $WSS_2 = 3$, $WSS_3 = 1$. Determine whether the system will thrash.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute total demand.

$$
D = WSS_1 + WSS_2 + WSS_3 = 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 \text{ frames}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with available frames.

$$
D = 6 > M = 5
$$

**Step 3:** Apply thrashing condition.

Since $\sum WSS_i = 6 > M = 5$, the thrashing condition is satisfied.

$$
\boxed{\text{The system will THRASH.}}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate maximum safe multiprogramming level.

$$
n_{\text{max}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{5}{\overline{WSS}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{5}{2} \right\rfloor = 2
$$

> **Logic Row:** Only 2 processes can safely coexist. The OS must **suspend** one process.

### Effective Frames per Process Analysis

| Processes $n$ | Frames per Process | WSS Sum | Thrashing? |
|:-------------:|:------------------:|:-------:|:----------:|
| 1 | 5.0 | (max) WSS$_1$ = 2 | No |
| 2 | 2.5 | WSS$_1$ + WSS$_2$ = 5 | Borderline |
| 3 | 1.67 | 6 | **YES** |
| 4 | 1.25 | $\geq 7$ | **YES (severe)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Thrashing Feedback Loop — Mermaid Flowchart

The following diagram captures the **vicious feedback cycle** that sustains thrashing. Memorize this for KTU descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Multiprogramming Level n] --> B{CPU Utilization Monitored by OS}
    B -- Low Utilization --> C[OS Admits More Processes into Memory]
    C --> D[Available Frames per Process Decreases]
    D --> E[Page Fault Frequency Spikes]
    E --> F[Pager Activated: Swap-Out and Swap-In]
    F --> G[Disk I/O Queue Saturates]
    G --> H[Ready Queue Empty of Runnable Processes]
    H --> I[CPU Idle Waits for Disk]
    I --> J[CPU Utilization Drops Further]
    J -- Triggers Again --> B
    F --> K[System Performance Collapse]
    K --> L[User Response Time Becomes Unacceptable]
```

### Reading Guide for the Diagram
- The loop $B \to C \to D \to E \to F \to G \to H \to I \to J \to B$ is the **thrashing positive-feedback cycle**.
- The branch $F \to K \to L$ represents the **user-visible failure mode**.

---

## 4.2 Working Set Window Visualization

The Denning working set model tracks which pages are referenced within the most recent $\Delta$ references.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TIME_AXIS [" "]
        T1["t - Delta + 1"] --- T2["..."] --- T3["..."] --- T4["t"]
    end
    P1["Page 2"] --> R1["Referenced at t-5"]
    P2["Page 5"] --> R2["Referenced at t-3"]
    P3["Page 2"] --> R3["Referenced at t-2"]
    P4["Page 7"] --> R4["Referenced at t-1"]
    P5["Page 5"] --> R5["Referenced at t"]
    WSS["Working Set at time t: {2, 5, 7}"]
    R1 --> WSS
    R2 --> WSS
    R3 --> WSS
    R4 --> WSS
    R5 --> WSS
```

> **Caption:** Within the sliding window $\Delta$, only pages **2, 5, and 7** are referenced. $WSS = 3$ pages.

---

## 4.3 Thrashing Prevention / Mitigation Architecture

The OS can apply **four canonical strategies** to prevent or recover from thrashing:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Thrashing Detected via PFF Monitor"]
    ROOT --> S1["Strategy 1: Swap Out Process - Reduce n"]
    ROOT --> S2["Strategy 2: Working Set Model - Adjust Delta"]
    ROOT --> S3["Strategy 3: Page Fault Frequency Control"]
    ROOT --> S4["Strategy 4: Local Replacement Policy"]
    S1 --> S1A["Suspend low-priority process to disk"]
    S1A --> S1B["Free frames allocated to active processes"]
    S2 --> S2A["Track pages referenced in last Delta refs"]
    S2A --> S2B["If WSS exceeds allocated frames, suspend process"]
    S3 --> S3A["If PFF above upper threshold: give more frames"]
    S3A --> S3B["If PFF below lower threshold: reclaim frames"]
    S4 --> S4A["A process can only evict its own pages"]
    S4A --> S4B["Limits cascading faults across processes"]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (CPU vs Pager Dynamics)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CPU_DOMAIN ["CPU Subsystem"]
        CPU["CPU Core"]
        RR["Ready Queue"]
        SCHED["CPU Scheduler"]
    end
    subgraph MEM_DOMAIN ["Memory Subsystem"]
        RAM["Physical RAM M Frames"]
        PT["Page Tables"]
    end
    subgraph DISK_DOMAIN ["Disk Subsystem"]
        DISK["Swap Space on Disk"]
        PFQ["Page Fault Service Queue"]
    end
    SCHED -- Selects Process --> RR
    RR -- Dispatch --> CPU
    CPU -- Memory Reference --> PT
    PT -- Page Hit --> RAM
    PT -- Page Miss --> PFQ
    PFQ -- Disk Read --> DISK
    DISK -- Page Brought In --> RAM
    RAM -- If Full --> PFQ
    PFQ -- Evicted Page --> DISK
    CPU -. Idle Waiting .-> PFQ
```

> **Key Takeaway:** The CPU enters an idle loop while the disk service queue is saturated — this is the defining signature of thrashing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ FIRST:**
> 1. Many students **confuse thrashing with swapping** — thrashing is *excessive* swapping; routine swapping is normal.
> 2. Do **NOT** write "thrashing = deadlock" — they are completely unrelated.
> 3. Always **state the condition $\sum WSS_i > M$ explicitly** in numericals; partial credit is given for the formula even if arithmetic slips.
> 4. For 14-mark questions, a **diagram is mandatory**. If you cannot draw it perfectly, a textual flow description still earns 2–3 marks.
> 5. Avoid vague phrases like *"memory becomes full"*. Use precise terms: **page frames**, **page fault rate**, **working set**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define thrashing. Mention any two consequences of thrashing.**

#### Model Answer
**Definition:** Thrashing is a phenomenon in a virtual memory system where the CPU spends more time swapping pages between main memory and disk than executing user processes, resulting in a drastic drop in system throughput.

**Two Consequences:**
1. **Severe degradation of CPU utilization** — the CPU remains idle waiting for page-in operations to complete.
2. **Excessive disk I/O activity** — the disk service queue becomes saturated, increasing response time for all processes.

**Auxiliary consequence (for extra credit):** System throughput approaches zero and interactive response becomes unusable.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the Working Set of a process? How does it help in preventing thrashing?**

#### Model Answer
**Definition:** The working set $W(t, \Delta)$ of a process at time $t$ is the set of pages referenced by the process during the most recent $\Delta$ memory references (or time window).

**Thrashing Prevention:**
- The OS monitors $WSS$ for each process.
- If the sum of working set sizes exceeds available frames ($\sum WSS_i > M$), the OS suspends one or more processes.
- This restores the inequality $\sum WSS_i \leq M$, preventing excessive paging.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`
**(a)** Explain the concept of thrashing in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss how the working set model is used to control thrashing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a system with **6 frames** and three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ having working set sizes **3, 4, and 2** respectively. Determine whether the system will thrash. Calculate the maximum safe multiprogramming level if the average working set size remains constant. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**1. Concept of Thrashing (3 marks):**
Thrashing occurs when a system is engaged in continuous paging activity. The phenomenon unfolds in a vicious cycle:

- The OS observes low CPU utilization.
- It admits more processes, reducing frames per process.
- Page fault frequency rises.
- Disk becomes the bottleneck.
- CPU utilization drops further — completing the cycle.

**2. Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.1** (Thrashing Feedback Loop). A neat labeled flowchart with the positive feedback loop is acceptable.

**3. Working Set Model (2 marks):**
- $W(t, \Delta)$ tracks the set of recently-used pages.
- The OS computes $WSS = \mid W(t, \Delta) \mid$.
- If $\sum WSS_i > M$, the OS **suspends** a process (medium-term scheduler) until memory is sufficient.
- This dynamically maintains the no-thrashing condition.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute total working set demand.

$$
D = WSS_1 + WSS_2 + WSS_3 = 3 + 4 + 2 = 9 \text{ frames}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with available frames.

$$
D = 9 \quad \text{vs.} \quad M = 6
$$

Since $D > M$:

$$
\boxed{\text{The system will THRASH.}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the average working set size.

$$
\overline{WSS} = \dfrac{9}{3} = 3 \text{ pages per process}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the maximum safe multiprogramming level.

$$
n_{\text{max}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{M}{\overline{WSS}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{6}{3} \right\rfloor = 2
$$

$$
\boxed{n_{\text{max}} = 2 \text{ processes}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The OS must limit the multiprogramming level to **2 processes** to avoid thrashing. The third process must be suspended (swapped out to disk).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternate Choice]`
**(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **Page Fault Frequency (PFF) strategy** for controlling thrashing. How does it differ from the working set model? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system has a memory access time of **200 ns** and an average page fault service time of **30 ms**. Calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT) when:
   - (i) Page fault rate $p = 0$ (no faults)
   - (ii) Page fault rate $p = 0.0001$
   - (iii) Page fault rate $p = 0.001$
   
   Comment on the impact of even a small page fault rate on system performance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**1. PFF Strategy Explanation (4 marks):**
The Page Fault Frequency strategy uses a running counter of page faults per process:

- The OS sets two thresholds: an **upper bound** and a **lower bound**.
- **If PFF > upper threshold:** The process is *thrashing-prone* — allocate it more frames (steal from less active processes).
- **If PFF < lower threshold:** The process has too many frames — reclaim excess frames.
- **If PFF > upper threshold AND no free frames exist:** Suspend the process entirely (medium-term scheduler invoked).

**2. Diagram (2 marks):**
A graph of PFF vs. number of frames allocated, showing the two horizontal threshold lines.

**3. Comparison with Working Set Model (1 mark):**

| Aspect | PFF Model | Working Set Model |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| Tracking | Page fault count over time | Pages referenced in last $\Delta$ references |
| Granularity | Indirect (fault count) | Direct (page-level tracking) |
| Overhead | Lower | Higher |

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:** $a = 200$ ns, $T_s = 30$ ms $= 30 \times 10^6$ ns.

**Formula:**

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot a + p \cdot T_s
$$

**Case (i): $p = 0$**

$$
EAT = (1 - 0)(200) + 0 \cdot (30 \times 10^6) = 200 \text{ ns}
$$

**Case (ii): $p = 0.0001$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - 0.0001)(200) + 0.0001 \cdot (30 \times 10^6) \\
&= (0.9999)(200) + 3000 \\
&= 199.98 + 3000 \\
&= 3199.98 \text{ ns} \approx 3.2 \; \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case (iii): $p = 0.001$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - 0.001)(200) + 0.001 \cdot (30 \times 10^6) \\
&= (0.999)(200) + 30{,}000 \\
&= 199.8 + 30{,}000 \\
&= 30{,}199.8 \text{ ns} \approx 30.2 \; \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Page Fault Rate $p$ | Effective Access Time | Slowdown vs. $p=0$ |
|:-------------------:|:---------------------:|:-------------------:|
| 0 | 200 ns | 1× (baseline) |
| 0.0001 | 3,200 ns | 16× |
| 0.001 | 30,200 ns | **151×** |

**Comment:**
Even a page fault rate as low as **0.001 (0.1%)** causes a **151× slowdown** in effective access time. This demonstrates that thrashing — characterized by elevated page fault rates — devastates system performance even when the fault rate appears "small" in absolute terms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — print this page the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Thrashing** = excessive paging causing CPU utilization collapse.
- ✅ Root cause: **$\sum WSS_i > M$** (total working set demand exceeds physical memory).
- ✅ **Vicious cycle**: low CPU → admit more → fewer frames per process → more faults → CPU idle.
- ✅ **Working Set $W(t,\Delta)$** = pages referenced in last $\Delta$ references (Denning, 1968).
- ✅ **Prevention Strategies**: (1) Swap out a process, (2) Working Set tracking, (3) PFF control, (4) Local replacement.
- ✅ **PFF Thresholds**: Upper bound → give more frames; Lower bound → reclaim frames.
- ✅ **Effective Access Time formula**: $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot a + p \cdot T_s$ — memorize cold.
- ✅ Typical numbers to remember: $a \approx 100\text{–}200$ ns, $T_s \approx 10\text{–}30$ ms.
- ✅ **Maximum safe multiprogramming level**: $n_{\text{max}} = \lfloor M / \overline{WSS} \rfloor$.
- ✅ Thrashing is **NOT** deadlock; thrashing is **NOT** starvation; thrashing is **NOT** swapping (though it involves heavy swapping).
- ✅ Always draw the **feedback loop diagram** in 14-mark questions.
- ✅ Use the **inequality symbol** $\sum WSS_i > M$ explicitly — examiners reward precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If the question says "explain with diagram" — *always* include a labeled flowchart. A diagram without labels loses marks; a textual description with arrows still earns 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: File Concept - Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** File Concept - Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a File

In the context of the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Operating Systems (PCITT403), a **File** is formally defined as:

> A named collection of related information that is recorded on secondary storage (such as a hard disk, SSD, or magnetic tape) and treated as a single logical entity by the operating system. From the user's perspective, a file is the smallest allotment of logical secondary storage; from the operating system's perspective, a file is a logical container mapped to a set of physical disk blocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**: A file is a *named* collection of *related* information recorded on *secondary storage*. It is the smallest logical storage unit visible to the user. This exact triplet (named, related, secondary storage) is a high-frequency exam phrase.

## 1.2 What are File Attributes?

**File Attributes** are the metadata — the structured descriptive data about a file — that the Operating System stores alongside (or about) the actual file content. The OS maintains this metadata in data structures such as **inodes (UNIX/Linux)**, **FCBs (File Control Blocks)**, or **MFT (Master File Table) entries (NTFS)**.

These attributes tell the OS *who owns the file*, *where it lives*, *how big it is*, *who can read it*, and *when it was last touched*.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Office Document Cabinet

Imagine a **physical office document cabinet** as your hard disk:

| Real-World Object | OS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A paper file inside the cabinet | A File (e.g., `report.pdf`) |
| The **tab label** sticking out of the folder | **File Name** attribute |
| The **barcode** printed on the folder | **Unique Identifier (Inode Number)** |
| The **color of the folder** (red = Finance, blue = HR) | **File Type** attribute |
| The **drawer & slot number** where the folder sits | **Location on Disk** attribute |
| The **thickness/weight** of the folder | **File Size** attribute |
| The **"Confidential: Director Only"** stamp | **Protection / Permission Bits** |
| The **date stamp** on the cover page | **Timestamps (Created/Modified/Accessed)** |
| The **employee ID** of the person who filed it | **User ID (Owner)** & **Group ID** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight**: The actual pages of the report are the **file data**. Everything else — the tab, barcode, color, date stamp, stamps — are **file attributes**. The OS never confuses data with attributes; they are stored in separate structures (typically the inode/FCB holds attributes, while data blocks hold content).

## 1.4 The Standard KTU Attribute Set

The seven canonical file attributes expected in any KTU 2024 OS exam answer are:

1. **Name** — Human-readable symbolic file name (e.g., `notes.txt`)
2. **Identifier** — Unique numeric tag (inode number) within the file system
3. **Type** — Indicates file format (text, binary, executable, directory)
4. **Location** — Pointer to the file's position on the storage device
5. **Size** — Current size of the file (and sometimes the allocated size)
6. **Protection** — Access control information (who can read/write/execute)
7. **Time, Date, and User Identification** — Timestamps + Owner ID + Group ID for bookkeeping

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Attribute Sheet

## 2.1 Exhaustive Breakdown of Each File Attribute

Below is a deep analytical breakdown of every attribute. Each is described in terms of **What it stores**, **Why it exists**, **How the OS uses it**, and **Engineering utility**.

### A. File Name
- **What**: A human-readable ASCII/Unicode string (e.g., `project_report.pdf`).
- **Why**: Humans cannot memorize inode numbers like `204813`. Names give semantic meaning.
- **How the OS uses it**: The OS resolves the name to an inode via directory lookups.
- **Engineering utility**: Search engines, file managers, command-line interfaces all rely on names.
- **KTU Fact**: Most modern file systems (NTFS, ext4, APFS) allow names up to **255 characters**.

### B. Identifier (Inode Number)
- **What**: A unique, non-negative integer assigned at file creation.
- **Why**: Names can be duplicated across directories, but the identifier is globally unique per file system.
- **How the OS uses it**: Acts as the primary key in the inode table; all disk block pointers and metadata are indexed by it.
- **Engineering utility**: Enables hard links (multiple names pointing to the same inode).

### C. Type
- **What**: An enumeration flag — regular file, directory, character device, block device, socket, symbolic link, FIFO.
- **Why**: The OS needs to know how to interpret and operate on the file.
- **How the OS uses it**: Determines which system calls are valid (e.g., `read()` works on regular files but not directories).
- **KTU Note**: UNIX uses a magic number in the first few bytes; Windows uses file extensions (`.exe`, `.txt`).

### D. Location
- **What**: A pointer (or set of pointers) to the actual disk blocks where data resides.
- **Why**: Decouples the logical view (name) from the physical view (cylinder/track/sector).
- **How the OS uses it**: Translates logical addresses to physical addresses via the file allocation table (FAT) or inode block pointers.
- **Engineering utility**: Allows the file to be **fragmented** (scattered across disk) while still being logically contiguous.

### E. Size
- **What**: Two fields typically exist — *current size* (logical bytes of user data) and *allocated size* (physical disk blocks reserved).
- **Why**: Sparse files can have logical size >> allocated size; the difference matters for disk quotas.
- **How the OS uses it**: Allocates additional blocks when the file grows; returns blocks when truncated.

### F. Protection
- **What**: A 9-bit (or 12-bit with ACLs) access control vector encoding Read/Write/Execute for **Owner**, **Group**, and **Others**.
- **Why**: Security — restricts unauthorized access in multi-user systems.
- **How the OS uses it**: Every `open()` system call checks the protection bits against the caller's UID/GID.
- **Engineering utility**: Foundation of OS security; base for ACLs, capabilities, and RBAC models.

### G. Time, Date, and User Identification
Three timestamps are nearly universal:
- **ctime / Creation time** — when the file was created.
- **mtime / Modification time** — when file *content* last changed.
- **atime / Access time** — when file was last read.

Plus: **Owner UID**, **Group GID**, and a link count.

> [!TIP]
> **Why three timestamps?** A backup system only needs to copy files whose `mtime` changed since the last backup — saving enormous time and disk space. A security audit checks `atime` to detect suspicious reads.

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Attribute Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table summarizes the high-yield facts every KTU 2024 OS student must memorize. Render with **\vert** in place of any vertical pipe.

| Attribute | Data Type / Size (Typical) | Example Value | OS Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| File Name | Variable string, up to **255 chars** (ext4) | `exam.pdf` | User-facing identification |
| Identifier (Inode) | **64-bit unsigned integer** (ext4) | `204813` | Unique internal key |
| Type | **4 to 16 bits** flag | `0x01 (regular)` | Determines valid operations |
| Location | Pointer array (12 direct + indirect) | Block \vert 8421 \vert | Maps logical \textrightarrow physical |
| Size (current) | **64-bit signed integer** | `2048 bytes` | `ls -l`, quotas |
| Size (allocated) | Blocks \times block-size | `4 \times 512 = 2048` | Disk-space accounting |
| Protection | **9-bit mode** \vert 3-bit sticky/setuid | `755` (rwxr-xr-x) | Access control |
| ctime / mtime / atime | 64-bit Unix epoch | `1719484800` | Backups, audits |
| Owner UID | **32-bit integer** | `1001` | Permission matching |
| Group GID | **32-bit integer** | `1001` | Permission matching |
| Link Count | **16-bit unsigned integer** | `1` | Detects orphan inodes |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database engines (MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL)** store every table as a file with attributes like `tablespace_id`, `page_count`, and `checksum` — direct extensions of the classic OS file attribute model.
- **Cloud storage systems (AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage)** expose file attributes as object metadata (`Content-Type`, `Last-Modified`, `ETag`) for lifecycle policies and search indexing.
- **Distributed file systems (HDFS, Ceph)** replicate file attributes across metadata servers to ensure consistency and fault tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Protection Bits to Octal Notation

The **protection field** is one of the most frequently tested attributes. Let us derive the canonical conversion from binary permission bits to the octal notation used by `chmod`.

### Step 1: Define the Three Permission Classes

The 9-bit protection field is divided into three consecutive triplets:

$$
\text{Protection} = \underbrace{r\,w\,x}_{\text{Owner}} \quad \underbrace{r\,w\,x}_{\text{Group}} \quad \underbrace{r\,w\,x}_{\text{Others}}
$$

Each `r`, `w`, `x` is a single bit with the fixed positional weight:

$$
\text{Weight}(r) = 2^2 = 4, \quad \text{Weight}(w) = 2^1 = 2, \quad \text{Weight}(x) = 2^0 = 1
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Numeric Value of a Triplet

For any triplet, the numeric value is the sum of weights where the bit is `1`.

**Example A** — Full permissions for owner: `rwx = 111`

$$
N_{\text{owner}} = (1 \times 4) + (1 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7
$$

**Example B** — Read & execute only for group: `r-x = 101`

$$
N_{\text{group}} = (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5
$$

**Example C** — Read only for others: `r-- = 100`

$$
N_{\text{others}} = (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (0 \times 1) = 4 + 0 + 0 = 4
$$

### Step 3: Concatenate the Three Triplets into a Three-Digit Octal

$$
\text{Mode}_{\text{octal}} = N_{\text{owner}} \, N_{\text{group}} \, N_{\text{others}}
$$

Substituting our computed values:

$$
\text{Mode}_{\text{octal}} = 7 \, 5 \, 4 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \texttt{rwx r-x r--}
$$

### Step 4: General Formula

For any triplet with bits $(b_2, b_1, b_0)$:

$$
N = b_2 \cdot 2^2 + b_1 \cdot 2^1 + b_0 \cdot 2^0
$$

The complete protection mode is the triplet $(N_{\text{owner}}, N_{\text{group}}, N_{\text{others}})$, producing a number in the inclusive range `[0, 7]` for each class, hence the full range `[000, 777]` octal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick**: They may write the mode in **symbolic form** (e.g., `-rwxr-xr--`) and ask you to convert to **octal** (754), or vice versa. Practice both directions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Allocated Disk Size from Logical File Size

When a file of logical size $S$ bytes is stored on a file system with **block size** $B$ bytes, the **allocated (physical) size** $A$ is given by:

$$
A = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil \times B
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling function.

### Worked Example

**Given**: A file with $S = 5000$ bytes, block size $B = 1024$ bytes.

**Step 1** — Compute the number of blocks required:

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5000}{1024} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2** — Evaluate the fraction:

$$
\frac{5000}{1024} = 4.8828125
$$

**Step 3** — Apply the ceiling function (round up to the next integer):

$$
n = \lceil 4.8828125 \rceil = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 4** — Compute the allocated size:

$$
A = 5 \times 1024 = 5120 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5** — Compute the internal fragmentation (wasted space):

$$
W = A - S = 5120 - 5000 = 120 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: Internal fragmentation is unavoidable in block-based file systems. It is why very small files (e.g., a 1-byte file on a 4 KiB block system) waste ~4095 bytes — a key design trade-off in choosing block size for SSDs vs HDDs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum File Size from Inode Block Pointers (Classic UNIX ext2/ext3)

The classic UNIX inode uses **12 direct block pointers**, **1 single-indirect**, **1 double-indirect**, and **1 triple-indirect** pointer. Given block size $B$ and address-word size $w$ bytes:

**Direct data capacity**:

$$
C_{\text{direct}} = 12 \times B
$$

**Single-indirect capacity** (each entry points to a block of $B/w$ further pointers):

$$
C_{\text{single}} = \left(\frac{B}{w}\right) \times B
$$

**Double-indirect capacity**:

$$
C_{\text{double}} = \left(\frac{B}{w}\right)^2 \times B
$$

**Triple-indirect capacity**:

$$
C_{\text{triple}} = \left(\frac{B}{w}\right)^3 \times B
$$

**Total maximum file size**:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{direct}} + C_{\text{single}} + C_{\text{double}} + C_{\text{triple}}
$$

### Numerical Plug-In (B = 1024 bytes, w = 4 bytes)

$$
C_{\text{direct}} = 12 \times 1024 = 12\,288 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{\text{single}} = \left(\frac{1024}{4}\right) \times 1024 = 256 \times 1024 = 262\,144 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{\text{double}} = (256)^2 \times 1024 = 65\,536 \times 1024 = 67\,108\,864 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{\text{triple}} = (256)^3 \times 1024 = 16\,777\,216 \times 1024 = 17\,179\,869\,184 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} \approx 17.18 \text{ GB} \quad (\text{for 1 KiB blocks, 32-bit pointers})
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Modern reality**: Modern ext4 uses **48-bit pointers** and **extent-based** indexing, pushing the limit to **1 EiB** (exbibyte) and beyond — but the structural principle remains identical.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: File Attribute Storage in Different File Systems

| File System | Attribute Container | Max File Size | Timestamp Granularity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FAT32** | 32-byte directory entry | 4 GiB \minus 1 | 2 seconds | No ownership; uses attributes byte |
| **NTFS** | Master File Table (MFT) record | 16 EiB | 100 ns | Owner + ACL + multiple streams |
| **ext2/ext3** | Inode (128 bytes) | 2 GiB (32-bit) | 1 second | Classic 12+1+1+1 pointer scheme |
| **ext4** | Inode (256 bytes) | 16 TiB | nanosecond | Extents replace triple-indirect |
| **APFS** | Object ID + B-tree | 8 EiB | nanosecond | Clone-on-write, snapshots |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Where File Attributes Live in the OS

Below is a high-fidelity Mermaid block diagram illustrating how file attributes are stored, retrieved, and used during a typical file operation (e.g., `open()`).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U[User Application e.g. open report.txt]:::user
    SYSCALL[System Call Interface open]:::sys
    VFS[VFS Layer Virtual File System]:::vfs
    DENTRY[Directory Entry Cache dentry]:::cache
    INODE[Inode / FCB Metadata Structure]:::inode
    ATTR[(File Attributes Table)]:::attr
    POINTER[Block Pointers Direct/Indirect]:::ptr
    DATABLK[(Data Blocks on Disk)]:::data

    U --> SYSCALL
    SYSCALL --> VFS
    VFS --> DENTRY
    DENTRY --> INODE
    INODE --> ATTR
    INODE --> POINTER
    POINTER --> DATABLK

    ATTR -.contains.-> A1[Name]
    ATTR -.contains.-> A2[Inode Number]
    ATTR -.contains.-> A3[Type]
    ATTR -.contains.-> A4[Location Pointer]
    ATTR -.contains.-> A5[Size]
    ATTR -.contains.-> A6[Protection Mode]
    ATTR -.contains.-> A7[Timestamps ctime mtime atime]
    ATTR -.contains.-> A8[Owner UID GID]

    classDef user fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#e2ecef,stroke:#3d5a80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef vfs fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cache fill:#cddafd,stroke:#3d5a80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inode fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef attr fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ptr fill:#ffadad,stroke:#9b2226,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#5f0f40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this**: When a user calls `open("report.txt")`, the **VFS** resolves the name via the **directory entry**, fetches the **inode**, reads its **attributes** (for permission checks, ownership, type), and follows the **block pointers** to reach the **data blocks**. Attributes are evaluated *before* any data is read — which is why permission denial happens instantly.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Permission Check Using File Attributes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[User opens file with UID u and GID g]:::user
    P2[OS reads file protection mode]:::sys
    P3{Compare u with file Owner UID}:::decide
    P4[Apply Owner triplet rwx bits]:::apply
    P5{Compare g with file Group GID}:::decide
    P6[Apply Group triplet rwx bits]:::apply
    P7[Apply Others triplet rwx bits]:::apply
    P8[Requested operation permitted?]:::decide
    P9[Access GRANTED]:::ok
    P10[Access DENIED EACCES]:::err

    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 -- Match --> P4 --> P8
    P3 -- No match --> P5
    P5 -- Match --> P6 --> P8
    P5 -- No match --> P7 --> P8
    P8 -- Yes --> P9
    P8 -- No --> P10

    classDef user fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#e2ecef,stroke:#3d5a80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decide fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef apply fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#ffadad,stroke:#9b2226,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaluation order is strict**: **Owner → Group → Others**. A user who is the owner always gets the Owner bits, even if they also belong to the group that would have granted more access. This is a classic KTU viva trap.

---

## 4.3 Attribute Lifecycle Across File Operations

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created: open O_CREAT
    Created --> Open: fd returned
    Open --> Read: read syscall
    Read --> Open: atime updated
    Open --> Write: write syscall
    Write --> Open: mtime and ctime updated size updated
    Open --> Closed: close syscall
    Closed --> [*]

    note right of Read: Attribute touched: atime
    note right of Write: Attributes touched: mtime, ctime, size
    note left of Created: Attributes initialized: name, inode, type=regular, protection=default, owner=UID, timestamps=now
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define a file. List any six file attributes maintained by the operating system.**

**Model Answer**:
A **file** is a named collection of related information that is recorded on secondary storage and is the smallest logical unit of storage visible to a user.

Six file attributes maintained by the OS are:
1. **Name** — human-readable label.
2. **Identifier** — unique numeric tag (inode number).
3. **Type** — indicates whether regular, directory, device, etc.
4. **Location** — pointer to physical storage location.
5. **Size** — current size in bytes.
6. **Protection** — access rights for owner/group/others.

*(Any six of the seven attributes listed in Section 1.4 accepted.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Repeated Pattern]`
**Explain the significance of the three timestamps ctime, mtime, and atime stored as file attributes.**

**Model Answer**:
- **ctime (Creation time)**: Set once when the file is created. Indicates when the inode entry first appeared. Helps in auditing file origins.
- **mtime (Modification time)**: Updated whenever the file's *content* changes. Used by backup utilities to perform **incremental backups** — only files whose `mtime > last_backup_time` are copied.
- **atime (Access time)**: Updated whenever the file is read. Used by caching algorithms to identify rarely-accessed files for eviction and by security auditors to detect suspicious reads.

Together, these three attributes enable efficient backup strategies, cache management, and security forensics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Describe the various file attributes stored by the operating system. Explain how the **protection** attribute is encoded using the UNIX 9-bit permission model. Convert the symbolic mode `-rwxr-x---` into its octal representation with full derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A file has logical size **5000 bytes** and is stored on a file system with block size **1024 bytes**. Calculate the **allocated disk size**, the **number of disk blocks** used, and the **internal fragmentation**. Explain why internal fragmentation is unavoidable in block-based file systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** [7 marks — awarded as: 3 marks listing attributes, 2 marks protection model, 2 marks conversion]

**Listing the attributes** (any 6 of the following for full marks): Name, Identifier, Type, Location, Size, Protection, Timestamps, Owner UID, Group GID.

**Protection encoding (UNIX 9-bit model)**:

The protection attribute is a 9-bit field divided into three consecutive triplets, one per access class:

$$
\text{Protection} = \underbrace{r\,w\,x}_{\text{Owner (7-bit)}} \quad \underbrace{r\,w\,x}_{\text{Group (5-bit)}} \quad \underbrace{r\,w\,x}_{\text{Others (1-bit)}}
$$

The bit weights are fixed:

$$
r = 4, \quad w = 2, \quad x = 1
$$

**Conversion of `-rwxr-x---` to octal**:

Step 1 — Strip the leading file-type character (`-` indicates a regular file); we work only with the 9 permission characters.

Step 2 — Split into triplets:

$$
\text{Owner} = r\,w\,x = 1\,1\,1, \quad \text{Group} = r\text{-}x = 1\,0\,1, \quad \text{Others} = \text{-}\text{-}\text{-} = 0\,0\,0
$$

Step 3 — Compute each octal digit:

$$
N_{\text{owner}} = (1 \times 4) + (1 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 7
$$

$$
N_{\text{group}} = (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 5
$$

$$
N_{\text{others}} = (0 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (0 \times 1) = 0
$$

Step 4 — Concatenate:

$$
\text{Octal Mode} = 7\,5\,0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \texttt{rwxr-x---}
$$

This corresponds to the command `chmod 750 file.txt` — owner has full access, group can read & execute, others have no access.

**(b)** [7 marks — 4 marks calculation, 3 marks explanation]

**Calculation**:

Given $S = 5000$ bytes, $B = 1024$ bytes.

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5000}{1024} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.8828... \rceil = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

$$
A = n \times B = 5 \times 1024 = 5120 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
W = A - S = 5120 - 5000 = 120 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Answer**: **5 blocks**, **allocated size = 5120 bytes**, **fragmentation = 120 bytes**.

**Why internal fragmentation is unavoidable**:
File systems allocate storage in fixed-size blocks to simplify addressing (block number × block size gives the byte offset). Because most files do not have sizes that are exact multiples of the block size, the **last block** is only partially filled. The unused portion of this last block is wasted — this is **internal fragmentation**. The only way to eliminate it would be byte-level allocation, which is infeasible due to massive metadata overhead and slow random access. Hence, block-based file systems *always* exhibit internal fragmentation, bounded above by $(B - 1)$ bytes per file.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Alternate Choice]`
**(a)** Explain the structure of a **UNIX inode** as a file attribute container. With reference to the **12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect** block pointer scheme, derive the maximum file size supported for a system with **block size 1024 bytes** and **4-byte pointers**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of file attributes in implementing **access control** and **backup strategies**. Illustrate with one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** [7 marks — 3 marks inode structure, 4 marks derivation]

**Inode Structure**:

A UNIX inode is a fixed-size data structure (typically **128 bytes** in ext2, **256 bytes** in ext4) stored in a dedicated inode table on disk. It is the canonical attribute container for a single file. The fields include:

- File type (regular, directory, device, etc.)
- Mode / protection (9 permission bits + sticky/setuid/setgid)
- Owner UID and Group GID
- File size (logical bytes)
- Three timestamps: ctime, mtime, atime
- Link count (number of hard links)
- Block count (number of 512-byte sectors used)
- **Address array**: 15 block pointers used to locate file data.

**Maximum File Size Derivation**:

Given $B = 1024$ bytes, $w = 4$ bytes (32-bit pointers).

The number of pointers that fit in one block:

$$
p = \frac{B}{w} = \frac{1024}{4} = 256
$$

**Capacity of each pointer class**:

$$
C_{\text{direct}} = 12 \times B = 12 \times 1024 = 12\,288 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{\text{single}} = p \times B = 256 \times 1024 = 262\,144 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{\text{double}} = p^2 \times B = 256^2 \times 1024 = 67\,108\,864 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{\text{triple}} = p^3 \times B = 256^3 \times 1024 = 17\,179\,869\,184 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Total maximum file size**:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{direct}} + C_{\text{single}} + C_{\text{double}} + C_{\text{triple}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 12\,288 + 262\,144 + 67\,108\,864 + 17\,179\,869\,184
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 17\,247\,252\,480 \text{ bytes} \approx 16.06 \text{ GiB}
$$

**Answer**: The maximum file size is **≈ 17.25 GB** (≈ 16.06 GiB).

**(b)** [7 marks — 3.5 marks each example]

**Role of attributes in access control**:

The **protection attribute** (9-bit mode + UID + GID) is consulted on every `open()`, `read()`, `write()` call. The OS compares the requesting user's UID/GID against the file's stored owner/group values and grants access only if the corresponding permission bit is set. This implements the principle of least privilege at the file level.

*Real-world example*: In a hospital information system, only doctors (group `medical`) may read patient records (`-rw-r-----`), while the lab technician (group `lab`) can append lab results but not view other patient histories. This is enforced entirely through file attributes.

**Role of attributes in backup strategies**:

The **mtime (modification time)** attribute is the cornerstone of incremental backup. A backup tool queries the OS for `mtime` of every file; only files whose `mtime > last_backup_timestamp` are copied. This minimizes backup time, network bandwidth, and storage.

*Real-world example*: `rsync` on Linux implements the `--update` and `--checksum` flags precisely on top of `mtime` — saving terabytes of redundant transfers nightly across data centers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Wrong evaluation order for permissions**: Students often apply Group bits when the user is also the Owner. The rule is strict: **Owner check first**. If matched, *only* the Owner bits are used.
> 2. **Forgetting internal fragmentation**: Many students compute allocated size but forget the *waste* term $W = A - S$. Examiners explicitly allocate **2 marks** for this.
> 3. **Confusing atime and mtime**: A `read()` updates **atime** only; a `write()` updates both **mtime and ctime**. Mixing these up costs 1 mark easily.
> 4. **Ceiling vs. floor in block allocation**: Always **ceiling** for "how many blocks does this file need?" — floor would truncate the last byte.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **File**: A *named* collection of *related* information on *secondary storage* — smallest logical storage unit.
- **Seven core attributes** to memorize: Name, Identifier, Type, Location, Size, Protection, Time/Date/User IDs.
- **Identifier (inode number)** is the OS-internal unique key — names are *not* unique.
- **Protection = 9 bits** in 3 triplets (Owner, Group, Others) of (r, w, x) with weights (4, 2, 1), giving an octal range of **000 to 777**.
- **Evaluation order for permissions**: **Owner → Group → Others** (strict, never overridden).
- **Three timestamps**: `ctime` (creation), `mtime` (content change), `atime` (read) — each updated by different operations.
- **Block allocation formula**: $A = \lceil S/B \rceil \times B$; internal fragmentation $W = A - S$.
- **Maximum file size in classic UNIX** = $12B + (B/w)B + (B/w)^2 B + (B/w)^3 B$.
- **Modern reality**: ext4, NTFS, APFS use extent-based or B-tree-based indexing for scalability up to EiBs.
- **Engineering uses**: Access control, incremental backups (`mtime`), cache eviction (`atime`), forensics (`ctime`), disk quotas (`size`).
- **Diagram reading tip**: Always remember that attributes (inode) and data (data blocks) are *separate* — permission checks happen on the inode before any data block is read.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operations

# 🗂️ FILE & DIRECTORY OPERATIONS

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> In the context of an Operating System, **Operations** refer to the set of **system calls (primitive kernel services)** that a user process invokes to interact with secondary storage entities — primarily **files** and **directories**. These operations form the Application Programming Interface (API) between the user-level file system interface and the underlying file system implementation, and they govern creation, manipulation, retrieval, and deletion of persistent data objects.

### 1.1 The Two Broad Categories of Operations

| Category | Sub-Operations | Kernel Object Affected |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|
| **File Operations** | Create, Open, Close, Read, Write, Append, Seek, Truncate, Delete, Rename | Individual file |
| **Directory Operations** | Create, Delete, Opendir, Readdir, Rename, Link, Unlink | Directory entry (i-node link) |
| **File System Operations** | Mount, Unmount, Sync, Stat | File system as a whole |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Library of Records 🏛️**
> Imagine a massive digital library. The **books** are *files* (containing data), the **catalogue drawers** are *directories* (indexed records of book locations), and the **librarian** is the *Operating System kernel*. You (the *user process*) cannot directly walk into the stacks and grab a book. Instead, you fill out a request slip — that slip is your **system call**. The librarian walks into the stacks, fetches the book, marks the ledger that you have it (the **open file table entry**), and hands it to your reading desk. When you finish, you return the book and the librarian clears the ledger entry. Every read, write, rename, or delete is another request slip. **All file/directory operations in an OS follow this "request–service–ledger" model.**

### 1.2 Why Are "Operations" Important in KTU Examinations?

In KTU board examinations, questions on "Operations" typically test the following cognitive levels (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy aligned with the 2024 scheme):

- **L1 – Remember:** Naming the six basic file operations.
- **L2 – Understand:** Explaining why `open()` and `close()` are required even though read/write suffice logically.
- **L3 – Apply:** Tracing the contents of **Open File Tables** during simultaneous file access.
- **L4 – Analyze:** Differentiating between *hard links* and *symbolic links* using reference counts.
- **L5 – Evaluate:** Comparing **lock-based protection** vs **permission-based protection** in a shared file scenario.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Keyword:** Any question phrased as *"Explain the various file operations the OS provides"* almost always expects the **six basic operations + open + close + append**, followed by an explanation of the **open file table structure**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Classic File Operations (Dijkstra's Model)

Modern operating systems extend the classical **Dijkstra six-operation model**. Each operation is a kernel-level system call:

1. **Create a File** — `creat()` / `open(..., O_CREAT)`
2. **Write a File** — `write(fd, buffer, n)`
3. **Read a File** — `read(fd, buffer, n)`
4. **Reposition (Seek) within a File** — `lseek(fd, offset, whence)`
5. **Delete a File** — `unlink()` / `remove()`
6. **Truncate a File** — `truncate()` / `ftruncate()`

Two additional modern operations are mandatory in all contemporary OS kernels:

7. **Open a File** — `open(pathname, flags, mode)`
8. **Close a File** — `close(fd)`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Crucial Question KTU Loves to Ask:** *"If a process already has read() and write(), why is open() necessary?"*
>
> **Answer:** The `open()` call performs three expensive tasks that read/write cannot afford to repeat on every call:
> 1. **Search** the directory structure (pathname resolution) and locate the file's i-node.
> 2. **Verify** the calling process's access permissions against the file's protection bits.
> 3. **Allocate** an entry in the *Per-Process Open File Table* and the *System-Wide Open File Table*, and return a non-negative integer **file descriptor (fd)**.
>
> Subsequent read/write/seek use the cheap file descriptor (an integer index) and skip these three steps.

## 2.2 Open File Table Architecture — The Heart of All File Operations

The kernel maintains **two distinct open file tables** to manage simultaneous access:

| Table | Scope | Per Entry Contains | Lifetime |
|-------|-------|---------------------|----------|
| **Per-Process Open File Table** | Private to each process | File descriptor number, pointer to system-wide table entry, file pointer (current offset), access mode flags | Process lifetime |
| **System-Wide Open File Table** | Global, shared across all processes | Disk location of file (i-node pointer), **Open Count** (number of processes sharing), file size, access mode, lock information | From first open() to last close() |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The *per-process table* is like your personal bookmark telling you *which page you are on* in a library book. The *system-wide table* is the librarian's master ledger that says *how many readers currently have the book checked out*.

### 2.3 The File Descriptor (fd) Concept

When a process calls `open()`, the kernel:
- Returns the **lowest unused non-negative integer** (0, 1, 2 are reserved for stdin, stdout, stderr in POSIX).
- This integer is an **index** into the per-process open file table.

$$ \text{fd} \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots, \text{OPEN\_MAX} - 1\} $$

The **Open Count** in the system-wide table is incremented. When all processes close, the count drops to zero, the system-wide entry is deallocated, and the file's resources (including the i-node) are released — *unless* the link count is non-zero (because hard links keep the file alive on disk).

## 2.4 Directory Operations — Beyond Simple Files

Directories are special files storing <filename, i-node> mappings. The standard directory operations are:

| Operation | Purpose | Kernel Action |
|-----------|---------|----------------|
| `mkdir(name)` | Create a new directory | Allocate new i-node, set type=directory, add entry to parent |
| `rmdir(name)` | Remove an empty directory | Verify empty, decrement parent's link count, free i-node |
| `opendir(path)` | Open directory for reading | Return DIR* stream handle |
| `readdir(DIR*)` | Read one entry | Return next <name, i-node> pair |
| `closedir(DIR*)` | Close the stream | Release directory search state |
| `link(old, new)` | Create a hard link | Add new dir entry pointing to same i-node, **increment link count** |
| `unlink(path)` | Remove a link | Decrement link count; free i-node only when count = 0 |

### 2.5 The Link Count (Reference Count) — KTU's Favourite Numerical Concept

The **link count** is stored in the i-node and governs file lifetime on disk:

$$ \text{File persists on disk} \iff \text{link\_count} > 0 $$

When a process opens a file, the **Open Count** rises. When the last process closes, the Open Count drops, but the file is not deleted unless `unlink()` has also driven the **Link Count** to zero. This separation is what allows a process to read a file that another process is currently deleting, provided the reading process already holds an open file descriptor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *Open Count* with *Link Count*. Remember:
> - **Open Count** → how many *processes* have the file open (memory state, in system-wide table).
> - **Link Count** → how many *directory entries* point to the file (disk state, in i-node).

## 2.6 File Protection via Locking Operations

For shared files, the OS provides two locking primitives:

| Lock Type | Scope | Behavior |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| **Shared Lock (Read Lock)** | Multiple readers allowed | `read` allowed, `write` blocked |
| **Exclusive Lock (Write Lock)** | Only one writer | `read` blocked, `write` allowed |

These translate into the POSIX advisory locking calls `flock()` and `fcntl(F_SETLK)`.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master the following table — every entry is a potential 2–3 mark question.**

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Variable Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|----------------|
| File Descriptor Validity | $\text{fd} \in [0, \text{OPEN\_MAX} - 1]$ | $\text{OPEN\_MAX}$ is the kernel constant (e.g., 1024 on Linux) | Integer index |
| Total Active Opens | $O = \sum_{p \in P} \text{opens}_p$ | $O$ = system-wide open count, $P$ = process set | Count (≥ 1) |
| File Lifetime Condition | $\text{disk\_exists} \iff L > 0$ | $L$ = link count stored in i-node | Boolean |
| File Reclaimable | $\text{reclaimable} \iff (L = 0) \land (O = 0)$ | Both link and open counts zero | Boolean |
| Effective File Size After Truncate | $S_{new} = \min(S_{old}, \text{offset})$ | $S$ = size, $\text{offset}$ = current write position | Bytes |
| Read/Write Byte Count | $n_{transferred} \leq n_{requested}$ | Actual bytes may be less than requested | Bytes |
| File Pointer Update | $P_{new} = P_{old} + n_{transferred}$ | Sequential read/write advances pointer | Bytes offset |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL):** Use `open()` + `flock()` to ensure only one writer modifies a table file, preventing data corruption.
- **Compilers:** Open dozens of source/header files simultaneously using many file descriptors — bounded by `OPEN_MAX`.
- **Operating System Kernels:** `mount()` and `unmount()` are critical operations for hot-pluggable storage (USB drives, cloud volumes) in cloud infrastructure (AWS EBS, Azure Disks).
- **Version Control (Git):** Implements hard links and symbolic links for efficient object storage in the `.git/objects` directory.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following worked examples build the analytical intuition required to solve KTU 14-mark problems. Every algebraic and logical step is explicitly expanded.**

## 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Sequential File Operations on Kernel State

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question):**
> Two processes, P1 and P2, execute the following code segment concurrently. Show the state of both the **Per-Process Open File Tables** and the **System-Wide Open File Table** after each step.

```
P1:  fd1 = open("data.txt", O_RDWR);          // Step 1
     read(fd1, buf, 100);                      // Step 2
     lseek(fd1, 200, SEEK_SET);                // Step 3
     write(fd1, buf, 50);                      // Step 4
     close(fd1);                               // Step 5

P2:  fd2 = open("data.txt", O_RDONLY);         // Step 6
     read(fd2, buf, 80);                       // Step 7
     close(fd2);                                // Step 8
```

**Assumption:** `data.txt` is initially a fresh file of size **0 bytes** on disk, with link count **1**.

### Step-by-Step State Evolution

**Initial State (Before Step 1):**

- *Per-Process Table of P1:* empty (no entries).
- *Per-Process Table of P2:* empty.
- *System-Wide Open File Table:* no entry for `data.txt`.
- *I-node of data.txt:* `{link_count = 1, size = 0, disk_blocks = {...}}`.

---

**Step 1 — `P1: open("data.txt", O_RDWR)`**

- Kernel searches the directory, finds i-node of `data.txt`.
- Permission check: P1 has read+write access → **granted**.
- Allocates **Entry-A** in the System-Wide Open File Table:
  - i-node pointer → `data.txt` i-node.
  - **Open Count = 1**.
  - access mode = `O_RDWR`.
- Allocates first free slot in P1's Per-Process Table → **fd = 3** (assuming 0,1,2 occupied).
- P1's Per-Process Table:

| fd | System-Wide Pointer | File Pointer | Access Mode |
|----|----------------------|--------------|-------------|
| 3  | → Entry-A            | 0            | RDWR        |

- System-Wide Table:

| Entry | i-node | Open Count | Disk Location |
|-------|--------|------------|----------------|
| A     | data.txt | 1        | (metadata)    |

---

**Step 2 — `P1: read(fd1=3, buf, 100)`**

- Since `size = 0` and no data has been written, the read returns **0 bytes** (`n_transferred = 0`).
- File pointer remains at **0** because: $P_{new} = P_{old} + n_{transferred} = 0 + 0 = 0$.
- No change in any table.

---

**Step 3 — `P1: lseek(fd1=3, 200, SEEK_SET)`**

- The seek call **sets the file pointer absolutely** to offset 200.
- $P_{new} = 200$ (SEEK_SET ignores the current offset).
- No data is read or written — this is purely a pointer operation.
- **KTU Pitfall:** A seek **does not** move the disk head; it merely updates the per-process file pointer.

---

**Step 4 — `P1: write(fd1=3, buf, 50)`**

- Writes 50 bytes starting at offset 200.
- The kernel may need to allocate new disk blocks (sparse file or block extension).
- File size update: $S_{new} = \max(S_{old}, P_{write\_end}) = \max(0, 200 + 50) = 250$ bytes.
- File pointer update: $P_{new} = 200 + 50 = 250$.
- I-node size field becomes **250**.

---

**Step 5 — `P1: close(fd1=3)`**

- Decrements system-wide **Open Count** from 1 to **0**.
- Marks P1's Per-Process Table entry fd=3 as free.
- **Does NOT delete the file** because `link_count = 1 > 0` (i-node still referenced by directory).

---

**Step 6 — `P2: open("data.txt", O_RDONLY)`**

- New permission check: P2 requests read-only access → **granted** (subset of RDWR).
- Allocates **Entry-B** in the System-Wide Open File Table.
  - *Important:* A **new** system-wide entry is created because access mode is different (`O_RDONLY` vs `O_RDWR`). The kernel tracks mode per system-wide entry.
  - Actually, in strict POSIX semantics, the system-wide entry stores the union of modes; some implementations reuse if access is a subset. For KTU, we assume a **new entry B is created** for clarity.
- Allocates first free slot in P2's Per-Process Table → **fd = 3**.
- System-Wide Table now:

| Entry | i-node | Open Count | Access Mode |
|-------|--------|------------|-------------|
| A     | data.txt | 0 (marked free) | RDWR     |
| B     | data.txt | 1          | RDONLY    |

---

**Step 7 — `P2: read(fd2=3, buf, 80)`**

- P2's file pointer starts at 0.
- Reads **80 bytes** from offsets [0, 79] into buf.
- $n_{transferred} = 80$.
- P2's file pointer: $P_{new} = 0 + 80 = 80$.

---

**Step 8 — `P2: close(fd2=3)`**

- Decrements system-wide Open Count (Entry B) from 1 to **0**.
- P2's Per-Process Table entry fd=3 is freed.
- I-node of `data.txt` still has `link_count = 1` and is **NOT deleted** from disk.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for File Operations

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the most concise way to answer "explain the effect of operation X on kernel state" — a recurring KTU pattern.**

| Operation | Per-Process Table Effect | System-Wide Table Effect | I-node / Disk Effect | Link Count Change |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| `create()` | New fd allocated | New entry, Open Count = 1 | New i-node allocated | +1 (parent dir entry) |
| `open()` | New fd allocated | New entry (or reuse), Open Count + 1 | None | 0 |
| `read()` | File pointer += n | None | None | 0 |
| `write()` | File pointer += n | None | Blocks updated, size may grow | 0 |
| `lseek()` | File pointer = offset | None | None | 0 |
| `close()` | fd freed | Open Count − 1 (deallocate if 0) | None | 0 |
| `unlink()` | None directly | None | Directory entry removed | −1 |
| `truncate()` | None | None | Size reduced, blocks freed | 0 |
| `link()` | None | None | New directory entry added | +1 |

## 3.3 Symbolic Derivation — The "Delete a File" Atomic Operation

In many OS implementations, deleting a file is a **two-step atomic operation**:

1. Decrement the link count: $L \leftarrow L - 1$.
2. Check reclaim condition: $\text{if } (L = 0) \land (O = 0) \text{ then deallocate i-node and data blocks}$.

The complete Boolean reclaim condition is:

$$ \text{deallocate}(i) \;=\; (L_i = 0) \;\land\; (O_i = 0) $$

where $L_i$ is the link count and $O_i$ is the open count of i-node $i$. Both must be zero for the file to be reclaimed by the disk space allocator. This derivation is the basis of the famous KTU question: *"Why does an open file continue to exist on disk even after it has been deleted using `rm`?"*

> [!TIP]
> **Answer Template for KTU:** "The file persists because the Open Count $O$ is still non-zero while the process holds an open file descriptor. The reclaim condition $(L = 0) \land (O = 0)$ is not yet satisfied. When the process calls `close()`, $O$ becomes zero, and only then will the file's disk blocks be returned to the free-space list."

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of File Operations

The following Mermaid diagram depicts how a user-level `write()` call propagates through the kernel layers to the physical disk — a classic 14-mark KTU question structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Application Code: write fd, buf, n"] --> B["POSIX Library Wrapper: libc write syscall"]
    B --> C["System Call Interface: Trap to Kernel Mode"]
```
