```mermaid
    C --> D["Virtual File System VFS Layer: Resolve fd to file object"]
    D --> E["Per-Process Open File Table Lookup: Retrieve file pointer and mode"]
    E --> F["System-Wide Open File Table Lookup: Locate i-node pointer"]
    F --> G["File System Specific Layer: Buffer Cache, Block Allocation"]
    G --> H["Device Driver Interface: Issue Disk Write Request"]
    H --> I["Disk Controller: Physical Write to Platter or SSD"]
    I --> J["Return Status: Bytes Written or Error Code"]
    J --> K["Update Per-Process File Pointer: P = P plus n"]
    K --> L["Return to User Process: write returns n"]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#831843,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#831843,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Open File Table State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Process P1: Per-Process Table"] -->|"fd=3"| PT1["File Pointer = 250, Mode = RDWR"]
    P2["Process P2: Per-Process Table"] -->|"fd=3"| PT2["File Pointer = 80, Mode = RDONLY"]

    PT1 -->|"pointer to"| SW1["System-Wide Entry A: i-node, Open Count"]
    PT2 -->|"pointer to"| SW2["System-Wide Entry B: i-node, Open Count"]

    SW1 --> INODE["I-node of data.txt: link count, size, block pointers"]
    SW2 --> INODE

    INODE --> DISK["Physical Disk Blocks"]

    style P1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style PT1 fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style PT2 fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style SW1 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style SW2 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style INODE fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style DISK fill:#831843,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 File Lifecycle State Machine (Create → Use → Delete)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NonExistent
    NonExistent --> Created: create syscall
    Created --> Open: open syscall
    Open --> ReadWrite: read or write syscall
    ReadWrite --> ReadWrite: lseek plus read or write
    ReadWrite --> Closed: close syscall
    Closed --> Open: open syscall again
    Closed --> Deleted: unlink and link count zero
    Deleted --> [*]

    note right of Created: I-node allocated, link count = 1
    note right of Open: Open count increments
    note right of Deleted: I-node freed, blocks returned to free list
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "open-close" pair:** Many students list only the six Dijkstra operations and lose 3–4 marks. Always include `open()` and `close()` in any "list of file operations" answer.
> 2. **Confusing link count with open count:** A classic 2-mark trap. The link count is a *disk attribute*; the open count is a *memory attribute*. They serve different lifetime purposes.
> 3. **Skipping the file descriptor return value:** When explaining `open()`, you must mention that a **non-negative integer file descriptor** is returned on success, and −1 on failure (`errno` set).
> 4. **Missing the open file table layer:** Tracing a 14-mark scenario question without showing both *per-process* and *system-wide* tables will cost you 4–5 marks easily.
> 5. **Ignoring mount/unmount:** If a question asks about "file system operations" at a higher level, mentioning `mount()` and `unmount()` is mandatory.

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**List and briefly explain the various operations that can be performed on a file in an operating system.**

**Model Answer (Concise, 3-mark version):**

The following operations can be performed on a file:

1. **Create a file:** A new file is created in the directory structure and added to the directory listing.
2. **Write a file:** Data is written to the file at the current file pointer position, which advances by the number of bytes written.
3. **Read a file:** Data is read from the file at the current file pointer position, which advances accordingly.
4. **Reposition (Seek):** The file pointer is moved to a specified offset within the file without reading or writing data.
5. **Delete a file:** The file is removed from the directory; if its link count becomes zero and no process has it open, the disk space is reclaimed.
6. **Truncate a file:** All contents beyond the current file pointer are discarded, reducing the file size to the current offset.
7. **Open a file:** The system locates the file via directory lookup, verifies access permissions, and prepares it for subsequent read/write operations by returning a file descriptor.
8. **Close a file:** The file descriptor is released, the open count is decremented, and resources are freed if no other process has the file open.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Why do we need an explicit `open()` system call when `read()` and `write()` operations are already available? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3-mark version):**

The `open()` system call is necessary because it performs several expensive preparatory tasks that should not be repeated for every `read()` or `write()` call:

1. **Directory Lookup:** The kernel traverses the directory hierarchy to locate the i-node of the file specified by the pathname. This is a costly disk operation.
2. **Permission Verification:** The kernel checks the requesting process's user/group identity against the file's permission bits to ensure the requested access mode is authorized.
3. **Resource Allocation:** The kernel creates entries in the **Per-Process Open File Table** and the **System-Wide Open File Table**, assigns a file descriptor, and initializes the file pointer to zero.

By performing these operations once at `open()` time and returning a simple integer file descriptor, the OS ensures that subsequent `read()` and `write()` calls are fast — they only need to index into the already-prepared tables.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Open File Table** structure maintained by the operating system. With the help of a neat diagram, describe the per-process open file table and the system-wide open file table, and explain how they interact when two processes open the same file.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a file `report.txt` with an initial link count of 1 and size 0. Process A opens it in read-write mode, writes 200 bytes, and seeks to offset 100. Process B then opens the same file in read-only mode and reads 150 bytes. Both processes then close the file, after which the file is unlinked. Trace the state of the **link count, open count, file size, and file pointer** at each step. State clearly when the file is finally reclaimed from disk.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### (a) Open File Table Structure — 7 Marks

**Conceptual Explanation:**

The OS maintains a **two-level open file table** to manage the state of all currently open files efficiently.

**Level 1 — Per-Process Open File Table:**

Each process has its own private table that contains an entry for every file the process has opened. Each entry contains:
- **File descriptor (fd):** A small non-negative integer used as an index (e.g., 0, 1, 2, 3, ...).
- **Pointer** to the corresponding entry in the system-wide table.
- **File pointer (current offset):** A per-process read/write position that moves forward after each read/write.
- **Access mode flags:** Indicates whether the file was opened for read, write, or both.
- **Status flags:** Such as append mode, non-blocking, etc.

**Level 2 — System-Wide Open File Table:**

There is exactly one such table for the entire OS, shared by all processes. Each entry contains:
- **I-node pointer (or File Control Block):** Identifies the file on disk.
- **Open Count:** The number of processes that currently have this file open. It is incremented on each `open()` and decremented on each `close()`.
- **File size** and disk location metadata.
- **Locking information:** Record locks held by processes.
- **Access mode:** The union of access modes requested by all sharing processes.

**Interaction When Two Processes Open the Same File:**

Suppose P1 and P2 both call `open("data.txt", O_RDWR)`. The kernel:
1. Performs the directory lookup and permission check for P1, creates **System-Wide Entry A** with Open Count = 1, and adds an entry in P1's per-process table pointing to Entry A.
2. When P2 calls `open()`, the kernel finds an existing system-wide entry A. It increments the Open Count to 2 and adds a new entry in P2's per-process table — also pointing to Entry A.
3. Both processes share the same i-node and disk location (via Entry A), but each maintains an **independent file pointer** in their respective per-process table entries.
4. This sharing allows P1 and P2 to read/write to the same file concurrently, with the OS using the file pointer and locks to coordinate access.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1T["P1 Per-Process Table: fd=3, file_ptr=200, mode=RDWR, points to Entry A"] --> SW1
    P2T["P2 Per-Process Table: fd=3, file_ptr=80, mode=RDONLY, points to Entry A"] --> SW1
    SW1["System-Wide Entry A: i-node pointer, open_count=2, mode=RDWR, lock_info"]
    SW1 --> INODE["I-node of data.txt: link count, size, block pointers"]
    INODE --> DISK["Physical Disk Blocks"]

    style P1T fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style P2T fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style SW1 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style INODE fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style DISK fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
```

**Key Advantage:** The two-level structure decouples *file identity* (in the system-wide table) from *process-specific state* (in the per-process table), enabling efficient sharing, independent file pointers, and proper cleanup on process exit.

---

#### (b) Tracing the State of `report.txt` — 7 Marks

| Step | Operation | Link Count (L) | Open Count (O) | File Size (S) | File Pointer (P_A, P_B) | Notes |
|------|-----------|----------------|----------------|---------------|--------------------------|-------|
| 0 | Initial state | 1 | 0 | 0 | — | File exists, no process has it open. |
| 1 | A: `open(report.txt, O_RDWR)` | 1 | 1 | 0 | P_A = 0 | New system-wide entry created, Open Count = 1. |
| 2 | A: `write(fd, buf, 200)` | 1 | 1 | 200 | P_A = 200 | Size grows to 200, pointer advances to 200. |
| 3 | A: `lseek(fd, 100, SEEK_SET)` | 1 | 1 | 200 | P_A = 100 | Pointer moves to 100, no data changed. |
| 4 | B: `open(report.txt, O_RDONLY)` | 1 | 2 | 200 | P_B = 0 | New per-process entry for B, Open Count = 2. |
| 5 | B: `read(fd, buf, 150)` | 1 | 2 | 200 | P_B = 150 | Reads bytes 0–149, pointer advances to 150. |
| 6 | A: `close(fd)` | 1 | 1 | 200 | — | Open Count = 1, A's fd freed. |
| 7 | B: `close(fd)` | 1 | 0 | 200 | — | Open Count = 0, B's fd freed. |
| 8 | `unlink(report.txt)` | 0 | 0 | 200 | — | Reclaim condition met: $L=0$ and $O=0$, so i-node and blocks are freed. |

**Final Reclamation Step:**

After Step 8, the reclaim condition is satisfied:

$$ \text{deallocate} \iff (L = 0) \land (O = 0) \iff (0 = 0) \land (0 = 0) \iff \text{true} $$

The i-node of `report.txt` is returned to the free i-node list, and all 200 bytes of data blocks are returned to the free-space bitmap. The file **ceases to exist** on disk.

**Key Observations:**
- The seek operation in Step 3 does not affect size or link count — it only modifies the per-process file pointer.
- After the lseek, if A were to write, it would create a "hole" (sparse file) at offsets 100–199 if those blocks were not yet allocated.
- The file's size remains 200 bytes throughout — it never shrinks to 100 after the seek. Only `truncate()` can reduce size.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the various **directory operations** supported by the operating system. Explain the concept of **hard links and symbolic links** with the help of suitable diagrams, and state two advantages of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a state diagram, explain the **complete lifecycle of a file** from creation to deletion. Show the transitions caused by `create()`, `open()`, `read/write()`, `close()`, and `unlink()` system calls, and indicate at which stage the file is physically removed from the disk.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### (a) Directory Operations and Links — 7 Marks

**Directory Operations:**

A directory in an OS is a special file that maintains a mapping between human-readable names and the underlying file identifiers (i-nodes). The following operations are provided:

1. **Create a directory** (`mkdir`): Allocates a new i-node, marks it as a directory type, and inserts an entry into the parent directory.
2. **Delete a directory** (`rmdir`): Removes the directory entry from the parent; only succeeds if the directory is empty.
3. **Open a directory** (`opendir`): Returns a directory stream handle for sequential reading.
4. **Read a directory** (`readdir`): Returns the next entry (name and i-node number) from the directory.
5. **Close a directory** (`closedir`): Releases the directory stream resources.
6. **Rename** (`rename`): Changes the name of a file or directory, or moves it across directories.
7. **Link** (`link`): Creates a new directory entry (hard link) that points to an existing i-node.
8. **Unlink** (`unlink`): Removes a directory entry; the file is reclaimed only when the link count reaches zero.

**Hard Link — Detailed Explanation:**

A **hard link** is a directory entry that directly references the i-node of an existing file. The same i-node may have multiple hard links pointing to it. The i-node stores a **link count** that tracks the number of hard links. When a hard link is created, the link count is incremented; when one is removed, it is decremented. The file persists until the link count becomes zero **and** no process has it open.

**Hard Link Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DIR1["Directory A: entry: report.txt"] --> INODE
    DIR2["Directory B: entry: doc.txt"] --> INODE
    INODE["I-node: link_count=2, size, blocks"]
    INODE --> DISK["Physical Disk Blocks"]

    style DIR1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style DIR2 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style INODE fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style DISK fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
```

**Symbolic (Soft) Link — Detailed Explanation:**

A **symbolic link** is a special file that contains the **pathname** of another file. It is essentially a pointer to a path, not to an i-node. When the OS encounters a symbolic link during pathname resolution, it transparently redirects to the target file. Deleting the original file leaves the symbolic link **dangling** (broken).

**Symbolic Link Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DIR1["Directory A: entry: shortcut.txt (symlink)"] --> SYMINODE["I-node of symlink: type=symlink, target=/home/user/report.txt"]
    SYMINODE -->|"pathname resolution"| TARGET["Target: /home/user/report.txt"]
    TARGET --> TARGETINODE["I-node of report.txt: link_count=1"]
    TARGETINODE --> DISK["Physical Disk Blocks"]

    style DIR1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style SYMINODE fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style TARGET fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style TARGETINODE fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style DISK fill:#831843,color:#ffffff
```

**Comparison — Advantages:**

| Feature | Hard Link | Symbolic Link |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **Advantage 1** | Independent of pathname — works even if the original name is moved or renamed. | Can cross file system boundaries (e.g., link to a file on a different partition). |
| **Advantage 2** | No extra disk space for the link itself — only the directory entry is added. | Can link to directories (hard links to directories are restricted to prevent cycles). |
| **Disadvantage** | Cannot span file systems; cannot link to directories in most OS. | Becomes dangling if target is deleted; small overhead for pathname resolution. |

---

#### (b) File Lifecycle State Diagram — 7 Marks

The complete lifecycle of a file consists of four primary states and several transitions triggered by file system operations.

**State Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NonExistent
    NonExistent --> Created: create syscall, i-node allocated, link count = 1
    Created --> Open: open syscall, open count = 1
    Open --> InUse: read or write or lseek, file pointer advances
    InUse --> Open: close syscall, open count decrements
    Open --> Closed: open count becomes 0, per-process entry freed
    Closed --> Open: open syscall again, open count increments
    Closed --> Unlinked: unlink syscall, link count decrements
    Unlinked --> Deleted: link count = 0 and open count = 0, i-node and blocks freed
    Deleted --> [*]

    note right of Created: I-node allocated, parent directory entry added
    note right of InUse: File pointer moves, data is read or written
    note right of Deleted: Physical disk blocks returned to free space
```

**Explanation of Transitions:**

1. **NonExistent → Created:** The `create()` system call allocates a new i-node, sets the file type, initializes the size to zero, and adds a directory entry in the parent directory. The link count becomes 1.

2. **Created → Open:** The `open()` system call performs directory lookup (redundant if the file was just created — the i-node is cached), verifies permissions, and creates entries in both the per-process and system-wide open file tables. The open count becomes 1.

3. **Open → InUse:** The `read()`, `write()`, and `lseek()` operations manipulate the file pointer and transfer data. These operations do not change the open count or link count.

4. **InUse → Open → Closed:** The `close()` system call decrements the open count. When the open count reaches zero, the system-wide table entry is deallocated.

5. **Closed → Unlinked:** The `unlink()` system call removes the directory entry and decrements the link count. If the link count was 1, it becomes 0.

6. **Unlinked → Deleted:** The file is **physically removed from the disk** only when both conditions are satisfied: the link count is zero **and** the open count is zero. At this point, the i-node is returned to the free i-node list, and the data blocks are returned to the free-space bitmap.

**Critical KTU Point:** If a process has the file open (open count > 0) when `unlink()` is called, the file's directory entry is removed, but the disk blocks are **not** freed until the process closes the file. This is why a process can read a file that has already been deleted from the directory.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ The **six classic file operations** are: **C**reate, **W**rite, **R**ead, **R**eposition (seek), **D**elete, **T**runcate.
- ✅ **Two additional modern operations** are **Open** and **Close** — always mention them in KTU answers.
- ✅ The **Open File Table** is **two-level**: *Per-Process* (private file pointer, mode) and *System-Wide* (shared i-node pointer, open count, locks).
- ✅ A **file descriptor (fd)** is a non-negative integer returned by `open()`; values 0, 1, 2 are reserved for stdin, stdout, stderr.
- ✅ **Link Count** = number of directory entries pointing to the i-node (disk attribute).
- ✅ **Open Count** = number of processes that have the file open (memory attribute).
- ✅ **Reclaim condition:** A file is deleted from disk **only** when both **link count = 0** **and** **open count = 0**.
- ✅ A process can read a **deleted file** if it held an open descriptor before deletion — the file is not reclaimed until the process closes it.
- ✅ **Hard links** share the same i-node and increment the link count; **symbolic links** are separate files containing a pathname.
- ✅ **Directory operations** include `mkdir`, `rmdir`, `opendir`, `readdir`, `closedir`, `link`, `unlink`, `rename`.
- ✅ The `lseek()` call **does not** perform any I/O — it only updates the per-process file pointer.
- ✅ The `write()` call may **increase the file size** (e.g., writing beyond the current end-of-file), but `truncate()` is required to **decrease** the size.
- ✅ **Mount** and **unmount** are file-system-level operations that make a file system accessible or inaccessible at a mount point.
- ✅ For every "trace the file operations" question, draw **both tables explicitly** and update them **step by step** — this is where most marks are awarded.
- ✅ Remember the **POSIX system call names**: `open`, `close`, `read`, `write`, `lseek`, `unlink`, `link`, `stat`, `chmod`, `mount`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: File Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** File Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of File Types

In an Operating System, a **file type** is a metadata classification tag assigned to a file (stored as a field in the file's **inode** or **File Control Block (FCB)**) that tells the OS *how to interpret, access, and operate* on the file's contents. The OS kernel uses this type field to route I/O requests to the correct handler (e.g., a text editor for `.txt`, a disk driver for a block device).

The UNIX/Linux model (POSIX standard) classifies files into **seven canonical types**:
1. **Regular File (-)**
2. **Directory File (d)**
3. **Character Special File (c)**
4. **Block Special File (b)**
5. **FIFO / Named Pipe (p)**
6. **Symbolic Link (l)**
7. **Socket (s)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCITT403 - Module 4):**
> "File concept, File types, File operations, File system structure, Access methods, Directory structure, File system mounting, File sharing & protection."
> *File Types* form the foundational classification unit upon which file operations, directory structure, and protection mechanisms are built.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **large office warehouse** (the hard disk) with thousands of storage containers (files). To manage them efficiently, the warehouse manager (OS) labels every container with a **colored sticker** (file type):
- 🟦 **Blue Sticker** → "Office Document" (regular text file) → Open with the reading room.
- 🟩 **Green Sticker** → "Yellow Pages Directory" (directory) → Don't open, use it to find other containers.
- 🟥 **Red Sticker** → "Power Plug Socket" (character/block device) → Not a container, but an electrical interface to a machine.
- 🟨 **Yellow Sticker** → "Shortcut Arrow" (symbolic link) → A pointer to another container's location.

Without these colored stickers, the warehouse manager wouldn't know whether to *read the contents*, *walk into it*, or *send electricity through it* — chaos would ensue! This sticker is the **file type**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following POSIX standard constants are used in UNIX file type encoding:

| Constant | Octal Value | Type |
|---|---|---|
| **S_IFMT** | **0170000** | Bit mask for file type field |
| **S_IFREG** | **0100000** | Regular file |
| **S_IFDIR** | **0040000** | Directory |
| **S_IFCHR** | **0020000** | Character device |
| **S_IFBLK** | **0060000** | Block device |
| **S_IFIFO** | **0010000** | FIFO / named pipe |
| **S_IFLNK** | **0120000** | Symbolic link |
| **S_IFSOCK** | **0140000** | Socket |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These octal constants are **MANDATORY** to remember for KTU ESE questions. They appear in bitwise operations on the `st_mode` field of the `stat` structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven File Types — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Regular File (`-`)
- **Most common file type** in any OS.
- Contains arbitrary user data — text, binary, executables, images, etc.
- Treated by the OS as a **flat byte stream**; the kernel does not interpret the contents.
- **Sub-categorization (logical, not OS-enforced):**
  * **Text File**: Characters organized as lines (newline `\n` separated). E.g., `.c`, `.txt`, `.html`.
  * **Binary File**: Raw bytes with internal structure. E.g., `.exe`, `.jpg`, `.pdf`.
  * **Source File**: Human-readable code. E.g., `program.c`.
  * **Object File**: Compiler output, relocatable machine code. E.g., `program.o`.
  * **Executable File**: Fully linked, ready-to-run program. E.g., `a.out`, `.exe`.
- **Internal structure on disk:** Organized into **logical blocks** (4 KB on Linux ext4) wrapped in **extents** for modern filesystems.

### 2.1.2 Directory File (`d`)
- A special file that contains **an array of directory entries**, each mapping a filename to an inode number.
- NOT a regular container — the OS treats it as a **lookup table**.
- **Hierarchical structure:** A directory can contain files and other directories (subdirectories).
- **Reserved entries:** `.` (current) and `..` (parent).
- Operations: `opendir()`, `readdir()`, `closedir()`, `mkdir()`, `rmdir()`.

### 2.1.3 Character Special File (`c`)
- Represents devices that perform **I/O one character (byte) at a time**, with no buffering/random access.
- Examples: `/dev/tty` (terminal), `/dev/null` (data sink), serial ports, keyboards.
- The file's *inode* does not point to data blocks; instead, it points to a **device driver routine** identified by a **major number** (driver) and **minor number** (specific device instance).

### 2.1.4 Block Special File (`b`)
- Represents devices that perform I/O in **fixed-size blocks (typically 512 B – 4 KB)** with **random access** capability.
- Examples: `/dev/sda` (SATA disk), `/dev/nvme0n1` (NVMe SSD), `/dev/loop0`.
- Allows the filesystem to **mount** the device and use buffered I/O for performance.
- **Distinction from Character Devices:** Block devices use a **buffer cache** in main memory; character devices stream raw data.

### 2.1.5 FIFO (Named Pipe) (`p`)
- A special file that acts as a **one-way communication channel** between two unrelated processes (unlike anonymous pipes which require a parent-child relationship).
- Created via `mkfifo()` system call.
- Data written by one process is read in FIFO order by another.
- Persists in the filesystem (unlike anonymous pipes).

### 2.1.6 Symbolic Link (`l`)
- A **soft link** — a tiny file whose only content is the **pathname string** of another file (the *target*).
- Created by `symlink()` / `ln -s`.
- **Broken link:** If the target is deleted, the symlink still exists but points to nothing (dangles).
- **Can cross filesystem boundaries**, unlike hard links.

### 2.1.7 Socket (`s`)
- A bidirectional communication endpoint used for **inter-process communication (IPC)** across the network or locally.
- Foundation of UNIX domain sockets and TCP/IP networking.
- Identified by a pathname (e.g., `/tmp/mysocket`) or IP address + port.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — File Type Identification

The Unix `stat()` system call returns a `struct stat` whose `st_mode` field encodes the file type in the **upper 4 bits** of the lower 16 bits.

$$
\text{st\_mode} = \underbrace{FILE\_TYPE\_BITS}_{4 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{PERMISSION\_BITS}_{12 \text{ bits}}
$$

| Operation | Formula / Macro | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Extract file type | `S_IFMT & st_mode` | Mask out the 4 type bits |
| Test regular file | `S_ISREG(st_mode)` | True if regular file |
| Test directory | `S_ISDIR(st_mode)` | True if directory |
| Test char device | `S_ISCHR(st_mode)` | True if character special |
| Test block device | `S_ISBLK(st_mode)` | True if block special |
| Test FIFO | `S_ISFIFO(st_mode)` | True if named pipe |
| Test symlink | `S_ISLNK(st_mode)` | True if symbolic link |
| Test socket | `S_ISSOCK(st_mode)` | True if socket |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-AND rule:** A file belongs to type T **iff** `(st_mode & S_IFMT) == S_IF<type>`.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems** | `/dev/ttyS0` (UART) → character device; `/dev/mmcblk0` (SD card) → block device. |
| **Databases** | MySQL `ibdata` → regular file, mapped via mmap for performance. |
| **Virtualization** | `/dev/kvm` (character device) — KVM hypervisor interface. |
| **Containerization** | Docker overlays use symlinks extensively to share image layers. |
| **System Programming** | Kernel uses `S_IFCHR` to dispatch `open()` calls to the correct driver's `file_operations` table. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Decoding a File Type from `st_mode`

The `st_mode` is a 16-bit field. The **upper 4 bits** (bits 12–15) hold the file type, and the **lower 12 bits** hold permissions. Let us derive the type of a file whose `st_mode = 100644` (octal).

### Step 1: Convert octal to binary

We split the octal into its digit components.

$$
100644_{8} = 1 \cdot 8^{5} + 0 \cdot 8^{4} + 0 \cdot 8^{3} + 6 \cdot 8^{2} + 4 \cdot 8^{1} + 4 \cdot 8^{0}
$$

**Row 1 — Converting each octal digit to a 3-bit binary group:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1_{8} &= 001_{2} \\
0_{8} &= 000_{2} \\
0_{8} &= 000_{2} \\
6_{8} &= 110_{2} \\
4_{8} &= 100_{2} \\
4_{8} &= 100_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 2 — Concatenating to get 18-bit binary:**

$$
100644_{8} = 001\ 000\ 000\ 110\ 100\ 100_{2}
$$

**Row 3 — Padding to a 16-bit mode field (dropping leading group):**

$$
\text{st\_mode}_{2} = 100\ 000\ 110\ 100\ 100_{2}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the file type mask `S_IFMT`

The mask `0170000` octal in binary is the upper 4 bits of a 16-bit word.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S\_IFMT} &= 0170000_{8} \\
&= 000\ 001\ 111\ 000\ 000\ 000_{2} \\
&= 0\ 1111\ 000\ 000\ 000\ 000_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Bitwise AND operation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{st\_mode} &= 100\ 000\ 110\ 100\ 100_{2} \\
\text{S\_IFMT}    &= 111\ 000\ 000\ 000\ 000_{2} \\
\hline
\text{st\_mode} \ \&\ \text{S\_IFMT} &= 100\ 000\ 000\ 000\ 000_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compare with known type constants

$$
\begin{aligned}
100\ 000\ 000\ 000\ 000_{2} &= 0100000_{8} \\
&= \text{S\_IFREG}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Conclusion

$$
\boxed{\text{File type} = \text{REGULAR FILE}}
$$

The remaining 12 bits `110 100 100` (octal `644`) decode to permissions: `rw- r-- r--` (owner: read/write, group: read, others: read).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for File Type Identification

| `st_mode` (Octal) | Top 4 bits (Binary) | Type Bits | File Type | Icon in `ls -l` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `100644` | `1000` | `0100000` | Regular File | `-` |
| `040755` | `0100` | `0040000` | Directory | `d` |
| `020666` | `0010` | `0020000` | Character Device | `c` |
| `060660` | `0110` | `0060000` | Block Device | `b` |
| `010666` | `0001` | `0010000` | FIFO (Named Pipe) | `p` |
| `120777` | `1010` | `0120000` | Symbolic Link | `l` |
| `140777` | `1100` | `0140000` | Socket | `s` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Only the **first octal digit** (top 4 bits) of the mode determines the file type in Unix.

## 3.3 Decision Logic Flow for File Dispatch

When a process calls `open(pathname, ...)`:

1. The VFS (Virtual File System) layer looks up the inode by pathname.
2. The kernel reads the inode's `i_mode` field.
3. It applies `(i_mode & S_IFMT)` to extract the type bits.
4. Based on the result, it dispatches to:
   * `regular_file_read()` for regular files,
   * `dir_read()` for directories,
   * `chrdev_open()` for character devices,
   * `blkdev_open()` for block devices, etc.
5. The file descriptor is returned to the user process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This dispatch is what makes UNIX's **"everything is a file"** philosophy possible — even hardware devices are accessed through the same `open()/read()/write()/close()` interface.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
