# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

> Differentiate between **character special files** and **block special files** in UNIX. Give two examples of each.

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | Character Special File | Block Special File |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Mode** | Stream of bytes, sequential | Fixed-size blocks, random access |
| **Buffering** | No buffer cache | Uses kernel buffer cache |
| **Examples** | `/dev/tty`, `/dev/null` | `/dev/sda`, `/dev/nvme0n1` |
| **I/O Granularity** | One character at a time | 512 B – 4 KB blocks |

*Distribution: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for examples.* ✅

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> Explain the concept of a **symbolic link**. How does it differ from a **hard link** in terms of inode usage and cross-filesystem behavior?

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A **symbolic link** is a special file of type `S_IFLNK` that contains only the **pathname** of another file (the target). It is created by `ln -s target linkname`.

| Feature | Symbolic Link | Hard Link |
|---|---|---|
| **Inode** | Has its own new inode | Shares the target's inode |
| **Cross-filesystem** | ✅ Yes (allowed) | ❌ No (same FS only) |
| **Dangling on deletion** | ✅ Breaks (points to nothing) | ❌ Still valid (data remains) |
| **Directory link** | ✅ Allowed | ❌ Restricted (no dir hard links) |

*Distribution: 1 mark symbolic link concept, 1 mark inode difference, 1 mark cross-FS behavior.* ✅

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **seven different file types** in the UNIX operating system. Show how each type is identified from the `st_mode` field using the `S_IFMT` mask. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a file with `st_mode = 040755` (octal), determine:
   1. The file type
   2. The permission bits
   3. The symbolic representation
   4. The `S_IFMT` mask result
   **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Seven File Types (7 marks):**

The UNIX VFS classifies files into seven types, identified by the upper 4 bits of the 16-bit `st_mode` field:

1. **Regular File (`-`)** — `S_IFREG = 0100000` octal
   Stores arbitrary user data (text, binary, executables). Treated as a flat byte stream.

2. **Directory File (`d`)** — `S_IFDIR = 0040000` octal
   Contains directory entries (filename → inode mapping). Hierarchical structure with `.` and `..` entries.

3. **Character Special File (`c`)** — `S_IFCHR = 0020000` octal
   Represents unbuffered, byte-stream devices (terminals, serial ports, `/dev/null`).

4. **Block Special File (`b`)** — `S_IFBLK = 0060000` octal
   Represents buffered, random-access block devices (disks, SSDs, `/dev/sda`).

5. **FIFO / Named Pipe (`p`)** — `S_IFIFO = 0010000` octal
   One-way IPC channel between unrelated processes. Persists in filesystem.

6. **Symbolic Link (`l`)** — `S_IFLNK = 0120000` octal
   Pointer file containing a pathname string. Can cross filesystems.

7. **Socket (`s`)** — `S_IFSOCK = 0140000` octal
   Bidirectional IPC endpoint (local UNIX socket or network socket).

**Identification formula:**
$$
\text{FILE TYPE} = (\text{st\_mode} \ \text{AND}\ \text{S\_IFMT})
$$
where `S_IFMT = 0170000` octal = `1111 000 000 000 000` binary.

The VFS layer extracts the top 4 bits and compares them with the seven `S_IF<type>` constants to dispatch I/O operations to the correct handler.

*Mark distribution: 1 mark per file type × 5 = 5 marks; 1 mark for diagram; 1 mark for identification formula.* ✅

**(b) Decoding `st_mode = 040755` (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert to binary (3 octal digits per group):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0_{8} &= 000_{2} \\
4_{8} &= 100_{2} \\
0_{8} &= 000_{2} \\
7_{8} &= 111_{2} \\
5_{8} &= 101_{2} \\
5_{8} &= 101_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenating:
$$
040755_{8} = 000\ 100\ 000\ 111\ 101\ 101_{2} = 100\ 000\ 111\ 101\ 101_{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply S_IFMT mask:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{st\_mode} &= 100\ 000\ 111\ 101\ 101_{2} \\
\text{S\_IFMT}    &= 111\ 000\ 000\ 000\ 000_{2} \\
\hline
\text{AND result} &= 100\ 000\ 000\ 000\ 000_{2} = 0040000_{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let me re-verify. $040755_8$ splits as $0,4,0,7,5,5$. The top **4 bits** correspond to the most significant 4 bits of the 16-bit field:

$$
040755_8 = 000\,100\,000\,111\,101\,101_2
$$

Dropping the leading 000:
$$
16\text{-bit} = 100\,000\,111\,101\,101_2
$$

Top 4 bits = `1000` = $010000_2$ octal part... Let me recompute precisely.

**Step 3 — Correct bit-level extraction (the upper 4 bits of the 16-bit value):**

The 16-bit binary representation of $040755_8$ is obtained by taking the first four octal digits in binary:

$$
040755_8 \rightarrow 0000\ 0100\ 0000\ 0111\ 0101\ 0101_2
$$

Truncating to 16 bits:
$$
\text{st\_mode}_{16} = 0100\ 0000\ 0111\ 0101_2
$$

**Step 4 — Apply S_IFMT mask ($0170000_8$ = $0000\ 0001\ 1111\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{st\_mode} &= 0100\ 0000\ 0111\ 0101_2 \\
\text{S\_IFMT}    &= 1111\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_2 \\
\hline
\text{AND result} &= 0100\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_2 = 0040000_8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Match against type table:**

$$
0040000_8 = \text{S\_IFDIR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{File type is DIRECTORY}
$$

**Step 6 — Decode permissions (lower 12 bits `000 111 101 101`):**

$$
000 = 0, \quad 111 = 7 = rwx, \quad 101 = 5 = r-x, \quad 101 = 5 = r-x
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. File Type: Directory} \\
&\text{2. Permission Bits: 0755 octal (rwxr-xr-x)} \\
&\text{3. Symbolic Form: } d\,rwx\,r\text{-}x\,r\text{-}x \\
&\text{4. S\_IFMT result: } 0040000_8 \text{ (matches S\_IFDIR)}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

*Mark distribution: 2 marks binary conversion, 2 marks AND operation, 1 mark type identification, 2 marks permission decoding.* ✅

---

### Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **"everything is a file"** in UNIX. How does it apply to **regular files, directories, character devices, and block devices**? Discuss the role of the **Virtual File System (VFS)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UNIX system has a file with mode `100777` (octal). The system administrator wants to:
   1. Identify the file type
   2. Verify whether it is executable
   3. Change it to a directory (conceptually explain what would change in the inode)
   
   Solve the above with step-by-step bitwise reasoning. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Everything is a File (7 marks):**

The UNIX philosophy **"everything is a file"** unifies diverse resources (data, hardware, IPC channels) under a single I/O interface: `open() / read() / write() / close() / lseek()`.

| Resource | File Type | How it is "a file" |
|---|---|---|
| User document | Regular File (`-`) | Byte stream on disk |
| Folder | Directory File (`d`) | File containing directory entries |
| Terminal | Character Special (`c`) | Inode points to terminal driver (major+minor) |
| Hard disk | Block Special (`b`) | Inode points to disk driver; mountable |
| Pipe | FIFO (`p`) | File-backed IPC channel |
| Shortcut | Symbolic Link (`l`) | File with pathname string |
| Network port | Socket (`s`) | File-based IPC endpoint |

**Role of VFS (Virtual File System):**
The VFS is an **abstraction layer** in the kernel that provides a uniform interface (`vnode` / `inode` operations) to user-space programs. It:
1. Receives system calls (e.g., `read()`).
2. Resolves the pathname to an inode.
3. Examines the file type via `(i_mode & S_IFMT)`.
4. Dispatches to the **filesystem-specific** or **device-specific** handler (e.g., `ext4_read()` for a file on disk, `tty_read()` for a terminal).
5. Returns data to the user process.

This abstraction enables **transparency** — programs don't need to know whether they're reading from a disk file, a terminal, or a network socket.

*Mark distribution: 2 marks concept, 3 marks table mapping, 2 marks VFS role.* ✅

**(b) Analysis of `st_mode = 100777` (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert `100777` to 16-bit binary:**

$$
100777_8 = 001\ 000\ 000\ 111\ 111\ 111_2
$$

Truncating the leading 3 bits (since 18 bits > 16 bits), we use the last 16 bits:

$$
100777_8 = 100\ 000\ 111\ 111\ 111_2 \quad (\text{16 bits})
$$

**Step 2 — Identify file type using S_IFMT mask:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{st\_mode} &= 100\ 000\ 111\ 111\ 111_{2} \\
\text{S\_IFMT}    &= 111\ 000\ 000\ 000\ 000_{2} \\
\hline
\text{AND}      &= 100\ 000\ 000\ 000\ 000_{2} = 0100000_{8} = \text{S\_IFREG}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result 1:** The file is a **Regular File** ✅

**Step 3 — Check executability (permission bits):**

Lower 12 bits = `000 111 111 111` = $0777_8$

Breaking down: owner `7` = `rwx`, group `7` = `rwx`, others `7` = `rwx`.

**Result 2:** The file is **executable by everyone** (rwxrwxrwx) ✅

**Step 4 — What would change to make it a directory?**

To convert to a directory, the type bits must change from `S_IFREG` (1000) to `S_IFDIR` (0100).

**Conceptual inode changes:**

| Field | Before (Regular File) | After (Directory) |
|---|---|---|
| Type bits | `1000` (S_IFREG) | `0100` (S_IFDIR) |
| Permission bits | `0777` | Typically `0755` |
| `i_block[0]` | Points to data blocks | Points to directory entry blocks |
| `i_size` | File size in bytes | Size of directory file |
| File operations | `regular_file_ops` | `dir_ops` (readdir, etc.) |

In practice, **`chmod` cannot change a file's type**. The OS provides `mkdir()` which allocates a **new inode** with `S_IFDIR` bits set. You cannot "transform" a regular file into a directory in place.

**Result 3:** A new directory inode must be created with the appropriate `S_IFDIR` type bits and `dir_ops` table; the existing regular file inode cannot be type-converted.

*Mark distribution: 2 marks type identification, 2 marks permission check, 3 marks inode conversion explanation.* ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Octal vs Decimal confusion:** `100777` is octal, not decimal! Converting it as decimal gives a totally wrong answer. Always state the base explicitly.
> 2. **S_IFMT masking error:** Students often AND the full `0777` (file type mask for permissions) instead of `0170000`. Remember: type = top 4 bits, perms = lower 12 bits.
> 3. **Forgetting the sign bit / padding:** When converting octal to 16-bit binary, pad the MSB side with zeros to make exactly 16 bits before masking.
> 4. **Confusing S_IFCHR and S_IFBLK:** `0020000` is character, `0060000` is block. Note the subtle difference.
> 5. **Not stating the dispatch mechanism:** When asked "how is the type used?" — you MUST mention the VFS dispatch and `file_operations` table, not just list the types.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — File Types**

- ⭐ **Seven file types** in UNIX: Regular, Directory, Character Special, Block Special, FIFO, Symbolic Link, Socket.
- ⭐ **Icon mnemonic for `ls -l`:** `-` regular, `d` directory, `c` char, `b` block, `p` pipe, `l` link, `s` socket.
- ⭐ **`S_IFMT = 0170000`** octal — the universal file-type mask. **MEMORIZE THIS.**
- ⭐ **Type bits = top 4 bits** of `st_mode`; **permission bits = lower 12 bits**.
- ⭐ **Character vs Block:** Character = unbuffered, sequential, byte-stream. Block = buffered, random access, block-based.
- ⭐ **Symbolic Link** = soft link with own inode, can span filesystems, breaks on target deletion.
- ⭐ **FIFO** = named pipe, persists in filesystem, one-way communication.
- ⭐ **Socket** = bidirectional IPC endpoint (local or network).
- ⭐ **VFS** dispatches I/O based on `(i_mode & S_IFMT)` to the correct `file_operations` table.
- ⭐ **"Everything is a file"** is the UNIX philosophy enabled by VFS abstraction.
- ⭐ **KTU exam pattern:** Always expect a decoding question (e.g., given `st_mode`, find type and permissions).
- ⭐ **For permission decode:** `r=4, w=2, x=1`. `7 = rwx`, `6 = rw-`, `5 = r-x`, `4 = r--`, `0 = ---`.
- ⭐ **Hard link** shares inode; **soft link** has its own inode. Hard link cannot cross filesystems.
- ⭐ **S_ISREG(), S_ISDIR(), S_ISCHR(), S_ISBLK(), S_ISFIFO(), S_ISLNK(), S_ISSOCK()** are the canonical POSIX macros for type testing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: File Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** File Structure # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of a File A **file** is a named collection of related information that is recorded on secondary storage (such as a hard disk, SSD, or magnetic tape). From the perspective of the Operating System, a file is the smallest logical unit of secondary storage that can be allocated, named, and accessed by the user. A file is a logical container, while the actual data resides as a sequence of bytes (or records) on the physical storage medium. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized)** > A file is a named logical storage unit that the Operating System abstracts to the user, hiding the physical properties of the storage device. According to Silberschatz (OS Concepts), a file is a **contiguous logical address space** mapped to physical blocks by the OS. ## 1.2 File Attributes (Metadata) Every file carries **metadata** — descriptive information about the file — stored in its directory entry (inode in UNIX-like systems). Common attributes include: | Attribute | Meaning | |---|---| | **Name** | Human-readable symbolic identifier (e.g., `report.pdf`). | | **Identifier / Inode Number** | Unique numeric tag for the file system (e.g., `inode 2401`). | | **Type** | Indicates format (`.txt`, `.exe`, `.jpg`, directory). | | **Location** | Pointer to the device and the physical block address on disk. | | **Size** | Current logical file size in bytes/blocks. | | **Protection** | Access control bits (read, write, execute; owner, group, others). | | **Time, Date, and User Identification** | Created, modified, accessed timestamps and owner IDs. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy: The Office Filing Cabinet** > Imagine a large office filing cabinet with thousands of labeled hanging folders. > - The **cabinet** = your hard disk. > - Each **hanging folder (label)** = a file name. > - The **papers inside** = the actual data/contents. > - The **index card on the front** of each folder (size, date, author) = file attributes (metadata). > - The **filing clerk** who retrieves, inserts, or removes papers on request = the **Operating System's file subsystem**. Just as a clerk enforces rules (you cannot pull a file without permission — protection), and rearranges folders to keep the cabinet organized (allocation), the OS manages files following well-defined structure rules. ## 1.4 File Operations The OS provides a set of system calls to manipulate files. The six **fundamental file operations** defined by KTU syllabus are: 1. **Create a file** — Allocate space and add a directory entry. 2. **Write a file** — Output data to the file at the current write pointer. 3. **Read a file** — Input data from the file at the current read pointer. 4. **Reposition within a file (Seek)** — Move the file pointer to a new offset (`lseek`). 5. **Delete a file** — Remove the directory entry and free disk blocks. 6. **Truncate a file** — Empty the contents but keep the attributes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the **six file operations** verbatim — this is a guaranteed 3-mark direct question in Part A of the KTU End Semester Examination. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logical vs. Physical File Structure | Aspect | Logical File Structure | Physical File Structure | |---|---|---| | **Visible to** | User / Application | Operating System / Device driver | | **Purpose** | How data appears to programs | How data is stored on disk | | **Examples** | Stream of bytes, records, tree | Sequential sectors, blocks, tracks | | **Level** | High-level abstraction | Low-level disk geometry | ## 2.2 Types of File Structure ### (a) None / Stream of Bytes (Unstructured) The OS views the file as an **unstructured sequence of bytes** (UNIX philosophy). The meaning of the bytes is entirely the responsibility of the application program. ### (b) Simple Record Structure The file is organized as a **sequence of fixed-length or variable-length records**, each with internal structure (fields). Used in early mainframe systems. ### (c) Complex Structures - **Sequential files** — Records are accessed only in sequential order. - **Indexed files** — A separate index block allows direct access; index points to the location of each record. - **Indexed-Sequential files** — Combines sequential order with an index for fast retrieval. - **Direct (Hashed) files** — A hash function maps a key directly to a disk block. ## 2.3 File Access Methods The three canonical access methods tested in KTU exams are: 1. **Sequential Access** — Information is read in order, byte by byte or record by record. The `read` operation advances the file pointer automatically. Tapes use this naturally. 2. **Direct Access (Random Access)** — The file is viewed as a numbered sequence of **logical blocks**. Any block can be read/written by specifying its **relative block number** (offset from the start). The `seek` operation repositions the file pointer without reading intervening data. Essential for databases. 3. **Indexed Access** — A two-tier scheme. The file has an **index block** containing pointers to data blocks. To access record *i*, the OS first reads index entry *i* to find the disk address, then reads the data block. Supports both sequential traversal (walk the index) and random access (jump via index). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Distinction** > - **Sequential Access** is the only option for **tape-based** systems. > - **Direct Access** requires **disk-based** storage with the ability to reposition the read/write head. > - **Indexed Access** is essentially **Direct Access on top of an index** — most efficient for large files with sparse access patterns. ## 2.4 Internal File Structure (On-Disk Layout) A file on disk is stored as a collection of **physical blocks** (typically **4 KB** each). The OS hides this from the user via logical-to-physical mapping. The internal structure is realized through disk allocation methods (covered in Module 5, but the structural relationship is mentioned here): - **Contiguous Allocation** — File occupies consecutive blocks. - **Linked Allocation** — Each block contains a pointer to the next block. - **Indexed Allocation** — A special index block contains pointers to all data blocks of the file. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | Maximum file size (contiguous) | $S_{max} = N \times B$ | $N$ = number of contiguous blocks, $B$ = block size (bytes) | | Block number from offset | $b = \lfloor \dfrac{\text{offset}}{B} \rfloor$ | $b$ = block index, $B$ = block size | | Offset within a block | $o = \text{offset} \mod B$ | Bytes from block start | | File pointer advance (sequential) | $P_{n+1} = P_n + R$ | $P$ = pointer, $R$ = record/byte size | | Direct access seek | $P = n \times R$ | $n$ = logical record number, $R$ = record size | | Index entry size | $E = \log_2(D)$ | $D$ = number of data blocks | | Index block max pointers | $K = \dfrac{B}{E}$ | $K$ = max data blocks addressable per index block | | Maximum indexed file size | $S_{max} = K \times B$ | One-level index | | Two-level max file size | $S_{max} = K^2 \times B$ | Multi-level index scheme | | Buffer hit ratio | $H = \dfrac{H_{hits}}{H_{hits} + H_{misses}}$ | $0 \le H \le 1$ | > [!WARNING] > **Do NOT use the vertical pipe `|` character inside any cell of the markdown tables above.** KTU valuation scripts may fail to render the table correctly. In exam answers, always wrap absolute-value expressions as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The concept of file structure is the backbone of: - **Database Management Systems (DBMS)** — uses indexed and hashed access. - **Multimedia streaming** — sequential access for predictable throughput. - **Version Control Systems (Git)** — uses content-addressable storage with hash-based direct access. - **Embedded file systems (FAT, ext4, NTFS)** — different physical structures optimized for flash, HDD, or network storage. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Block Number and Offset from a Byte Offset A file of $L$ bytes is stored in a sequence of blocks. Given a byte offset $X$, derive the **block number** and **offset within block** to read. ### Step-by-Step: **Step 1 — Identify the block containing byte X.** The block size is $B$ bytes, numbered from block $0$. The block that contains byte $X$ is given by integer division of $X$ by $B$. $$ b = \left\lfloor \frac{X}{B} \right\rfloor $$ **Step 2 — Identify the byte's position within that block.** This is the remainder when $X$ is divided by $B$. $$ o = X \bmod B $$ **Step 3 — Reconstruct $X$ from $b$ and $o$ (verification).** $$ X = b \cdot B + o $$ ### Worked Numerical Example Let $B = 4096$ bytes per block, and a program issues a `read` at offset $X = 12345$ bytes. $$ b = \left\lfloor \frac{12345}{4096} \right\rfloor $$ $$ = \left\lfloor 3.0146\ldots \right\rfloor = 3 $$ $$ o = 12345 \bmod 4096 $$ $$ = 12345 - 3 \cdot 4096 = 12345 - 12288 = 57 $$ **Verification:** $$ X = 3 \cdot 4096 + 57 = 12288 + 57 = 12345 \;\checkmark $$ So the OS reads **block 3** and then returns the data starting at **byte 57** of that block. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum File Size for Indexed Allocation A disk uses **$B$ bytes per block** and a disk block address (pointer) requires **$p$ bytes**. Find the maximum size of a file using a **single-level indexed allocation**. ### Step-by-Step: **Step 1 — Compute the number of pointers that fit in one index block.** $$ K = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{p} \right\rfloor $$ This is the maximum number of data blocks a single index block can address. **Step 2 — Each data block stores $B$ bytes of file data, so total file size is:** $$ S_{max} = K \cdot B $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $K$ from Step 1:** $$ S_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{p} \right\rfloor \cdot B $$ **Step 4 — Example with numbers.** Let $B = 4096$ bytes and $p = 4$ bytes (32-bit block addresses). $$ K = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 $$ $$ S_{max} = 1024 \cdot 4096 = 4\,194\,304 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB} $$ **Step 5 — Extension to two-level indexing.** A two-level index uses an outer index that points to inner index blocks, each of which points to data blocks. The number of addressable data blocks becomes $K^2$, giving: $$ S_{max}^{(2)} = K^2 \cdot B $$ For the same $B = 4096$ and $p = 4$: $$ S_{max}^{(2)} = 1024^2 \cdot 4096 = 4\,\text{GB} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Access Time Comparison For a disk with: - **Seek time** $T_s$ - **Rotational latency** $T_r$ (half a rotation) - **Transfer time per block** $T_{tr}$ Derive the average time to access the $k$-th block under sequential vs. direct access. ### Step-by-Step: **Step 1 — Sequential access time to read the $k$-th block from start.** The read head must pass over $k - 1$ previous blocks first, so total time is: $$ T_{seq}(k) = (k - 1) \cdot T_{tr} + T_s + T_r + T_{tr} $$ Simplified (amortized over many reads, the per-block cost is dominated by $T_{tr}$): $$ T_{seq} \approx T_s + T_r + T_{tr} \quad \text{(after initial seek)} $$ **Step 2 — Direct access time to read the $k$-th block from start.** A new seek and rotational wait are required for every block (no prefetch benefit): $$ T_{dir}(k) = T_s + T_r + T_{tr} $$ **Step 3 — Comparison.** For a single block read, $T_{seq} = T_{dir}$. The advantage of sequential access appears when reading **multiple consecutive blocks** — the head is already positioned, so the average cost per block drops to approximately $T_{tr}$. **Step 4 — Quantitative Example.** Let $T_s = 8\,\text{ms}$, $T_r = 4\,\text{ms}$, $T_{tr} = 0.5\,\text{ms}$. Reading 100 consecutive blocks: - **Direct access (worst case, random placement):** $100 \times (8 + 4 + 0.5) = 1250\,\text{ms}$. - **Sequential access (one seek, no further head movement):** $(8 + 4) + 100 \times 0.5 = 62\,\text{ms}$. **Speedup factor:** $$ \frac{1250}{62} \approx 20.16 \times $$ This demonstrates why **multimedia, log files, and database table scans** use sequential access whenever possible. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Layered View of File Structure This block diagram shows the abstraction layers from the user down to the physical disk. ```mermaid graph TD A[APPLICATION PROGRAM
C / Python / Java] B[STANDARD I/O LIBRARY
fopen, fread, FILE stream] C[OPERATING SYSTEM
File Subsystem] D[FILE SYSTEM DRIVER
ext4 / NTFS / FAT] E[DISK CONTROLLER
DMA and Interrupt Logic] F[PHYSICAL STORAGE
HDD Platters or SSD NAND] A -->|read / write call| B B -->|open, read, write, seek| C C -->|logical block number| D D -->|CHS or LBA address| E E -->|magnetic or electric signal| F ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Each layer translates the request from the layer above into a more primitive form. - The **file subsystem (C)** is the heart of file management — it maintains the open file table, protection rules, and maps logical file offsets to physical disk blocks. - The user never touches the **disk controller (E)** or the **physical media (F)** — full abstraction. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — File Access Method Comparison ```mermaid graph LR subgraph SEQ["SEQUENTIAL ACCESS"] S0[Block 0] --> S1[Block 1] --> S2[Block 2] --> S3[Block 3] end subgraph DIR["DIRECT ACCESS"] D0[Block 0] D1[Block 1] D2[Block 2] D3[Block 3] PTR[File Pointer
moves to ANY block] PTR --> D0 PTR --> D1 PTR --> D2 PTR --> D3 end subgraph IDX["INDEXED ACCESS"] IB[Index Block
ptr0 ptr1 ptr2 ptr3] IB --> B0[Data Block 0] IB --> B1[Data Block 1] IB --> B2[Data Block 2] IB --> B3[Data Block 3] end ``` **Key takeaway from the diagram:** - **Sequential** has a fixed traversal order (good for tapes, log files). - **Direct** allows the file pointer to jump to any block (good for databases). - **Indexed** uses a lookup structure to decouple logical order from physical placement. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — File Subsystem Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD USER[User Process] OPN[Open File Table
per process] SON[System-wide Open File Table] DIR[Directory Layer] FCM[FCM - File Control Module] ADM[ADM - Access Control Module] PAM[PAM - Physical Access Module] BUF[Buffer Cache] DEV[Disk Device Driver] USER -->|open| OPN OPN --> SON SON --> DIR DIR --> FCM FCM --> ADM ADM --> PAM PAM --> BUF BUF --> DEV ``` **Explanation of modules:** - **FCM (File Control Module)** — Manages file operations like open, close, read, write. - **ADM (Access Control Module)** — Enforces protection (who can read/write/execute). - **PAM (Physical Access Module)** — Converts logical block requests into physical device I/O. - **Buffer Cache** — Holds recently read/written disk blocks in RAM to minimize physical I/O. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *Define a file. List any four file attributes with their significance.* #### Model Answer (3 Marks): A file is a **named collection of related information** recorded on secondary storage, treated by the OS as the smallest unit of logical storage. Four file attributes: 1. **Name** — Human-readable identifier (e.g., `notes.txt`). 2. **Type** — Indicates the kind of data (text, binary, executable). 3. **Size** — Current file length in bytes. 4. **Protection** — Access permissions for owner, group, and others. *(Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for listing four attributes with one-line meaning.)* --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *Differentiate between sequential access and direct access methods with one example each.* #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Aspect | Sequential Access | Direct Access | |---|---|---| | Order | Records read in order | Any record accessed by block number | | Mechanism | `read_next()` auto-advances pointer | `seek(n)` then `read()` | | Suitable for | Magnetic tape, log files | Magnetic disk, databases | | Example | Reading a music playlist end-to-end | Looking up employee record by ID | *(Marks: 1 per row × 2, 1 for example.)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Part B questions have an **internal choice** — the student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B, both of which are from the same module. Both are presented below as per the protocol. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`** *(a)* Explain the various file access methods in detail. **(7 Marks)** *(b)* Describe the internal structure of a file on disk. How does the OS map logical offsets to physical blocks? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) File Access Methods (7 Marks):** The OS supports three primary access methods: **1. Sequential Access (2 Marks):** The most common and simplest method. Data is read one record after another in the order they appear in the file. The read/write pointer automatically advances after every operation. There is no explicit `seek` — only `read_next` and `write_next`. **Best suited for tape storage** and log files where records are processed in order. **2. Direct Access (Random Access) (3 Marks):** Here the file is modeled as a numbered sequence of **logical blocks** (or fixed-length records). The file pointer can be moved to any block `n` via a `seek(n)` operation, followed by `read` or `write`. **Database management systems** rely on direct access to retrieve a record in O(1) time using a key. The formula for the byte offset of the $n$-th record of size $R$ is: $$ \text{offset}(n) = n \times R $$ **3. Indexed Access (2 Marks):** Combines the advantages of both. A separate **index file** contains a list of pointers (key → block address). To read record $i$, the OS first reads the $i$-th index entry, then uses the block address stored there to read the data block. Supports both **sequential walks** (scan the index) and **random lookups** (jump via the index). The maximum file size with a single-level index of block size $B$ and pointer size $p$ is: $$ S_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{p} \right\rfloor \times B $$ --- **(b) Internal File Structure & Logical-to-Physical Mapping (7 Marks):** **Internal Structure (3 Marks):** Physically, a file is a sequence of **fixed-size disk blocks** (commonly 4 KB). The OS hides the physical layout from the user. Internally, a file may be stored: - **Contiguously** — all blocks adjacent on disk (fast access, suffers from external fragmentation). - **Linked** — each block contains a pointer to the next (no fragmentation, slow random access). - **Indexed** — one or more index blocks hold pointers to data blocks (good random access, index block overhead). **Logical-to-Physical Mapping (4 Marks):** Given a user request to read byte offset $X$ from the start of a file, the OS performs the following mapping: $$ b = \left\lfloor \frac{X}{B} \right\rfloor $$ $$ o = X \bmod B $$ The OS then issues a physical I/O request for disk block `file_start_block + b`, and returns the data starting at byte `o` within that block. The whole computation is invisible to the application, which only deals with logical byte offsets. The file's starting block number is stored in the **inode** (or equivalent directory entry), along with the allocation method used. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`** *(a)* List and explain the fundamental file operations provided by the Operating System. **(7 Marks)** *(b)* With a neat diagram, explain the file subsystem architecture and the role of buffer cache. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Fundamental File Operations (7 Marks):** | # | Operation | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Create** | A new file is defined by name and a directory entry is created with empty contents. | | 2 | **Write** | Data is output to the file at the current write pointer position. | | 3 | **Read** | Data is input from the file at the current read pointer position. | | 4 | **Seek** | Repositions the file pointer to a specified offset without performing I/O. | | 5 | **Delete** | Removes the directory entry and frees all disk blocks belonging to the file. | | 6 | **Truncate** | Empties the file contents but keeps its attributes and directory entry. | Additionally, the OS may provide **Open** (load file metadata into memory tables) and **Close** (flush buffers and update metadata) operations for efficient repeated access. *(1 mark each for the six operations, 1 mark for the sentence-level description.)* --- **(b) File Subsystem Architecture & Buffer Cache (7 Marks):** ``` User Process -> Open File Table -> System-wide Open File Table -> Directory Layer -> FCM -> ADM -> PAM -> Buffer Cache -> Disk ``` **Role of each module (4 Marks):** - **Directory Layer** — Translates file names to unique identifiers (inodes). - **File Control Module (FCM)** — Handles `open`, `close`, `read`, `write` requests and maintains the open file table entries. - **Access Control Module (ADM)** — Checks user credentials against file protection bits. - **Physical Access Module (PAM)** — Converts logical block numbers to physical disk addresses and queues I/O requests. **Buffer Cache (3 Marks):** The buffer cache is a **reserved region of main memory** that holds copies of recently used disk blocks. When a read request arrives, the OS first checks the cache. A **cache hit** returns the data instantly (avoiding expensive disk I/O). A **cache miss** triggers a physical read, and the block is stored in the cache for future reuse. The hit ratio $H$ directly impacts system throughput — a hit ratio of **0.9** reduces effective average access time by nearly an order of magnitude, since cache access is in nanoseconds while disk access is in milliseconds. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Mixing logical and physical terminology** — Do not say "file pointer moves to physical block 5"; say "file pointer moves to logical record 5, which the OS maps to a physical block." Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion. > 2. **Confusing access method with allocation method** — Sequential/direct/indexed are *access methods*. Contiguous/linked/indexed are *allocation methods*. The wording is similar but the concepts are distinct. KTU has tested this trap repeatedly. > 3. **Forgetting to state the system-call names** — When listing file operations, mention the corresponding UNIX system calls (`open`, `read`, `write`, `lseek`, `unlink`, `truncate`) for half a mark of bonus credit. > 4. **Skipping the buffer cache explanation** — It is a guaranteed sub-question in Part B. Always define hit ratio and amortized access time. > 5. **Wrong formula for indexed file size** — A common error is to use $K \times K \times B$ for single-level indexing. The correct single-level formula is $K \times B$. Two-level uses $K^2 \times B$. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **file** is a named logical storage unit; its physical form is a sequence of disk blocks of size $B$ bytes (commonly 4 KB). - The **six fundamental file operations** are: create, write, read, seek, delete, truncate. (Plus `open` and `close` in most systems.) - File **attributes** include name, identifier, type, location, size, protection, timestamps, and owner. - **Three access methods:** sequential, direct (random), and indexed. Sequential is for tapes; direct and indexed are for disks. - **Indexed access** uses a two-tier lookup — index block holds pointers to data blocks. - The **block number** for a given byte offset $X$ is $b = \lfloor X / B \rfloor$ and the **offset within the block** is $o = X \bmod B$. - The **maximum indexed file size** for one-level index is $S_{max} = (B / p) \times B$ where $p$ is pointer size. - The **buffer cache** in main memory drastically reduces disk I/O by caching blocks; hit ratio is the key performance metric. - The **file subsystem** consists of FCM, ADM, and PAM modules layered over the directory and disk driver. - Distinguish carefully between **access method** (how the user reads data) and **allocation method** (how the OS places data on disk). - For KTU 2024, expect at least **one 3-mark direct question** on file operations/attributes and one **14-mark question** pairing access methods with internal structure.

topics.title: File Access Methods (Sequential Access, Direct Access, Indexed Access)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** File Access Methods (Sequential Access, Direct Access, Indexed Access)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of File Access Methods

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Operating Systems syllabus, **File Access Methods** are formally defined as the systematic procedures and mechanisms employed by the operating system's file management subsystem to **read data from** and **write data into** the stored files. A file access method essentially determines the logical pathway through which an application program references, retrieves, and manipulates the logical records of a file that resides on secondary storage media (magnetic disks, SSDs, optical media, etc.).

The KTU syllabus classifies file access methods into three principal categories:

1. **Sequential Access** – The classical linear, record-by-record traversal model.
2. **Direct Access (also termed Random Access / Relative Access)** – The block-oriented positional model.
3. **Indexed Access** – The associative key-pointer based model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The choice of file access method is a **fundamental design decision** that directly impacts the file's **physical structure on disk, the directory entries, the metadata stored in the i-node / FCB (File Control Block), the access time (latency), and the suitability of the file for specific application domains** (e.g., banking software, compilers, video streamers, database engines).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

### Sequential Access — *"Reading a Novel from Cover to Cover"*
Imagine you are reading a physical novel. You start at **Page 1**, read line-by-line, and to reach **Page 350**, you *must* turn every page in between. There is no jumping mechanism. This is **sequential access** — data is read or written in a strict linear order. To revisit a page, you must *re-read* from the beginning, or you can *rewind* (reset to start) and re-traverse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** Sequential access is closely modelled on the behaviour of **magnetic tape storage**, which historically inspired the concept. It is still used in **log files, audit trails, and stream-processing pipelines** where the natural data flow is one-way and time-ordered.

### Direct Access — *"Random Access Memory (RAM) or a Dictionary"*
Now consider a **hard-bound dictionary**. You can flip directly to the letter **'M'**, then within seconds, leaf through to the exact word. You are not forced to read 'A' through 'L' first. Similarly, **direct access** allows a program to specify the **logical record number (or block number) `n`** and immediately jump to it without traversing prior records. This is modelled on the **disk's seek + rotational latency** model, where any cylinder/sector is addressable in roughly equal time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** Direct access is the foundation of **relational databases (DBMS), airline reservation systems, ATM transactions, and real-time inventory systems** where the N-th customer record must be fetched instantly.

### Indexed Access — *"The Index Page at the Back of a Reference Book"*
Consider a thick engineering textbook. The **last few pages** contain an alphabetical **Index** — terms like *"Virtual Memory ... page 142"*, *"Paging ... page 98"*. To find a topic, you first search the (small) index, find the page number, and *then* flip to that page. This two-stage process is **indexed access**. The OS maintains a smaller auxiliary structure (the **index**) that maps **search-keys to physical disk block pointers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** Indexed access underpins **hash-table-backed file systems, B-Tree based database indexes (Oracle, MySQL InnoDB), library catalog systems, and search engines** (think of Google as a giant index).

---

## 1.3 Quick-Reference Comparison Box

| Aspect | Sequential | Direct | Indexed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Underlying Storage | Magnetic Tape Model | Disk Model | Disk + Index |
| Access Pattern | Linear | Positional (`n`) | Key-lookup |
| Speed of Random Read | **Slow (O(n))** | **Fast (O(1) ideal)** | **Fast (O(log n) tree) / O(1) hash** |
| Storage Overhead | **Minimal (0% extra)** | **Minimal (0% extra)** | **High (index file overhead)** |
| Typical Use Case | Logs, Streaming | DBMS Records | Catalogs, Lookup tables |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sequential Access — Detailed Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Core Characteristics
- The file is treated as an **ordered sequence of logical records** `R₁, R₂, R₃, ..., Rₙ`.
- A **file pointer / read-write head (R/W head)** is maintained by the OS, indicating the position of the *next* record to be processed.
- The pointer advances **automatically by one record length** after every read or write operation.

### 2.1.2 Primitive Operations (Galvin / Silberschatz Standard)

| Operation | Function | Analogous Tape Command |
|---|---|---|
| `read_next()` | Reads the current record and advances pointer | `READ` (with auto-advance) |
| `write_next()` | Writes a record at current position, advances pointer | `WRITE` |
| `reset()` | Resets pointer to the beginning of the file (BOF) | `REWIND` |
| `skip(n)` *(extension)* | Advances/rewinds pointer by `n` records | `SPACE / BACKSPACE` |
| `append()` *(extension)* | Adds record at EOF | Requires positioning at EOF |

### 2.1.3 File Position Pointer Mathematics
At any instant `t`, the pointer's logical position `P(t)` after `k` operations evolves as:

$$P(t) = P_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta_i$$

where $P_0$ is the initial position (typically $0$ after `reset()`) and $\Delta_i \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ is the per-operation increment (since most sequential file operations advance by exactly one record).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Sequential Access Used?**
> 1. **Simplicity** — minimal OS kernel complexity; no index management.
> 2. **Predictable I/O** — useful for streaming media (audio/video) where buffering absorbs latency.
> 3. **No wasted disk space** — zero metadata overhead.
> 4. **Cache-friendly** — linear reads maximize OS page-cache and disk prefetch hit ratios.

### 2.1.4 Limitations of Sequential Access
- **No direct random read**: locating the `k`-th record requires reading all `(k-1)` preceding records.
- **Update in place is dangerous** — overwriting a record of different length corrupts the file.
- **Poor fit for interactive applications** (e.g., "edit record #4500" requires O(n) traversal).

---

## 2.2 Direct Access (Random / Relative Access) — Detailed Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Core Characteristics
- The file is conceptualized as a **numbered sequence of fixed-size logical blocks** or records: `Block 0, Block 1, Block 2, ..., Block (n-1)`.
- The application can supply an **arbitrary block number `n`** to the OS, and the OS calculates the corresponding **physical address** using:

$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Base Address} + (n \times \text{Block Size})$$

- There is **no implied ordering** between successive reads/writes — the file is treated as a **random-access array**.

### 2.2.2 Primitive Operations

| Operation | Function |
|---|---|
| `read_n(n)` | Reads the `n`-th block (zero-indexed or one-indexed per OS) |
| `write_n(n, data)` | Writes data into the `n`-th block, potentially overwriting |
| `position_to(n)` | Moves the file pointer to block `n` without I/O |
| `read_next()` / `write_next()` | Optional — performs sequential read/write *from* the current position |

### 2.2.3 File Control Block (FCB) / i-node Metadata for Direct Access
For direct access to work, the OS's **File Control Block** must store:
- **Base address (starting block number)** of the file on disk.
- **Block size** (in bytes).
- **Total number of blocks** in the file.

The effective offset of block `n` (in bytes) is:

$$\text{Offset}(n) = n \times B$$

where $B$ is the fixed block size in bytes. The physical disk location is then computed by the disk driver using **CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector)** translation or **LBA (Logical Block Addressing)**.

### 2.2.4 Why "Relative" Access?
The name "**relative access**" is sometimes used in textbooks (e.g., Stallings) because the block number `n` is a **relative offset from the file's starting block (block 0)**, not an absolute disk sector. This makes the method **device-independent** and **portable across different disk geometries**.

### 2.2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Database Management Systems (DBMS):** MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL — tables are heap files accessed via row IDs.
- **Real-time control systems:** Automotive ECUs must read sensor data block `n` within strict deadlines.
- **Virtual memory paging:** Page tables map virtual page `n` directly to a physical frame — analogous to direct file access.

---

## 2.3 Indexed Access — Detailed Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 Core Characteristics
- Indexed access is a **two-stage retrieval scheme**:
  - **Stage 1:** Search the (small) **index file** to find the pointer corresponding to the desired **search-key**.
  - **Stage 2:** Use the pointer (block address) to perform a **direct read** of the actual data record.
- The **index** is itself a file containing **(search-key, pointer)** pairs.

### 2.3.2 Index File Structure

A simplified index entry is:

$$\text{Index Entry} = \langle \text{SearchKey}, \text{BlockPointer} \rangle$$

For a file with `N` records, the index contains `N` such entries (in the simple case). The index is **sorted by SearchKey** for fast binary search.

### 2.3.3 Types of Indexed Access

| Index Type | Description | Search Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Level Index** | One flat index file; searched via linear/binary search | $O(\log N)$ binary, $O(N)$ linear |
| **Multi-Level Index** | Index-of-index; hierarchical (e.g., B-Tree, B+Tree) | $O(\log_b N)$ where `b` is fanout |
| **Hashed Index** | Search-key is hashed to compute pointer directly | $O(1)$ average, $O(N)$ worst-case |
| **Clustered Index** | Index entries dictate the physical order of data | Same as above |
| **Secondary (Non-Clustered) Index** | Logical ordering differs from physical ordering | $O(\log N)$ |

### 2.3.4 Primitive Operations

| Operation | Function |
|---|---|
| `search(key)` | Searches the index for `key`; returns block pointer |
| `read_key(key)` | Searches index → fetches pointer → reads data block |
| `insert(record)` | Inserts data block → updates all relevant index entries |
| `delete(key)` | Searches index → deletes data block → removes index entry |

### 2.3.5 Multi-Level Index Recursion
For a 2-level index, the search proceeds:

$$\text{Top Index} \rightarrow \text{Second-Level Index} \rightarrow \text{Data Block}$$

If the top index has $E$ entries (each pointing to a second-level index of $E$ entries), and the data file has $N$ records, then:

$$E^2 = N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E = \sqrt{N}$$

Extending to `k` levels (a B+Tree-like generalization):

$$E^k = N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E = N^{1/k}$$

The search complexity becomes $O(k \cdot \log E) = O(k \cdot \frac{1}{k} \log N) = O(\log N)$.

### 2.3.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Database Indexes:** B+Tree indexes in MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle.
- **File systems:** NTFS MFT (Master File Table), ext4 inode tables, HFS+ catalog.
- **Search engines:** Inverted index for web search (Google, Elasticsearch).
- **Library systems:** Card catalog → book shelf pointer (classic 19th-century analogy).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the table below. Most numerical and short-answer questions on this topic reduce to applying one of these formulas.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sequential file pointer advance | $P_{k+1} = P_k + 1$ | Record index |
| 2 | Sequential search time (worst case) | $T_{seq} = n \cdot t_{record}$ | $n$ = total records, $t_{record}$ = per-record access time |
| 3 | Direct access byte offset | $\text{Offset}(n) = n \times B$ | $B$ = block size in **bytes** |
| 4 | Direct access physical block | $\text{Cyl} = (B_{file} + n) \div (\text{heads} \times \text{sectors})$ | CHS geometry |
| 5 | Direct access average time | $T_{direct} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$ | **≈ 10 ms typical HDD** |
| 6 | Single-level index binary search | $T_{idx} = \log_2 N$ comparisons | $N$ = index entries |
| 7 | Multi-level index entries per level | $E = N^{1/k}$ | $k$ = number of levels |
| 8 | Total multi-level search comparisons | $k \cdot \log_2(N^{1/k}) = \log_2 N$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Hashed index average search | $O(1)$ | Constant time, expected |
| 10 | Hashed index worst-case | $O(N)$ | All keys hash to same bucket |
| 11 | Sequential file overhead | $0\%$ | No extra metadata |
| 12 | Indexed file overhead (approximate) | $\frac{I}{I+D} \times 100\%$ | $I$ = index size, $D$ = data size |

> **Exam Tip:** In KTU university exams, you may be given a numeric problem like *"A file has 10,000 records. Find the number of index levels and entries per level for a 3-level index."* — Just apply formulas **#6, #7, #8** above.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Comparisons in Sequential vs Indexed Search

**Problem Setup (Classic KTU-style):**
A file contains `N = 1,000,000` records. A specific record must be located.
- **Sequential search** scans from the beginning.
- **Indexed search** uses a single-level sorted index searched via binary search.

**Step 1 — Sequential Search Analysis**

The average number of comparisons for a successful sequential search is given by:

$$\text{Avg. Comparisons} = \frac{N + 1}{2}$$

Substituting $N = 1{,}000{,}000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Avg. Comparisons}_{seq} &= \frac{1{,}000{,}000 + 1}{2} \\
&= \frac{1{,}000{,}001}{2} \\
&= 500{,}000.5 \text{ comparisons}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **unsuccessful search**, all `N` records are examined:

$$\text{Worst Comparisons}_{seq} = N = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ comparisons}$$

**Step 2 — Binary Search on Sorted Index**

Binary search on a sorted array of `N` elements has complexity:

$$\text{Comparisons}_{binary} = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1 \text{ (for unsuccessful)}$$

$$\text{Comparisons}_{binary} = \lfloor \log_2 N \rfloor + 1 \text{ (for successful, average)}$$

Substituting $N = 1{,}000{,}000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_2(1{,}000{,}000) &= \log_2(10^6) \\
&= 6 \cdot \log_2(10) \\
&= 6 \times 3.32193 \\
&\approx 19.93
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Comparisons}_{idx} = \lceil 19.93 \rceil + 1 = 20 + 1 = 21 \text{ comparisons}$$

**Step 3 — Performance Gain Factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup Factor} &= \frac{\text{Avg. Comparisons}_{seq}}{\text{Comparisons}_{idx}} \\
&= \frac{500{,}000.5}{21} \\
&\approx 23{,}809.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The indexed search is approximately **23,810 times faster** than the sequential search for this dataset. This quantifies the engineering value of index structures.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Multi-Level Index Design for a Given File

**Problem Setup:**
A file contains `N = 65,536` records. We need to design a **2-level index** such that:
- The **outer (top-level) index** fits in **one disk block**.
- Each **second-level index** also fits in **one disk block**.

**Given Parameters:**
- Disk block size: `B = 4096 bytes` (= 4 KB)
- Index entry size: `E = 8 bytes` (4 bytes key + 4 bytes block pointer)

**Step 1 — Compute Number of Index Entries per Block**

The number of index entries that fit in a single block is:

$$n_{entries} = \frac{B}{E}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{entries} &= \frac{4096 \text{ bytes}}{8 \text{ bytes/entry}} \\
&= 512 \text{ entries/block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the Required Number of Second-Level Index Blocks**

The total number of data records is $N = 65{,}536$. Each second-level index can hold pointers to $n_{entries} = 512$ data records. Therefore, the number of second-level index blocks needed is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{L2} &= \frac{N}{n_{entries}} \\
&= \frac{65{,}536}{512} \\
&= 128 \text{ second-level index blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the Required Number of Top-Level Index Blocks**

The top-level index must hold one pointer per second-level index block. Since one block holds $n_{entries} = 512$ pointers, the number of top-level blocks is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{L1} &= \frac{n_{L2}}{n_{entries}} \\
&= \frac{128}{512} \\
&= 0.25 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result**

Since $n_{L1} < 1$, **a 2-level index is over-engineered**. The entire top-level index (128 entries) easily fits inside **a single 4-KB block**. So the design reduces to a **single-level index** that can be binary-searched in memory after one disk read.

**Step 5 — Compute Disk I/O for a Lookup**

- **Single-level index:** 1 disk read (load index) + 1 disk read (load data) = **2 I/Os**
- **Two-level index:** 1 read (L1) + 1 read (L2) + 1 read (data) = **3 I/Os**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Access Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **high-yield for KTU design-type questions** (e.g., *"Suggest a suitable file access method for a payroll system with 50,000 employees."*).

| Application Scenario | File Size | Read Pattern | Update Frequency | **Recommended Access Method** | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Audio/Video streaming | Very Large | Linear | Rare | **Sequential** | Maximizes throughput, cache-friendly |
| Compiler source file | Small–Medium | Linear | Write at end | **Sequential** | Natural code-flow traversal |
| Bank account ledger | Medium | Random by Account# | Frequent | **Direct** | Fast positional lookup of record `n` |
| Airline reservation | Medium–Large | Random by PNR | Frequent | **Direct / Indexed** | PNR-based lookup requires index |
| Library catalog | Large | Random by Title/Author | Rare | **Indexed** | Key-based search over books |
| Student database (university) | Large | Random by Roll# | Frequent | **Indexed** | Roll# → student record |
| Log file (system) | Very Large | Append-only | Append-only | **Sequential** | Linear write, occasional linear read |
| Hash-table based cache | Large | Random by key | Frequent | **Indexed (Hashed)** | O(1) average lookup |
| Sensor data (IoT) | Very Large | Time-range queries | Append | **Sequential + Indexed (timestamp)** | Dual access pattern |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Time Complexity of Sequential vs Direct vs Indexed Access

Let us define:
- $N$ = total number of records
- $t_r$ = average time to read one record (disk access + transfer)
- $t_s$ = disk seek time
- $t_{rot}$ = rotational latency (½ revolution on average)
- $t_{trans}$ = transfer time per block

**Sequential Access Time to read the $k$-th record:**

$$T_{seq}(k) = k \cdot t_r$$

**Direct Access Time to read the $k$-th record:**

$$T_{direct}(k) = t_s + t_{rot} + t_{trans} \approx \text{constant}$$

**Indexed Access Time to read the record with key `K`:**

$$T_{indexed}(K) = t_s + t_{rot} + t_{trans} \quad \text{(load index block)} \ + \ t_s + t_{rot} + t_{trans} \quad \text{(load data block)}$$

For a **2-level index**, this becomes $3 \times (t_s + t_{rot} + t_{trans})$. For a **hash-based index**, ideally:

$$T_{hash} = t_s + t_{rot} + t_{trans} \quad \text{(single block fetch after hash computation)}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 File Access Methods — High-Level Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the three file access methods relate to each other, the underlying storage, and the File Control Block (FCB).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1[Application Program]
    sys1[Operating System File Manager]
    fcb1[File Control Block FCB i-node]
    storage1[Secondary Storage Disk SSD]

    subgraph AccessMethods
        seq1[Sequential Access]
        dir1[Direct Access Random]
        idx1[Indexed Access]
    end

    app1 --> sys1
    sys1 --> AccessMethods
    AccessMethods --> fcb1
    fcb1 --> storage1

    seq1 -.linear traversal.-> storage1
    dir1 -.positional jump.-> storage1
    idx1 -.two stage search.-> storage1
```

---

## 4.2 Sequential Access — Pointer Advancement Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rec1[Record R1]
    rec2[Record R2]
    rec3[Record R3]
    rec4[Record Rn]
    ptr1[File Pointer P]

    rec1 --> rec2
    rec2 --> rec3
    rec3 --> rec4
    ptr1 -.currently at.-> rec1
```

**Reading the file in sequential order:**
1. `reset()` → `P` points to `R1`.
2. `read_next()` → reads `R1`, `P` advances to `R2`.
3. `read_next()` → reads `R2`, `P` advances to `R3`.
4. ... continue until `EOF` (End of File) marker is reached.

---

## 4.3 Direct Access — Block Numbering Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    b0[Block 0]
    b1[Block 1]
    b2[Block 2]
    b3[Block 3]
    b4[Block n minus 1]
    req[Request read block 2]

    req --> b2
    b0 -.not read.-> b1
    b1 -.not read.-> b2
    b2 -.read.-> b3
    b3 -.not read.-> b4
```

**Key takeaway:** Only `Block 2` is fetched; all other blocks are skipped. The OS computes the disk offset using `Offset = n × BlockSize`.

---

## 4.4 Indexed Access — Two-Stage Retrieval Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app[Application: read record with key K]
    idx1[Index File Block 0]
    idx2[Index File Block 1]
    idx3[Index File Block 2]
    data1[Data Block P1]
    data2[Data Block P2]
    data3[Data Block P3]
    result[Return Record with key K]

    app --> idx1
    idx1 --> idx2
    idx2 --> idx3
    idx3 --> data1
    idx3 --> data2
    idx3 --> data3
    data2 --> result
```

**Process flow:**
1. **Stage 1:** Binary search the index to find the pointer `P2` corresponding to key `K`.
2. **Stage 2:** Use pointer `P2` to read `Data Block P2` directly.
3. Return the requested record to the application.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Architecture — Sequential vs Direct vs Indexed

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SequentialModel
        s1[Reset to BOF]
        s2[Read Record 1]
        s3[Read Record 2]
        s4[Read Record n]
        s5[EOF Reached]
        s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5
    end

    subgraph DirectModel
        d1[Issue read n]
        d2[Compute Offset n times BlockSize]
        d3[Seek to physical address]
        d4[Return Block n]
        d1 --> d2 --> d3 --> d4
    end

    subgraph IndexedModel
        i1[Issue read key K]
        i2[Search index for K]
        i3[Get block pointer P]
        i4[Direct read of block P]
        i5[Return record]
        i1 --> i2 --> i3 --> i4 --> i5
    end
```

---

## 4.6 FCB / i-node Structure for Direct & Indexed Access

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fcb[File Control Block]
    f1[File Name]
    f2[File Type]
    f3[Base Address]
    f4[Block Size B]
    f5[Total Blocks N]
    f6[Index Block Pointer]

    fcb --> f1
    fcb --> f2
    fcb --> f3
    fcb --> f4
    fcb --> f5
    fcb --> f6
```

**For Sequential Access:** Only `f1, f2, f3, f4, f5` are strictly needed.
**For Direct Access:** Same fields, but `f5` allows `n` to be validated.
**For Indexed Access:** All fields plus `f6` (pointer to top-level index block) are required.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]
**Q: Differentiate between Sequential Access and Direct Access methods. Mention one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Sequential Access | Direct Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Traversal Order** | Records are accessed in a fixed linear sequence, one after another. | Records can be accessed in any order based on a record/block number. |
| **Mechanism** | Uses a file pointer that advances automatically by one record after each I/O. | Uses positional addressing: `Offset = n × BlockSize`. |
| **Underlying Storage** | Best suited to magnetic tape model. | Best suited to magnetic disk model. |
| **Time to Read N-th Record** | $O(N)$ — must read all preceding records. | $O(1)$ — direct jump (ignoring seek time). |
| **Example** | Reading a log file, processing a payroll file in order. | Bank account lookup by account number, ATM transaction. |

**Real-world examples:**
- **Sequential:** Reading a `.csv` data file line-by-line in a Python data-analysis pipeline.
- **Direct:** Fetching a YouTube video stored at server block `n` for streaming.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: What is an index file? Why is indexed access faster than sequential access for large files?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **index file** is a small auxiliary file that contains **(search-key, block-pointer)** pairs, where each search-key corresponds to a data record's key and the block-pointer gives the disk address of the record.

**Why indexed access is faster:**
1. The index is **much smaller** than the data file and can often be **kept entirely in main memory**, enabling fast binary search.
2. A binary search on the index takes $O(\log_2 N)$ comparisons, whereas a sequential scan takes $O(N)$ comparisons in the worst case.
3. The index provides the **direct disk address** of the desired record, eliminating the need to scan through unrelated records.

**Example:** Searching for student roll number `CSB21047` in a database of 10,000 students — sequential search requires up to 10,000 comparisons, but an indexed binary search requires only $\lceil \log_2(10000) \rceil = 14$ comparisons.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - For **direct access numerical problems**, students **forget to multiply by block size** and lose 3–4 marks.
> - For **index problems**, students often compute the **wrong number of levels** because they confuse the *number of index blocks* with the *number of records per index block*. Always clearly define your variables.
> - For **comparison questions**, do NOT write only advantages. Examiners explicitly allocate **2 marks for limitations** of each method.
> - **Diagrams fetch full marks.** Even a hand-drawn (textual) diagram of the access flow earns 2 bonus marks in the KTU valuation scheme.
> - Always conclude design-type questions with a **justification paragraph** ("Therefore, indexed access is recommended because…").

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [Choice Option 1]
**Q: A file contains 16,384 records, each of size 128 bytes. The disk has a block size of 512 bytes. Design a single-level index for the file. Compute:**
**(a) The number of blocks required for the data file.**
**(b) The number of index entries, assuming one index entry is 8 bytes (4 bytes key + 4 bytes block pointer).**
**(c) The number of index blocks required.**
**(d) The total number of disk block accesses to read a specific record using the indexed method. Justify the design with a diagram.**

#### Model Solution (14 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Number of records: $N = 16{,}384$
- Record size: $R = 128$ bytes
- Disk block size: $B = 512$ bytes
- Index entry size: $E = 8$ bytes

**Step 1 — Records per Block**

$$\text{Records per block} = \frac{B}{R}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Records per block} &= \frac{512 \text{ bytes}}{128 \text{ bytes/record}} \\
&= 4 \text{ records/block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Data Blocks (Part a — 4 marks)**

$$n_{data} = \frac{N}{\text{Records per block}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{data} &= \frac{16{,}384}{4} \\
&= 4{,}096 \text{ data blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Index Entries (Part b — 3 marks)**

In a single-level index, **one index entry is created per data block** (not per record, assuming block-level indexing):

$$n_{entries} = n_{data} = 4{,}096 \text{ entries}$$

**Step 4 — Index Entries per Block**

$$\text{Entries per index block} = \frac{B}{E} = \frac{512}{8} = 64 \text{ entries/block}$$

**Step 5 — Number of Index Blocks (Part c — 4 marks)**

$$n_{idx} = \frac{n_{entries}}{\text{Entries per index block}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{idx} &= \frac{4{,}096}{64} \\
&= 64 \text{ index blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Disk Block Accesses (Part d — 3 marks)**

To read a specific record using the index:
- 1 disk access to **read the relevant index block** (containing the pointer).
- 1 disk access to **read the data block** containing the record.

$$\text{Total Disk Accesses} = 1 \text{ (index)} + 1 \text{ (data)} = 2 \text{ block accesses}$$

**Justification with Diagram (Diagram block — included below for clarity):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    appQ[Query Search Key K]
    idxBlk[Index Block 64 blocks]
    dataBlk[Data Block 4096 blocks]
    resQ[Return Record]

    appQ --> idxBlk
    idxBlk --> dataBlk
    dataBlk --> resQ
```

**Conclusion:** This design is efficient because the index (64 blocks) is only **1.56%** the size of the data file (4096 blocks), yet it enables random access in just **2 disk I/Os** — vastly superior to sequential scan which would require up to 4096 block reads.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Choice Option 2]
**Q: (a) Explain the three major file access methods in detail with suitable diagrams.**
**(b) A banking application stores customer records. The bank has 50,000 customers. Each customer record is 256 bytes. The disk block size is 1024 bytes. Suggest and justify the most suitable file access method for this application. Also compute the worst-case number of comparisons required.**

#### Model Solution (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — Detailed Explanation of Three Access Methods (7 marks)**

The three major file access methods in operating systems are:

**1. Sequential Access:**
- Records are read/written in a fixed linear order.
- The OS maintains a file pointer that advances automatically after each operation.
- Primitive operations: `read_next`, `write_next`, `reset`, `skip(n)`, `append`.
- Best suited to **magnetic tape** and **streaming applications**.
- Time to read the $k$-th record: $O(k)$.

**2. Direct Access (Random/Relative Access):**
- The file is viewed as a numbered sequence of fixed-size blocks.
- The application supplies a block number $n$, and the OS computes:
  $$\text{Offset}(n) = n \times B$$
  to locate the physical address.
- Primitive operations: `read_n(n)`, `write_n(n)`, `position_to(n)`.
- Best suited to **disk-based databases** and **transaction systems**.
- Time to read the $k$-th record: $O(1)$ (ignoring seek time).

**3. Indexed Access:**
- A separate **index file** stores `(search-key, block-pointer)` pairs.
- Retrieval is two-stage: search the index, then directly read the data block.
- Supports **single-level, multi-level, and hashed** index structures.
- Time complexity: $O(\log N)$ for tree-based, $O(1)$ for hashed.
- Best suited to **large databases, catalogs, and search engines**.

**Diagram (textual representation for the answer sheet):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Three
        A1[Sequential: Pointer advances linearly]
        A2[Direct: Jump to block n via Offset]
        A3[Indexed: Search index, then jump to data]
    end
```

**Part (b) — Suitable Access Method for Banking Application (7 marks)**

**Application Analysis:**
- The bank has $N = 50{,}000$ customers.
- Each record is 256 bytes.
- Disk block size $B = 1024$ bytes.
- The application must **fetch a specific customer record by Account Number** in real time (e.g., at an ATM or teller counter).
- Lookup pattern is **random** (any account number can be queried at any time).

**Suggested Access Method: Indexed Access (with Hashing for O(1) average lookup).**

**Justification:**
1. Sequential access is **unsuitable** because the worst-case comparison count is $\frac{N+1}{2} = 25{,}000.5$ comparisons, which is unacceptably slow for real-time banking.
2. Direct access would require knowing the customer's **relative block number** in advance; however, account numbers are not contiguous in a way that maps naturally to block numbers — this would need a separate translation table, which is essentially an index.
3. Indexed access allows the bank to maintain an index keyed by **Account Number**, and a hashed index provides $O(1)$ average lookup time, ideal for transaction processing.

**Worst-Case Comparison Count (Binary Search on Sorted Index):**

The number of data blocks is:

$$n_{data} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ records} \times 256 \text{ bytes/record}}{1024 \text{ bytes/block}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{data} &= \frac{12{,}800{,}000}{1024} \\
&= 12{,}500 \text{ data blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming one index entry per data block, the index contains $n_{idx} = 12{,}500$ entries.

For a **binary search** on the index, the worst-case number of comparisons is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Comparisons}_{binary} &= \lceil \log_2(12{,}500) \rceil + 1 \\
&= \lceil 13.61 \rceil + 1 \\
&= 14 + 1 \\
&= 15 \text{ comparisons}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **hashed index** (the truly optimal banking solution), the worst-case comparisons are $O(1)$ average and $O(N)$ worst-case, but in practice hash collisions are managed via chaining or open addressing, yielding ~1 comparison for lookup.

**Comparison Table for Clarity:**

| Method | Worst-Case Comparisons | Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| Sequential | 50,000 | Unacceptable |
| Direct (by Account#) | 1 (if mapping exists) | Requires account#→block mapping table |
| Indexed (Binary Search) | 15 | **Highly suitable** |
| Indexed (Hashed) | ~1 | **Most suitable** |

**Conclusion:** A **hashed indexed access method** is recommended for the banking application, as it minimizes lookup time to near-constant $O(1)$ complexity while supporting frequent updates (transactions) and random access by account number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Kit — Read this 3 times before the KTU exam.**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- ✅ **Sequential Access:** Linear, one-record-at-a-time traversal; pointer advances automatically; tape model.
- ✅ **Direct Access (Random/Relative):** Jump to block number `n` using `Offset = n × B`; disk model; constant-time.
- ✅ **Indexed Access:** Two-stage retrieval via an index file containing `(key, pointer)` pairs; supports single-level, multi-level, and hashed variants.

### 🔑 Key Formulas
- ✅ Direct offset: $\text{Offset}(n) = n \times B$
- ✅ Sequential search (avg): $\frac{N+1}{2}$
- ✅ Binary search on index: $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$
- ✅ Multi-level index entries: $E = N^{1/k}$
- ✅ Total multi-level search: $\log_2 N$

### 🔑 Typical Application Mapping
- ✅ **Sequential** → logs, streaming, compilers, backup tapes.
- ✅ **Direct** → real-time systems, simple DBMS, ATM transactions.
- ✅ **Indexed** → large DBs, search engines, file systems, library catalogs.

### 🔑 Advantages & Limitations (Must know for "Differentiate" questions)
- ✅ Sequential: **+** Simple, no overhead, cache-friendly. **−** Slow random access, hard to update.
- ✅ Direct: **+** Fast positional access, no extra storage. **−** No associative search by key.
- ✅ Indexed: **+** Fast key-based search. **−** Index overhead, index maintenance on update.

### 🔑 Numerical Patterns to Practice
- ✅ Compute data blocks from records and block size.
- ✅ Compute number of index entries and index blocks.
- ✅ Compute disk accesses for single-level vs multi-level index.
- ✅ Compare sequential vs indexed search complexity.
- ✅ Design index for a given file size.

### 🔑 Common Examiner Traps
- ❌ Confusing **physical disk block number** with **logical record number**.
- ❌ Forgetting to **multiply by record size** when computing byte offsets.
- ❌ Stating **$O(1)$ for all indexed access** — must specify whether it's hashed vs tree-based.
- ❌ Forgetting **collision handling** in hashed index answers.

### 🔑 One-Line Memory Hooks
- 🧠 *"**Sequential = Tape**, **Direct = Disk Array**, **Indexed = Disk + Lookup Table**."*
- 🧠 *"**Pointer advances in Sequential, Jumps in Direct, Resolves in Indexed**."*
- 🧠 *"**The index is a map; the file is the territory**."*

---

> **END OF NOTES — File Access Methods (Sequential, Direct, Indexed) | PCITT403 Module 4 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: File Allocation Methods (Contiguous, Linked, and Indexed Allocation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** File Allocation Methods (Contiguous, Linked, and Indexed Allocation)** ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is File Allocation? In an operating system, a **file** is a logical container of related information, but on the physical disk it must occupy a sequence of **disk blocks** (also called *sectors* or *allocation units*). The method by which these disk blocks are assigned, addressed, and linked to form a single logical file is called the **File Allocation Method**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *File Allocation Method is the disk-space management strategy used by the file system to map the logical file (as seen by the user/process) onto the physical disk blocks, defining how blocks are organized, addressed, and tracked by the operating system.* The choice of allocation method directly impacts: 1. **Access Speed** — Sequential vs Random access efficiency. 2. **Space Utilization** — Internal and External Fragmentation. 3. **Scalability** — Maximum file size the system can support. 4. **Reliability** — Behavior when a block gets corrupted. 5. **Metadata Overhead** — Storage consumed by pointers/indexes. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three Library Models" Imagine a university library that must store 5 chapters of a book (File A) on its shelves: | Model | Real-World Analogy | How Chapters Are Stored | |---|---|---| | **Contiguous Allocation** | All 5 chapters are placed **side-by-side** on one single long shelf. | A starting shelf number + length is noted in the catalog card. | | **Linked Allocation** | Chapters are scattered on different shelves, but each chapter cover has a **"see shelf number..." note** pointing to the next. | The catalog card only stores the starting shelf number. | | **Indexed Allocation** | A **separate index card (catalog)** is created that lists all shelf numbers where the chapters are kept. | The catalog card points to the index card; the index card lists all shelves. | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The *trade-off* is always between **fast random access** (Contiguous/Indexed) and **flexibility with no external fragmentation** (Linked). No single method is universally optimal — this is why modern file systems (e.g., **ext4**, **NTFS**, **XFS**) use *hybrid* schemes. ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme - It maps directly to **Course Outcome CO4**: *“Design and implement file system mechanisms including directory structures and allocation strategies.”* - It tests understanding of **Bloom's Taxonomy Level L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)** — students must compare allocation tables, derive maximum file sizes, and evaluate fragmentation. - It forms the foundation for advanced modules like **disk scheduling** and **RAID structures**. --- ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Allocation Methods — Operational Breakdown #### **A. Contiguous Allocation** Each file occupies a set of **consecutive disk blocks** on the disk. The directory entry needs only two values: - **Starting Block Number** - **Length (number of blocks)** To access the *i-th* block of the file: $$\text{Block Address} = \text{Starting Block} + i$$ where $0 \le i < \text{Length}$. **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. When a file is created, the OS scans the **free-space list** (bit vector or linked list) for a contiguous run of blocks equal to the file size. 2. If found, the entire range is reserved. 3. The directory entry stores `[start, length]`. 4. For read/write, the OS performs simple arithmetic to compute the block address — *no indirection*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** Contiguous allocation supports **both sequential and direct access** with excellent **read-ahead / pre-fetching** performance, making it ideal for magnetic tapes and early CD-ROM file systems (ISO 9660). #### **B. Linked Allocation** Each disk block contains: - A **data payload** - A **pointer to the next block** of the same file The directory entry stores only the **starting block number** (no length, no index). The last block's pointer is `NULL` (or `-1`). **Operational Logic:** 1. OS picks any free block for the first chunk of the file. 2. Each subsequent block stores a pointer to the next. 3. To read the *i-th* block, the OS must traverse *i* pointers — sequential access only. > [!WARNING] > **Critical Drawback:** Random access to block *i* requires *i* disk I/O operations. This makes linked allocation **catastrophic for direct/random access** workloads like databases. **Variant — File Allocation Table (FAT):** Instead of storing the next-pointer inside each data block (which wastes data-block space), the OS keeps a separate array — the **FAT** — in memory. Each entry `FAT[k]` gives the next block number after block `k`. This trades pointer-space for memory overhead but speeds up traversal. #### **C. Indexed Allocation** A dedicated block called the **Index Block** is created for each file. The index block contains an array of pointers to all data blocks of that file. The directory entry stores only the **index block address**. **Operational Logic:** 1. Create the file → allocate one index block. 2. As data is written, allocate new data blocks and record their addresses in the index block. 3. To read block *i*, the OS reads the index block, looks up the *i-th* entry, and goes directly to the data block. **Sub-variants for huge files:** - **Single-level index:** Simple, but max file size = (pointers per index block) × (block size). - **Two-level index:** Index block points to secondary index blocks (good for very large files). - **Combined scheme (UNIX inode):** The inode has **12 direct pointers + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect**. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symbols Used (Standard KTU Convention):** > - $B$ = Disk block size (bytes) — typical: **4096 bytes** or **512 bytes** > - $P$ = Pointer size (bytes) — typical: **4 bytes** (32-bit) or **8 bytes** (64-bit) > - $N$ = Number of pointers per index block > - $F_{\max}$ = Maximum file size supported $$ N = \frac{B}{P} $$ **Maximum File Size Formulas (Examination Hotspot):** | Allocation Scheme | Maximum File Size $F_{\max}$ | |---|---| | Contiguous |
