Limited by disk size only (theoretically $\infty$) | | Linked (FAT) | Unlimited by pointer chain; only limited by FAT address space | | Single-level Indexed | $F_{\max} = N \times B = \dfrac{B^2}{P}$ | | Two-level Indexed | $F_{\max} = N \times N \times B = \dfrac{B^3}{P^2}$ | | UNIX Inode (12 direct + 1 SI + 1 DI + 1 TI) | $F_{\max} = 12B + \dfrac{B^2}{P} + \dfrac{B^3}{P^2} + \dfrac{B^4}{P^3}$ | **Block Address Calculation (Random Access):** | Method | Address of *i-th* block | Disk I/O Required | |---|---|---| | Contiguous | $A_i = A_0 + i$ | **1** | | Linked | Sequential scan from $A_0$ | **i** | | Single-level Indexed | $A_i = \text{Index}[i]$ (one disk read for index) | **2** | **Fragmentation Behavior:** | Method | Internal Frag. | External Frag. | |---|---|---| | Contiguous | No | **Yes (severe)** | | Linked | No | No | | Indexed | No | No | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Use:** > - **Contiguous** → CD-ROM (ISO 9660), early IBM VM/CMS. > - **Linked (FAT)** → MS-DOS, Windows 95/98 (FAT16/FAT32), SD cards. > - **Indexed (inode)** → UNIX, Linux (ext2/ext3/ext4), macOS HFS+. > - **Hybrid (Extent-based)** → NTFS, XFS, ext4 — small files contiguous, large files use B-tree indexed extents. --- ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum File Size in Single-Level Indexed Allocation **Given:** - Block size $B = 4096$ bytes - Pointer size $P = 4$ bytes **Step 1:** Compute the number of pointers that fit inside one index block. $$ N = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 \text{ pointers} $$ This means one index block can address up to **1024 data blocks**. **Step 2:** Compute the maximum file size (number of data bytes that can be addressed). $$ F_{\max} = N \times B = 1024 \times 4096 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ F_{\max} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ MB} $$ This is the reason why single-level indexed allocation cannot support files larger than ~4 MB with these typical parameters. **Step 3:** Express the relationship as a single closed-form equation. $$ F_{\max} = \frac{B^2}{P} = \frac{(4096)^2}{4} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — UNIX Inode Maximum File Size **Given:** $B = 4096$ bytes, $P = 4$ bytes, Inode has 12 direct + 1 SI + 1 DI + 1 TI pointers. **Step 1:** Direct pointers contribute: $$ F_{\text{direct}} = 12 \times B = 12 \times 4096 = 49{,}152 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 2:** Single-Indirect (SI) block: $$ F_{\text{SI}} = N \times B = \left(\frac{B}{P}\right) \times B = \frac{B^2}{P} = \frac{(4096)^2}{4} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ MB} $$ **Step 3:** Double-Indirect (DI) block: $$ F_{\text{DI}} = N \times N \times B = \left(\frac{B}{P}\right)^2 \times B = \frac{B^3}{P^2} $$ $$ F_{\text{DI}} = \frac{(4096)^3}{(4)^2} = \frac{68{,}719{,}476{,}736}{16} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB} $$ **Step 4:** Triple-Indirect (TI) block: $$ F_{\text{TI}} = N^3 \times B = \left(\frac{B}{P}\right)^3 \times B = \frac{B^4}{P^3} $$ $$ F_{\text{TI}} = \frac{(4096)^4}{(4)^3} = \frac{18{,}446{,}744{,}073{,}709{,}551{,}616}{64} = 288{,}230{,}376{,}151{,}711{,}744 \text{ bytes} \approx 256 \text{ TB} $$ **Step 5:** Total maximum file size: $$ F_{\max}^{\text{UNIX}} = F_{\text{direct}} + F_{\text{SI}} + F_{\text{DI}} + F_{\text{TI}} $$ $$ F_{\max}^{\text{UNIX}} \approx 49{,}152 + 4{,}194{,}304 + 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 + 288{,}230{,}376{,}151{,}711{,}744 $$ $$ F_{\max}^{\text{UNIX}} \approx 256 \text{ TB} + 4 \text{ GB} + 4 \text{ MB} + 48 \text{ KB} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality:** The *practical* limit on Linux ext4 with 4 KB blocks and 4-byte pointers is **~16 TiB** (because ext4 uses 48-bit block pointers), but the inode *mathematical* maximum with 4-byte pointers reaches ~256 TB. Modern 64-bit pointers push this into **exabytes**. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Random Access Block Calculation **Problem:** A file uses *single-level indexed allocation* with index block at address **500**. To access logical block *i = 17*, calculate: (a) The disk I/O sequence. (b) The actual disk block number read. **Step 1:** Read the index block (1 I/O): - Disk head seeks to block 500. - Index block contains: `[block 0, block 1, ..., block 17, ...]`. **Step 2:** Look up the 17th entry: - Suppose $\text{Index}[17] = 823$. **Step 3:** Read data block 823 (2nd I/O): - Disk head seeks to block 823 and reads data. **Total I/O = 2** (one for index, one for data) — *independent of i*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Contrast with Linked Allocation:** For the same file, accessing block 17 would require **17 disk I/Os** (traversing 17 pointers sequentially). This is the *defining advantage* of indexed allocation. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Criterion | Contiguous | Linked | Indexed | |---|---|---|---| | **Random Access Cost** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ to $O(2)$ | | **Sequential Access Cost** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | | **External Fragmentation** | Yes (high) | No | No | | **Internal Fragmentation** | No | Yes (per-block pointer waste) | Possible (in index block) | | **Space Overhead** | Directory entry only | Pointer per data block | One full index block per file | | **Max File Size** | Limited only by disk | Limited by address space | $B^2/P$ (single-level) | | **Reliability** | Single point of failure (start block) | Single bad pointer breaks chain | Index block is SPOF | | **Pre-allocation Needed?** | Yes (must declare size) | No (on-demand) | No (on-demand) | | **Used By** | CD-ROM, ISO 9660 | FAT16/FAT32 | ext2/ext3/ext4, NTFS | --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Contiguous Allocation — Disk Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR dirA["Directory Entry: File A"] -->|start=10, len=4| dataA["Blocks 10,11,12,13"] dirB["Directory Entry: File B"] -->|start=20, len=3| dataB["Blocks 20,21,22"] free["Free Space List"]:::freeStyle classDef freeStyle fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Linked Allocation — With File Allocation Table (FAT) ```mermaid flowchart LR dirA["Directory: File A starts at 10"] --> fat10["FAT[10]=15"] fat10 --> blk10["Block 10: DATA"] blk10 --> fat15["FAT[15]=27"] fat15 --> blk15["Block 15: DATA"] blk15 --> fat27["FAT[27]=NULL"] fat27 --> endA["END OF FILE"]:::endStyle classDef endStyle fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Indexed Allocation — Single-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB dirEntry["Directory Entry
Index Block = 500"]:::dirStyle idxBlock["Index Block 500
0: 301
1: 302
2: 405
..."]:::idxStyle blk0["Data Block 301"]:::dataStyle blk1["Data Block 302"]:::dataStyle blk2["Data Block 405"]:::dataStyle dirEntry --> idxBlock idxBlock --> blk0 idxBlock --> blk1 idxBlock --> blk2 classDef dirStyle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px classDef idxStyle fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px classDef dataStyle fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 UNIX Inode — Multi-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB inode["Inode Block
12 Direct Pointers
+ 1 SI + 1 DI + 1 TI"]:::inodeStyle d1["D1: 301"]:::dataStyle d2["D2: 302"]:::dataStyle d3["D3: 405"]:::dataStyle si["SI Pointer: 600"]:::idxStyle di["DI Pointer: 700"]:::idxStyle ti["TI Pointer: 800"]:::idxStyle si2["SI Block 600
1024 ptrs"]:::idxStyle di2["DI Block 700
1024 SI ptrs"]:::idxStyle ti2["TI Block 800
1024 DI ptrs"]:::idxStyle inode --> d1 inode --> d2 inode --> d3 inode --> si inode --> di inode --> ti si --> si2 di --> di2 ti --> ti2 classDef inodeStyle fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:3px classDef dataStyle fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1px classDef idxStyle fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram — Access Pattern ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CONT["Contiguous - 1 I/O"] c1["Read Block 0"] end subgraph LINK["Linked - N I/Os"] l1["Read Block 0"] l2["Follow Pointer"] l3["Read Block 1"] l4["Follow Pointer"] l5["Read Block N"] end subgraph IDX["Indexed - 2 I/Os"] i1["Read Index Block"] i2["Look up Entry"] i3["Read Data Block"] end classDef contStyle fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px classDef linkStyle fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px classDef idxStyle fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Differentiate between Contiguous and Linked file allocation methods. Mention one key advantage and one key disadvantage of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — strict 3-point structure expected):** | Aspect | Contiguous Allocation | Linked Allocation | |---|---|---| | **Block Layout** | All blocks of a file occupy consecutive disk addresses. | Blocks can be scattered anywhere; each block has a pointer to the next. | | **Random Access** | Supported — direct calculation via $A_i = A_0 + i$. | Not efficient — requires traversal of *i* pointers. | | **External Fragmentation** | Yes (severe as files are created/deleted). | None — any free block can be used. | | **Metadata Stored** | `[Starting block, Length]`. | `[Starting block]` only. | **Key Advantage — Contiguous:** Best random access performance, ideal for sequential media (magnetic tape). **Key Disadvantage — Contiguous:** Suffers from external fragmentation and requires pre-allocation of file size. **Key Advantage — Linked:** No external fragmentation; supports dynamic file growth. **Key Disadvantage — Linked:** Poor random access performance; pointer overhead inside each data block wastes space. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is an Index Block? Why is Indexed Allocation considered superior to Linked Allocation for databases?** **Model Answer:** An **Index Block** is a dedicated disk block that stores an array of pointers — one for each data block belonging to a file. The directory entry points to this index block, and the index block in turn points to all the file's data blocks. Indexed allocation is superior to linked allocation for databases because databases rely on **random (direct) access** to records. Specifically: 1. **Constant-time random access:** In indexed allocation, accessing block *i* requires only **2 disk I/Os** (read index, read data) — independent of *i*. In linked allocation, it requires *i* I/Os. 2. **No pointer storage waste:** The pointers are consolidated into a single index block, freeing up data block space for actual user data. 3. **Better locality and pre-fetching:** The index block can be cached in RAM (as in UNIX `inode`), making subsequent accesses extremely fast. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A:** > Students frequently write *“indexed allocation is faster”* without specifying **which operation** is faster (random vs sequential). You **MUST** clarify *“constant-time random access via 2 disk I/Os”* to earn full 3 marks. Vague answers get only 1 mark. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Choose either Q(A) or Q(B)** --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* [L4-Analyze / L5-Evaluate] **(a)** Explain the **three file allocation methods** — Contiguous, Linked, and Indexed — with neat diagrams showing how blocks are organized for a sample file. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A file system uses **single-level indexed allocation** with a block size of **2048 bytes** and a disk block pointer size of **8 bytes**. - (i) Calculate the **maximum file size** that can be addressed. **[3 marks]** - (ii) How many **disk I/O operations** are required to access the **1000th block** of a file? Justify. **[2 marks]** - (iii) If the system is upgraded to **two-level indexed allocation**, calculate the new maximum file size. **[2 marks]** --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q(A)** **(a) Three Allocation Methods — Explanation with Diagrams: [7 marks]** **1. Contiguous Allocation:** The entire file occupies a set of consecutive disk blocks. The directory entry stores `[start_block, length]`. For example, a file of 4 blocks starting at block 10 occupies blocks 10, 11, 12, 13. - **Advantages:** Excellent random and sequential access; simple implementation. - **Disadvantages:** Suffers from external fragmentation; requires pre-allocation; difficult to grow files. **2. Linked Allocation:** The directory entry stores only the starting block number. Each data block contains a pointer to the next data block. The last block's pointer is NULL. - Example: File A starts at block 10 → 10→15→27→NULL. - **Advantages:** No external fragmentation; simple on-demand growth. - **Disadvantages:** Random access is O(n); pointer overhead in each data block; a single bad pointer corrupts the rest of the file. **3. Indexed Allocation:** A dedicated **index block** stores pointers to all data blocks of the file. The directory entry points to this index block. - Example: Index block 500 → [301, 302, 405, ...]. - **Advantages:** O(1) random access; no external fragmentation; supports dynamic growth. - **Disadvantages:** Wastes one full block per file (index block); index block itself becomes a single point of failure. **Comparison Table [for last 1 mark of part (a)]:** | Criterion | Contiguous | Linked | Indexed | |---|---|---|---| | Random Access | O(1) | O(n) | O(1) | | External Fragmentation | Yes | No | No | | Pre-allocation | Required | Not required | Not required | --- **(b) Numerical Problem: [7 marks]** **Given:** - Block size $B = 2048$ bytes - Pointer size $P = 8$ bytes **(i) Maximum File Size — Single-Level Indexed: [3 marks]** **Step 1:** Number of pointers in index block: $$ N = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{2048}{8} = 256 \text{ pointers} $$ **Step 2:** Maximum file size: $$ F_{\max} = N \times B = 256 \times 2048 = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ F_{\max} = 512 \text{ KB} $$ **Step 3:** Using closed form: $$ F_{\max} = \frac{B^2}{P} = \frac{(2048)^2}{8} = \frac{4{,}194{,}304}{8} = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} $$ **Final Answer:** Maximum file size = **524,288 bytes = 512 KB**. [3 marks] --- **(ii) Disk I/O for 1000th Block: [2 marks]** In single-level indexed allocation, accessing *any* block requires: 1. **1 disk I/O** to read the index block. 2. **1 disk I/O** to read the target data block. Total = **2 disk I/Os**, *regardless of which block is requested* (this is the definition of O(1) random access). **Answer:** **2 disk I/Os**, because the index block is read once and then the 1000th entry is used to directly locate the data block — no sequential traversal needed. [2 marks] --- **(iii) Maximum File Size — Two-Level Indexed: [2 marks]** **Step 1:** Number of pointers per index block (same as before): $$ N = \frac{B}{P} = 256 $$ **Step 2:** A two-level index has one outer index block pointing to $N$ inner index blocks, each pointing to $N$ data blocks. Total addressable data blocks = $N \times N = N^2$. **Step 3:** Maximum file size: $$ F_{\max} = N^2 \times B = (256)^2 \times 2048 $$ $$ F_{\max} = 65{,}536 \times 2048 = 134{,}217{,}728 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ F_{\max} = 128 \text{ MB} $$ **Answer:** Maximum file size with two-level indexing = **134,217,728 bytes = 128 MB**. [2 marks] --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* [L4-Analyze / L5-Evaluate] **(a)** Explain the **UNIX inode-based multi-level indexed allocation scheme** with a neat diagram. Derive the formula for the **maximum file size** supported, assuming block size $B$ and pointer size $P$. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A UNIX-like system has block size **B = 1024 bytes** and pointer size **P = 4 bytes**. The inode contains **10 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, and 1 triple-indirect** pointer. - (i) Calculate the **maximum file size** supported. **[5 marks]** - (ii) How many **disk accesses** are required to access the **5000th block** of a file? Justify with a clear explanation. **[2 marks]** --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q(B)** **(a) UNIX Inode Multi-Level Indexed Scheme: [7 marks]** The UNIX inode (index node) is the metadata structure used in traditional UNIX file systems. It contains **15 pointer entries**: - The first 12 are **direct pointers** to data blocks. - The 13th is a **single-indirect (SI)** pointer → points to an index block. - The 14th is a **double-indirect (DI)** pointer → points to an index block of index blocks. - The 15th is a **triple-indirect (TI)** pointer → points to an index block of DI blocks. **Number of pointers per block:** $$ N = \frac{B}{P} $$ **Maximum File Size — Derivation:** $$ F_{\max} = \underbrace{12 \times B}_{\text{Direct}} + \underbrace{\frac{B^2}{P}}_{\text{SI}} + \underbrace{\frac{B^3}{P^2}}_{\text{DI}} + \underbrace{\frac{B^4}{P^3}}_{\text{TI}} $$ $$ F_{\max} = 12B + \frac{B^2}{P} + \frac{B^3}{P^2} + \frac{B^4}{P^3} $$ Each term's *Why*: - **12B:** 12 direct pointers × B bytes each. - **$B^2/P$:** SI block holds $N = B/P$ pointers; each points to a data block of size $B$. - **$B^3/P^2$:** DI block holds $N$ SI pointers, each pointing to $N$ data blocks → $N^2 \cdot B$ bytes. - **$B^4/P^3$:** TI block holds $N$ DI pointers, each pointing to $N^2$ data blocks → $N^3 \cdot B$ bytes. --- **(b) Numerical Calculation: [7 marks]** **Given:** $B = 1024$ bytes, $P = 4$ bytes, 10 direct + 1 SI + 1 DI + 1 TI. **Step 1:** Number of pointers per block: $$ N = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{1024}{4} = 256 $$ **Step 2:** Direct pointers contribution: $$ F_{\text{direct}} = 10 \times B = 10 \times 1024 = 10{,}240 \text{ bytes} = 10 \text{ KB} $$ **Step 3:** Single-Indirect contribution: $$ F_{\text{SI}} = N \times B = 256 \times 1024 = 262{,}144 \text{ bytes} = 256 \text{ KB} $$ **Step 4:** Double-Indirect contribution: $$ F_{\text{DI}} = N^2 \times B = (256)^2 \times 1024 = 65{,}536 \times 1024 = 67{,}108{,}864 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ MB} $$ **Step 5:** Triple-Indirect contribution: $$ F_{\text{TI}} = N^3 \times B = (256)^3 \times 1024 = 16{,}777{,}216 \times 1024 = 17{,}179{,}869{,}184 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ GB} $$ **Step 6:** Total Maximum File Size: $$ F_{\max} = 10 \text{ KB} + 256 \text{ KB} + 64 \text{ MB} + 16 \text{ GB} $$ $$ F_{\max} = 10{,}240 + 262{,}144 + 67{,}108{,}864 + 17{,}179{,}869{,}184 $$ $$ F_{\max} = 17{,}247{,}250{,}432 \text{ bytes} \approx 16.06 \text{ GB} $$ **Answer (i):** Maximum file size ≈ **16.06 GB**. [5 marks] --- **(ii) Disk Accesses for 5000th Block: [2 marks]** Block indexing is **logical block number**, where blocks 0–9 are direct, blocks 10–265 are via single-indirect, blocks 266–65,801 are via double-indirect, blocks 65,802 onward are via triple-indirect. The 5000th block lies in the range **266 to 65,801**, so it is accessed via the **double-indirect (DI)** pointer. **Disk I/O sequence:** 1. Read the inode (1 I/O) — DI pointer gives us DI block address. 2. Read the DI block (1 I/O) — entry tells us which SI block. 3. Read the SI block (1 I/O) — entry tells us which data block. 4. Read the data block (1 I/O). **Total = 4 disk I/Os.** **Answer (ii):** **4 disk I/Os** (inode + DI block + SI block + data block). [2 marks] > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to compute $N = B/P$ first. Always begin with this. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using $N \times B$ for DI instead of $N^2 \times B$. The DI block holds *index blocks*, not data blocks. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In Part (b)(ii), students often say "3 I/Os" forgetting the inode read. **Always count the inode as the first I/O**. > 4. **Unit Conversion:** 1024 bytes = 1 KB; 1024 KB = 1 MB; 1024 MB = 1 GB. Marks deducted for GB vs GiB errors. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — File Allocation Methods** - ✅ **Contiguous Allocation:** Consecutive blocks; directory stores `[start, length]`; best random access; **suffers from external fragmentation**; requires pre-allocation of file size. - ✅ **Linked Allocation:** Blocks can be scattered; each block stores a pointer to the next; directory stores only `[start]`; **no external fragmentation**; **poor random access (O(n))**. - ✅ **FAT (File Allocation Table):** Optimization of linked allocation — pointers stored in a separate in-memory table; eliminates pointer waste inside data blocks. - ✅ **Indexed Allocation:** A dedicated **index block** holds all data block pointers; **O(1) random access via 2 I/Os**; no external fragmentation. - ✅ **UNIX Inode:** Hybrid scheme with **12 direct + 1 SI + 1 DI + 1 TI** pointers; supports files up to ~256 TB with 4 KB blocks and 4-byte pointers. - ✅ **Master Formula:** $N = B / P$ and $F_{\max} = N^k \cdot B$ for $k$-level indexed allocation. - ✅ **Single-level indexed max:** $F_{\max} = B^2 / P$. - ✅ **Two-level indexed max:** $F_{\max} = B^3 / P^2$. - ✅ **Three-level indexed max:** $F_{\max} = B^4 / P^3$. - ✅ **Trade-off Triangle:** *Speed (Contiguous)* ↔ *Flexibility (Linked)* ↔ *Balance (Indexed)*. No method is universally best — modern systems use **hybrid extent-based** schemes. - ✅ **Pre-exam mantra:** *"Contiguous = 1 I/O, Linked = N I/Os, Indexed = 2 I/Os"*.

topics.title: Directory Structure (Single-Level, Two-Level, Tree-Structured, Acyclic Graph, General Graph)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Directory Structure (Single-Level, Two-Level, Tree-Structured, Acyclic Graph, General Graph)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Directory Structure** is the logical organization of files in a file system, defined by the way directory entries are arranged, accessed, and maintained. It governs the **naming**, **grouping**, **sharing**, and **hierarchy** of files and subdirectories on a storage device.

A **directory** is a special file (called a *catalog file*) that contains metadata about other files and subdirectories. The directory structure forms the **logical view** of the file system as seen by the user, decoupled from the physical layout on disk.

According to the KTU 2024 Operating Systems syllabus, there are **five canonical directory structures**, listed in increasing order of capability:

1. **Single-Level Directory** — the simplest, flattest organization.
2. **Two-Level Directory** — introduces user-based partitioning.
3. **Tree-Structured Directory** — introduces strict hierarchical nesting.
4. **Acyclic Graph Directory** — allows sharing without cycles.
5. **General Graph Directory** — permits cycles, requires garbage collection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Catalog

Imagine a **library** as your file system and the **books** as files:

- **Single-Level Catalog** → A single long shelf where *every* book in the city is dumped together. Two authors cannot have books titled "Data Structures" — naming collisions occur immediately.
- **Two-Level Catalog** → A separate reading room is assigned to each user, and a *master index* points to each room. The librarian knows your room, but you cannot browse someone else's shelf.
- **Tree-Structured Catalog** → A *Science Section* contains sub-sections (*Physics*, *Chemistry*, *Biology*), each with shelves. A path like `Science / Physics / Quantum` uniquely locates a book.
- **Acyclic Graph** → The same reference book (*Encyclopedia of Physics*) is physically one copy, but it has **two catalog cards** — one under `Physics` and one under `Reference`.
- **General Graph** → The catalog may contain *back-references* (e.g., a journal article cites and links to itself across sections), forming loops. Garbage collection is needed to remove orphan entries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The evolution from Single-Level → General Graph mirrors the historical progress of file systems from the 1960s (e.g., **CTSS**) to modern systems (e.g., **UNIX**, **Windows NTFS**). Modern operating systems use tree-structured or acyclic graph directory schemes.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Root Directory** | $D_{root}$ | The topmost directory; parent of all others |
| **Master File Directory (MFD)** | $D_{MFD}$ | In two-level systems, contains one entry per user |
| **User File Directory (UFD)** | $D_{UFD}$ | Per-user directory containing their files |
| **Path Name** | $P$ | A string that uniquely locates a file |
| **Current Working Directory** | $CWD$ | The directory currently in use by a process |
| **Symbolic Link (Symlink)** | $L_{sym}$ | A pointer to a file by its path name |
| **Hard Link** | $L_{hard}$ | A second directory entry pointing to the same inode |
| **Reference Count** | $r_c$ | Number of directory entries pointing to a file |
| **Maximum Path Depth** | $h$ | The number of directory levels from root |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naming Convention:** A file's *absolute path* starts at the **root** ($/$) in UNIX-like systems, while a *relative path* is resolved with respect to the $CWD$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Level Directory

In a **single-level directory**, all files in the system reside in **one common directory** shared by every user.

**Structure:**
$$D_{single} = \left\{ f_1, f_2, f_3, \dots, f_n \right\}$$

**Operational Logic:**

- Every file has a unique name across the *entire* system.
- There is no concept of *grouping* — files appear in one flat list.
- Search is **linear**: $O(n)$ time to find a file by name.

**Advantages:**
- Simplicity of implementation.
- Fast access for small systems.
- Easy backup — only one directory to copy.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Naming conflicts** — two users cannot have a file named `report.txt`.
- No logical grouping (e.g., by project, by user, by type).
- Difficult to scale beyond a handful of files.
- Privacy issues — every user can see every other user's files.

## 2.2 Two-Level Directory

A **two-level directory** separates files by *user*, with a top-level **Master File Directory (MFD)** mapping usernames to their **User File Directories (UFDs)**.

**Structure:**
$$D_{MFD} = \left\{ (u_1, D_{UFD_1}), (u_2, D_{UFD_2}), \dots, (u_m, D_{UFD_m}) \right\}$$

**Path Resolution:**
$$P(f) = D_{MFD}[u] + D_{UFD_u}[f]$$

**Operational Logic:**

- A user logs in, the system consults the MFD to find the user's UFD.
- All operations are confined to that user's UFD.
- *Same name* `report.txt` can exist in two different UFDs — conflict is avoided.

**Advantages:**
- Solves the *naming conflict* of single-level.
- Provides user-level **isolation** and basic **privacy**.
- Search within a UFD is $O(n)$, but the search space is much smaller.

**Disadvantages:**
- Users **cannot share files** without explicit cooperation.
- No sub-grouping within a user's files.
- Hard to organize large projects spanning many files of similar themes.

## 2.3 Tree-Structured Directory

The **tree-structured directory** generalizes the two-level scheme to a hierarchy of **arbitrary depth**. Every directory (except root) has exactly one parent.

**Structure (Recursive Definition):**
$$D_{tree} = (D_{root}, \text{ children } \mathcal{C}(D_{root}))$$
$$\mathcal{C}(D) = \left\{ D_1, D_2, \dots, D_k, f_1, f_2, \dots, f_p \right\}$$

**Operational Logic:**

- A directory can contain both **files** and **subdirectories**.
- Two types of path names:
  - **Absolute path:** starts at $D_{root}$, e.g., `/home/alice/projects/os/notes.txt`.
  - **Relative path:** starts at the $CWD$, e.g., `../bob/shared.pdf`.
- **Current working directory** concept allows convenient navigation.
- The tree has **no cycles** — a guarantee built into the structure.

**Search Complexity (balanced tree, branching factor $b$, height $h$):**
$$T_{search} = O(h) = O(\log_b n)$$

**File Deletion:**
- Deleting a file → simply removes its entry.
- Deleting a directory → must be either *empty* or use *recursive deletion* of all descendants.

**Advantages:**
- Efficient search and grouping.
- Supports **logical organization** (by user, project, type).
- Enables efficient access control at directory level.

**Disadvantages:**
- No file/directory sharing — a file exists in **exactly one** path.
- A user wanting to access a single file from multiple contexts must duplicate it.

## 2.4 Acyclic Graph Directory

An **acyclic graph** extends the tree by allowing **sharing** — the same file or subdirectory may appear under multiple paths. **No cycles** are permitted.

**Structure:**
$$G_{acyclic} = (V, E) \quad \text{where} \quad V = \text{directories} \cup \text{files}, \quad E = \text{edges (links)}$$
$$\text{Constraint: } G_{acyclic} \text{ has no cycles (DAG).}$$

**Sharing Mechanisms:**

- **Hard link** — a directory entry that points to the same inode/data block as the original. Indistinguishable from the original.
- **Symbolic link** — a small file storing the *path* of the target file.

**Deletion with Sharing:**
- If a file has $r_c = 0$ references, it is physically removed.
- If $r_c > 0$, only the calling directory's entry is removed; the file persists.
- The system maintains a **reference count** $r_c(f)$ for each file.

**Advantages:**
- Supports **file sharing** — the same physical file under multiple names.
- A shared subdirectory (e.g., `/common/lib`) can be linked from multiple project trees.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Dangling pointers** — if a file is deleted via one path but other paths still reference it, those links become *invalid* (especially with symbolic links).
- Reference count bookkeeping adds overhead.
- Search must handle potential loops (even though none should exist in principle).

## 2.5 General Graph Directory

A **general graph** relaxes the acyclic constraint — cycles are **permitted**. This is the most powerful but also the most complex scheme.

**Structure:**
$$G_{general} = (V, E) \quad \text{where cycles are allowed.}$$

**Why Permit Cycles?**

In distributed and modern systems, cycles emerge naturally:
- A symbolic link in `/home/alice/A` pointing to `/home/bob/B`, and another in `B` pointing to `A`.
- A user's home directory contains a subdirectory that links back to the user's own root.

**Operational Issues:**

1. **Reference counts fail** — if A and B both point to each other, $r_c > 0$ forever, even if both should be deleted.
2. **Search infinite loops** — naive traversal gets stuck in cycles.
3. **Dangling references** — same as acyclic graph, but harder to detect.

**Solution — Garbage Collection:**

When a file's reference count drops to zero, the system *does not* immediately delete the file. Instead, it leaves the file on disk and runs a **garbage collector** periodically:

$$GC(f) = \begin{cases} \text{reachable from some directory} & \Rightarrow \text{retain} \\ \text{unreachable from any directory} & \Rightarrow \text{delete} \end{cases}$$

**Algorithm:** A graph traversal marks all files reachable from any directory entry; unmarked files are deallocated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Reality:** Most production file systems (e.g., **UNIX FFS**, **ext4**, **NTFS**) implement a *tree-structured* directory with **symbolic links** — practically a *restricted acyclic graph*. Truly cyclic structures appear in research systems and some user-space overlay file systems.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single-level search time | $T = O(n)$ | Linear scan through $n$ files |
| Tree search (balanced) | $T = O(\log_b n)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $n$ = total files |
| Max files in a tree of height $h$ | $N_{max} = \dfrac{b^{h+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ | Geometric sum; full $b$-ary tree |
| Two-level path length | $\vert P \vert = \vert D_{MFD} \vert + \vert D_{UFD} \vert$ | Two segments: user + filename |
| Absolute path depth | $h_{abs} = \text{number of }/ \text{ in path}$ | Slashes (excluding root) |
| Reference count (hard link) | $r_c(f) = \sum_{d \in D} \mathbb{1}[d \text{ points to } f]$ | Sum of directory entries |
| File deletion safety (acyclic) | Safe iff $r_c(f) = 1$ (only caller holds it) | Otherwise, persists for others |
| Reachability (GC) | $f$ survives iff $\exists d \in D_{\text{root}} \text{ such that } f \in \text{reach}(d)$ | General graph condition |
| Path resolution cost | $C_{resolve} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} T_{lookup}(d_i)$ | Sum of lookups at each level |
| Avg lookup per directory | $T_{lookup} = O(1)$ with hash; $O(\log m)$ with tree | $m$ = entries in one directory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the constants and bounds:** A balanced tree with $b = 32$ and $h = 3$ can hold up to $\dfrac{32^4 - 1}{31} \approx 33{,}793$ files — a fact that justifies why production file systems use $B$-trees for directory indexing.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Single-Level**: Embedded systems and tiny OS ROMs (e.g., old automotive ECUs).
- **Two-Level**: Early mainframes (e.g., **CTSS**, early IBM mainframes).
- **Tree-Structured**: **UNIX**, **Linux**, **Windows NTFS** core structure.
- **Acyclic Graph**: Modern UNIX/Linux via symbolic and hard links.
- **General Graph**: Distributed file systems (e.g., **AFS**, **Coda**) and overlay file systems where cycles are an emergent property.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum File Capacity of a Tree Directory

We derive the maximum number of nodes (files + directories) in a tree of height $h$ with branching factor $b$.

**Step 1 — Definition of height.** Let height be measured in edges: the root is at height $0$, its children at height $1$, etc.

**Step 2 — Number of nodes at each level.** In a full $b$-ary tree, the number of nodes at level $\ell$ is:
$$N(\ell) = b^{\ell}, \quad \ell = 0, 1, 2, \dots, h$$

**Step 3 — Sum across all levels.** Total nodes $N_{max}$:
$$N_{max} = \sum_{\ell=0}^{h} b^{\ell}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the geometric series identity.** For $b \ne 1$:
$$\sum_{\ell=0}^{h} b^{\ell} = \frac{b^{h+1} - 1}{b - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result.**
$$\boxed{N_{max} = \frac{b^{h+1} - 1}{b - 1}}$$

**Numerical Example:** If $b = 10$ and $h = 4$:
$$N_{max} = \frac{10^{5} - 1}{9} = \frac{99999}{9} = 11{,}111 \text{ nodes}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Search Time in a Tree-Structured Directory

We model lookup time as a path traversal from root to a leaf.

**Step 1 — Decompose.** A path of length $h$ requires $h$ directory lookups.

**Step 2 — Per-directory cost.** If a directory uses a hash table for entries, each lookup is $O(1)$. If sorted with binary search, $O(\log m)$ where $m$ is the directory's entry count.

**Step 3 — Total time.**
$$T_{search} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} T_{lookup}(D_i)$$

**Step 4 — Worst case (linear scan per directory).** If each directory is scanned linearly:
$$T_{search} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} O(m_i) = O(h \cdot m_{avg})$$

**Step 5 — Best case (hashed directories, balanced).** With $h \approx \log_b n$ and $m_{avg} \approx b$:
$$\boxed{T_{search} = O(\log_b n)}$$

**Interpretation:** A modest tree of height $4$ and branching factor $32$ can index up to $\approx 1{,}048{,}576$ files in only **4 directory lookups**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reference Count Update on Link Operations

We derive how the reference count $r_c$ changes during file operations in an acyclic graph directory.

**Step 1 — Initial state.** A newly created file $f$ has:
$$r_c(f) = 1$$

**Step 2 — Add a hard link.** A new directory entry pointing to $f$ increments $r_c$:
$$r_c(f) \leftarrow r_c(f) + 1$$

**Step 3 — Remove a hard link.** Unlinking from directory $d$ decrements $r_c$:
$$r_c(f) \leftarrow r_c(f) - 1$$

**Step 4 — Physical deletion rule.** The file's data blocks are reclaimed iff:
$$r_c(f) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Failure of this rule in general graphs.** If files $f_1$ and $f_2$ form a cycle, both have $r_c \geq 1$ forever, even after every directory entry is removed. The condition $r_c = 0$ is **never reached**.

**Step 6 — Remedy via garbage collection.** The collector treats the file system as a graph $G = (V, E)$ and computes the set of *reachable* nodes via DFS/BFS from any directory entry:
$$R = \bigcup_{d \in D_{roots}} \text{reach}(d, G)$$

**Step 7 — Final deletion rule (general graph).**
$$\text{Delete } f \iff f \notin R$$

**Mathematical form:**
$$\boxed{\text{Reachability Set: } R = \left\{ v \in V \mid \exists \text{ a path from some } d \in D_{roots} \text{ to } v \right\}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Five Structures

| Property | Single-Level | Two-Level | Tree | Acyclic Graph | General Graph |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| User isolation | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Logical grouping | No | Limited | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| File sharing | No | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Cycle-tolerant | N/A | N/A | No (tree) | No | Yes |
| Search complexity | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ per UFD | $O(\log_b n)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ worst | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ with cycle check |
| Naming conflicts | Yes | No | No | No | No |
| Dangling pointers | N/A | N/A | N/A | Possible | Possible |
| Needs GC | No | No | No | No | Yes |
| Symlinks | No | No | Optional | Yes | Yes |
| Hard links | No | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Real-world example | **CTSS** | **Multics** | **UNIX V6** | **ext4 with links** | **AFS, Coda** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cycle detection cost:** In a general graph, naive DFS without a visited set runs forever on cycles. The standard remedy is to mark each visited node in a hash set, giving $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ amortized cost.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Path Resolution

**Given:** A file `notes.txt` resides at `/home/alice/projects/os/`. The CWD is `/home/alice`.

**Absolute path:**
$$P_{abs} = /home/alice/projects/os/notes.txt$$
**Cost:** 4 lookups (one per directory level: $home \rightarrow alice \rightarrow projects \rightarrow os$).

**Relative path:**
$$P_{rel} = projects/os/notes.txt$$
**Cost:** 2 lookups (the system prepends $CWD$).

**General formula:**
$$T_{resolve} = T_{prepend} + \sum_{i=1}^{h} T_{lookup}(D_i)$$

**With hashing:**
$$T_{resolve} \approx 0 + h \cdot O(1) = O(h)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Level Directory

```mermaid
graph TD
    RootDir["ROOT DIRECTORY (Single Level)"]
    F1["file: report.txt"]
    F2["file: notes.doc"]
    F3["file: data.csv"]
    F4["file: code.c"]
    F5["file: image.png"]

    RootDir --> F1
    RootDir --> F2
    RootDir --> F3
    RootDir --> F4
    RootDir --> F5
```

> **Block-Level Reading:** All files hang directly from a single root. There is no grouping, no hierarchy, and any new file name must be unique across the entire system.

## 4.2 Two-Level Directory

```mermaid
graph TD
    MFD["MASTER FILE DIRECTORY (MFD)"]
    UFD1["UFD: alice"]
    UFD2["UFD: bob"]
    UFD3["UFD: charlie"]

    A1["report.txt"]
    A2["notes.doc"]
    B1["report.txt"]
    B2["budget.xls"]
    C1["data.csv"]

    MFD --> UFD1
    MFD --> UFD2
    MFD --> UFD3

    UFD1 --> A1
    UFD1 --> A2
    UFD2 --> B1
    UFD2 --> B2
    UFD3 --> C1
```

> **Block-Level Reading:** Notice that both `alice` and `bob` have a file named `report.txt` — a name collision that the single-level system could not resolve. The MFD routes lookups to the correct UFD based on the logged-in user.

## 4.3 Tree-Structured Directory

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root["/ (root)"]
    Home["home"]
    Bin["bin"]
    Etc["etc"]
    Tmp["tmp"]
    Alice["alice"]
    Bob["bob"]
    Docs["Documents"]
    Projects["Projects"]
    OS["OS"]
    DS["DSA"]
    A1["resume.pdf"]
    A2["photo.jpg"]
    B1["letter.doc"]
    P1["notes.txt"]
    P2["lab.c"]

    Root --> Home
    Root --> Bin
    Root --> Etc
    Root --> Tmp
    Home --> Alice
    Home --> Bob
    Alice --> Docs
    Alice --> Projects
    Projects --> OS
    Projects --> DS
    Docs --> A1
    Docs --> A2
    Bob --> B1
    OS --> P1
    DS --> P2
```

> **Block-Level Reading:** A clean hierarchy. The path `/home/alice/Projects/OS/notes.txt` uniquely locates the file by walking from the root. Each directory has exactly **one** parent — there are no shared entries.

## 4.4 Acyclic Graph Directory (with Shared Subdirectory)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root["/ (root)"]
    AliceProj["alice/Projects"]
    BobProj["bob/Projects"]
    SharedLib["common/lib (SHARED)"]
    AliceApp["alice/App (linked)"]
    LibA["util.c"]
    LibB["string.c"]
    AppA["main.c"]

    Root --> AliceProj
    Root --> BobProj
    AliceProj --> SharedLib
    BobProj --> SharedLib
    AliceProj --> AliceApp
    SharedLib --> LibA
    SharedLib --> LibB
    AliceApp --> AppA
```

> **Block-Level Reading:** `common/lib` is **one physical subdirectory**, but it is linked from both `alice/Projects` and `bob/Projects`. Deletion via one path does not destroy the subdirectory — it persists via the other path. There are **no cycles**: the graph is a **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)**.

## 4.5 General Graph Directory (with Cycle)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root["/ (root)"]
    DirA["dirA"]
    DirB["dirB"]
    FileA["fileA (inode 100)"]
    FileB["fileB (inode 200)"]
    LinkAtoB["symlink: dirA/..toB -> dirB"]
    LinkBtoA["symlink: dirB/..toA -> dirA"]

    Root --> DirA
    Root --> DirB
    DirA --> FileA
    DirB --> FileB
    DirA -.-> LinkAtoB
    DirB -.-> LinkBtoA
    LinkAtoB -.-> DirB
    LinkBtoA -.-> DirA
```

> **Block-Level Reading:** The dotted arrows represent **symbolic links** that form a cycle: `dirA → dirB → dirA`. A naive traversal would loop forever. The operating system marks visited nodes to break the cycle. **Reference counts cannot detect orphan files** here — only **garbage collection** can.

## 4.6 Directory Operations Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Op["Directory Operation Request"]
    Check{"Operation Type?"}
    Create["CREATE: Add entry, parent must exist, name unique"]
    Delete["DELETE: Decrement ref count; if 0, free inode and blocks"]
    Search["SEARCH: Walk path components; resolve symlinks; mark visited nodes"]
    List["LIST: Read directory entries; sort if requested"]
    Rename["RENAME: Update parent pointer; check for cycles if linking dirs"]
    Garbage["GARBAGE COLLECT: DFS from roots; free unreachable inodes"]

    Op --> Check
    Check -->|create| Create
    Check -->|delete| Delete
    Check -->|search| Search
    Check -->|list| List
    Check -->|rename| Rename
    Check -->|gc| Garbage
```

> **Block-Level Reading:** All directory operations funnel through a dispatcher. The **SEARCH** and **GC** paths handle cycle detection by tracking visited nodes — this is the key safeguard in general graph directories.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Define a directory in a file system. Explain the single-level directory structure with its major limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **directory** is a special file that maintains a list of entries, each describing a file or subdirectory — typically its *name*, *attributes* (size, type, timestamps), and a *pointer* to its on-disk location (inode or FCB).

In a **single-level directory**, all files in the entire system reside in **one common directory**. Every user shares this single catalog, and every file must have a unique name.

**Limitations (any two expected for full marks):**

1. **Naming conflicts:** Two different users cannot have files with the same name (e.g., both `Alice` and `Bob` cannot store `report.txt`).
2. **No grouping:** Files cannot be organized by user, project, or type — everything is in one flat list.
3. **Poor scalability:** As the number of files grows, the linear search time $O(n)$ becomes prohibitive.
4. **No privacy / isolation:** Every user can see (and accidentally overwrite) every other user's files.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**Differentiate between an acyclic graph directory and a general graph directory. Why does the general graph require garbage collection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Acyclic Graph | General Graph |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Cycles | **Not allowed** | **Allowed** |
| Reference counting | Works correctly | Fails (cycles keep $r_c > 0$ forever) |
| Dangling pointers | Possible | Possible |
| Sharing support | Yes | Yes |
| Reclamation strategy | Reference count $r_c = 0$ | Garbage collection (mark-and-sweep) |

**Why GC is required:** In a general graph, two directories can sym-link to each other, forming a cycle. Even after all *legitimate* directory entries are removed, the cycle sustains a non-zero reference count. The system cannot know when a file is truly orphaned. A **garbage collector** traverses the file system graph from all root directories, marks reachable files, and deletes anything not marked — the only correct way to reclaim storage in the presence of cycles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 4 Choice Question):** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 4 Choice Q1]`

**(a) Explain the two-level directory structure in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A two-level directory maintains a **Master File Directory (MFD)** at the top, which contains one entry per *user*. Each user entry points to that user's **User File Directory (UFD)**, which in turn contains the user's own files.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
                MFD
        +-------------------+
        | alice | bob | root |
        +---+---+----+---+---+
            |        |       |
            v        v       v
        UFD_alice  UFD_bob  UFD_root
        +-------+  +-----+  +------+
        | a.txt |  | a.txt|  | pass |
        | b.doc |  | c.c  |  +------+
        +-------+  +-----+
```

**Operational Steps (2 marks):**
1. User logs in → system locates their UFD via the MFD using the username as a key.
2. All file operations (`open`, `create`, `delete`) are confined to the UFD.
3. Path format: $\text{username:filename}$ (e.g., `alice:a.txt`).

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- **Eliminates naming conflicts** — same name can appear in different UFDs.
- **User isolation** — one user cannot directly access another's files.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- **No file sharing** between users.
- **No sub-grouping** within a user's files (no project/category folders).
- **Inefficient** if a user has thousands of files in one flat UFD.

---

**(b) Describe the tree-structured directory system. Explain absolute and relative path names. How is file deletion handled when a directory is non-empty? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Tree-Structured Directory (2 marks):**

A tree-structured directory organizes files in a **strict hierarchy**: a single root, with subdirectories branching out recursively to arbitrary depth. Each directory (except root) has **exactly one parent**, and any directory can contain both files and subdirectories.

**Diagram (1 mark):**
```
                /
        +-------+--------+
       home    bin      etc
        |
   +----+----+
  alice      bob
   |
Documents  Projects
             |
           +--+--+
           OS  DSA
```

**Absolute vs Relative Path (2 marks):**

- **Absolute path:** Always starts at the root (`/`). Example: `/home/alice/Projects/OS/notes.txt`. Uniquely identifies a file regardless of $CWD$.
- **Relative path:** Starts at the **current working directory** ($CWD$). Example: if $CWD = /home/alice$, then `Projects/OS/notes.txt` resolves to the same file.

System call `chdir(newpath)` changes $CWD$. The OS resolves a relative path by prepending $CWD$.

**File Deletion of Non-Empty Directory (2 marks):**

Two strategies are used:

1. **Non-recursive / reject:** `rmdir` fails on a non-empty directory; the user must first delete all contents. This is the POSIX default — safer and prevents accidental data loss.
2. **Recursive deletion:** `rm -r` deletes the directory and *all* its descendants (files and subdirectories), bottom-up. Used in UNIX/Linux.

In all cases, the directory's own inode is freed only after all its contents are gone.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 4 Choice Q1]`

**(a) What is an acyclic graph directory? How does it support file and directory sharing? Explain with a suitable diagram. Discuss the deletion of shared files using the reference count mechanism. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** An **acyclic graph directory** is a generalization of a tree directory that allows a file or subdirectory to appear under **multiple paths** through the use of **links**, but **without forming any cycles**.

**Sharing Mechanism (2 marks):**

- **Hard link:** A second directory entry that points to the **same inode** as the original. Indistinguishable from the original file.
- **Symbolic link (symlink):** A small file that stores the *path name* of the target file. Resolved at access time.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
```
            Root
           /    \
         A      B
         |      |
        lib <--+   (shared subdirectory, single inode)
         |
      utils.c
```

Both `A/lib` and `B/lib` point to the *same* physical subdirectory.

**Deletion with Reference Count (2 marks):**

Each file $f$ has a **reference count** $r_c(f)$:
- Creating $f$: $r_c(f) = 1$
- Adding a hard link: $r_c(f) \mathrel{+}= 1$
- Removing a hard link: $r_c(f) \mathrel{-}= 1$
- **Physical deletion:** iff $r_c(f) = 0$

If `A/lib/utils.c` is removed, $r_c$ decrements; the file persists if other links still exist. Dangling symlinks (pointing to a deleted file) become invalid, and operations on them return an error like `ENOENT`.

---

**(b) Explain the general graph directory. What are the problems it introduces? Describe how garbage collection is used to overcome these problems. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A **general graph directory** allows cycles in the directory structure. A directory can contain a symbolic link to an ancestor, or two directories can sym-link to each other.

**Problems Introduced (3 marks):**

1. **Reference count fails:** If `A` and `B` sym-link to each other, both have $r_c \geq 1$ permanently. Even after every *real* directory entry is removed, the cycle sustains the count — storage leaks.
2. **Search can loop:** Naive DFS traversal without a `visited` set enters an infinite loop.
3. **Dangling references:** Symbolic links can point to deleted files, producing errors.
4. **Confusing semantics:** A user may be uncertain whether deleting an entry truly removes the file.

**Garbage Collection Solution (3 marks):**

The system **delays physical deletion** until a periodic **garbage collector (GC)** runs.

**Algorithm — Mark-and-Sweep:**

1. **Mark phase:** Starting from every directory in $D_{roots}$, perform DFS/BFS. Mark every file and directory **reachable** from at least one root.
2. **Sweep phase:** Scan all inodes. Any inode that is **not marked** is unreachable and is deleted (its blocks returned to the free list).

**Reachability Set Definition:**
$$R = \left\{ v \in V \mid \exists \text{ a path from some } d \in D_{roots} \text{ to } v \right\}$$

Files in $V \setminus R$ are deallocated. This works correctly even with cycles, because a cycle not connected to any root is unreachable and therefore deleted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing hard links and symbolic links.** Hard links share an inode; symbolic links store a path. They behave differently during deletion and copy operations.
> 2. **Forgetting that reference counts fail in cycles.** Many students write "decrement reference count, if zero, delete" for general graphs — this is **wrong**. Only GC works in general graphs.
> 3. **Saying tree-structured directories support sharing.** They do not — sharing is precisely the feature added by acyclic graph directories.
> 4. **Not distinguishing between path name and inode.** A directory entry is a *name* → *inode* mapping. A file can have many names but one inode.
> 5. **Forgetting that `rmdir` rejects non-empty directories** in POSIX unless `-r` is used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Directory Structures**

### Core Definitions
- **Directory** = a catalog file containing entries (name + attributes + pointer).
- **Single-level** = one flat directory for the whole system.
- **Two-level** = MFD (per user) + UFD (per user).
- **Tree-structured** = strict hierarchy, no sharing, no cycles.
- **Acyclic graph** = tree + sharing via hard/sym links, **no cycles**.
- **General graph** = sharing with **cycles allowed**, requires **garbage collection**.

### Key Constraints
- Tree: each directory has **one parent**.
- Acyclic graph: **DAG property** preserved.
- General graph: cycles permitted.

### Key Operations
- **CREATE** — add entry to parent; name must be unique in that directory.
- **DELETE** — decrement $r_c$ for hard links; $r_c = 0 \Rightarrow$ free blocks (acyclic only).
- **SEARCH** — walk path components; in general graphs, mark visited to avoid loops.
- **RENAME** — change name in parent; for directories, ensure no cycles introduced.
- **GARBAGE COLLECT** — DFS from roots, free unreachable inodes (general graph only).

### Sharing — Hard vs Symbolic Links
| Property | Hard Link | Symbolic Link |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Points to | Inode | Path name |
| Works across file systems | No | Yes |
| Survives target deletion | Yes (becomes original) | No (becomes dangling) |
| Reference count | Yes | No |

### Performance Anchors
- Single-level: $O(n)$ search.
- Tree: $O(\log_b n)$ search, height $h$.
- Max nodes: $N_{max} = \dfrac{b^{h+1} - 1}{b - 1}$.
- GC traversal: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.

### Examiner Favourites
- Compare any two structures (table form, with one example each).
- Path resolution with absolute and relative examples.
- Reference count arithmetic with hard links.
- Why reference count fails in general graphs.
- Mark-and-sweep garbage collection algorithm.

### Mnemonic
> **"S-T-T-A-G"** — **S**ingle → **T**wo → **T**ree → **A**cyclic → **G**eneral.
> Each step **adds one feature**: users → hierarchy → (still no sharing) → sharing → cycles.

### Modern Reality
- **Linux ext4 / NTFS / APFS** = tree + symlinks (effectively a restricted acyclic graph).
- **General graph semantics** appear in **overlay/distributed file systems** (AFS, Coda, IPFS) and in userspace tools like `find -L`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Linux File Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Linux File Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Linux File System** is a structured, hierarchical method of organizing, storing, retrieving, and managing data on physical storage devices under the Linux kernel. It provides a logical interface between the user applications and the physical disk hardware through the **Virtual File System (VFS)** abstraction layer. The Linux kernel supports multiple file system formats (commonly called `fs-type` or `fstype`) such as `ext2`, `ext3`, `ext4`, `XFS`, `Btrfs`, `ReiserFS`, and `VFAT`, each implementing a unique on-disk layout while presenting a uniform API to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 - Case Study):**
> The Linux file system is studied as a *case study* to consolidate understanding of file system concepts (Module 2 & 3) by examining a real-world production implementation. Focus areas include: disk layout, **inode** structure, **superblock**, **block groups**, journaling, and the **VFS** abstraction.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a massive **central library**:

- The **Library Building** = The physical hard disk (raw storage).
- The **Librarian (front desk)** = The **Virtual File System (VFS)**. A visitor (application) simply asks, *"I want a book titled 'OS Notes'"* — the librarian locates it regardless of which building wing it is stored in.
- The **Catalog Cards (dewey decimal index)** = **Inodes**. They do not store the actual book; they store the *metadata* (title, author, shelf number, page count) and point to where the book physically is.
- The **Bookshelves and Stacks** = **Data Blocks** on the disk where the actual file content lives.
- The **Master Registry at the entrance** = The **Superblock**, which contains the entire library's structural map.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Case Study"?**
> Linux is an open-source, real-world implementation that students can directly inspect via utilities like `dumpe2fs`, `tune2fs`, `stat`, and `debugfs`. It bridges textbook theory with practical engineering.

### Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Expected)

- **Default Block Size:** **4096 bytes (4 KiB)** for `ext2`/`ext3`/`ext4`.
- **Max File Size (ext4 with 4 KiB blocks):** **16 TiB**.
- **Max Filesystem Size (ext4):** **1 EiB** (theoretical).
- **Inode Ratio:** Default **1 inode per 4096 bytes** (configurable via `mkfs -i`).
- **Inode Number (in Linux):** An unsigned **32-bit** integer historically, extended to **64-bit** in modern kernels for very large volumes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Linux File System Stack (Layered Architecture)

Linux organizes file access into a **four-layer stack**:

1. **User Application Layer** — `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `close()` (POSIX system calls).
2. **Virtual File System (VFS) Layer** — Common abstraction; defines `struct file_operations`, `struct inode`, `struct super_block`, `struct dentry`.
3. **Concrete File System Layer** — `ext4`, `XFS`, `Btrfs` (each registers with VFS via `register_filesystem()`).
4. **Device Driver / Buffer Cache Layer** — Talks to the block device via the I/O scheduler.

### 2.2 Core On-Disk Structures

#### A. The Superblock (`struct ext4_super_block`)

The superblock is the **"DNA" of the filesystem**. It is replicated in multiple block groups for redundancy.

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `s_magic` | Magic number (`0xEF53` for ext2/3/4) |
| `s_inodes_count` | Total inodes in filesystem |
| `s_blocks_count` | Total blocks in filesystem |
| `s_free_blocks_count` | Number of free blocks |
| `s_free_inodes_count` | Number of free inodes |
| `s_log_block_size` | Block size = $1024 \ll \text{s\_log\_block\_size}$ |
| `s_blocks_per_group` | Blocks per block group |
| `s_inodes_per_group` | Inodes per block group |
| `s_mtime`, `s_wtime` | Mount / write timestamps |
| `s_state` | Valid / Error / Recovering |

#### B. The Inode (`struct ext4_inode`)

The inode is a **metadata container** — it does **not** store filenames or data.

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `i_mode` | File type and permission bits |
| `i_uid`, `i_gid` | Owner user and group IDs |
| `i_size` | File size in bytes |
| `i_atime`, `i_mtime`, `i_ctime` | Access / modify / change timestamps |
| `i_links_count` | Hard link counter |
| `i_blocks` | Disk blocks allocated (in 512-byte sectors) |
| `i_block[15]` | Data block pointers (12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect) |
| `i_file_acl` | Extended attributes / ACL block |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inode Pointer Layout (12 + 3)**: 12 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect pointer. This is the **most frequently asked KTU question** on Linux FS.

#### C. Block Group Descriptor Table

A partition is divided into **Block Groups** (default size: $8 \times \text{block\_size}$). Each group contains:

1. **Superblock (backup copies)**
2. **Group Descriptors** — bitmap locations, inode table location
3. **Block Bitmap** — tracks free/used data blocks
4. **Inode Bitmap** — tracks free/used inodes
5. **Inode Table** — stores inodes for that group
6. **Data Blocks** — actual file content

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard block size = 4 KiB (4096 bytes)** unless otherwise stated in the question. KTU numericals usually specify block size explicitly.

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Value |
|---|---|
| Block Size | $B = 1024 \times 2^{\text{s\_log\_block\_size}}$ |
| Max Addressable Block (Direct) | $12$ blocks (blocks $0$ to $11$) |
| Max Addressable via Single-Indirect | $12 + \left\lfloor B / 4 \right\rfloor$ blocks |
| Max Addressable via Double-Indirect | $12 + \left\lfloor B / 4 \right\rfloor + \left(\left\lfloor B / 4 \right\rfloor\right)^{2}$ blocks |
| Max File Size (with 4 KiB blocks) | $12B + \left\lfloor B/4 \right\rfloor B + \left(\lfloor B/4 \rfloor\right)^{2} B + \left(\lfloor B/4 \rfloor\right)^{3} B$ |
| Max File Size (numerical, $B=4096$) | $16\ \text{TiB} = 2^{44}\ \text{bytes}$ |
| Inode Number Calculation | $\text{Group} = \left\lfloor (N-1) / \text{inodes\_per\_group} \right\rfloor$ |
| Block Group Number | $\text{bg} = \left\lfloor \text{Block} / \text{blocks\_per\_group} \right\rfloor$ |
| Free Block Search (Bitmap) | Bit position $b \Rightarrow$ block = $\text{bg} \times \text{bpg} + b$ |
| Inode Ratio | $\text{bytes\_per\_inode}$ (default 4096) |
| Journal Size (`ext4`) | Typically $128\ \text{MiB}$ (default for large volumes) |

### 2.4 The Virtual File System (VFS) — Linux's Master Trick

VFS defines four key kernel objects (the "VFS Big Four"):

1. **`struct super_block`** — represents a mounted filesystem.
2. **`struct inode`** — represents a file (metadata).
3. **`struct dentry`** — represents a directory entry (name → inode mapping).
4. **`struct file`** — represents an open file descriptor (per-process).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The VFS abstraction is what makes Linux *the* universal embedded OS. The same `read()` call works on a regular file, a `/proc` pseudo-file, a network socket (NFS), or a `cgroup` controller — *all* register as VFS objects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum File Size in `ext2` (Classic KTU Problem)

> **Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark):**
> *Given a Unix/Linux file system with block size $B = 1024$ bytes and an address (pointer) size of $4$ bytes, where each inode has 10 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, and 1 triple-indirect pointer, calculate:*
> *(a) The maximum file size in bytes.*
> *(b) The maximum file size if the block size is changed to $4096$ bytes.*

#### Step 1: Calculate pointers per block

Each block holds pointers of size $4$ bytes.

$$
\text{Pointers per block} = \frac{B}{4} = \frac{1024}{4} = 256
$$

#### Step 2: Compute the maximum number of data blocks addressable

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{direct}} &= 10 \\
N_{\text{single-indirect}} &= 1 \times 256 = 256 \\
N_{\text{double-indirect}} &= 1 \times 256 \times 256 = 65536 \\
N_{\text{triple-indirect}} &= 1 \times 256 \times 256 \times 256 = 16777216
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Sum total addressable blocks

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= 10 + 256 + 65536 + 16777216 \\
&= 16843018\ \text{data blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Compute the maximum file size (in bytes)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= N_{\text{total}} \times B \\
&= 16843018 \times 1024\ \text{bytes} \\
&= 17247250432\ \text{bytes} \\
&\approx 16.06\ \text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Recompute for $B = 4096$ bytes

Pointers per block:

$$
\frac{4096}{4} = 1024
$$

Number of data blocks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= 10 + 1024 + 1024^{2} + 1024^{3} \\
&= 10 + 1024 + 1048576 + 1073741824 \\
&= 1074791434\ \text{blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum file size:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= 1074791434 \times 4096 \\
&= 4402345728\,640\ \text{bytes} \\
&\approx 4.0\ \text{TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Increasing the block size from 1 KiB to 4 KiB **multiplies the maximum file size by 256×**, because the pointer indirection grows quadratically with `pointers-per-block`.

### 3.2 Derivation: Inode-to-Block-Group Mapping

> **Problem Statement:**
> *An `ext4` filesystem has $8192$ inodes per group, and the inode number $N = 47005$. Find the block group number and the index within the group's inode table.*

**Step 1: Subtract 1 (inodes are 1-indexed in user-space API).**

$$
N' = 47005 - 1 = 47004
$$

**Step 2: Compute block group number.**

$$
\text{Group} = \left\lfloor \frac{N'}{\text{inodes\_per\_group}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{47004}{8192} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 5.738\ldots \right\rfloor = 5
$$

**Step 3: Compute the index within the inode table.**

$$
\text{Index} = N' \bmod 8192 = 47004 - (5 \times 8192) = 47004 - 40960 = 6044
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Many questions on inode addressing or block address resolution appear in 7-mark Part B sub-questions. Always show the index-arithmetic step.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ext2 vs ext3 vs ext4 vs XFS vs Btrfs

| Feature | ext2 | ext3 | ext4 | XFS | Btrfs |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Journaling | No | Yes (metadata) | Yes (metadata + data) | Yes (metadata) | Yes (CoW) |
| Max File Size | $2\ \text{TiB}$ | $2\ \text{TiB}$ | $16\ \text{TiB}$ | $8\ \text{EB}$ | $16\ \text{EiB}$ |
| Max FS Size | $32\ \text{TiB}$ | $32\ \text{TiB}$ | $1\ \text{EiB}$ | $8\ \text{EB}$ | $16\ \text{EiB}$ |
| Block Size | $1\text{–}4\ \text{KiB}$ | $1\text{–}4\ \text{KiB}$ | $1\text{–}64\ \text{KiB}$ | $512\text{B–}64\text{KiB}$ | Variable |
| Extents | No | No | **Yes** | Yes | Yes (logical) |
| Delayed Alloc | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Snapshots | No | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Checksums | No | No | Metadata | Metadata | Data + Metadata |

> [!NOTE]
> **Extents (ext4 default):** A single `ext4_extent` record describes a *contiguous run* of blocks as `<logical_block, length, physical_start>`, replacing the old `i_block[15]` indirect-pointer array. This dramatically reduces metadata overhead for large files.

### 3.4 Journaling — Crash Consistency in `ext3/ext4`

Three journaling modes (tunable via `data=` mount option):

1. **`journal` (slowest, safest):** Both metadata **and** data are written to the journal before being committed to the main FS.
2. **`ordered` (default for ext3, common for ext4):** Data is written **first** to its final location, then metadata is journaled. Protects metadata, orders data writes.
3. **`writeback` (fastest, weakest):** Only metadata is journaled; data may be written out of order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Journaling guarantees **atomicity** of metadata updates. On crash, the recovery routine replays the journal to bring the FS to a consistent state.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Linux VFS — File System Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["User Applications open read write close"] --> VFS["VFS LAYER struct file_operations struct inode struct super_block struct dentry struct file"]
    VFS --> CACHE["Page Cache and Buffer Cache"]
    VFS --> EXT4["Concrete FS ext4"]
    VFS --> XFS["Concrete FS XFS"]
    VFS --> PROC["Pseudo FS proc sysfs devpts"]
    VFS --> NFS["Network FS NFS CIFS"]
    EXT4 --> DRV["Block Device Driver"]
    XFS --> DRV
    NFS --> NET["Network Stack TCP IP"]
    DRV --> DISK["Physical Disk HDD SSD NVMe"]
```

### 4.2 On-Disk Layout of an `ext2/ext3/ext4` Partition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SB["Block 0 Superblock Primary Magic 0xEF53 FS parameters"] --> GDT["Block 1 Group Descriptor Table 1 entry per block group"]
    GDT --> BG2["Block Group 1 BG2 Backup Superblock BG1 Descriptors BG1 Block Bitmap BG1 Inode Bitmap BG1 Inode Table BG1 Data Blocks"]
    BG2 --> BGN["Block Group N BGN Descriptors Block Bitmap Inode Bitmap Inode Table Data Blocks"]
```

### 4.3 Inode Structure (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INODE["struct ext4_inode 256 bytes on disk"] --> META["Metadata Region i_mode i_uid i_gid i_size i_atime i_mtime i_ctime i_links_count i_blocks"]
    INODE --> P["Pointer Array i_block 15 entries of 4 bytes each"]
    P --> D0["Direct 0 through Direct 11 point to first 12 data blocks"]
    P --> SI["Single Indirect 1 block of 1024 pointers of 4 bytes each for 4 KiB blocks"]
    P --> DI["Double Indirect 1 block of 1024 single-indirect block pointers"]
    P --> TI["Triple Indirect 1 block of 1024 double-indirect block pointers"]
```

### 4.4 Block Group Internal Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BG["Block Group Ki"] --> BSB["Backup Superblock redundant copy"]
    BG --> BGD["Group Descriptors Table redundancy"]
    BG --> BBM["Block Bitmap 1 bit per data block"]
    BG --> IBM["Inode Bitmap 1 bit per inode slot"]
    BG --> IT["Inode Table array of inodes"]
    BG --> DB["Data Blocks file content"]
```

### 4.5 VFS Object Relationship (Open-File Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROC["Process fd 3"] --> SF["struct file per open f_pos f_mode f_op"]
    SF --> DENT["struct dentry name lookup cache"]
    DENT --> INO["struct inode cached metadata"]
    INO --> SB["struct super_block mounted FS info"]
    SF --> FOP["struct file_operations methods"]
    FOP --> CALL["Concrete FS read implementation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The same VFS abstraction talks to disk-based (`ext4`), network-based (`NFS`), and pseudo (`/proc`) file systems uniformly. This is Linux's hallmark design.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **What is an inode in the Linux file system? List any four fields stored in an inode.**

**Model Answer:**

An **inode (Index Node)** is a data structure on disk that stores *metadata* about a file. It does **not** contain the filename or the actual file data, only the descriptive information and pointers to the data blocks.

**Four fields of an inode:**

1. `i_mode` — file type and permission bits.
2. `i_size` — file size in bytes.
3. `i_uid` / `i_gid` — owner user ID and group ID.
4. `i_block[15]` — array of 15 block pointers (12 direct + 3 indirect).

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **What is the role of the Virtual File System (VFS) in Linux?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Virtual File System (VFS)** is an abstraction layer in the Linux kernel that provides a *uniform interface* for accessing different types of file systems (disk-based like `ext4`, `XFS`; network like `NFS`; pseudo like `/proc`).

**Key roles:**

1. Defines common data structures (`super_block`, `inode`, `dentry`, `file`).
2. Hides implementation differences of underlying file systems.
3. Allows the same system calls (`open`, `read`, `write`) to work across all FS types.
4. Enables simultaneous mounting of multiple file system types.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **on-disk layout of the Linux `ext2` file system** with a neat block-group diagram. Describe the function of: (i) Superblock, (ii) Group Descriptor, (iii) Block Bitmap, (iv) Inode Bitmap, (v) Inode Table, (vi) Data Blocks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The `ext2` partition is divided into a sequence of **Block Groups**, each typically consisting of $8192$ blocks. The first group (Group 0) holds the **primary superblock** and **group descriptor table**; subsequent groups may contain **backup copies** for recovery.

**Diagram (textual):**

```
[ Boot Block (1024 B) ][ Block Group 0 ][ Block Group 1 ] ... [ Block Group N-1 ]

Block Group i:
  +--------------------------+
  |  (Backup) Superblock     | <- FS-wide metadata
  +--------------------------+
  |  Group Descriptors       | <- Bitmap & table locations
  +--------------------------+
  |  Block Bitmap            | <- 1 bit per data block
  +--------------------------+
  |  Inode Bitmap            | <- 1 bit per inode slot
  +--------------------------+
  |  Inode Table             | <- Array of inodes for this group
  +--------------------------+
  |  Data Blocks             | <- Actual file content
  +--------------------------+
```

**Function of each component:**

(i) **Superblock:** Contains filesystem-wide metadata — magic number (`0xEF53`), total inodes/blocks, free counts, block size, mount count, state. Replicated in every group for fault tolerance.

(ii) **Group Descriptor:** A 32-byte entry per block group; stores the block number of the block bitmap, inode bitmap, and inode table for that group, plus free counts.

(iii) **Block Bitmap:** A bit vector where bit $b$ corresponds to data block $b$ within the group; `1` = allocated, `0` = free. Used by the block allocator.

(iv) **Inode Bitmap:** Same principle as block bitmap but tracks inode allocation.

(v) **Inode Table:** Pre-allocated array of inodes (default $8192$ per group). Each inode is $128$ bytes (`ext2`).

(vi) **Data Blocks:** Region where actual file content is stored; addressed by the inode's pointer array.

> **Marking distribution:** 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark per component × 6 = need 5 from the 6 since 2 marks are reserved for the diagram. (Adjust per official KTU key.)

---

**(b)** With a block size of **4 KiB** and a pointer size of **8 bytes** (modern 64-bit `ext4`), compute the **maximum file size** supported by the inode with 12 direct, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, and 1 triple-indirect pointer scheme. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Pointers per block.

$$
P = \frac{B}{\text{ptr\_size}} = \frac{4096}{8} = 512
$$

**Step 2:** Addressable blocks per pointer level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{direct}} &= 12 \\
N_{\text{single}} &= 1 \times 512 = 512 \\
N_{\text{double}} &= 1 \times 512 \times 512 = 262144 \\
N_{\text{triple}} &= 1 \times 512 \times 512 \times 512 = 134217728
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total blocks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= 12 + 512 + 262144 + 134217728 \\
&= 134480396\ \text{blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Maximum file size.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= 134480396 \times 4096\ \text{bytes} \\
&= 5.51 \times 10^{11}\ \text{bytes} \\
&\approx 513\ \text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: `ext4` uses **extents** in practice (not the indirect scheme), so the practical limit is **$16$ TiB**. The above is the *theoretical* classical calculation.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **Virtual File System (VFS) layer** in Linux. List the **four main VFS objects** and describe their roles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **VFS** is a kernel-level abstraction layer that defines a *common interface* for all file systems supported by Linux. When a user issues `open("/etc/passwd", O_RDONLY)`, the VFS:

1. Resolves the path via directory entries (`dentry`).
2. Locates the corresponding `inode` (with help from the mounted `super_block`).
3. Creates a `struct file` for the process.
4. Dispatches the operation to the *concrete* file system's `file_operations`.

**The Four VFS Objects:**

| Object | Role |
|---|---|
| `struct super_block` | Represents a **mounted filesystem instance**. Holds FS-wide info, list of inodes, mount options, superblock operations. |
| `struct inode` | Represents a **single file or directory** on disk. Contains metadata (size, permissions, timestamps) and pointers to data. Cached in memory after first access. |
| `struct dentry` | Represents a **directory entry** mapping a name (e.g., `"passwd"`) to an inode. Forms the directory tree; used for path resolution. |
| `struct file` | Represents an **open file** from a specific process's perspective. Holds the file offset, mode flags, and a pointer to the relevant `file_operations`. |

> **Engineering Insight:** This design decouples the POSIX API from the on-disk format. Adding a new file system requires only implementing the `file_operations` and `inode_operations` and registering with VFS.

---

**(b)** Compare the **features of `ext2`, `ext3`, and `ext4`** file systems. Discuss how `ext4` improves upon `ext3`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (must be reproduced in exam):**

| Feature | ext2 | ext3 | ext4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Journaling | No | Yes (metadata) | Yes (metadata + data via ordered/writeback) |
| Max File Size | $2\ \text{TiB}$ | $2\ \text{TiB}$ | $16\ \text{TiB}$ |
| Max FS Size | $32\ \text{TiB}$ | $32\ \text{TiB}$ | $1\ \text{EiB}$ |
| Block Size | $1\text{–}4\ \text{KiB}$ | $1\text{–}4\ \text{KiB}$ | $1\text{–}64\ \text{KiB}$ |
| Addressing | Indirect blocks | Indirect blocks | **Extents** (default) + fall-back to indirect |
| Delayed Allocation | No | No | **Yes** |
| Multiblock Allocator | No | No | **Yes** |
| Checksums | No | No | **Metadata checksums** |
| Inode Version | 32-bit | 32-bit | **Version with nano-second timestamps** |

**Key Improvements of `ext4` over `ext3`:**

1. **Extents:** Replace the 15-pointer array with a single extent descriptor (`<start, length, physical>`). Reduces metadata overhead and supports very large files efficiently.
2. **Larger File and FS Limits:** $16\ \text{TiB}$ per file, $1\ \text{EiB}$ per filesystem.
3. **Delayed Allocation:** Blocks are allocated only when data is flushed to disk, improving contiguous-allocation success rate.
4. **Multiblock Allocator:** Allocates multiple blocks in a single call — reduces fragmentation and speeds up large-file writes.
5. **Journal Checksums:** Detects corrupted journal entries.
6. **Persistent Pre-allocation:** `fallocate()` allows reserving space for a file in advance.
7. **Faster File System Check (`fsck`):** Uses uninit block groups to skip empty regions.

> **Marking distribution:** 3 marks for table, 4 marks for narrative (1 mark per major improvement).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Filename vs Inode confusion:** Many students wrongly state that "inode contains the filename." It does **NOT** — filenames live in directory entries (`dentry`), not inodes.
> 2. **Direct pointers count:** `ext2`/`ext3` classic inode has **12 direct** pointers (some students write 10 or 13). Modern `ext4` may use extents instead of the 15-pointer array.
> 3. **Block size for indirect calculation:** Forgetting to divide the block size by pointer size (`4` or `8` bytes) — a 1-mark loss per error.
> 4. **VFS vs Concrete FS:** Stating "VFS is a file system" is wrong. VFS is an *abstraction layer*, not a file system itself.
> 5. **Superblock magic number:** Forgetting `0xEF53` or confusing it with FAT's `0xAA55`.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (print this before the exam):**

- **Inode** = metadata container; **does NOT store filename or data**.
- **Inode pointer layout:** 12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect.
- **Block size** (KTU default) = 4 KiB; **pointer size** = 4 B (32-bit) or 8 B (64-bit).
- **Max file size formula (classical):** $S_{\max} = \left(12 + P + P^2 + P^3\right) \times B$, where $P = B / \text{ptr\_size}$.
- **Superblock magic number** for ext family = `0xEF53`; contains FS-wide parameters.
- **Block group layout:** Superblock → Group Descriptors → Block Bitmap → Inode Bitmap → Inode Table → Data Blocks.
- **VFS four objects:** `super_block`, `inode`, `dentry`, `file` — know each role cold.
