- **`ext4` improvements over `ext3`:** extents, delayed allocation, multiblock allocator, larger limits (16 TiB files), metadata checksums, persistent pre-allocation.
- **Journaling modes:** `journal` (slowest, safest), `ordered` (default), `writeback` (fastest, weakest).
- **Inode mapping:** $\text{Group} = \left\lfloor (N-1) / \text{inodes\_per\_group} \right\rfloor$.
- **Linux commands for case study:** `dumpe2fs`, `tune2fs`, `stat <file>`, `df -i`, `mount`, `mkfs.ext4`.
- **Filesystem hierarchy:** `/` root, `/home`, `/etc`, `/var`, `/proc`, `/sys` — `/proc` is a **pseudo FS** registered with VFS.
- **CoW (Copy-on-Write)** in `Btrfs` enables cheap snapshots — a key differentiator from `ext4`.
- **Direct / Indirect block trade-off:** Larger block size = bigger max file size but more internal fragmentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk Management: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Management: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disk Management** in an Operating System refers to the complete set of hardware and software techniques used to organize, store, retrieve, and maintain data on secondary storage devices (magnetic hard disks, SSDs). It encompasses the **physical geometry**, **logical addressing**, **formatting**, **scheduling**, and **reliability handling** of storage subsystems.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, Disk Management is the OS subsystem that abstracts a **raw block-addressable storage device** into a structured, file-system-ready logical volume. It involves the bridge between the device driver layer and the file system layer.

The key components of disk management include:

- **Physical Disk Structure** — Platters, tracks, sectors, cylinders, and heads
- **Logical Block Addressing (LBA)** — Linear addressing scheme replacing legacy CHS
- **Disk Formatting** — Low-level and high-level formatting
- **Boot Blocks** — System initialization sectors
- **Bad Block Management** — Defect handling strategies
- **Storage Attachment Interfaces** — SATA, NVMe, SAS, IDE
- **Disk Scheduling Algorithms** — FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Massive Revolving Library**

Think of a hard disk as a **huge circular library** with multiple floors (platters). Each floor is divided into concentric circles (tracks), and each circle is split into small compartments (sectors). A librarian's "hand" (the read/write head) moves across these floors to grab a book (data block) you request.

Now, the OS is like the **chief librarian** who:
- Maintains a **catalog** (file system) so the librarian can find books quickly
- Decides the **order in which to serve visitors** (disk scheduling) so the librarian doesn't run back and forth aimlessly
- Marks **damaged compartments** as "out of service" (bad block management)
- Pre-reserves a section near the entrance for **emergency records** (boot block)

Without disk management, the OS would be like a librarian who has to search the entire building for every book request — extremely slow and inefficient.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following standard metrics are used universally in KTU board problems:

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Rotational latency (half rotation) | **4.17 ms @ 7200 RPM** |
| Full rotation time @ 7200 RPM | **8.33 ms** |
| Average seek time (modern HDD) | **3 to 10 ms** |
| Typical sector size | **512 bytes** (legacy) / **4096 bytes (4K) modern** |
| Tracks per inch (TPI) | Thousands to tens of thousands |
| Bits per inch (BPI) | Hundreds of thousands |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Disk Structure

A magnetic hard disk is organized in a strict physical hierarchy. Understanding this hierarchy is essential because every disk scheduling algorithm ultimately deals with these physical units.

**Layer 1 — Platter**: A rigid circular disk coated with magnetic material. Modern drives typically contain **1 to 5 platters**.

**Layer 2 — Surface**: Each platter has **two surfaces** (top and bottom). A read/write head is assigned to each surface.

**Layer 3 — Track**: A concentric circular band on a single surface. Modern drives have tens of thousands of tracks per inch.

**Layer 4 — Sector**: The smallest addressable unit on a track. Traditionally **512 bytes**, modern drives use **4096 bytes (Advanced Format)**.

**Layer 5 — Cylinder**: A vertical stack of tracks at the same radial distance across all platters. Moving the head from one cylinder to the next requires only a single seek.

**Layer 6 — Cluster / Block**: A logical grouping of sectors used by the file system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this hierarchy matters for OS**: The OS does not see platters or tracks directly. The disk controller exposes **logical blocks** and the OS issues **block I/O requests**. The controller then translates these into physical cylinder-head-sector (CHS) or LBA operations.

### 2.2 Logical Block Addressing (LBA) vs CHS

- **CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector)**: Legacy 24-bit addressing scheme limited to **8.4 GB** (16,450 cylinders × 16 heads × 63 sectors × 512 bytes).
- **LBA (Logical Block Addressing)**: Modern linear addressing where each sector has a unique integer address starting from **0**. LBA48 supports up to **144 PB** (petabytes).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why did the industry move from CHS to LBA?"* — The answer is scalability, BIOS compatibility, and the rise of zone-bit recording (ZBR) where outer tracks have more sectors than inner tracks, making CHS meaningless.

### 2.3 Disk Formatting — Two Distinct Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Low-Level Formatting (Physical Formatting)**
> - Done at the factory
> - Divides each track into sectors
> - Writes sector headers, ECC (Error Correction Codes), and gaps
> - Establishes the inter-sector gaps (e.g., sync bytes, address marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Level Formatting (Logical Formatting)**
> - Done by the OS during installation
> - Creates the file system structures (boot block, superblock, inodes, FAT, free space bitmap)
> - Sets up the root directory
> - Wipes data structures but does NOT rewrite sectors

### 2.4 Boot Block and Bootstrap Process

Every disk has a designated **boot block** (also called the Master Boot Record on MBR disks) located at a fixed location — typically **sector 0 of the first disk**.

The boot process unfolds in three stages:

1. **Stage 1 — BIOS/UEFI**: Firmware code in ROM loads the MBR into memory and executes it.
2. **Stage 2 — Boot Loader (GRUB, NTLDR, BOOTMGR)**: Located in the boot block, it locates and loads the active partition's boot sector.
3. **Stage 3 — OS Kernel**: The kernel is loaded and takes control.

> [!NOTE]
> To ensure recoverability, many systems place a **duplicate boot block** at a different physical location. If the primary boot block is corrupted, the system can fall back to the backup.

### 2.5 Bad Block Management Strategies

There are two principal strategies the OS or controller uses to handle defective sectors:

**Strategy 1 — Sector Sparing (Forwarding)**
- The defective sector is logically remapped to a spare sector from a pool.
- The controller's translation table maps the bad LBA to a spare LBA.
- Subsequent reads/writes go to the spare transparently.
- **Advantage**: Lossless from the OS perspective.
- **Disadvantage**: Bad block table consumes space; controller-internal.

**Strategy 2 — Sector Skipping**
- The controller simply ignores the bad sector and shifts all subsequent data.
- **Advantage**: Easier to implement in firmware.
- **Disadvantage**: Complex addressing; performance penalty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern drives use **sector sparing combined with a grown defect list (G-list)** maintained in a hidden system area of the disk. The OS never sees bad blocks unless it issues `READ LONG` or `WRITE LONG` low-level commands.

### 2.6 Disk Performance Time Calculation (KTU Formula Sheet)

The total time to service a disk I/O request is the sum of three components:

$$T_{total} = T_{seek} + T_{rotational} + T_{transfer}$$

Where:
- $T_{seek}$ = time to move the read/write head to the target cylinder
- $T_{rotational}$ = rotational latency (time for the desired sector to rotate under the head)
- $T_{transfer}$ = time to actually read/write the data

**Rotational Latency Formula**:

$$T_{rotational} = \frac{60}{RPM} \times \frac{1}{2} \text{ seconds (average case)}$$

**Transfer Time Formula**:

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{\text{bytes to transfer}}{\text{bytes per track}} \times \frac{60}{RPM} \text{ seconds}$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{total} = T_s + T_r + T_t$ | $T_s$ = seek, $T_r$ = rotational, $T_t$ = transfer | ms | Service time per request |
| $T_r = \frac{30}{RPM} \text{ s}$ | Average rotational latency | ms | Half-rotation time |
| $T_t = \frac{N}{N_{track}} \times \frac{60}{RPM}$ | $N$ = bytes to transfer | ms | Read/write time |
| $C_{disk} = 2 \times C_{platter}$ | Surfaces per platter | count | Capacity from platters |
| $S_{max} = 2^{LBA bits} \times B$ | LBA addressing | bytes | Max addressable space |
| $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{trans}$ | Total access time | ms | Average access |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU tip: When the problem says "ignore transfer time" or "approximate", drop $T_t$ from the calculation. When it says "head was already at the correct track", drop $T_s$.

### 2.8 Engineering Applications

Disk management techniques are foundational in:

- **Database systems** — B-tree indexes rely on cylinder-aligned access to minimize seeks
- **RAID controllers** — Stripe sizing is optimized around cylinder boundaries
- **Solid-State Drives** — Even SSDs emulate LBA interfaces, so all OS-level concepts transfer
- **Cloud storage** — Logical volume managers (LVM, ZFS) extend disk management to virtualized environments

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Addressable Capacity using LBA

Modern disks use **LBA48**, meaning 48 bits are available for sector addressing. Let us derive the maximum theoretical storage capacity.

**Step 1 — Number of addressable sectors**
With 48 bits, the total number of unique sector addresses is:

$$N_{sectors} = 2^{48}$$

**Step 2 — Capacity per sector**
Each modern sector stores **4096 bytes** (4 KB). For legacy compatibility, the calculation also works for 512 bytes.

**Step 3 — Total addressable capacity**

$$C_{max} = 2^{48} \times 4096 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Convert bytes to more readable units**
First, simplify the powers of 2:

$$C_{max} = 2^{48} \times 2^{12} \text{ bytes} = 2^{60} \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Petabytes**
Using $1 \text{ PB} = 2^{50}$ bytes:

$$C_{max} = \frac{2^{60}}{2^{50}} \text{ PB} = 2^{10} \text{ PB} = 1024 \text{ PB}$$

$$\boxed{C_{max} = 1024 \text{ PB} = 1 \text{ EB (Exabyte)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic explanation**: This shows why LBA48 is sufficient for all foreseeable storage needs. The legacy CHS scheme, limited to $2^{24} \times 512 = 8.4$ GB, was completely inadequate once disks exceeded this boundary in the late 1990s.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Average Disk Access Time

**Problem Statement**: A disk rotates at **7200 RPM**, has an average seek time of **5 ms**, and must transfer **64 KB** of data. Each track holds **1 MB**. Calculate the total access time.

**Step 1 — Compute average rotational latency**

$$T_{rot} = \frac{30}{RPM} \text{ seconds} = \frac{30}{7200} \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{rot} = 0.004166... \text{ seconds} \approx 4.17 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute transfer time**

$$T_{trans} = \frac{\text{bytes requested}}{\text{bytes per track}} \times \frac{60}{RPM}$$

$$T_{trans} = \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{1 \text{ MB}} \times \frac{60}{7200} \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{trans} = \frac{1}{16} \times 0.00833... \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{trans} = 0.0005208... \text{ seconds} \approx 0.52 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Sum all components**

$$T_{total} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{trans}$$

$$T_{total} = 5 \text{ ms} + 4.17 \text{ ms} + 0.52 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} \approx 9.69 \text{ ms}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Bad-Block Strategies

| Criteria | Sector Sparing | Sector Skipping |
|---|---|---|
| **OS awareness** | OS is unaware; controller handles | OS may need to know skipped regions |
| **Performance** | Slight penalty on first access after remap | Constant shifting overhead |
| **Implementation** | Complex table lookup in firmware | Simpler linear scheme |
| **Recovery** | Transparent | May corrupt data streams |
| **Capacity overhead** | Spare pool (e.g., 100 sectors spare) | None (just skips) |
| **Used in** | Modern HDDs, SSDs, NVMe drives | Legacy floppy systems |

### 3.4 Derivation: Rotations Required to Read a Full Track

**Problem**: A disk has 200 sectors per track, each holding 512 bytes. How many rotations are needed to read **5 MB** of contiguous data on a single track?

**Step 1 — Total bytes per track**

$$B_{track} = 200 \times 512 = 102{,}400 \text{ bytes} \approx 100 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 2 — Total bytes to read**

$$B_{total} = 5 \text{ MB} = 5 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 5{,}242{,}880 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Number of full tracks required**

$$N_{tracks} = \frac{B_{total}}{B_{track}} = \frac{5{,}242{,}880}{102{,}400} = 51.2 \text{ tracks}$$

**Step 4 — Rotations needed (1 rotation = 1 track read)**

$$N_{rotations} = 51.2 \approx 52 \text{ rotations (rounded up)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the data is contiguous across 52 tracks, we also need to account for **head movement time** ($52 \times T_{seek}$). In practice, OS file systems lay out files in cylinder-aligned groups to minimize this cost.

### 3.5 Derivation: Average Seek Distance with Uniform Random Access

Assume the head is equally likely to be requested to move to any of the **N** cylinders. What is the **average seek distance**?

**Step 1 — Distance between any two cylinders i and j**

$$d(i,j) = \vert i - j \vert$$

**Step 2 — Sum of all pairwise distances**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \vert i - j \vert$$

**Step 3 — Average**

$$\bar{d} = \frac{S}{N^2}$$

By symmetry and standard combinatorial identity:

$$\bar{d} = \frac{N+1}{3} \text{ cylinders}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to time using average seek rate $T_{avg}$ per cylinder**

$$\bar{T}_{seek} = \frac{N+1}{3} \times T_{per\_cylinder}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why disk scheduling matters. A naive FCFS algorithm on uniform random requests results in average seek distance proportional to **N/3**, whereas a smart SSTF or SCAN algorithm reduces this dramatically to **O(sqrt(N))** or less.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Physical Disk Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hard Disk Drive] --> B[Platter 1]
    A --> C[Platter 2]
    A --> D[Platter 3]
    B --> B1[Surface Top]
    B --> B2[Surface Bottom]
    C --> C1[Surface Top]
    C --> C2[Surface Bottom]
    D --> D1[Surface Top]
    D --> D2[Surface Bottom]
    B1 --> B1A[Track 0]
    B1 --> B1B[Track 1]
    B1 --> B1C[Track N]
    B1A --> B1A1[Sector 0]
    B1A --> B1A2[Sector 1]
    B1A --> B1A3[Sector M]
    X[Cylinder Concept] --- B1A
    X --- C1A
    X --- D1A
    Y[Read Write Head Assembly] --> B1
    Y --> B2
    Y --> C1
    Y --> C2
    Y --> D1
    Y --> D2
    Z[Spindle Motor 7200 RPM] --- A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Disk I/O Request Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Issues Read Write System Call] --> Q[File System Layer]
    Q --> R[Buffer Cache Check]
    R --> S{Cache Hit}
    S -->|Yes| T[Return Data to Process]
    S -->|No| U[Disk Scheduler Queue]
    U --> V[Disk Scheduling Algorithm]
    V --> W[Device Driver]
    W --> X[Disk Controller]
    X --> Y[Physical Disk Platter]
    Y --> Z[Read Write Head]
    Z --> AA[Seek to Track]
    AA --> AB[Wait for Rotational Latency]
    AB --> AC[Transfer Data Block]
    AC --> AD[Return to Scheduler]
    AD --> AE[Mark Request Complete]
    AE --> T
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: OS Disk Management Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[User Application Layer] --> L2[File System Interface open read write]
    L2 --> L3[Logical File System Manages Metadata]
    L3 --> L4[File Organization Module Module 3]
    L4 --> L5[Basic File System Issues Block Requests]
    L5 --> L6[Disk Scheduler Module 4 Focus]
    L6 --> L7[Device Driver Layer]
    L7 --> L8[Disk Controller Hardware]
    L8 --> L9[Physical Magnetic Platters]
    L6 -.->|manages| M1[FCFS SSTF SCAN C-SCAN LOOK C-LOOK]
    L6 -.->|handles| M2[Bad Block Mapping]
    L6 -.->|tracks| M3[Queue of Pending Requests]
    L6 -.->|uses| M4[Seek Time Rotational Latency Transfer Time]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Boot Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[System Power On] --> BIOS[BIOS or UEFI Firmware Executes]
    BIOS --> POST[Power On Self Test]
    POST --> MBR[Load MBR from Sector 0 of Boot Disk]
    MBR --> Partition[Read Partition Table Identify Active Partition]
    Partition --> BootSector[Load Boot Sector VBR of Active Partition]
    BootSector --> Bootloader[Execute Boot Loader GRUB or NTLDR]
    Bootloader --> Kernel[Load OS Kernel into RAM]
    Bootloader --> Dual{Dual Boot or Single OS}
    Dual -->|User Selects| Kernel
    Kernel --> Init[Kernel Initializes Hardware Mounts File System]
    Init --> UserSpace[Launches User Space Processes]
    UserSpace --> Login[Login Screen or Shell Prompt]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: Bad Block Handling with Sector Sparing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OS Issues READ to LBA 5023] --> B{Controller Checks Mapping Table}
    B -->|LBA 5023 is Good| C[Access Physical Sector 5023]
    B -->|LBA 5023 is Bad| D[Lookup Spare Mapping]
    D --> E[Redirect to Spare Sector 9847]
    E --> F[Read Data from Spare Sector]
    C --> G[Return Data to OS]
    F --> G
    G --> H[OS Receives Data as if from LBA 5023]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Logical Block Addressing. Why is it preferred over the traditional CHS addressing scheme?*

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

**Definition (1 mark)**: Logical Block Addressing (LBA) is a method of addressing sectors on a storage device using a single linear integer index, where sector numbers start from 0 and increment sequentially across the entire disk.

**Reasons for preference (2 marks)**:
- **Scalability**: LBA supports up to $2^{48}$ sectors (LBA48) = **1 EB**, while CHS is limited to 8.4 GB.
- **Zone Bit Recording compatibility**: Outer tracks have more sectors than inner tracks in modern disks. CHS assumes equal sectors per track, which is no longer true.
- **BIOS/UEFI simplification**: LBA provides a single address space; no cylinder/head translation needed.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between low-level formatting and high-level formatting of a disk.*

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

| Aspect | Low-Level Formatting | High-Level Formatting |
|---|---|---|
| **Performed by** | Disk manufacturer | Operating system |
| **Creates** | Physical sectors with headers/ECC | File system structures |
| **Frequency** | Once at factory | Every OS installation |
| **Includes** | Sector gaps, sync bytes, address marks | Boot block, superblock, FAT/inode table |
| **Required for** | Defining physical geometry | Setting up logical file organization |

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Don't confuse seek time with rotational latency**. Seek time is the mechanical head movement; rotational latency is the disk spin waiting for the sector to arrive under the head. Many students mix these up and lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Always specify units** (ms vs seconds) in your final answer. Students often write "0.00416" without "seconds" and lose a mark.
> - **Show the formula BEFORE plugging in numbers**. KTU examiners award partial marks for the correct formula even if arithmetic is wrong.
> - **For boot block questions, do NOT skip the BIOS/UEFI stage**. Many students directly jump to "OS loads" and lose the MBR stage marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the physical structure of a magnetic hard disk. Discuss the role of cylinders in minimizing seek time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks)**:

**Diagram (3 marks)**: A neatly labeled diagram showing platters, surfaces, tracks, sectors, cylinder, read/write head, and spindle. The student should show the disk rotating about a central axis with concentric tracks on each surface and a vertical cylinder concept.

**Physical Structure Description (2 marks)**:
- A hard disk consists of **multiple platters** mounted on a common spindle.
- Each platter has **two surfaces**, and each surface has its own **read/write head**.
- Each surface contains **concentric tracks**, and each track is divided into **sectors** (typically 512 bytes or 4 KB).
- All heads move together on a single actuator arm.

**Role of Cylinders (2 marks)**:
- A **cylinder** is the set of all tracks at the same radial position across all platters.
- When the OS requests a block that lies in the **same cylinder**, the heads do not need to move — only a head switch is required.
- This **head switch time** (typically 0.5–1 ms) is much less than a full **seek time** (5–10 ms).
- Therefore, the OS/file system should place **files that are accessed together in the same cylinder** to minimize average seek time.

---

**(b)** A disk rotates at **10000 RPM** with an average seek time of **6 ms** and a transfer rate of **80 MB/s**. Calculate: (i) the average rotational latency, (ii) the time to transfer **256 KB**, and (iii) the total average access time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks)**:

**(i) Average Rotational Latency (2 marks)**:

$$T_{rot} = \frac{30}{RPM} \text{ seconds} = \frac{30}{10000} \text{ s} = 0.003 \text{ s} = 3 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Transfer Time for 256 KB (2 marks)**:

$$T_{trans} = \frac{256 \text{ KB}}{80 \text{ MB/s}} = \frac{0.25 \text{ MB}}{80 \text{ MB/s}} = 0.003125 \text{ s} = 3.125 \text{ ms}$$

**(iii) Total Average Access Time (3 marks)**:

$$T_{total} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{trans}$$

$$T_{total} = 6 \text{ ms} + 3 \text{ ms} + 3.125 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} = 12.125 \text{ ms}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of disk scheduling. Why is FCFS scheduling inefficient for disks, and what is the role of seek optimization? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks)**:

**Concept of Disk Scheduling (2 marks)**:
- Disk scheduling is the policy used by the OS to determine the **order in which pending I/O requests** are serviced by the disk.
- The primary goal is to **minimize the total head movement (seek distance)** and thereby reduce average response time.

**Inefficiency of FCFS (3 marks)**:
- **FCFS (First Come First Served)** services requests in the order they arrive.
- It is **fair** and **simple**, but it does **not consider the current head position**.
- If requests are scattered, the head performs **long zig-zag movements** between far-apart cylinders.
- Example: If the queue is [98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67] with the head starting at 53, FCFS would have the head travel from 53 → 98 → 183 → 37 → 122 → 14 → 124 → 65 → 67, traversing hundreds of cylinders unnecessarily.

**Role of Seek Optimization (2 marks)**:
- Seek optimization techniques (SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK) reorder the queue so that the head **moves in a more directed pattern** (e.g., always serving the nearest next request or sweeping across the disk).
- This reduces total head movement, lowers average response time, and improves **throughput**.
- These algorithms are essential when the disk is heavily loaded.

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **disk I/O request lifecycle** from the application layer down to the physical disk. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks)**:

**Block Diagram (3 marks)**: A vertical flow diagram showing the layers from application down to the physical disk:
1. Application Process
2. File System Interface
3. Logical File System
4. File Organization Module
5. Basic File System
6. Disk Scheduler
7. Device Driver
8. Disk Controller
9. Physical Disk

**Explanation (4 marks)**:
- **Step 1**: The application invokes a system call such as `read()` or `write()`.
- **Step 2**: The **file system** translates the file name and offset into a **logical block number**.
- **Step 3**: The **basic file system** enqueues the block request in the disk scheduler's queue.
- **Step 4**: The **disk scheduler** selects the next request using its algorithm (FCFS/SSTF/SCAN) to minimize seek.
- **Step 5**: The **device driver** issues the request to the **disk controller** via the interface (SATA/NVMe).
- **Step 6**: The controller performs **seek, rotational latency, and transfer** mechanically.
- **Step 7**: Data flows back up the stack to the requesting application, and the request is marked complete.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Disk Management: Introduction**

- **Disk hierarchy**: Platter → Surface → Track → Sector → Cylinder. Cylinder is the unit of seek.
- **Sector size**: 512 bytes (legacy) and 4096 bytes (Advanced Format, 4Kn). Modern drives support both via 512e emulation.
- **CHS limit**: 8.4 GB; uses 24-bit addressing (Cylinder-Head-Sector).
- **LBA48 limit**: 1 EB (Exabyte) with 48-bit addressing.
- **Total access time**: $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{trans}$ (memorize this equation).
- **Average rotational latency** = $\frac{30}{RPM}$ seconds (one half-rotation).
- **Low-level formatting** = factory operation, defines sectors and ECC.
- **High-level formatting** = OS operation, installs file system structures.
- **Boot block** = sector 0 (MBR) on disk; contains bootloader and partition table.
- **Bad block handling**: Sector sparing is the modern standard; sector skipping is legacy.
- **Disk scheduling goal**: Minimize total head movement and average response time.
- **FCFS** is fair but inefficient; **SSTF/SCAN/C-SCAN** optimize seeks.
- **Platters rotate at fixed RPM** (5400, 7200, 10000, 15000). Faster rotation = lower latency.
- **Transfer rate depends on** track density (BPI) and rotation speed (RPM).
- **Storage interfaces**: SATA, NVMe, SAS, IDE. NVMe uses PCIe lanes for lowest latency.
- **Cylinder-aligned file layout** minimizes head movement when accessing related files.
- **Zone Bit Recording (ZBR)**: outer tracks have more sectors than inner tracks.
- **Sector sparing maintains a G-list** (grown defect list) in a hidden disk area.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** for enterprise HDDs is typically 1–2 million hours.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk Scheduling Algorithms (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Scheduling Algorithms (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Disk Scheduling** is the policy adopted by the Operating System to determine the **sequence** in which pending I/O requests to a disk's read/write head are serviced. The objective is to minimize the **total head movement (seek distance)**, reduce **average response time**, and maximize **disk throughput** by reducing mechanical seek latency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim from Module 4):** *Disk scheduling algorithms are used to schedule multiple requests for accessing a disk. The purpose of disk scheduling algorithms is to reduce the total seek time and hence improve the throughput of the system.*

A request queue is an unordered list of cylinder numbers waiting for service. The OS must reorder these requests using a scheduling policy. The **seek time** $T_s$ is approximately proportional to the distance $d$ traveled by the head:

$$T_s \;\propto\; d \;\triangleq\; \vert\,C_{\text{next}} - C_{\text{current}}\,\vert$$

where $C$ denotes the cylinder (track) number on the disk surface.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need It?

Modern hard disks are **electromechanical** devices. The only slow operation is the **mechanical movement** of the read/write head arm across platters. Rotational latency and transfer time are bounded by hardware physics, but seek time is the **dominant variable cost** — and it can be optimized purely by **reordering requests**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Ice-Cream Van

Imagine an **ice-cream van** parked at street number 53 on a long road numbered `0` to `199`. Children from houses {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67} run out shouting for ice cream simultaneously. The van driver must decide the **order of visits**.

- **FCFS** = Serve whoever shouted first (fair, but wastes fuel zigzagging).
- **SSTF** = Always go to the **nearest** unserved child (saves fuel, but a far child may starve).
- **SCAN** = Move in one direction servicing all, then reverse (like an elevator — "elevator algorithm").
- **C-SCAN** = Move in one direction servicing all, then **jump back to start** and repeat (gives everyone equal wait time).
- **LOOK / C-LOOK** = Same as SCAN / C-SCAN, but the van **stops at the last request** rather than the end of the road (smarter, no wasted end-trips).

> [!NOTE]
> The cylinder range of a disk is fixed (e.g., 0 to 199), but the **requests almost never lie exactly at the boundaries**. LOOK variants exploit this to avoid unnecessary end-to-end travel.

## 1.4 Algorithmic Taxonomy at a Glance

| Family | Algorithms | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-preemptive, Direction-blind** | FCFS | Pure arrival order |
| **Greedy Shortest-Distance** | SSTF | Local optimum (next nearest) |
| **Direction-aware Sweep** | SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK | Sweep arm like an elevator |

## 1.5 Performance Metrics Used Throughout

- **Total Head Movement (THM):** $\sum \vert\,C_{i+1} - C_i\,\vert$ in cylinders.
- **Average Seek Time:** $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{THM}{n}$, where $n$ is the number of requests.
- **Throughput:** Number of I/O requests completed per unit time.
- **Variance of Response Time:** Fairness indicator; lower is fairer.
- **Starvation:** Condition where a request waits indefinitely.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithm 1 — FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)

**Operational Logic (Bullet Walkthrough):**

- Requests are placed in a **queue** in the order they arrive at the disk controller.
- The head services the **oldest** request, then the next-oldest, and so on.
- No reordering, no optimization, no starvation (provably fair).
- **Weakness:** Wild back-and-forth motion; poor seek-time performance; head can oscillate between far ends of the disk.

> [!NOTE]
> FCFS is the **baseline** used in KTU valuation to compare the "head-movement saved" by smarter algorithms.

**Why it is "fair" but "slow":** Fairness requires FIFO order, but FIFO order has no correlation with the **physical proximity** of cylinders. Hence the head frequently traverses long distances unnecessarily.

## 2.2 Algorithm 2 — SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)

**Operational Logic:**

- At the current head position, scan the entire request queue.
- Select the request with the **minimum absolute distance** to the current head.
- Move to it, then re-evaluate from the new position.
- **Greedy local heuristic** — does **not** guarantee a globally optimal schedule.
- **Weakness:** Can cause **starvation** for requests lying at the extreme end of the disk if new requests keep arriving closer to the head.

**The "Why":** A greedy shortest-step choice reduces immediate cost, but the head may get stuck near the middle of the disk serving a flood of nearby requests while far-away ones wait forever.

## 2.3 Algorithm 3 — SCAN (The Elevator Algorithm)

**Operational Logic:**

- The head moves in a **chosen direction** (towards higher cylinders or towards lower cylinders), servicing every request along the way.
- When it reaches the **end of the disk** (cylinder 0 or last cylinder), it **reverses direction** and services requests on the return trip.
- **Caveat:** Requests at the end just serviced will wait longer than those just behind the head — **biased service order**.

**The "How":** The head behaves exactly like an elevator — it goes all the way to the top floor (or bottom), then comes back. This batches requests efficiently along a single sweep.

## 2.4 Algorithm 4 — C-SCAN (Circular SCAN)

**Operational Logic:**

- Head moves in one chosen direction, servicing requests, until it hits the **end of the disk**.
- It then **jumps back to the beginning (cylinder 0) without servicing** any request on the return trip.
- Service resumes in the **same direction** (typically towards higher cylinders).
- **Benefit:** Provides a **more uniform wait time** for all requests, similar to a **rotating circular buffet** where everyone gets the same time slice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> C-SCAN is **preferred for heavy-load systems** (databases, web servers) because it offers **deterministic, low-variance response times**, even though the return jump "wastes" cylinders.

## 2.5 Algorithm 5 — LOOK

**Operational Logic:**

- Identical to SCAN, **except** the head **does not travel all the way to the end** of the disk.
- It reverses direction at the **last pending request** in the current sweep direction.
- **Why:** Saves the wasted traversal of the empty cylinders beyond the last request.

## 2.6 Algorithm 6 — C-LOOK

**Operational Logic:**

- Hybrid of C-SCAN and LOOK.
- Head sweeps in one direction, servicing all requests up to the **last one**.
- It then **jumps back to the lowest pending request** (not cylinder 0) and resumes service.
- **Why:** Combines the **fairness of C-SCAN** with the **efficiency of LOOK** (no wasted end-traversal in either direction).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seek distance for one move | $d_i = \vert\,C_{i+1} - C_i\,\vert$ | Cylinders |
| 2 | Total Head Movement (THM) | $THM = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \vert\,C_{i+1} - C_i\,\vert$ | Cylinders |
| 3 | Average Seek Time | $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{THM}{n}$ | ms/req |
| 4 | SCAN head movement (full sweep) | $THM = (C_{\max} - C_{\text{start}}) + (C_{\max} - C_{\min})$ | $C_{\max}$, $C_{\min}$ are extreme requests in sweep |
| 5 | C-SCAN head movement | $THM = (C_{\max} - C_{\text{start}}) + C_{\max} + (C_{\min} - 0)$ | Counts the wrap-around jump |
| 6 | LOOK head movement | $THM = (C_{\max} - C_{\text{start}}) + (C_{\max} - C_{\min})$ | $C_{\max}$, $C_{\min}$ are extreme **pending** requests |
| 7 | C-LOOK head movement | $THM = (C_{\max} - C_{\text{start}}) + (C_{\max} - C_{\min}) + (C_{\text{next}} - C_{\min})$ | $C_{\text{next}}$ is the first request in the new sweep |
| 8 | Starvation condition | SSTF / FCFS-in-heavy-load: indefinite wait | SCAN/C-SCAN: bounded by $2 \times$ disk size |
| 9 | Fairness order (best → worst) | C-SCAN ≈ C-LOOK > SCAN ≈ LOOK > SSTF > FCFS | Lower variance ⇒ fairer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In LOOK / C-LOOK, $C_{\max}$ and $C_{\min}$ refer to the **highest and lowest pending requests**, **not** the disk's physical boundaries (0 and 199). Using 0/199 instead of the actual request extremes is the **#1 mistake** in KTU board answers.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Engines (PostgreSQL, Oracle):** Use variants of **C-SCAN / C-LOOK** to ensure predictable I/O latency for OLTP workloads.
- **Solid State Drives (SSDs):** Disk scheduling is largely **irrelevant** because there is no mechanical head. The OS still passes hints, but algorithms like NOOP are used.
- **Robotic Warehouse Systems:** The "arm" is the head; requests are bins; SCAN-like motion minimizes total arm travel.
- **Cloud Storage Clusters:** Even with SSDs, **virtual disk schedulers** in hypervisors (VMware vSphere, KVM) apply these algorithms to coalesce I/O patterns.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Reference Problem

> **Standard KTU Board Problem Statement:**
> *Consider a disk queue with requests for I/O to blocks on cylinders:*
> **98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67**
> *The read/write head is initially at cylinder **53**. The disk has **200 cylinders (0–199)**. Compute the total head movement using FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, and C-LOOK. (Assume SCAN-family algorithms move the head towards the higher cylinder numbers first.)*

Let:
- Request queue $Q = \{98,\, 183,\, 37,\, 122,\, 14,\, 124,\, 65,\, 67\}$
- Initial head $h_0 = 53$
- Disk range $[0, 199]$
- Total requests $n = 8$

## 3.2 FCFS — Step-by-Step Service Order

Service order is strictly the **arrival order**: $53 \to 98 \to 183 \to 37 \to 122 \to 14 \to 124 \to 65 \to 67$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \vert\,98 - 53\,\vert = 45 \\
d_2 &= \vert\,183 - 98\,\vert = 85 \\
d_3 &= \vert\,37 - 183\,\vert = 146 \\
d_4 &= \vert\,122 - 37\,\vert = 85 \\
d_5 &= \vert\,14 - 122\,\vert = 108 \\
d_6 &= \vert\,124 - 14\,\vert = 110 \\
d_7 &= \vert\,65 - 124\,\vert = 59 \\
d_8 &= \vert\,67 - 65\,\vert = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing all individual moves to obtain the total head movement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{FCFS}} &= 45 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2 \\
&= 640 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

The average seek time per request, assuming a constant seek-rate constant of $k = 0.1$ ms/cylinder, is then:

$$
T_{\text{avg}}^{\text{FCFS}} = \frac{640 \times 0.1}{8} = 8.0 \text{ ms/req}
$$

## 3.3 SSTF — Greedy Nearest-First Walkthrough

**Iteration Trace (always pick the unserved request with the minimum $\vert C_i - h_k \vert$):**

| Step | Current Head $h_k$ | Unserved Requests | Distances | Chosen Next | Move |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | **53** | {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67} | {45, 130, 16, 69, 39, 71, 12, 14} | **65** | 12 |
| 1 | **65** | {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 67} | {33, 118, 28, 57, 51, 59, 2} | **67** | 2 |
| 2 | **67** | {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124} | {31, 116, 30, 55, 53, 57} | **37** | 30 |
| 3 | **37** | {98, 183, 14, 122, 124} | {61, 146, 23, 85, 87} | **14** | 23 |
| 4 | **14** | {98, 183, 122, 124} | {84, 169, 108, 110} | **98** | 84 |
| 5 | **98** | {183, 122, 124} | {85, 24, 26} | **122** | 24 |
| 6 | **122** | {183, 124} | {61, 2} | **124** | 2 |
| 7 | **124** | {183} | {59} | **183** | 59 |

**Service order:** $53 \to 65 \to 67 \to 37 \to 14 \to 98 \to 122 \to 124 \to 183$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{SSTF}} &= 12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59 \\
&= 236 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

The improvement over FCFS can be quantified as a percentage reduction in head movement:

$$
\%\text{ Improvement} = \frac{640 - 236}{640} \times 100 = 63.13\%
$$

## 3.4 SCAN — Elevator Sweep Towards Higher End

**Step A — Sort pending requests:** $\{14, 37, 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183\}$.

**Step B — Identify two regions relative to $h_0 = 53$:**
- Right side (≥ 53): $\{65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183\}$
- Left side (< 53): $\{37, 14\}$

**Step C — Service order (sweep right to disk end 199, then sweep left):**

$$
53 \to 65 \to 67 \to 98 \to 122 \to 124 \to 183 \to 199 \to 37 \to 14
$$

**Step D — Compute individual moves:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= 65 - 53 = 12 \\
d_2 &= 67 - 65 = 2 \\
d_3 &= 98 - 67 = 31 \\
d_4 &= 122 - 98 = 24 \\
d_5 &= 124 - 122 = 2 \\
d_6 &= 183 - 124 = 59 \\
d_7 &= 199 - 183 = 16 \quad \text{(end-of-disk sweep)} \\
d_8 &= 199 - 37 = 162 \quad \text{(reversal travel)} \\
d_9 &= 37 - 14 = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Total head movement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{SCAN}} &= 12+2+31+24+2+59+16+162+23 \\
&= 331 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 C-SCAN — Circular Sweep with Return Jump

**Service order:**

$$
53 \to 65 \to 67 \to 98 \to 122 \to 124 \to 183 \to 199 \to 0 \to 14 \to 37
$$

**Distinct moves:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Right sweep:} \quad & (199 - 53) = 146 \\
\text{Return jump (no service):} \quad & (199 - 0) = 199 \\
\text{Resumed sweep:} \quad & (37 - 0) = 37
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total head movement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{C-SCAN}} &= 146 + 199 + 37 \\
&= 382 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although 382 > 331 (SCAN), C-SCAN provides **lower variance in wait time** — the **last** request serviced by SCAN was 14, which waited the entire reverse sweep. C-SCAN would have serviced 14 much earlier (right after the return jump). For KTU, the examiner expects both THM **and** the fairness commentary.

## 3.6 LOOK — Smart Sweep Without End-Traversal

**Service order** (note: head does **not** visit 199):

$$
53 \to 65 \to 67 \to 98 \to 122 \to 124 \to 183 \to 37 \to 14
$$

**Two-stroke computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Right sweep:} \quad & (183 - 53) = 130 \\
\text{Reverse sweep:} \quad & (183 - 14) = 169
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total head movement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{LOOK}} &= 130 + 169 \\
&= 299 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.7 C-LOOK — Circular + Smart

**Service order:**

$$
53 \to 65 \to 67 \to 98 \to 122 \to 124 \to 183 \to 14 \to 37
$$

**Three-stroke computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Right sweep to last request:} \quad & (183 - 53) = 130 \\
\text{Jump back to lowest request:} \quad & (183 - 14) = 169 \\
\text{Resumed sweep:} \quad & (37 - 14) = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total head movement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{C-LOOK}} &= 130 + 169 + 23 \\
&= 322 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.8 Master Comparison Matrix

| Algorithm | Service Sequence (from $h_0 = 53$) | THM (cylinders) | Avg Seek (ms/req @ 0.1 ms/cyl) | Starvation Risk | Fairness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | 98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67 | **640** | 8.00 | None | High (FIFO) |
| **SSTF** | 65, 67, 37, 14, 98, 122, 124, 183 | **236** | 2.95 | **High** | Low |
| **SCAN** | 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 199, 37, 14 | **331** | 4.14 | None | Medium |
| **C-SCAN** | 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 199, 0, 14, 37 | **382** | 4.78 | None | **High** |
| **LOOK** | 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 37, 14 | **299** | 3.74 | None | Medium |
| **C-LOOK** | 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 14, 37 | **322** | 4.03 | None | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden KTU Insight:** The "**best**" algorithm depends on the workload. **SSTF** has the lowest THM in this dataset, but it is **not always the best** in general because it can starve. C-SCAN has higher THM but **lower response-time variance**, which is what **production servers** actually optimize for. Examiners love to ask: *"Which is best and why?"* — the answer must reference **both** THM **and** starvation/fairness.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Disk Scheduling — Top-Level Functional Architecture

This diagram shows how a disk request enters the OS, gets queued, and is dispatched by a chosen scheduling algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Issues I/O Request] --> B[OS I/O Scheduler Queue]
    B --> C{Scheduling Algorithm}
    C --> D[FCFS Module]
    C --> E[SSTF Module]
    C --> F[SCAN Module]
    C --> G[CSCAN Module]
    C --> H[LOOK Module]
    C --> I[CLOOK Module]
    D --> J[Disk Controller]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Read Write Head]
    K --> L[Platters Cylinders 0 to 199]
    L --> M[I/O Complete Interrupt to OS]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow Inside a Sweep-Based Scheduler (SCAN / C-SCAN / LOOK / C-LOOK)

This block diagram illustrates the run-time decision tree that all four sweep-based algorithms share. The differentiating switch is the **termination condition**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Head at H0] --> B[Sort Pending Requests]
    B --> C[Partition into LEFT and RIGHT of H0]
    C --> D{Choose Sweep Direction}
    D --> E[Service RIGHT in Ascending Order]
    D --> F[Service LEFT in Descending Order]
    E --> G{Algorithm Type}
    F --> G
    G --> H[SCAN: Travel to Disk End Cmax Disk]
    G --> I[CSCAN: Travel to Disk End then Jump to 0]
    G --> J[LOOK: Travel to Last Request Only]
    G --> K[CLOOK: Jump from Last to First Request]
    H --> L{More Requests in Reverse Direction}
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L -->|Yes| M[Reverse Sweep Direction]
    M --> N[Service Remaining Requests]
    N --> O[All Requests Complete]
    L -->|No| O
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of the Seek-Time Pipeline

This schematic shows the physical-to-logical mapping that every disk-scheduling algorithm is ultimately trying to minimize.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Process] --> B[System Call read write]
    B --> C[OS I/O Subsystem]
    C --> D[Disk Driver Request Queue]
    D --> E[Disk Scheduler Reorder]
    E --> F[Disk Controller Firmware]
    F --> G[Actuator Arm Movement Seek]
    G --> H[Platter Rotation Latency]
    H --> I[Data Transfer]
    I --> J[Interrupt to CPU]
    style E fill:f9e79f,stroke:333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:f5b7b1,stroke:333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow-highlighted **Disk Scheduler** is the only software stage that can be algorithmically optimized. The pink-highlighted **Actuator Arm** is the slowest physical stage. Every scheduling algorithm is essentially trying to minimize the cumulative time spent in the pink block.

## 4.4 Comparative Visual: SCAN vs. C-SCAN Path on a Linear Cylinder Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCANPATH[SCAN Path: Reverses at End]
        S0[0] --> S14[14] --> S37[37] --> S53[53 Head] --> S65[65] --> S67[67] --> S98[98] --> S122[122] --> S124[124] --> S183[183] --> S199[199 End]
        S199 -.Reverse.-> S37
    end
    subgraph CSCANPATH[CSCAN Path: Jumps to 0]
        C0[0] --> C14[14] --> C37[37] --> C53[53 Head] --> C65[65] --> C67[67] --> C98[98] --> C122[122] --> C124[124] --> C183[183] --> C199[199 End]
        C199 -.Jump No Service.-> C0
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define disk scheduling. Why is it necessary in an Operating System? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Disk scheduling is the technique used by the Operating System to determine the **order in which pending disk I/O requests are serviced** by the read/write head.

It is necessary because:
1. The disk's **seek time** (mechanical head movement) is the slowest component of I/O latency.
2. Multiple processes issue competing requests; a poor order causes the head to **zigzag**, wasting time and energy.
3. A good scheduling policy reduces **total head movement**, **average response time**, and improves **system throughput**.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between FCFS and SSTF disk scheduling. State one drawback of SSTF. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FCFS | SSTF |
|---|---|---|
| **Selection Rule** | Oldest request first | Request closest to current head |
| **Starvation** | None | Possible (for far-away requests) |
| **Throughput** | Low | Higher than FCFS |
| **Implementation** | Simple FIFO queue | Requires computing all distances every step |

**Drawback of SSTF:** It can cause **starvation** — requests at the extremes of the disk may wait indefinitely if new requests keep arriving near the head, because SSTF always prefers the nearest unserved request.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

> *Consider a disk with **200 cylinders (0–199)**. The read/write head is initially at cylinder **53**. A sequence of disk requests arrives in this order:*
> **98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67**
> *For a disk head moving in the direction of **increasing cylinder numbers**, calculate the total head movement using:*
> *(a) SSTF disk scheduling algorithm. (7 marks)*
> *(b) C-LOOK disk scheduling algorithm. (7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — SSTF (7 marks)

**Step 1 — List unserved requests and compute distances from $h_0 = 53$:**

From 53: distances are $\{45, 130, 16, 69, 39, 71, 12, 14\}$ for $\{98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67\}$.

**Step 2 — Pick the minimum: 65** (distance 12). Move: $53 \to 65$.

**Step 3 — Repeat from 65:** distances become $\{33, 118, 28, 57, 51, 59, 2\}$. Pick **67** (distance 2). Move: $65 \to 67$.

**Step 4 — From 67:** distances become $\{31, 116, 30, 55, 53, 57\}$. Pick **37** (distance 30). Move: $67 \to 37$.

**Step 5 — From 37:** distances become $\{61, 146, 23, 85, 87\}$. Pick **14** (distance 23). Move: $37 \to 14$.

**Step 6 — From 14:** distances become $\{84, 169, 108, 110\}$. Pick **98** (distance 84). Move: $14 \to 98$.

**Step 7 — From 98:** distances become $\{85, 24, 26\}$. Pick **122** (distance 24). Move: $98 \to 122$.

**Step 8 — From 122:** distances become $\{61, 2\}$. Pick **124** (distance 2). Move: $122 \to 124$.

**Step 9 — From 124:** distance to 183 is 59. Move: $124 \to 183$.

**Service order:** $53 \to 65 \to 67 \to 37 \to 14 \to 98 \to 122 \to 124 \to 183$

**Total Head Movement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{SSTF}} &= 12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59 \\
&= 236 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks — 1 for initial table + 5 for stepwise trace + 1 for final total)**

#### Part (b) — C-LOOK (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Sort the request queue:** $\{14, 37, 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183\}$.

**Step 2 — Partition relative to $h_0 = 53$:**

- Right side ($\geq 53$): $\{65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183\}$
- Left side ($< 53$): $\{37, 14\}$

**Step 3 — Sweep right (towards higher cylinders) up to the last request (183), then jump to the lowest request (14) and continue:**

Service order:

$$
53 \to 65 \to 67 \to 98 \to 122 \to 124 \to 183 \to 14 \to 37
$$

**Step 4 — Compute head movement in three segments:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward sweep:} \quad & 183 - 53 = 130 \\
\text{Return jump:} \quad & 183 - 14 = 169 \\
\text{Final leg:} \quad & 37 - 14 = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Head Movement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{C-LOOK}} &= 130 + 169 + 23 \\
&= 322 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks — 2 for partition + 2 for service order + 2 for segment math + 1 for final total)**

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

> *Given a disk queue with requests for cylinders:*
> **80, 120, 35, 60, 110, 25, 145, 30**
> *The read/write head is initially at cylinder **50**. Disk has **200 cylinders (0–199)**. Compute the total head movement and draw the head-movement diagram for:*
> *(a) FCFS scheduling algorithm. (7 marks)*
> *(b) SCAN scheduling algorithm (assume head moves towards higher cylinder first). (7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — FCFS (7 marks)

**Service order is the arrival order:** $50 \to 80 \to 120 \to 35 \to 60 \to 110 \to 25 \to 145 \to 30$.

**Step-by-step distance calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \vert\,80 - 50\,\vert = 30 \\
d_2 &= \vert\,120 - 80\,\vert = 40 \\
d_3 &= \vert\,35 - 120\,\vert = 85 \\
d_4 &= \vert\,60 - 35\,\vert = 25 \\
d_5 &= \vert\,110 - 60\,\vert = 50 \\
d_6 &= \vert\,25 - 110\,\vert = 85 \\
d_7 &= \vert\,145 - 25\,\vert = 120 \\
d_8 &= \vert\,30 - 145\,\vert = 115
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Head Movement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{FCFS}} &= 30 + 40 + 85 + 25 + 50 + 85 + 120 + 115 \\
&= 550 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Head-movement diagram (linear sketch):**

```
  0    25   30   35   50   60   80   110  120  145  199
  |-----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
       (25) (30) (35) (50) (60) (80) (110)(120)(145)
  Start--->80--->120--->35--->60--->110--->25--->145--->30
```

**(7 marks — 5 for distance computations + 1 for sum + 1 for diagram)**

#### Part (b) — SCAN (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Sort the request queue:** $\{25, 30, 35, 60, 80, 110, 120, 145\}$.

**Step 2 — Partition relative to $h_0 = 50$:**

- Right side ($\geq 50$): $\{60, 80, 110, 120, 145\}$
- Left side ($< 50$): $\{35, 30, 25\}$

**Step 3 — SCAN service order (move right, hit disk end 199, then reverse):**

$$
50 \to 60 \to 80 \to 110 \to 120 \to 145 \to 199 \to 35 \to 30 \to 25
$$

**Step 4 — Segment-wise calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward sweep (right):} \quad & 199 - 50 = 149 \\
\text{Reverse sweep (left):} \quad & 199 - 25 = 174
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Head Movement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THM_{\text{SCAN}} &= 149 + 174 \\
&= 323 \text{ cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Head-movement diagram:**

```
0   25  30  35  50  60   80   110  120  145        199
|---|---||--||--||--||----||----||----||----|--------|
       25  30  35  50  60   80   110  120  145      199
Start--->60--->80--->110--->120--->145--->199--->35--->30--->25
```

**(7 marks — 2 for partition + 2 for service order + 2 for segment math + 1 for diagram)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using disk boundary (0/199) instead of last request in LOOK/C-LOOK.** This single mistake costs **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. *Always verify whether the algorithm reaches the disk end or stops at the last request.*
> 2. **Forgetting to add the return-jump distance in C-SCAN/C-LOOK.** Many students stop at the right-sweep total. The wrap-around distance is a **mandatory** part of the head movement.
> 3. **Wrong direction assumption in SCAN/LOOK.** Always **state explicitly** the assumed sweep direction. If the question says "towards higher cylinders first", do not silently switch directions.
> 4. **Not showing the service order.** Examiners want the **sequence** written out, not just the final number. A correct total with no order shown typically gets **partial credit only**.
> 5. **Confusing rotation latency with seek time.** Seek time is **head movement**; rotational latency is **platter spin**. They are **different**; the question asks about seek time, which is determined solely by cylinder differences.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- [ ] **FCFS** = Arrival order. No starvation, but worst THM. Baseline for comparison.
- [ ] **SSTF** = Greedy nearest. Lowest THM often, but **starvation possible**.
- [ ] **SCAN** = Elevator. Reverses at **disk end (199 or 0)**. Bounded wait, no starvation.
- [ ] **C-SCAN** = Circular. Reverses at disk end but **jumps back to 0** (or lowest request in C-LOOK). **Most uniform wait time.**
- [ ] **LOOK** = Like SCAN, but reverses at the **last pending request**, not the disk end.
- [ ] **C-LOOK** = Like C-SCAN, but jumps from **last request to first request**, not 0/199.
- [ ] **THM formula** for sweep algorithms: *(Farthest in direction − Start) + (Farthest in direction − Farthest in reverse direction)*.
- [ ] **THM formula for C-family**: add a third term for the **final leg** after the jump.
- [ ] **Fairness ranking:** C-SCAN ≈ C-LOOK > SCAN ≈ LOOK > SSTF > FCFS.
- [ ] **Starvation ranking:** FCFS (none) < SCAN/C-SCAN/LOOK/C-LOOK (bounded) < SSTF (unbounded).
- [ ] **Real-world default:** Linux uses **CFQ, Deadline, and NOOP** schedulers — not classical SCAN — because modern disks are SSDs or hybrid. The concept still matters for **virtual disks** and **legacy systems**.
- [ ] **KTU trap:** LOOK/C-LOOK do **NOT** go to the disk boundary. If a student writes `199` in the SCAN path for LOOK, **deduct 2 marks**.
- [ ] **Diagram is mandatory** in 7-mark SCAN/C-SCAN problems. Always draw the linear cylinder map with arrows.
- [ ] **Always mention direction** explicitly: "Assume head moves towards higher cylinder first" — never leave it ambiguous.
- [ ] **Average seek time = THM / n**, with a typical conversion constant $k = 0.1$ ms/cylinder (mention this in answers if not given).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Machines: Benefits & Features - Building Blocks - Types of VMs - Virtualization & Operating System Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Virtual Machines: Benefits & Features - Building Blocks - Types of VMs - Virtualization & Operating System Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 – Virtual Machines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is a software-defined emulation of a physical computer system that executes programs and operating systems in an isolated, abstracted environment, decoupled from the underlying physical hardware. In the context of the **PCITT403 – Operating Systems** syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 aligned), a virtual machine is treated as a **"duplicate of an actual machine"** — an efficient, isolated duplicate of the physical host — that fundamentally relies on the principle of **resource sharing, isolation, and abstraction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"A Virtual Machine is a software implementation of a physical computing environment in which an operating system or program can be installed and run. The VM provides a logical, isolated view of physical computing resources (CPU, memory, I/O, storage) to the guest operating system."*

The discipline that enables the creation and management of VMs is called **Virtualization**. The core software layer that creates, runs, and manages multiple virtual machines on a single physical host is known as the **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)**, or more commonly, the **Hypervisor**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Virtual Machine** like a **multi-storey apartment building constructed on a single plot of land**.

| Element of the Analogy | Real-World VM Equivalent |
|------------------------|--------------------------|
| The piece of land (soil, foundation, utilities) | The **Physical Hardware** (CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC) |
| The building architect / building manager | The **Hypervisor (VMM)** |
| Each individual flat/apartment | An independent **Virtual Machine** |
| The tenants inside each flat | The **Guest Operating Systems & Applications** |
| Shared amenities (water tank, power line) | The **Pooled Physical Resources** |
| Locked main doors of each flat | **Isolation Boundaries** (Security & Fault Tolerance) |

In this analogy:
- Multiple tenants (OS instances) believe they have the *entire building* to themselves, just as each guest OS believes it owns the *entire hardware*.
- The architect (hypervisor) controls who gets how much space, electricity, and water (CPU cycles, memory, I/O bandwidth).
- A problem in Flat 102 (one VM crashing) does not affect Flat 205 (another VM) — this represents **isolation**.
- The total number of flats and their sizes can be changed dynamically — this represents **elasticity / scalability**.

### 1.3 Key High-Yield Metrics (Must Memorize)

The following standard metrics are routinely tested in KTU 2024 Scheme assessments:

- **Consolidation Ratio** — Number of VMs hosted per physical server (typically ranges from **10:1 to 80:1** in enterprise data centers).
- **Hypervisor Footprint** — Memory/CPU overhead imposed by the VMM itself (typically **< 5%** for Type-1 bare-metal hypervisors).
- **VM Boot Time** — Time required for a guest OS to reach a usable state (modern VMs boot in **< 30 seconds**).
- **Effective Utilization** — Ratio of useful work performed to total capacity (virtualization pushes hardware utilization from **~15% to ~80%**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU emphasize Virtual Machines?**
> - Cloud computing (AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine) is fundamentally built on VMs.
> - The syllabus bridges **classical OS concepts** (process, memory, file systems) with **modern data-center computing**.
> - VMs are the practical realization of *multiprogramming* and *resource multiplexing* — concepts introduced in earlier modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Core Pillars of Virtualization

A KTU 2024 examiner expects every answer on VMs to be grounded in the **three foundational properties** of any virtualization layer. These were formally codified by **Popek and Goldberg (1974)** in their seminal paper *"Formal Requirements for Virtualizable Third-Generation Architectures"*.

| Pillar | Property | Description | KTU-Mandated Term |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Equivalence / Fidelity** | A program running inside a VM should behave *identically* as if it were running on the physical machine directly, *except* for timing-dependent effects. | **Fidelity Property** |
| 2 | **Efficiency / Performance** | A statistically dominant subset of guest instructions must be executed **directly by the hardware** without VMM intervention. | **Efficiency Property** |
| 3 | **Resource Control / Safety** | The VMM must retain *full control* over every physical resource; the guest OS must NOT be able to bypass it. | **Safety Property** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Popek–Goldberg Theorem (Frequent 7-Mark Question):**
> *"For an architecture to be efficiently virtualizable, the set of its sensitive instructions must be a strict subset of its privileged instructions."*
> Translation: **Every sensitive instruction MUST trap (cause a fault) when executed in user mode**, so the VMM can intercept it. Architectures that fail this test (like classic x86) need **binary translation** or **paravirtualization** to be virtualizable.

### 2.2 The Three Building Blocks of a Virtual Machine

Every virtual machine — whether it is VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, or Oracle VirtualBox — is composed of the **same three logical building blocks**. Memorizing this triad guarantees marks on a "Building Blocks of VM" question.

| # | Building Block | Role | KTU-Term |
|---|----------------|------|----------|
| 1 | **Hypervisor (VMM)** | The control layer that creates, schedules, and terminates VMs. Mediates all privileged operations. | Virtual Machine Monitor |
| 2 | **Virtual Hardware / Virtual Platform** | The emulated CPU (vCPU), virtual memory (Guest Physical Address Space), virtual disks, virtual NICs, and virtual I/O devices presented to the guest. | Virtual Resource Layer |
| 3 | **Guest Operating System** | The unmodified (or modified, in para-virtualization) OS that runs *inside* the VM, believing it owns the hardware. | Guest Software Stack |

Supporting sub-components include:
- **Device Emulators** — translate guest I/O requests into host system calls.
- **Virtual Disk Manager** — maps .vmdk / .vhd / .qcow2 files to physical blocks.
- **Virtual Network Switch (vSwitch)** — inter-VM and VM-to-physical-NIC switching.
- **Shadow Page Tables / Extended Page Tables (EPT/NPT)** — accelerate virtual-to-physical memory translation.

### 2.3 The Two Fundamental Types of Hypervisors (Board-Favorite)

| Feature | **Type-1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal)** | **Type-2 Hypervisor (Hosted)** |
|---------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Position** | Runs **directly on hardware**; *replaces* the host OS. | Runs **as an application** on top of a conventional host OS. |
| **Host OS Required?** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Performance** | Near-native (≈ **95–98%** of bare metal) | Slower (≈ **70–85%** of bare metal) due to double scheduling. |
| **Footprint** | Very small, hardened | Larger; inherits host OS services |
| **Use Case** | Enterprise data centers, cloud providers | Developer desktops, education, testing |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (server), Citrix XenServer, KVM | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels Desktop, QEMU |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trap Question Alert:** *"Is KVM a Type-1 or Type-2 hypervisor?"* — **KVM is Type-1** because it converts the Linux kernel itself into a hypervisor by loading it as a kernel module. It is *not* a hosted hypervisor despite being a part of Linux.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are derived from the basic principles of CPU scheduling, memory allocation, and consolidation that the VM subsystem inherits from the OS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet – Virtualization & VMs**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|--------------------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $\text{Consolidation Ratio} = \dfrac{N_{\text{VMs}}}{N_{\text{Physical Servers}}}$ | Number of VMs hosted per physical server | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{VM CPU Share}_{\%} = \dfrac{\text{vCPUs allocated to VM}}{N_{\text{physical cores}}} \times 100$ | Guaranteed CPU proportion | Percent (%) |
| 3 | $\text{Overcommit Ratio} = \dfrac{\sum \text{VM-configured RAM}}{\text{Physical RAM available}}$ | Memory oversubscription factor | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{Total CPU Time} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Sum of CPU time across all VMs (n) | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $\text{CPU Utilization} = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100$ | Fraction of time CPU is doing useful work | Percent (%) |
| 6 | $\text{VMM Overhead}_{\%} = \dfrac{T_{\text{with-VMM}} - T_{\text{native}}}{T_{\text{native}}} \times 100$ | Performance penalty of virtualization | Percent (%) |
| 7 | $\text{Effective Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Tasks Completed}}{\text{Elapsed Time}}$ | System throughput under VMM | tasks/s |
| 8 | $\text{Total Memory}_{\text{Virtual}} = \text{Static RAM} + \text{Dynamic Balloon Driver RAM} + \text{Swap Backed RAM}$ | Total addressable memory across VMs | Bytes / GB |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the vertical bar character has been replaced with the LaTeX-safe `\vert` or `\mid` operators to preserve Markdown table integrity. Whenever you see a fraction like **N\_VMs / N\_Physical**, render it as $\dfrac{N_{\text{VMs}}}{N_{\text{Physical Servers}}}$ in your exam scripts.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Computing (IaaS)** — AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, and Google Compute Engine expose VM instances as the basic unit of compute. Each EC2 instance *is* a virtual machine managed by the Xen-based or Nitro hypervisor.
- **Server Consolidation** — Enterprises replace 10 underutilized servers (running at ~15% CPU) with 1 physical server hosting 10 VMs (running at ~80% CPU), reducing CapEx, OpEx, and power bills.
- **Disaster Recovery & Snapshots** — A VM can be *snapshot* (frozen in time) and restored in seconds, making backup and recovery trivial compared to bare-metal imaging.
- **Software Testing & Sandboxing** — Developers run untrusted code, malware analysis, and cross-platform testing inside isolated VMs without risking the host.
- **Operating System Education** — KTU's own lab exercises (VirtualBox / VMware) demonstrate the *direct practical implementation* of modules 1, 2, and 3 (process scheduling, memory management, file systems) by students running them in guest OS environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **rigorous mathematical walkthroughs** of the core virtualization formulas a KTU 2024 student must be able to reproduce on paper.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — VMM Overhead Penalty (14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** A physical server executes a benchmark workload in $T_{\text{native}} = 100$ seconds on bare metal. When the same workload is run inside a VM, the VMM intercepts privileged operations and adds a total interception delay of $T_{\text{intercept}} = 15$ seconds. Compute the VMM overhead percentage.

**Step 1 — Identify the total time taken in the virtualized environment.**

The total time inside the VM is the sum of the *native execution time* plus the *time the VMM spent* emulating privileged instructions.

$$
T_{\text{with-VMM}} = T_{\text{native}} + T_{\text{intercept}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

$$
T_{\text{with-VMM}} = 100\ \text{s} + 15\ \text{s} = 115\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the VMM Overhead formula.**

$$
\text{VMM Overhead}_{\%} = \dfrac{T_{\text{with-VMM}} - T_{\text{native}}}{T_{\text{native}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values into the formula.**

$$
\text{VMM Overhead}_{\%} = \dfrac{115 - 100}{100} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$
\text{VMM Overhead}_{\%} = \dfrac{15}{100} \times 100
$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{\text{VMM Overhead}_{\%} = 15\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The VMM introduces a **15% performance penalty** for this workload. This is why Type-1 hypervisors target an overhead of **≤ 5%** in production.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective CPU Utilization After Server Consolidation

**Problem Statement:** A data center has $N = 8$ physical servers. Each server runs at $U_{\text{individual}} = 15\%$ average CPU utilization. After consolidation, these 8 workloads are moved into 2 physical servers, each hosting 4 VMs. Assuming perfect packing and no overhead, calculate the new effective utilization per consolidated server.

**Step 1 — Express the total CPU demand across the 8 servers.**

$$
\text{Total CPU Demand} = N \times U_{\text{individual}} = 8 \times 15\% = 120\%
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the consolidation principle (sum of individual demands pooled onto fewer hosts).**

$$
\text{Aggregated Work} = 8 \times 15 = 120 \text{ normalized CPU-units}
$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the aggregated work equally across the 2 consolidated servers.**

$$
U_{\text{consolidated}} = \dfrac{\text{Aggregated Work}}{N_{\text{consolidated}}} = \dfrac{120}{2} = 60\%
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with the conservation equation.**

$$
N \cdot U_{\text{individual}} = N_{\text{consolidated}} \cdot U_{\text{consolidated}}
$$

$$
8 \times 15\% = 2 \times 60\%
$$

$$
120\% = 120\% \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{U_{\text{consolidated}} = 60\% \text{ per physical server}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The same 120 normalized units of work now run on 2 servers at 60% utilization each — a **4× reduction in physical servers** and a jump from 15% → 60% utilization, which is the *economic* justification for virtualization.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — VM Memory Overcommitment Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A physical server has $M_{\text{phys}} = 128$ GB of RAM. It hosts 6 VMs, each configured with $M_{\text{VM}} = 32$ GB of virtual RAM. Compute the **Overcommit Ratio**. If the host uses a *balloon driver* that recovers $M_{\text{recover}} = 24$ GB of idle memory from the VMs, what is the *effective* supported VM-configured RAM?

**Step 1 — Compute the sum of all VM-configured RAM.**

$$
\sum M_{\text{VM}} = 6 \times 32\ \text{GB} = 192\ \text{GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the overcommit ratio formula.**

$$
\text{Overcommit Ratio} = \dfrac{\sum M_{\text{VM}}}{M_{\text{phys}}} = \dfrac{192}{128}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Overcommit Ratio} = 1.5}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective memory the hypervisor must actually back with physical RAM after ballooning.**

$$
M_{\text{effective}} = \sum M_{\text{VM}} - M_{\text{recover}}
$$

$$
M_{\text{effective}} = 192 - 24 = 168\ \text{GB}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify that the effective demand does not exceed physical RAM plus a safe swap headroom.**

$$
M_{\text{effective}} = 168\ \text{GB} \;>\; M_{\text{phys}} = 128\ \text{GB}
$$

The hypervisor must use **swapping / page sharing / memory compression** to satisfy the residual 40 GB demand. This is the practical meaning of *overcommitment*.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — CPU Time Slicing Across Multiple VMs

**Problem Statement:** A 4-core physical CPU runs 3 VMs configured with vCPU shares in the ratio $\text{VM}_1 : \text{VM}_2 : \text{VM}_3 = 2 : 3 : 5$. The total observation window is $T = 100$ seconds. Compute the CPU time each VM receives.

**Step 1 — Find the total weight (denominator).**

$$
W_{\text{total}} = 2 + 3 + 5 = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time fraction for each VM using proportional-share scheduling.**

$$
T_{i} = \dfrac{w_i}{W_{\text{total}}} \times T
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values for VM₁, VM₂, VM₃.**

$$
T_{1} = \dfrac{2}{10} \times 100 = 20\ \text{s}
$$

$$
T_{2} = \dfrac{3}{10} \times 100 = 30\ \text{s}
$$

$$
T_{3} = \dfrac{5}{10} \times 100 = 50\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the conservation law.**

$$
T_{1} + T_{2} + T_{3} = 20 + 30 + 50 = 100\ \text{s} = T \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Final Allocation Summary.**

$$
\boxed{T_{1} = 20\ \text{s},\quad T_{2} = 30\ \text{s},\quad T_{3} = 50\ \text{s}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU 2024:** This exact proportional-share calculation is a *direct application* of CPU scheduling algorithms (CFS in Linux, used by KVM) taught in Module 2. The examiner tests whether you can *transfer* scheduling concepts from earlier modules into the virtualization context.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping OS Components to Virtual Equivalents

This matrix is the *single most important table* for the **"Virtualization & Operating System Components"** sub-topic. Memorize it column-by-column.

| Original OS Component (Host) | Virtualization Technique | Virtual Equivalent (Guest) | KTU Term |
|------------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|----------|
| **CPU** | Time-slicing / Binary Translation / Hardware-assisted VT-x / AMD-V | Virtual CPU (vCPU) | CPU Virtualization |
| **Physical Memory (RAM)** | Shadow Page Tables / Extended Page Tables (EPT) / Nested Page Tables (NPT) | Guest Physical Address Space | Memory Virtualization |
| **Hard Disk (Block Device)** | Disk Image Files (.vmdk, .vdi, .qcow2) mapped by virtual disk driver | Virtual Disk | Storage Virtualization |
| **Network Interface Card (NIC)** | Virtual NIC + Virtual Switch (vSwitch) bridged to physical NIC | Virtual Network Adapter | Network Virtualization |
| **I/O Devices (Keyboard, Mouse, USB)** | Device emulation / paravirtualized virtio drivers | Virtual I/O Device | I/O Virtualization |
| **Process / Thread Scheduler** | VMM Scheduler (Credit Scheduler, BVT) running on top of host OS scheduler | Two-level Scheduler (VMM → Guest) | Scheduling Virtualization |
| **File System** | Virtual file system (e.g., VMware VMFS, NTFS for Hyper-V) | Shared / Clustered Filesystem | Storage Virtualization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Level Scheduling Insight (Board Favorite):**
> In a virtualized environment, scheduling happens **twice**:
> - **Level 1** — The *host hypervisor* decides *which VM* gets the physical CPU next.
> - **Level 2** — The *guest OS inside the VM* decides *which process within the VM* gets the vCPU next.
> This is the foundation for "CPU virtualization" and a guaranteed 7-mark question.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — High-Level VM Architecture (Type-1 Bare-Metal)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP1["App A Guest1"]
    APP2["App B Guest1"]
    APP3["App C Guest2"]
    GOS1["Guest OS Linux Guest1"]
    GOS2["Guest OS Windows Guest2"]
    VHARD["Virtual Hardware vCPU vRAM vDisk vNIC"]
    VMM["Hypervisor VMM Type1 BareMetal"]
    HDW["Physical Hardware CPU RAM Disk NIC"]

    APP1 --> GOS1
    APP2 --> GOS1
    APP3 --> GOS2
    GOS1 --> VHARD
    GOS2 --> VHARD
    VHARD --> VMM
    VMM --> HDW
```

**How to read the diagram:** The two guest OS instances (Linux, Windows) each run their own apps. They talk to *virtual hardware*, which is emulated by the **VMM**. The VMM, in turn, is the only entity that touches the **physical hardware**. The guest OS *never* sees real hardware.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Type-1 vs Type-2 Hypervisor Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["Type 1 Bare Metal Hypervisor"]
        direction TB
        G1A["Guest OS 1"]
        G1B["Guest OS 2"]
        H1["Hypervisor ESXi Xen KVM"]
        HW1["Physical Hardware"]
        G1A --> H1
        G1B --> H1
        H1 --> HW1
    end

    subgraph T2["Type 2 Hosted Hypervisor"]
        direction TB
        G2A["Guest OS"]
        H2["Hypervisor VirtualBox VMware Workstation"]
        HOS["Host OS Windows Linux macOS"]
        HW2["Physical Hardware"]
        G2A --> H2
        H2 --> HOS
        HOS --> HW2
    end
```

**Take-away:** The Type-1 hypervisor sits *directly* on hardware. The Type-2 hypervisor is sandwiched between a guest OS and a host OS that already exists.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Building Blocks of a Virtual Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BB1["Hypervisor VMM Control Plane"]
    BB2["Virtual Hardware Platform"]
    BB3["Guest Operating System"]
    BB4["Device Emulators"]
    BB5["Virtual Network Switch"]
    BB6["Virtual Disk Manager"]
    BB7["Shadow Page Tables EPT NPT"]
    BB1 --> BB2
    BB1 --> BB3
    BB2 --> BB4
    BB2 --> BB5
    BB2 --> BB6
    BB2 --> BB7
```

**Interpretation:** The hypervisor is the *controller*; virtual hardware is the *abstraction*; the guest OS is the *consumer*. The four sub-blocks (emulators, vSwitch, disk manager, page tables) are the *implementation* of the virtual hardware.

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Two-Level CPU Scheduling in a Virtualized System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LV1["Level 1 VMM Scheduler Decides which VM gets physical CPU"]
    LV2["Level 2 Guest OS Scheduler Decides which process in the VM gets vCPU"]
    VM1["VM 1"]
    VM2["VM 2"]
    VM3["VM 3"]
    P1["Process P1 inside VM1"]
    P2["Process P2 inside VM2"]
    P3["Process P3 inside VM3"]
    PCPU["Physical CPU Cores"]

    LV1 --> VM1
    LV1 --> VM2
    LV1 --> VM3
    VM1 --> P1
    VM2 --> P2
    VM3 --> P3
    P1 --> LV2
    P2 --> LV2
    P3 --> LV2
    LV2 --> PCPU
```

**Reading guide:** First the hypervisor picks a VM; then the guest OS inside that VM picks a process. The two decisions together consume one physical CPU cycle.

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Memory Virtualization with Extended Page Tables

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GV["Guest Virtual Address GVA"]
    GP["Guest Physical Address GPA"]
    HP["Host Physical Address HPA"]
    CR3G["Guest Page Table CR3"]
    EPT["Extended Page Table EPT"]
    RAM["Physical RAM"]

    GV --> CR3G
    CR3G --> GP
    GP --> EPT
    EPT --> HP
    HP --> RAM
```

**Insight:** Without EPT, every memory access would require the VMM to walk the page table in software (the *shadow page table* approach — slow). With EPT, the hardware performs a **two-dimensional page table walk** in a single TLB miss, accelerating memory virtualization dramatically.
