## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
*Define a Virtual Machine. List any four benefits of virtualization in modern data centers.*

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

A Virtual Machine is a software-emulated replica of a physical computer that provides an isolated execution environment for an operating system and its applications.

**Four benefits:**
1. **Server Consolidation** — Multiple VMs on one physical server increase utilization from ~15% to ~80%.
2. **Isolation** — A crash in one VM does not affect other VMs or the host.
3. **Snapshots & Easy Backup** — A VM state can be frozen and restored in seconds.
4. **Hardware Independence** — VMs can be migrated live (vMotion) across physical hosts without downtime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's Tip:** *If the student writes "VM is software that runs multiple OS" they get 2/3. Only adding benefits with percentages/metrics pushes the answer to 3/3.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
*Differentiate between Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|-------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Position | Runs directly on hardware | Runs as an app on host OS |
| Host OS | Not required | Required |
| Example | VMware ESXi, KVM, Xen | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |
| Performance | Near-native | Slower (double scheduling) |

> [!NOTE]
> *Any 3 correct points of difference fetch full 3 marks. The example is mandatory.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Pattern, 14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**(a)** Explain the **three Popek–Goldberg requirements** that an architecture must satisfy to be considered *efficiently virtualizable*. State the theorem formally. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **two-level scheduling** performed inside a virtualized system. Why is this a *performance bottleneck*? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Step 1 — State the theorem (2 marks).**
> *Popek and Goldberg (1974) Theorem:* "A computer architecture is efficiently virtualizable **if and only if** the set of **sensitive instructions** is a **strict subset** of the set of **privileged instructions**."

**Step 2 — Define sensitive and privileged instructions (2 marks).**
- **Privileged Instructions** — Instructions that trap (cause a fault) when executed in user mode.
- **Sensitive Instructions** — Instructions whose behavior or result depends on the privilege mode *or* the contents of relocation/address registers.

**Step 3 — List the three required properties (3 marks).**
1. **Fidelity / Equivalence** — Any program run under the VMM must exhibit identical behavior to native execution (except for timing).
2. **Efficiency** — A statistically significant majority of guest instructions must be executed *directly by the hardware* without VMM intervention.
3. **Safety / Resource Control** — The VMM must retain complete control over all physical resources; the guest OS must never be able to directly manipulate hardware.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (a):**
> - Students *routinely lose 2 marks* by failing to state the theorem's *if-and-only-if* condition. The examiner awards full marks **only** when the strict-subset relation is mentioned.
> - Writing *"sensitive instructions = privileged instructions"* (without the **strict subset** word) is a half-answer worth 1 mark only.
> - Do **not** confuse Popek–Goldberg with virtualization in general — the theorem applies to *CPU* virtualization only.

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the two-level scheduling problem (2 marks).**
In a virtualized system, scheduling decisions must be made **twice** for every CPU dispatch:
- **Level 1** — The **VMM scheduler** (e.g., Xen Credit Scheduler, VMware VMX scheduler) selects *which VM* gets the physical CPU next.
- **Level 2** — The **Guest OS scheduler** (e.g., Linux CFS, Windows UMS) inside the selected VM decides *which process* in that VM gets the vCPU next.

**Step 2 — Draw / describe the architecture (2 marks).** *(See Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram for the standard block diagram.)*

**Step 3 — Explain the performance bottleneck (2 marks).**
- The two schedulers operate *independently* and may make **conflicting decisions**, e.g., the VMM may pre-empt a VM exactly when its guest OS is about to schedule a critical real-time process.
- **Scheduling latency** = Time spent in VMM queue + Time spent in Guest OS queue. Both are non-zero, doubling the latency vs bare metal.
- **CPU Overhead** = Each VM exit (vmexit) costs **~1000–5000 CPU cycles** to switch to VMM and back.

**Step 4 — Mitigation (1 mark).**
Modern hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) and techniques like **VCPU affinity pinning** reduce this overhead to acceptable levels.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - *Most students draw only one scheduler.* A diagram showing **both** Level-1 and Level-2 schedulers in a single flowchart is worth 2 extra marks.
> - The phrase "**vmexit cost**" appears in the model answer key. Mentioning the cycle count (≈ 1000–5000 cycles) elevates the answer from 5/7 to 7/7.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **building blocks of a virtual machine**. Identify and briefly explain any **four components** of the virtual hardware layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A physical server has 8 CPU cores and 64 GB RAM. It hosts 5 VMs, each configured with 4 vCPUs and 16 GB RAM. Compute the (i) CPU consolidation ratio, (ii) Memory overcommit ratio, and (iii) VMM overhead, given that the same workload takes 80 s on bare metal and 86 s inside the VM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Step 1 — Draw the block diagram of the three building blocks (3 marks).** *(Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram.)*
- **Hypervisor (VMM)**
- **Virtual Hardware**
- **Guest OS**

**Step 2 — Identify four components of the virtual hardware layer (4 marks).**
1. **vCPU (Virtual CPU)** — A scheduled time-slice of a physical core. Created and managed by the VMM.
2. **Guest Physical Memory** — The contiguous address space seen by the guest OS, backed by physical RAM via shadow page tables or EPT.
3. **Virtual Disk** — A file (.vmdk, .vdi, .qcow2) on the host that emulates a block device. The host can pre-allocate or grow-on-demand.
4. **Virtual Network Interface (vNIC)** — A software-emulated NIC connected to a virtual switch, which bridges to the physical NIC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (a):**
> - Writing "VM has CPU, RAM, disk, NIC" without specifying they are *virtual* and *emulated* loses 2 marks.
> - Naming file extensions (.vmdk, .qcow2) shows depth and is a 1-mark bonus.

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute CPU consolidation ratio (2 marks).**

$$
\text{CPU Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Total vCPUs allocated}}{N_{\text{physical cores}}} = \dfrac{5 \times 4}{8} = \dfrac{20}{8} = 2.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Memory Overcommit Ratio (2 marks).**

$$
\text{Mem Ratio} = \dfrac{\sum M_{\text{VM-configured}}}{M_{\text{phys}}} = \dfrac{5 \times 16}{64} = \dfrac{80}{64} = 1.25
$$

**Step 3 — Compute VMM Overhead (2 marks).**

$$
\text{Overhead}_{\%} = \dfrac{T_{\text{VM}} - T_{\text{native}}}{T_{\text{native}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{86 - 80}{80} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Overhead}_{\%} = \dfrac{6}{80} \times 100 = 7.5\%
$$

**Step 4 — Final Answers (1 mark).**

$$
\boxed{\text{CPU Ratio} = 2.5,\quad \text{Mem Overcommit} = 1.25,\quad \text{VMM Overhead} = 7.5\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - Forgetting to **multiply by 5 VMs** in Step 1 or 2 is the most common error (loses 2 marks).
> - The units of consolidation ratio are **dimensionless**, not percent. Writing "2.5%" instead of "2.5" loses 1 mark.
> - The 7.5% overhead is realistic for a Type-1 hypervisor; if your answer is > 20%, you have made a calculation error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Virtual Machines — Last-Minute Revision Bullets**

- A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is a *software emulation* of a physical computer. It uses a **Hypervisor / VMM** to multiplex hardware.
- The **three building blocks** of any VM: **Hypervisor**, **Virtual Hardware**, and **Guest OS**.
- **Hypervisors come in two types**:
  * **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** — Direct on hardware. Examples: ESXi, KVM, Xen, Hyper-V Server.
  * **Type-2 (Hosted)** — On top of host OS. Examples: VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels.
- **Popek–Goldberg Theorem**: A machine is *efficiently virtualizable* iff **sensitive instructions ⊂ privileged instructions** (strict subset).
- **Three required properties** of a VMM: **Fidelity, Efficiency, Safety** (recall order: F-E-S).
- **Two-level scheduling** is inherent: VMM picks VM → Guest OS picks process inside VM.
- **Hardware-assisted virtualization** uses **Intel VT-x / AMD-V** to reduce vmexit costs.
- **Memory virtualization** uses **Shadow Page Tables** (software) or **EPT / NPT** (hardware-accelerated).
- **CPU Virtualization** strategies: *Full Virtualization* (binary translation), *Para-virtualization* (modified guest kernel), *Hardware-assisted Full Virtualization* (VT-x).
- **Container virtualization** (Docker, LXC) shares the host kernel — *not* a true VM but a related concept; KTU 2024 distinguishes it from traditional VMs.
- **Key formulas to memorize**:
  * $\text{Consolidation Ratio} = N_{\text{VMs}} / N_{\text{physical servers}}$
  * $\text{Overcommit Ratio} = \sum M_{\text{VM}} / M_{\text{phys}}$
  * $\text{VMM Overhead}_{\%} = (T_{\text{VM}} - T_{\text{native}}) / T_{\text{native}} \times 100$
  * $\text{Proportional Share Time} = (w_i / W_{\text{total}}) \times T_{\text{total}}$
- **Five benefits of VMs** (must be able to list 5): *Consolidation, Isolation, Snapshots, Portability, Hardware Independence*.
- **The vmexit cost** is **~1000–5000 CPU cycles** per VM exit — quote this in any performance-related answer.
- **KVM is Type-1** (not Type-2). It is the *Linux kernel* acting as a hypervisor.
- **Virtualization is the technical foundation of cloud IaaS** (AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Day Mnemonic — "V-H-G"** to recall the three building blocks of a VM:
> **V** → Virtual Hardware, **H** → Hypervisor, **G** → Guest OS.
